<b>Preface</b><br>
This book Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools, is written specifically for<br>
Form Five Biology Students in the United Republic of Tanzania. The book is prepared<br>
according to the 2009 Biology Syllabus for Advanced Level Secondary Education<br>
Form V-VI, issued by the Ministry of Education and Vocational Training.<br>
The Book consists of seven chapters, which are: Cytology, Principles of classification,<br>
Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms, Coordination, Nutrition,<br>
Gaseous  exchange and respiration, and Regulation.  In addition to the content, each<br>
chapter contains activities, illustrations, exercises and revision questions. Learners<br>
are encouraged to do all activities and answer all questions so as to enhance their<br>
understanding, and promote acquisition of the intended skills, knowledge, and attitudes.<br>
<b>Tanzania Institute of Education</b><br>

<b>Introduction</b><br>
All living organisms are made up of units called cells. A cell is the basic structural,<br>
fundamental and functional unit of life. Understanding and learning about cells<br>
supports the learning of other biological processes. In this chapter, you will learn about<br>
the concept of cytology, cell theory, types of cells, and cell structure and function. You<br>
will also learn about cell differentiation, organic constituents of cells, and water as a<br>
constituent of the cell.<br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>One</b><br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
<b>1.1  Concept of cytology</b><br>
<b>Cytology is the branch of biology that</b><br>
deals with the study of the structure<br>
and function of the cells. The cell is the<br>
basic structural and functional unit of<br>
all organisms. All living organisms may<br>
be composed of a single or many cells.<br>
<b>The unicellular organisms consist of a</b><br>
single cell while multicellular organisms<br>
consist of multiple cells.  For multicellular<br>
organisms, the cells are differentiated and<br>
organised into groups to form different<br>
structures that perform various functions.<br>
<b>Robert Hooke, who is also known as the</b><br>
father of cytology, was the ﬁrst scientist to<br>
study the cell structure using a primitive<br>
microscope. In 1665, he examined a<br>
cross-section of the back of a cork tree<br>
and observed its structure (Figure 1.1).<br>
<b>He found that the cork was made up of</b><br>
small chambers surrounded by walls; he<br>
gave the spaces the name “cell” meaning<br>
“little room” or “cavity”. He thought the<br>
objects he had discovered looked like the<br>
individual rooms in a monastery, which<br>
were known as cells. The cells observed<br>
by Hooke gave no indication of the<br>
nucleus and other organelles found in most<br>
living cells. However, in his invention,<br>
<b>Hooke did not discover the true biological</b><br>
function of cells.<br>
<b>Chambers of</b><br>
the cork cell<br>
<b>Figure 1.1  First view of the cork cells by Robert</b><br>
<b>Hooke</b><br>
<b>Source: http://ucmp.berkeley.edu</b><br>
1<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   1</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
2<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>He was later followed by Anton von</b><br>
<b>Leeuwenhoek, who in 1674 discovered free</b><br>
living cells in pond water. Leeuwenhoek<br>
made a microscope consisting of a single<br>
high-quality lens which could magnify an<br>
object 270 times. This instrument was at that<br>
time referred to as a compound microscope.<br>
<b>With this microscope, Leeuwenhoek was</b><br>
able to make a number of  important<br>
scientific discoveries, including single-<br>
celled animals, plants, bacteria, and<br>
spermatozoa.<br>
<b>The importance of studying cytology</b><br>
<b>The study of cytology helps us to</b><br>
understand the role of cells as the<br>
building blocks of all living organisms,<br>
including their anatomy and physiology.<br>
<b>Additionally, the signiﬁcance of studying</b><br>
cytology includes the following:<br>
a)	 Helps to realise the role of cells<br>
in metabolic processes such as<br>
respiration, protein synthesis, excretion,<br>
and growth.<br>
b)	 Aids in understanding the detailed<br>
structure and functions of different<br>
cells.<br>
c)	 Enhance an understanding on the<br>
structural (anatomical and chemical)<br>
composition of cells.<br>
d)	 Gives knowledge about the process and<br>
signiﬁcance of cellular differentiation,<br>
that is, a process in which cells are<br>
specialised for their functions, such<br>
as reproductive cells, red blood cells,<br>
absorptive cells, and nerve cells.<br>
e)	 Enables us to understand how similar<br>
cells are organised into tissues.<br>
f)	 Helps to show how organisms are<br>
evolutionarily related. For example,<br>
in the biochemical evolution theory<br>
of origin of life on the earth, various<br>
chemical compounds were combined<br>
and mobilized into prokaryotes, which<br>
gave rise to eukaryotes in the course of<br>
evolution.<br>
g)	 Helps to understand the basis of<br>
genetics;<br>
the<br>
location,<br>
structure,<br>
chemistry, and role of nucleic acid<br>
(DNA and RNA).<br>
h)	 Cytopathology, a study of cellular<br>
abnormalities,<br>
helps<br>
to<br>
diagnose<br>
diseases by using cellular changes.<br>
<b>1.2  The Cell Theory</b><br>
<b>The origin of the concept of “cell theory”</b><br>
can be traced to as 1830’s when two<br>
scientists, Matthias Schleiden a Belgian<br>
botanist (1838) and Theodor Schwann,<br>
a German zoologist (1839) provided the<br>
ﬁrst deﬁnition of the cell. It was stated<br>
that, all living organisms, both simple<br>
and complex, are made up of one or more<br>
cells. It was described that, the cell is the<br>
structural and functional unit of life. This<br>
led to the formation of the concept of cell<br>
theory. The theory states that “All living<br>
organisms are made up of cells”. In 1855,<br>
<b>Rudolf Virchow, a German physiologist,</b><br>
was among the first scientists to accept<br>
and extend the work of Robert Hooke.<br>
<b>He showed that, the origins of cells was</b><br>
the division of pre-existing cells, and<br>
concluded that “new cells arise from pre-<br>
existing cells”.<br>
1.2.1 The main ideas of the cell theory<br>
<b>The principal ideas of the cell theory are</b><br>
as follows:<br>
a)	 All living organisms are composed of<br>
one or more cells and cell products.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   2</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
3<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
b)	 Cells are the basic or fundamental<br>
units of life, as all life processes are<br>
controlled by cells.<br>
c)	 All new cells arise from pre-existing<br>
cells.<br>
<b>However, in the modern version of the</b><br>
cell theory, the following were added:<br>
a)	 Cells contain genetic material which<br>
carry hereditary information (DNA)<br>
that is passed from one generation to<br>
another.<br>
b)	 Energy flow occurs within cells in<br>
which all metabolic processes of life<br>
occur.<br>
c)	 All cells have the same basic chemical<br>
composition, and the structure and<br>
functions of cells complement each<br>
other. The living organism’s activities<br>
depend upon the combined actions of<br>
individual cells.<br>
d)	 All new cells arise from pre-existing<br>
cells through cell division.<br>
1.2.2  Shortcomings of the cell theory<br>
<b>Due to increased understanding on cell</b><br>
biology, the concept of “cell theory” has<br>
been challenged based on the modern<br>
knowledge of virology, blood cells<br>
and origin of life. The study of viruses<br>
indicated that these living infectious<br>
particles are non-cellular. Schleiden and<br>
<b>Schwann could not explain the structure</b><br>
and functioning of a virus. They said<br>
that the body function is coordinated by<br>
the cell, which is not the case in a virus<br>
because it only starts its functioning when<br>
hosted by bacterial, plant or animal cell.<br>
<b>Thus, as per the cell theory, they are non-</b><br>
living creatures (akaryotes). However,<br>
when they are in host cells, they exhibit<br>
living characteristics. Regarding the cell<br>
theory, there should be genetic material<br>
in every cell, but mature human red blood<br>
cells and sieve tube cells of angiosperm<br>
are some of the cells that lack genetic<br>
material. Furthermore, the cell theory<br>
revealed that new cells arise from the pre-<br>
existing cells by cell division. However, it<br>
does not show the origin of the ﬁrst cell.<br>
<b>Exercise 1.1</b><br>
1.	 Explain the importance of studying<br>
cytology.<br>
2.	 Analyse the main ideas of the cell<br>
theory and its shortcomings.<br>
3.	 Explain why the cell is regarded as<br>
the basic unit of life.<br>
<b>1.3  Types of cells</b><br>
<b>Based on the level of cell organisation,</b><br>
cells can be divided into two main types,<br>
namely prokaryotic cells and eurykaryotic<br>
cells.   Prokaryotic cells have simple cell<br>
organisation, while eukaryotic cells   have<br>
high level of cell organisation.<br>
1.3.1 The prokaryotic cells<br>
<b>The word prokaryotes comes from two</b><br>
<b>Greek words pro meaning ‘primitive’</b><br>
or ‘before’ and karyo (karyon) meaning<br>
‘nucleus.’ From this basic implication,<br>
prokaryotic cells refer to those cells which<br>
lack true or well organised nuclei such that<br>
the nuclear materials are freely suspended<br>
in the cytoplasm. The common examples<br>
of prokaryotic cells are bacteria and blue-<br>
green algae. Prokaryotic cells have the<br>
following general characteristics:<br>
a)	 They are microscopic with an average<br>
diameter of 0.5-10 nm.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   3</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
4<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
b)	 They lack well organised nuclei.<br>
<b>That is, their nucleus has no nuclear</b><br>
membrane, therefore, the nuclear<br>
materials are freely suspended in the<br>
cytoplasm.<br>
c)	 They have small, circular and naked<br>
<b>DNA. That is, the DNA is not</b><br>
associated with histone protein coat to<br>
form chromosomes.<br>
d)	 They have few and small ribosomes of<br>
70s sedimentation coefﬁcient.<br>
e)	 They lack membrane bound organelles<br>
such as mitochondria, Golgi bodies,<br>
and plastids.<br>
f)	 The cell wall is chemically composed<br>
of a carbohydrate-protein complex<br>
called peptidoglycan or murein.<br>
g)	 Cilia and ﬂagella, if present do not<br>
arise from basal bodies, and they lack<br>
microtubules of a “9+2” arrangement<br>
pattern.<br>
<b>Structure of prokaryotic cells</b><br>
<b>The prokaryotic cell does not have a deﬁned</b><br>
nucleus, as it lacks a nuclear membrane.<br>
<b>Almost all prokaryotes have a protective</b><br>
cell wall that prevents them from bursting<br>
in hypotonic conditions.  Such cell walls<br>
have different components depending on<br>
the type of organism.<br>
<b>However, most of the prokaryotic cells</b><br>
have cell walls containing major organic<br>
molecules of proteins, carbohydrates and<br>
lipids. Bacterial cell walls have a unique<br>
molecule known as peptidoglycan. This<br>
component of the cell walls allows scientists<br>
to classify bacteria as either Gram-positive<br>
or Gram-negative. The Gram–positive<br>
bacterium contains many layers of<br>
peptidoglycan in the cell wall and lacks the<br>
outer membrane, while the Gram-negative<br>
bacterium contains a thin cell wall made<br>
up of a few layers of peptidoglycan and<br>
possesses the outer membrane.<br>
<b>The glycocalyx or capsule is a layer</b><br>
surrounding the cell wall of some bacteria.<br>
<b>This layer protects the bacteria from drying</b><br>
out, especially in hypertonic conditions. The<br>
ﬁmbriae are structures that help bacteria to<br>
adhere to target cells. They thus play a major<br>
role in bacterial virulence. The ﬂagella are<br>
long whip-like extensions that help bacteria<br>
to move about in the environment. The axial<br>
ﬁlaments or endoﬂagella are long structures<br>
which move in waves enabling the bacteria<br>
to spin.<br>
<b>Beneath the cell wall, there is a plasma</b><br>
membrane, which is a double layer of<br>
phospholipids associated with proteins<br>
and other molecules. It protects the<br>
intracellular materials and regulates the<br>
movement of materials into and out of the<br>
cell. In some bacteria, there is infolding of<br>
the cell membrane that forms mesosome<br>
(which appeared to be associated with<br>
<b>DNA during cell division, and also used</b><br>
in respiration), photosynthetic membrane<br>
(which contain photosynthetic pigment<br>
example bacteriochlorophyll used for<br>
photosynthesis) and or nitrogen fixing<br>
membrane (for nitrogen fixing bacteria).<br>
<b>Beneath the cell membrane, there is</b><br>
cytoplasm, a gel-like ﬂuid ﬁlling the cell.<br>
<b>It is a place where cellular organelles such</b><br>
as ribosomes are suspended. Ribosomes<br>
(70s) are small structures in the cytoplasm<br>
that play crucial role in protein synthesis<br>
within the bacterial cell (Figure 1.2).<br>

<b>Cytology</b><br>
<b>Additional structures sometimes</b><br>
present<br>
<b>Flagellum</b><br>
<b>Capsule</b><br>
<b>Infolding of cell surface membrane</b><br>
<b>Mesosomes</b><br>
<b>Plasmid</b><br>
<b>Pili</b><br>
<b>Ribosomes</b><br>
<b>Structures always present</b><br>
<b>Cell wall</b><br>
<b>Cell surface membrane</b><br>
<b>Cytoplasm</b><br>
<b>Circular DNA</b><br>
<b>Figure 1.2 The structure of a typical prokaryotic cell</b><br>
<b>The prokaryotic cell consists of the</b><br>
nucleoid, a region of the prokaryotic<br>
cytoplasm containing the genome, which<br>
is the main genetic material (Figure 1.3).<br>
<b>It possesses a single, circular DNA with</b><br>
a double-stranded DNA molecule. Some<br>
bacteria have additional genes located<br>
in small circular molecules of DNA<br>
called plasmids. These genes play roles<br>
in maintaining virulence to bacteria,<br>
for instance, by developing resistance<br>
to drugs. The resistant genes can be<br>
exchanged between bacteria through pili<br>
during conjugation process.<br>
<b>Most bacteria have a slender tubule-</b><br>
like structure on the cell wall called pili.<br>
<b>There are two types of pili. The ﬁrst type</b><br>
is used by related bacteria to exchange<br>
genetic material through the process of<br>
conjugation while the second type of<br>
pili enables bacteria to stick to their host<br>
or substrate which increases chances of<br>
infection.<br>

<b>Plasmid</b><br>

<b>Functions of parts of prokaryotic cells</b><br>
a)	 The cell wall of prokaryotic cells<br>
provides structural and protective<br>
functions. In some prokaryotes, the<br>
cell wall is surrounded by a thin sheath,<br>
while in others, it is surrounded by a<br>
slim capsule.<br>
b)	 The cytoplasm of prokaryotes is<br>
surrounded by a single cell membrane,<br>
and all metabolic processes such as<br>
protein synthesis, respiration, and<br>
replication take place within the<br>
cytoplasm.<br>
c)	 Genetic material in the form of a<br>
single circular DNA located in the<br>
speciﬁed region within the cytoplasm.<br>
<b>This controls hereditary functions of</b><br>
the cell.<br>
d)	 The ﬂagellum found in most aquatic<br>
and soil bacteria aids in movement<br>
by a unique process of spinning on an<br>
axis like a propeller.<br>
e)	 The pili, a structure located on the<br>
cell wall help bacteria to increase the<br>
chances of survival by undergoing<br>
conjugation or by enabling anchoring<br>
to its host or substrate.<br>
f)	 The mesosomes which are infoldings<br>
of the cell surface membrane which<br>
act as sites of respiratory enzymes.<br>
g)	 Granules of food stores are used as<br>
respiratory substrates.<br>
1.3.2 Eukaryotic cells<br>
<b>The term Eukaryote comes from two</b><br>
<b>Greek words eu meaning ‘true’ and</b><br>
karyon meaning ‘nucleus.’ Eukaryotic<br>
cells are those cells whose nuclei are<br>
bounded by nuclear membranes. Cells are<br>
typically composed of plasma membrane,<br>
cytoplasm, nucleus, and organelles such<br>
as mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum,<br>
ribosomes, and Golgi apparatus. The<br>
examples of eukaryotic cells are plant and<br>
animal cells. These cells are characterised<br>
by the following features:<br>
a)	 They are relatively large in size, ranging<br>
from microscopic to macroscopic.<br>
b)	 They have true or well organized<br>
nuclei with nuclear membranes.<br>
c)	 They have large and numerous<br>
ribosomes with the sedimentation<br>
speed of 80s (The 's' stand for the<br>
name Svedberg, but is also a unit of<br>
measurement).<br>
d)	 They have membrane bound organelles,<br>
such as mitochondria and plastids.<br>
e)	 The cell walls, if present, are<br>
chemically composed of cellulose<br>
and/or chitin.<br>
f)	 They have large, helical DNA which<br>
is associated with histone protein to<br>
form chromosomes.<br>
g)	 Cilia and ﬂagella, if present, arise from<br>
basal bodies and contain microtubules<br>
that are arranged in a “9+2” pattern.<br>
<b>The plant cell</b><br>
<b>Plant cells are eukaryotic and present in</b><br>
organisms of the kingdom Plantae. They<br>
have a true nucleus along with specialized<br>
structures called organelles that carry<br>
out different functions. Plant cells differ<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   6</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>

7<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
from the cells of other organisms in<br>
that they have cell walls, chloroplasts,<br>
and central vacuoles. Therefore, the<br>
distinctive features of plant cells include<br>
the presence of cell walls containing<br>
cellulose, hemicelluloses and pectin, and<br>
the presence of chloroplasts capable of<br>
performing photosynthesis.<br>
<b>Structure of the plant cell</b><br>
<b>The general structure of the plant cell</b><br>
can be viewed by using an electron<br>
microscope (Figure 1.4). A plant cell has<br>
an outer layer surrounding the cell, called<br>
a cell wall. It is composed of cellulose,<br>
which is a stiff carbohydrate. The cell wall<br>
provides protection, structural support<br>
and mechanical strength to the cell. The<br>
presence of cellulose also helps the plant<br>
cell to maintain its regular shape. The<br>
cell wall has perforations that connect<br>
cytoplasm of the neighbouring cells called<br>
plasmodesmata. Beneath the cell wall,<br>
there is a cell membrane, which is semi-<br>
permeable. Cell membrane controls the<br>
passage of materials in and out of the cell.<br>
<b>The adjacent cells are usually bound to</b><br>
one another by a thin layer called middle<br>
lamella.<br>
<b>The cytoplasm is a ﬂuid content that ﬁlls the</b><br>
cell giving its shape; it contains proteins and<br>
dissolved ions used in cellular activities.<br>
<b>It is a place where all cell organelles</b><br>
are housed. Most of the plant cells have<br>
large<br>
organelles<br>
called<br>
chloroplasts.<br>
<b>Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll which is</b><br>
a green pigment used for photosynthesis.<br>
<b>Other vital organelles found in the plant</b><br>
cells include mitochondria, endoplasmic<br>
reticulum, ribosomes and golgi vesicles.<br>
<b>Mitochondrion acts as a powerhouse of</b><br>
the cell; because it releases energy used<br>
for all cell activities.<br>
<b>The Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER) is a</b><br>
large folded membrane system found in<br>
the cytoplasm of the cell. Some ER are<br>
associated with ribosomes while others<br>
are not. The ER that are associated<br>
with ribosomes are known as Rough<br>
<b>Endoplasmic Reticulum (RER), whereas</b><br>
those not associated with ribosomes are<br>
known as Smooth Endoplasmic Reticulum<br>
<b>(SER).</b><br>
<b>Ribosomes are the organelle responsible</b><br>
for protein synthesis within a cell. Hence,<br>
the RER provides a surface for protein<br>
synthesis while the SER provides a surface<br>
for transportation of liquids and nutrients<br>
in the cell. There are small vesicles in the<br>
cytoplasm called Golgi vesicles. These are<br>
responsible for storage and transportation<br>
of secretions of the cells. In addition, the<br>
plant cell has a large central vacuole which<br>
is responsible for storage of nutrients and<br>
water. The nucleus is a vital part of the<br>
cell, as it controls all cell activities such as<br>
growth, cell division, DNA replication and<br>
its transcription to RNA. It also contains<br>
hereditary material which transmit traits<br>
from parents to the offsprings.<br>

<b>Activity 1.1 Observation of a plant</b><br>
cell under a light microscope<br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>Onion</b><br>
bulb,<br>
a<br>
light<br>
microscope,<br>
microscope slide, slide cover, petri dish<br>
or watch glass, iodine solution, water,<br>
forceps, a needle, and a knife or surgical<br>
blade or scalpel.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Slice the onion longitudinally into<br>
small parts by using a knife or<br>
scalpel.<br>
b)	 Using forceps, remove the thin<br>
transparent inner lining.<br>
c)	 Put a specimen on a watch glass or<br>
petri dish containing some water to<br>
prevent shrinking.<br>
d)	 Mount the specimen at the centre of<br>
the microscope slide.<br>
e)	 Add a drop of iodine solution.<br>
f)	 With the help of a needle, place the<br>
cover slip gently. Make sure there are<br>
no air bubbles under the cover slip.<br>
g)	 Observe the specimen under the light<br>
microscope at low, medium, and<br>
high power objectives. The images<br>
tend to be smaller under low power<br>
objective lens.  However, if more<br>
magnification is needed, change<br>
objective lenses to medium and high<br>
power. Structures such as nucleus,<br>
cytoplasm, chloroplasts, vacuole,<br>
cell wall, and cell membrane can be<br>
clearly seen.<br>

<b>Be careful when working with sharp</b><br>

<b>The animal cell</b><br>
<b>Animal cells are eukaryotic cells with</b><br>
a membrane bound nucleus. Unlike the<br>
eukaryotic cells of plants and fungi, animal<br>
cells do not have a cell wall. Animal cell<br>
comprises of different cell organelles and<br>
cell structures which perform specific<br>
functions necessary for the cell. The cell<br>
organelles have a vast range of functions<br>
to perform. The animal cells contains<br>
centrioles, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi<br>
apparatus, lysosomes, microfilaments,<br>
microtubules,<br>
mitochondria,<br>
nucleus,<br>
peroxisomes,<br>
plasma<br>
membrane,<br>
ribosomes, cilia, and flagella.<br>
<b>Structure of the animal cell</b><br>
<b>The outer boundary of the animal cell</b><br>
is a cell membrane; which is selectively<br>
permeable to substances and controls the<br>
exchange of materials between the cell<br>
and its environment. Beneath the cell<br>
surface membrane, there is a protoplasm<br>
which is made up of cytoplasm and the<br>
nucleus (Figure 1.5). Like in the plant<br>
cell, the cytoplasm of the animal  cell<br>
is jelly-like ﬂuid ﬁlling the cell and it<br>
houses all cell organelles. The cytosol<br>
or cytoplasmic matrix is a ﬂuid found in<br>
the cell organelles, such as mitochondria<br>
and ribosomes. The cytosol is a site of<br>
metabolic activities in the cell organelles.<br>
<b>Mitochondria are called the powerhouses</b><br>
of the cell; since they release energy in<br>
the form of ATP through the process of<br>
respiration. The produced energy is used<br>
to run metabolic activities of the cell.<br>
<b>The nucleus is the central part containing</b><br>
the hereditary (genetic) material. It also<br>
controls cell activities such as growth, cell<br>
division, DNA replication, and protein<br>
synthesis.<br>
<b>Ribosomes are the smallest organelles</b><br>
responsible for protein synthesis in the<br>
cell. Some ribosomes are located on the<br>
surface of the endoplasmic reticulum<br>
which is a large folded membrane system<br>
in the cytoplasm. Like plant cell, animal<br>
cell has rough endoplasmic reticulum and<br>
smooth endoplasmic reticulum. Moreover,<br>
the animal cell has small vesicles in the<br>
cytoplasm known as Golgi vesicles which<br>
are responsible for the storage of enzymes<br>
and formation of lysosomes. Lysosomes<br>
are small structures in the cytoplasm which<br>
contain digestive juices responsible for<br>
breaking down of old cell parts. There are<br>
also long structures called microtubules or<br>
microﬁlaments. These are responsible for<br>
all cell movements, and they provide the<br>
cell with cytoskeletal support.<br>

<b>Activity 1.2 Observation of an animal</b><br>
cell under a light microscope<br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>A plastic spoon, a light microscope,</b><br>
slides, cover slips, a needle, and<br>
methylene blue.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Gently scrub the inside of your cheek<br>
with a plastic spoon to obtain a cell<br>
sample. Plastic spoons are used to<br>
avoid damaging the cheek cells.<br>
b)	 Place the cheek cells sample on a<br>
clean slide.<br>
c)	 Add a drop of methylene blue and<br>
mix using a pointed needle.<br>
d)	 Cover the specimen with a cover<br>
slip. Make sure that there are no air<br>
bubbles under the cover slip.<br>
e)	 Observe the specimen under the light<br>
microscope at low, medium, and high<br>
power objectives. You will see that,<br>
at the low power objective, the cells<br>
appear very small; in contrast, at the<br>
high power objective, they appear to<br>
be relatively larger.<br>

Figure 1.5.<br>

1.	 Be careful when working with sharp<br>
objects such as needle, knife and<br>
surgical blade.<br>
2.	 Do not share instruments used to<br>
obtain cell sample.<br>

and animal cells as they are seen<br>
under high power magnification of<br>
light microscope.<br>

in (a) above.<br>

cytoplasm and its functions.<br>
3.	Explain how the discovery of the<br>
electron microscope improved<br>
knowledge on cell structure.<br>
4.	Describe the contribution of each<br>
of the following scientists in cell<br>
biology:<br>
a)	 Robert Hooke (1665).<br>
b)	 Theodor Schwann and Mathias<br>
Schleiden.<br>
<b>Structure and functions of the cell</b><br>
<b>In general, a cell consists of cell</b><br>
membrane, the nucleus and the cytoplasm.<br>
<b>Every cell in the body is enclosed by a</b><br>
cell membrane. The cell membrane is<br>
the protective barrier that surrounds the<br>
cell, which separates the material outside<br>
the cell (extracellular), from the material<br>
inside the cell (intracellular). It is the<br>
barrier that maintains the integrity of a cell<br>
and controls passage of materials into and<br>
out of the cell. Apart from cell membrane,<br>
other cells such as plant and fungal cells<br>
have an additional outer layer called a cell<br>
wall. It is formed on the outside of the<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   11</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
12<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
cell membrane to give the cell an extra<br>
support, protection, as well as efficiency<br>
and regulates exchange of materials.<br>
<b>The nucleus is the largest cellular</b><br>
structure, located inside eukaryotic cell<br>
and acts as the control centre of a cell.<br>
<b>The material between the cell membrane</b><br>
and the nuclear envelope is known as<br>
cytoplasm. Within the cytoplasm lies<br>
an intricate arrangement of fine fibres<br>
and thousands of miniscule but distinct<br>
structures called cytoplasmic organelles<br>
and cytoplasmic inclusion such as stored<br>
nutrients, secretory products, droplets and<br>
pigment granules. Cytoplasmic organelles<br>
are small structures that are suspended<br>
in the cytoplasm of the cell. Examples<br>
of<br>
cytoplasmic<br>
organelles<br>
include:<br>
endoplasmic<br>
reticulum,<br>
ribosomes,<br>
mitochondria, plastids, vacuole, lysosome,<br>
microbodies, cytoskeleton, and Golgi<br>
apparatus. Each type of organelle has a<br>
definite structure and a specific role in the<br>
cell. Cytoplasmic inclusions do not have<br>
any membrane or specific shapes.<br>
<b>Plasma membrane</b><br>
<b>This is also called plasmalemma or cell</b><br>
membrane. It surrounds most of the cell<br>
organelles, separates the contents of the<br>
cell from the external environment, and<br>
controls the exchange of materials. In<br>
animal cells, plasma membrane is the<br>
outermost layer, whereas in plant cells it<br>
is located beneath the cell wall.<br>
<b>Structure of the cell membrane</b><br>
<b>The cell membrane is made up of proteins</b><br>
and<br>
lipids<br>
(phospholipids).<br>
<b>Protein</b><br>
molecules are embedded in the layers of<br>
the phospholipids. Phospholipids form a<br>
bilayer structure which is fundamental to<br>
the selective permeability function of the<br>
membrane. Each phospholipid molecule<br>
consists of a hydrophobic (water hating)<br>
tail of two fatty acids and the hydrophilic<br>
(water loving) phosphate head. In the<br>
cell membranes, phospholipids arrange<br>
themselves in a layer of two molecules<br>
thick (bilayer), with their hydrophobic tails<br>
pointing inwards, away from the water<br>
both inside and outside the cell, while the<br>
hydrophilic heads face outwards. There<br>
are two models suggested to describe<br>
the structure of the plasma membrane,<br>
namely Danielli-Davson’s model (1935)<br>
and Fluid Mosaic model (1972).<br>
<b>Danielli-Davson’s Model</b><br>
<b>According to Danielli and Davson, the</b><br>
membrane is structurally composed of two<br>
chemical substances made up of proteins<br>
and lipids that form trilaminar layers<br>
(Figure. 1.6). The outer and inner layers<br>
are made up of protein molecules which<br>
sandwich the phospholipids bilayer. The<br>
heads of phospholipids are polar, oriented<br>
towards the protein layers to form the<br>
hydrophilic region.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   12</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
13<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
<b>Protein layer</b><br>
<b>Hydrophilic phospholipid head</b><br>
<b>Hydrophobic phospholipid tail</b><br>
<b>Phospholipid</b><br>
bilayer<br>
Figure 1.6 Model of plasma membrane as proposed by Daniel and Davson<br>
<b>The model explains the presence of polar</b><br>
head (hydrophilic) and non-polar tail<br>
(hydrophobic) in the phospholipids. The<br>
proteins bilayers are continuous and are of<br>
the same size. The model did not explain<br>
how material enters or leaves the cell. The<br>
model also indicate that the membrane is<br>
static, never change its structure and water<br>
passes freely between the adjacent protein<br>
molecules. The protein bilayers are on the<br>
outside part, while phospholipids bilayers<br>
are at the middle part (intermediately). The<br>
model explains the presence of proteins<br>
and phospholipids. Moreover, this model<br>
does not explain how cell recognizes<br>
external signals and due to this weakness,<br>
further studies were conducted and a new<br>
model was developed. This was the Fluid<br>
Mosaic Model.<br>
<b>The Fluid Mosaic Model</b><br>
<b>The model was proposed by Seymour</b><br>
<b>J. Singer and Garth L. Nicolson in</b><br>
1972. According to this model, protein<br>
molecules are dispersed and inserted into a<br>
phospholipid bilayer, which is hydrophilic<br>
when exposed to water (Figure 1.7). This<br>
provides maximum contact of hydrophilic<br>
regions and heads of phospholipids with<br>
water while providing the hydrophobic<br>
part with a non-aqueous environment.<br>
<b>Due to the ﬂuid nature of phospholipids</b><br>
and the arrangement pattern of protein<br>
molecules in the phospholipid bilayer, the<br>
model is referred to as the Fuid Mosaic<br>
Model.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   13</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
14<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Lipids and lipid-soluble substances are</b><br>
allowed to cross the hydrophobic region<br>
while non-lipids are not allowed to<br>
cross. Large molecules, such as glucose,<br>
fatty acids, glycerol and amino acids<br>
are repelled by the hydrophobic region<br>
and therefore, they diffuse through<br>
special transport proteins called channel<br>
proteins and carrier proteins by facilitated<br>
diffusion.<br>
<b>The strength of the model is that, it</b><br>
realizes the presence of the phospholipid<br>
bilayer and protein layer. It also realizes<br>
the presence of pores in the membrane<br>
and the presence of cholesterol in the<br>
membrane. The model explains the<br>
presence of polar head (hydrophilic)<br>
and non-polar (hydrophobic) tail in<br>
phospholipids. Furthermore, the model<br>
explains the presence of glycolipid, as<br>
receptors that allow the cell to respond<br>
to chemical messengers which regulate<br>
the activities of the cell. It is true that the<br>
membrane is a dynamic (ever-changing its<br>
structure) with phospholipids in constant<br>
motion. The weakness of the model is that<br>
it does not indicate or explain the presence<br>
of electrolytes in the plasmalemma.<br>
<b>Functions of the plasma membrane</b><br>
a)	 It covers the surface of every living cell<br>
and acts as a barrier which separates<br>
the cytoplasm from extracellular<br>
environment.<br>
b)	 It allows passage of some materials<br>
in and out of the cells (selective<br>
permeability).<br>
c)	 It facilitates the transmission of nerve<br>
impulse in the nervous system.<br>
d)	 It functions as a receptor site for<br>
hormonal and neural transmission of<br>
chemical stimuli.<br>
e)	 It aids cell-to-cell recognition when<br>
membranes of two cells come together.<br>
f)	 It serves as a base for attachment of<br>
the cytoskeleton in some organisms<br>
and as the cell wall in others. Thus, the<br>
cell membrane supports the cell and<br>
maintains its shape.<br>
<b>Adaptations of the cell surface</b><br>
membrane<br>
<b>The cell membrane is specialised to its</b><br>
functions due to:<br>
a)	 Presence of glycolipids, glycoproteins<br>
and phospholipids for detection of<br>
stimuli, such as antigens and antibodies.<br>
<b>Glycoprotein (a branch of</b><br>
carbohydrate attached to protein)<br>
<b>Peripheral</b><br>
protein<br>
<b>Phospholipid</b><br>
bilayer<br>
<b>Trasmembrane protein</b><br>
<b>Protein on one side of the membrane</b><br>
<b>Channel protein with pore</b><br>
<b>Cholesterol</b><br>

the membrane over a wide range of<br>
temperature.<br>

<b>Cell wall</b><br>
<b>Plant cells, fungal cells, and bacterial cells</b><br>
are surrounded by fairly rigid, non-living<br>
walls called cell walls (Figure 1.8). The<br>
chemical composition of cell walls among<br>
these organisms differs. The plant cell wall<br>
is made up of cellulose. The fungal cell<br>
wall is made up of chitin. The bacterial<br>
cell wall, is made up of peptidoglycan<br>
called murein.<br>
<b>A cell wall is a protective layer around the</b><br>
plasma membrane. It also determines the<br>
shape of plant cells. Originally, the cell<br>
wall in plants is a product of cytoplasm.<br>
<b>The cytoplasmic organelles such as</b><br>
the endoplasmic reticulum and Golgi<br>
apparatus play a very important role in the<br>
formation of cell walls. The formation of<br>
a new cell wall usually takes place in a<br>
dividing cell immediately after the nuclear<br>
division.<br>
<b>Structure of the cell wall</b><br>
<b>Plant cell wall, which is made up of</b><br>
cellulose and strengthened by other<br>
polysaccharides; is a protective layer<br>
around the cell surface membrane. The<br>
primary cell wall in plants is composed<br>
of cellulose, hemicelluloses and pectin.<br>
<b>This primary wall is laid down during cell</b><br>
division in plants. The primary cell wall<br>
thickens into secondary cell wall. During<br>
secondary thickening some cells undergo<br>
ligniﬁcation (hardening) whereby lignin<br>
is deposited in all cellulose layers. Lignin<br>
cements and anchors ﬁbres together,<br>
making the cell wall very hard and<br>
increases tensile strength. Suberin and<br>
cutin are also found in the cell wall during<br>
secondary thickening. These are waxy<br>
materials acting as a water proof coat<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   15</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>

<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
which helps to prevent excessive water loss from the plant. Middle lamella is a thin<br>
layer of pectin materials (calcium and magnesium) which joins neighbouring cell walls<br>
together.<br>
<b>Close group of</b><br>

pit field) – primary cell<br>
walls are thinner in this<br>
area as a result of slower<br>
growth<br>
‘Pit membrane’ – middle<br>

<b>Figure 1.8 Structure of a cell wall</b><br>
<b>Functions of the cell wall</b><br>
a)	 It provides mechanical support and<br>
strength to cells and the plant as a<br>
whole, due to presence of lignin.<br>
b)	 It maintains the shape of the cell, since<br>
the cell wall is rigid.<br>
c)	 It prevents the osmotic bursting<br>
of the cell by inhibiting excessive<br>
endosmosis due to its capability to<br>
resist expansion.<br>
d)	 It protects the plant cells against<br>
pathogens and water loss due to the<br>
presence of waxy cutin on the cell<br>
wall.<br>
e)	 The walls of xylem vessels, tracheids<br>
and phloem sieve tubes are tubular;<br>
therefore, they allow movements of<br>
materials to long distances.<br>
f)	 Cell walls possess small pores or<br>
pits through which the materials can<br>
pass from one cytoplasm to another<br>
(symplastic movement).<br>
g)	 Cutin and suberin deposits prevent<br>
loss of water from the cell surface<br>
through evaporation.<br>
h)	 The cell walls of root endodermal<br>
cells are suberized to form casparian<br>
strips. These, among other functions,<br>
regulate the amount of water to be<br>
admitted into the plant.<br>
i)<br>
Cell walls of some cells store food.<br>

<b>Cytoplasm and cytoplasmic organelles</b><br>
(sub-cellular units)<br>
<b>The space between the cell membrane</b><br>
and the nucleus is ﬁlled with translucent,<br>
homogenous and colloidal ﬂuid called<br>
a cytoplasmic liquid. The latter is an<br>
aqueous substance containing different<br>
types of cell organelles, water and mineral<br>
salts. It also contains organic compounds,<br>
such as carbohydrates, lipids, proteins,<br>
nucleic acids and enzymes.<br>
<b>Functions of cytoplasm</b><br>
a)	 It provides medium for chemical<br>
reactions such as glycolysis to take<br>
place.<br>
b)	 It stores useful materials such as<br>
starch, glycogen, and lipids.<br>
c)	 It stores waste materials such as<br>
nitrogenous wastes.<br>
d)	 Movement of materials takes place<br>
within the cytoplasm.<br>
e)	 It harbours and organises cell<br>
organelles that perform different<br>
functions such as protein synthesis<br>
in ribosomes and lipid synthesis in<br>
smooth endoplasmic reticulum.<br>
<b>Cytoplasmic organelles</b><br>
<b>Cytoplasmic matrix contains numerous</b><br>
membranous internal structures called<br>
organelles. Organelles can be deﬁned<br>
as tiny distinct parts with specialised<br>
structures and functions found inside<br>
the<br>
cell. The<br>
organelles<br>
are<br>
sub-<br>
cellular structures with characteristic<br>
morphological forms, distinctive chemical<br>
constitutions and deﬁnite functions. The<br>
organelles perform speciﬁc functions<br>
such as oxidative phosphorylation and<br>
generation of energy in the form of ATP in<br>
the mitochondria, formulation and storage<br>
of carbohydrates in chloroplast, protein<br>
synthesis in ribosomes of the rough<br>
endoplasmic reticulum and concentration<br>
and packing secretions in Golgi apparatus.<br>
<b>Other functions include synthesis of lipid</b><br>
and hormones in smooth endoplasmic<br>
reticulum, degradation of macromolecules<br>
in the lysosomes, regulation of all cellular<br>
activities by nucleus, and organisation of<br>
spindle apparatus by centrioles.<br>
<b>The nucleus</b><br>
The nucleus is a functional unit of a cell.<br>
<b>It is a membrane-bound structure, located</b><br>
at the periphery of the cell membrane<br>
more or less to the centre of the cell and it<br>
contains the cell’s hereditary information.<br>
<b>It controls all cellular activities and is</b><br>
the most prominent organelle in the cell.<br>
<b>The cell nucleus is bounded by a double</b><br>
membrane called the nuclear envelope.<br>
<b>It is a selectively permeable membrane,</b><br>
allowing some materials to pass in and out<br>
and separates the contents of the nucleus<br>
from the cytoplasm. The envelope regulates<br>
the ﬂow of molecules into and out of the<br>
nucleus through nuclear pores. The pores<br>
allow exchange of substances between<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   17</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
18<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
the nucleus and cytoplasm. The pore has<br>
a deﬁnite structure formed by fusion of<br>
the outer and inner membranes of the<br>
envelope. The nucleus contains chromatin,<br>
consisting of DNA which is bounded by<br>
basic proteins called histones. During<br>
nuclear division, chromatin stains more<br>
intensely and becomes more conspicuous<br>
because it condenses into more tightly<br>
coiled<br>
threads<br>
called<br>
chromosomes.<br>
<b>However, some remains tightly coiled</b><br>
and continue to stain intensely and this is<br>
called heterochromatin. It is seen as a dark<br>
patch, usually occurring near the nuclear<br>
envelope. The remaining loosely coiled<br>
chromatin is euchromatin. It contains<br>
<b>DNA which is genetically active during</b><br>
interphase.<br>
<b>The nucleolus</b><br>
<b>The nucleolus is a cellular structure</b><br>
present in the nucleus of a eukaryotic<br>
cell. The nucleolus appears as a rounded<br>
and darkly stained structure inside the<br>
nucleus. It contains nucleolar organisers<br>
that synthesise ribosomes by transcribing<br>
and assembling ribosomal RNA (Figure<br>
1.9). The nucleoli vary in number from<br>
one to many nucleoli within a single cell<br>
nucleus of a plant or an animal cell.<br>
<b>Ribosomes</b><br>
<b>Nucleolus</b><br>
<b>Chromatin</b><br>

envelope<br>
<b>Rough endoplasmic</b><br>
reticulum<br>
<b>Figure 1.9 Structure of a nucleus</b><br>
<b>Functions of the nucleus</b><br>
a)	 It controls all metabolic activities of<br>
the cell.<br>
b)	 It contains hereditary information of<br>
the cell, hence, it transmits genetic<br>
information to the new cell.<br>
c)	 It takes part in the formation of<br>
ribosomes and RNA, thus it controls<br>
protein synthesis.<br>
d)	 It controls cell division when required<br>
since DNA replication is essential for<br>
cell division.<br>

2.	 Explain the role of nucleolus.<br>
3.	 Why is the nucleus said to be the<br>
controller of all cellular activities?<br>
<b>Endoplasmic reticulum</b><br>
<b>The endoplasmic reticulum is a network</b><br>
of flattened membranes bound sacs called<br>
cisternae. Some of the endoplasmic<br>
reticula are encrusted with ribosomes<br>
and termed Rough Endoplasmic Reticula<br>
(RER), while those without ribosomes are<br>
known as Smooth Endoplasmic Reticula<br>
(SER). The quantity of both rough and<br>
smooth endoplasmic reticula in a cell<br>
can slowly interchange from one type<br>
to the other, depending on the changing<br>
metabolic activities of the cell.<br>
<b>Rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER)</b><br>
<b>The surface of the rough endoplasmic</b><br>
reticulum contains protein-manufacturing<br>
ribosomes, giving it a “rough” appearance<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   18</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>

19<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
<b>Functions of rough endoplasmic</b><br>
reticulum<br>
<b>The rough endoplasmic reticulum has the</b><br>
following functions:<br>
a)	 They are sites for protein synthesis,<br>
due to the presence of ribosomes.<br>
b)	 They<br>
provide<br>
pathways<br>
for<br>
transportation of materials through the<br>
cell, such as proteins synthesized on<br>
the ribosomes.<br>
c)	 They provide a large surface area for<br>
chemical reactions to take place.<br>
<b>Smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER)</b><br>
<b>This type of endoplasmic reticulum</b><br>
possesses smooth walls, because the<br>
ribosomes are not attached to its membranes<br>
(Figure 1.10b). The smooth endoplasmic<br>
reticulum occurs mostly in those cells<br>
which are involved in metabolism of<br>
lipids such as steroids and glycogen. The<br>
smooth endoplasmic reticula are generally<br>
found in adipose cells, interstitial cells,<br>
and glycogen storing cells of the liver.<br>
<b>They also occur in conduction ﬁbres of</b><br>
the heart, spermatocytes and leucocytes.<br>
<b>The muscle cells are also rich in smooth</b><br>
endoplasmic reticulum known as the<br>
sarcoplasmic reticulum.<br>
<b>Functions of smooth endoplasmic</b><br>
reticulum<br>
a)	 For synthesis, secretion, and storage<br>
of lipids, carbohydrate and other non-<br>
protein products.<br>
b)	 It contains enzymes which breakdown<br>
chemical substances in liver cells.<br>
c)	 For synthesis of steroids which later<br>
form hormones.<br>

bound to it at any one time are not a stable<br>
part of this organelle’s structure as they are<br>
constantly being bound and released from<br>
the membrane. A ribosome only binds to<br>
the endoplasmic reticulum once a speciﬁc<br>
protein-nucleic acid complex forms in the<br>
cytosol. This special complex forms when<br>
a free ribosome begins to translate the<br>
mRNA.<br>

<b>Mitochondrion</b><br>
<b>A mitochondrion (plural mitochondria) is</b><br>
an organelle bound by double membranes<br>
and it is found in eukaryotic cells.<br>
<b>Mitochondria have been described as</b><br>
‘the powerhouses of the cell’ because<br>
they generate most of the cell’s chemical<br>
energy originally contained in respiratory<br>
substrates. This energy is liberated in the<br>
form of adenosine triphosphate (ATP).<br>
<b>The structure of the mitochondrion</b><br>
<b>Each mitochondrion is bounded by two</b><br>
highly specialised membranes. Thus, it is<br>
a double membrane organelle. The outer<br>
membrane is quite smooth, it has many<br>
copies of a transport protein called porins,<br>
which forms large aqueous channels<br>
through the lipid bilayer. On the inside is<br>
the inner membrane separated from the<br>
outer membrane by a space, measuring<br>
6 - 8 nm. The inner membrane is deeply<br>
folded into infolding known as cristae,<br>
which project in the matrix space.<br>
<b>Generally, the organelle has an oval shape,</b><br>
although it can change from one form to<br>
another depending on the physiological<br>
conditions of the cells. The matrix contains<br>
few small ribosomes (70s), phosphate<br>
granules, respiratory enzymes (such as<br>
<b>ATP synthase) and small mitochondrial</b><br>
<b>DNA which is circular and naked (Figure</b><br>
1.11).<br>
<b>Porins in the outer</b><br>
membrane<br>
<b>Stalked particles</b><br>
<b>Cristae</b><br>
<b>Phosphate granule</b><br>
<b>Ribosome</b><br>
<b>Inter membrane space</b><br>
<b>Inner membrane</b><br>
<b>Outer membrane</b><br>
<b>Mitochondrial DNA</b><br>
<b>Matrix</b><br>
<b>Figure 1.11 A structure of a mitochondrion</b><br>
<b>Functions of mitochondria</b><br>
a)	 It is a site for aerobic respiration, hence<br>
releases energy in the form of ATP.<br>
b)	 It is an intermediate site for synthesis<br>
of important biomolecules such as<br>
chlorophyll, cytochromes, steroids,<br>
and fatty acids.<br>
c)	 The mitochondrion can store and<br>
release calcium ions when required.<br>
<b>Hence, it regulates calcium ion</b><br>
concentration in the cell.<br>
<b>Adaptations of mitochondria</b><br>
<b>The mitochondrion is specialised to its</b><br>
function because:<br>
a)	 The outer membrane contains lots<br>
of porins that allow the passage of<br>
molecules for respiration.<br>
b)	 The inner membrane is highly folded<br>
into cristae that increase its surface<br>
area.<br>
c)	 The membranes are permeable to<br>
allow the passage of all diffusable<br>

10/10/2019   14:05<br>

21<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
materials. This allows raw materials in<br>
and products out.<br>
d)	 The<br>
matrix<br>
contains<br>
appropriate<br>
enzymes used in respiratory pathways<br>
such as Kreb’s cycle.<br>
a)	 Presence of windows or fenestra in the<br>
membranes facilitates the passage of<br>
materials in and out.<br>
<b>Plastids</b><br>
<b>These are membrane bound organelles</b><br>
found in the cells of plants, algae, and<br>
other eukaryotic organisms. They often<br>
contain pigments and the type of pigment<br>
determines the cell colour. They are<br>
mainly responsible for activities related<br>
to making and storing food. The plastids<br>
include<br>
chromoplasts,<br>
gerontoplasts,<br>
leucoplasts, and chloroplasts.<br>
<b>Chromoplasts.  Pigmented  plastids found</b><br>
in ﬂowers, aging leaves, and fruits such<br>
as tomato and red pepper. They contain<br>
carotenoid pigments; mainly red, orange<br>
or yellow which give different colours<br>
seen in different parts of the plant. They<br>
assist in pollination and seed dispersal.<br>
<b>Gerontoplasts. Plastids that develop from</b><br>
the chloroplasts of the leaves or other<br>
parts of the plant that are going through<br>
the ageing process (senescence) or are<br>
converted into different organelles. This<br>
occurs when such parts of the plant are no<br>
longer carrying out photosynthesis.<br>
<b>Leucoplasts. Colourless plastids found in</b><br>
non-photosynthetic parts of the plant such<br>
as roots, seeds, and bulbs. They are used<br>
for storage of starch, lipids, and proteins<br>
particularly in roots and tubers. They<br>
are subdivided into three different types,<br>
namely<br>
amyloplasts,<br>
proteinoplasts,<br>
and elaioplasts. Each of these has a<br>
distinctive<br>
function.<br>
<b>For</b><br>
instance,<br>
amyloplasts are responsible for storage<br>
of starch, proteinoplasts are responsible<br>
for storage of proteins, while elaioplasts<br>
are responsible for storage of fats and oils<br>
which are needed by plants. They are also<br>
used for synthesis of amino acids and fatty<br>
acids.<br>
<b>Chloroplasts. These are most commonly</b><br>
known plastids which play an essential<br>
role in enabling plants and some<br>
algae to make their own food through<br>
photosynthesis. They are found in green<br>
parts of plants, mainly leaves and in some<br>
other organisms such as photosynthetic<br>
bacteria and green algae. The term<br>
chloroplast comes from two Greek words:<br>
chloros, meaning ‘green’ and plast,<br>
meaning ‘form’. It contains green pigment<br>
known as chlorophyll, enzymes and other<br>
molecules that function in photosynthesis.<br>
<b>Structure of chloroplasts</b><br>
<b>The contents of chloroplasts are portioned</b><br>
from the cytosol by an envelope consisting<br>
of two membranes separated by a very<br>
narrow inter-membrane space. Inside<br>
the chloroplast is another membranous<br>
system in the form of ﬂattened sacs called<br>
thylakoids or lamellae. In some regions,<br>
thylakoids pile up like a stack of coins to<br>
form grana (singular, granum).  The ﬂuid<br>
outside the thylakoids is the stroma, which<br>
contains the chloroplast DNA, ribosomes<br>
(70s), starch granules as well as many<br>
photosynthetic enzymes. The chlorophyll<br>
and carotenoids are present in thylakoids<br>
and grana (Figure 1.12).<br>

<b>Functions of the chloroplast</b><br>
a)	 The chloroplast is the fundamental<br>
site for the photosynthesis process.<br>
b)	 It is the site for protein synthesis, due<br>
to the presence of ribosomes in the<br>
stroma.<br>
c)	 It stores starch and lipids for plant<br>
use.<br>
<b>Adaptation of the chloroplasts</b><br>
<b>Chloroplasts are specialised for their</b><br>
function as follows:<br>
a)	 They have permeable membranes<br>
which allow movement of raw<br>
materials in and products out.<br>
b)	 The presence of numerous grana<br>
provides a large surface area for<br>
photosynthetic pigments, electron<br>
carriers and ATP synthase involved<br>
in the light dependent reaction.<br>
c)	 Arrangement<br>
of<br>
photosynthetic<br>
pigments in photosystems allows<br>
maximum absorption of light energy.<br>
d)	 Proteins embedded in the grana hold<br>
photosystems and their chlorophyll in<br>
proper position for harvesting light.<br>
e)	 The stroma contain enzymes needed<br>
to catalyse the reactions in the light<br>
independent phase of photosynthesis.<br>
f)	 Contains chloroplast DNA for self-<br>
replication and inheritance.<br>
g)	 They have ribosomes needed for<br>
protein synthesis.<br>

and<br>

prokaryotic cells in the eukaryotic<br>
cells.<br>

the following organelles:<br>
a)	 Chloroplast<br>

23<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
<b>Vacuoles</b><br>
<b>Vacuoles are membrane bounded sacs</b><br>
which are found within the cytoplasm<br>
of a cell. The vacuoles of plant cells are<br>
bounded by single membranes called<br>
tonoplast. These vacuoles are formed<br>
when vesicles released by the endoplasmic<br>
reticulum and Golgi apparatus merge<br>
together. Young plants consist of a number<br>
of smaller vacuoles which during growth<br>
and development, they fuse to form a large<br>
central permanent vacuole. Therefore, in a<br>
mature plant cell, cytoplasm is displaced<br>
and seen as a thin layer near the periphery<br>
of the cell surrounding the large central<br>
vacuole. The central positioning of the<br>
vacuole push the nucleus more or less to<br>
the periphery or near to the cell surface<br>
membrane. At this stage, the vacuole is<br>
ﬁlled with cell sap, which contains water,<br>
phenol, mineral salts, alkaloids, pigments,<br>
sugars, and proteins. Animal cell lack<br>
vacuoles, but if present they are small<br>
and temporary, associated with storage of<br>
food and other materials to be secreted,<br>
transported or removed. Example, food<br>
vacuole or vesicle (phagocytotic or<br>
pinocytotic vesicles).<br>
<b>Functions of vacuoles</b><br>
<b>Vacuoles in plant cells play the following</b><br>
roles:<br>
a)	 They support herbaceous plants and<br>
herbaceous parts of woody plants by<br>
providing an osmotic system which<br>
creates a pressure potential.<br>
b)	 They act as temporary stores for<br>
different substances, such as food,<br>
enzymes, and waste materials.<br>
c)	 They have hydrolytic enzymes which<br>
destroy dead cells or the entire plant<br>
cell<br>
(autolysis).<br>
<b>Plant</b><br>
vacuoles<br>
can function in the same way as in<br>
lysosomes of animal cells as they both<br>
participate in an automatic cell death<br>
by autolysis. This process occurs when<br>
the tonoplast ruptures and releases its<br>
contents (enzymes) into the cytoplasm<br>
which digests the entire cell.<br>
d)	 They play vital role in primary growth,<br>
since the pressure exerted by fusion<br>
of small vacuoles leads to elongation<br>
of the cells. Hence, this increases the<br>
length of a plant organ.<br>
e)	 They contain anthocyanins which<br>
provide colour to ﬂowers, fruits,<br>
and buds. These pigments facilitate<br>
pollination and seed dispersal.<br>
<b>Ribosomes</b><br>
<b>Ribosomes are tiny organelles found in</b><br>
the matrix of mitochondria, chloroplasts,<br>
and cytoplasm of the cells. They occur in<br>
both, prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells. In<br>
prokaryotic cells they are found freely in<br>
the cytoplasm, whereas in the eukaryotic<br>
cells they are either attached to the outside<br>
of the endoplasmic reticulum (or nuclear<br>
envelope) or occur freely in the cytoplasm.<br>
<b>Structurally, each ribosome is made up of</b><br>
two sub-units, a small sub-unit and a large<br>
sub-unit (Figure 1.13). Basing on their<br>
size and sedimentation coefficient, there<br>
are two types of ribosomes. These are:<br>
a)	 70s ribosomes: These are relatively<br>
smaller, with sedimentation coefﬁcient<br>
of 70s and  molecular weight of  2.7x106<br>
<b>Daltons. They occur in prokaryotic</b><br>
cells, such as in blue-green algae and<br>
bacteria.They also occur in eukaryotic<br>
cells organelles, such as mitochondria<br>
and chloroplasts.<br>

b)	 80s ribosomes: These have the<br>
sedimentation<br>
coefﬁcient<br>
of<br>
80s<br>
and the molecular weight of 40x106<br>
<b>Daltons. The 80s ribosomes occur in</b><br>
eukaryotic cells.<br>
<b>The ribosomes of mitochondria and</b><br>
chloroplasts are always smaller than<br>
cytoplasmic ribosomes and are comparable<br>
to prokaryotic ribosomes in both size and<br>
sensitivity to antibiotics.<br>

<b>Figure 1.13 Structure of ribosome</b><br>
<b>Functions of ribosomes</b><br>
a) 	Ribosomes<br>
are<br>
sites<br>
of<br>
protein<br>
synthesis;<br>
they<br>
provide<br>
surface<br>
area or space for polypeptide chain<br>
construction and enzymes for the<br>
synthesis of peptide bonds between<br>
amino acid monomers. Hence, they<br>
are known as protein factories. The<br>
ribosomes are adapted for protein<br>
synthesis, as they have ribosomal RNA<br>
(rRNA) which provides attachment<br>
points for both messenger RNA<br>
(mRNA) and transfer RNA (tRNA).<br>
b) 	Ribosomes have receptor sites on the<br>
small sub-unit where mRNA binds<br>
itself and they are able to “read” and<br>
“translate” the message contained in<br>
the mRNA codes.<br>
c)  They have enzymes that catalyse the<br>
synthesis of peptide bonds.<br>
<b>Lysosomes</b><br>
<b>Lysosomes</b><br>
are<br>
membrane-enclosed<br>
organelles that contain an array of enzymes<br>
capable of breaking down all types of<br>
biological polymers proteins, nucleic<br>
acids, carbohydrates, and lipids. The word<br>
lysosomes come from the two words lyso<br>
means ‘digestive’ and soma means ‘body.’<br>
<b>They are also referred to as suicide bags</b><br>
since they undergo self-destruction, hence<br>
the digestion of the entire cell (autolysis).<br>
<b>They mostly occur in animal cells and</b><br>
few or rare in plant cells.  Lysosomes<br>
are not present in bacteria and mature<br>
erythrocytes, while a few of them occur<br>
in muscle cells. Leucocytes, especially<br>
granulocytes, are particularly rich sources<br>
of lysosomes. In addition, they are<br>
numerous in the epithelial cells of lungs<br>
and uterus.<br>
<b>Structure of lysosomes</b><br>
<b>Lysosomes are round, with vacuolar</b><br>
structure which remains ﬁlled with dense<br>
material and bounded by a unit membrane.<br>
<b>They consist of digestive enzymes,</b><br>
which are synthesized in the ribosomes<br>
of the rough endoplasmic reticulum. The<br>
enzymes, which always have acidic pH,<br>
are transported to the Golgi apparatus<br>
for modiﬁcation. The Golgi apparatus in<br>
the cytoplasm concentrates and packs the<br>
modiﬁed enzymes in the Golgi vesicles,<br>
which pinch off as lysosomes. Their shape<br>
and density vary greatly from cell to cell<br>
and time to time.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   24</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
25<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
<b>Functions of lysosomes</b><br>
<b>The main functions of lysosome include</b><br>
heterophagy, autophagy and autolysis.<br>
<b>Heterophagy</b><br>
<b>This is a process whereby lysosomes</b><br>
perform the intercellular digestion of<br>
materials that are gathered from outside<br>
the cell by endocytosis, which are<br>
pinocytosis (taking in liquid materials) and<br>
phagocytosis (taking in solid materials). The<br>
endocytotically ingested food material is<br>
collected in a membrane bounded vesicle<br>
called heterophagosomes (pinosomes or<br>
phagosomes) and eventually fuse with<br>
lysosomes to form phagolysomes in which<br>
the engulfed material is digested. Example<br>
when bacteria are ingested by phagocytosis,<br>
the ingested bacteria are packed in a<br>
phagocytic vesicle (phagosomes).This<br>
vesicle fuses with a primary lysosome,<br>
forming a secondary lysosome. Thereafter,<br>
enzymes of the lysosome digest the bacterial<br>
macromolecules.<br>
<b>Autophagy</b><br>
<b>Autophagy allows the orderly degradation</b><br>
and recycling of cellular components.<br>
<b>This process occurs when cytoplasmic</b><br>
components become enclosed in a double<br>
membrane (phagophore) to form a<br>
compartment known as autophagosome<br>
for degradation (Figure 1.14). This is<br>
the regulated mechanism of the cell that<br>
disintegrates unnecessary or dysfunctional<br>
components. Autophagy starts with the<br>
formation of a phagophore from the<br>
membrane; this expands and engulfs the<br>
molecules or “cargo” for degradation. The<br>
cargo can include redundant organelles or<br>
any other chemical aggregates and once<br>
engulfed, they are contained within the<br>
autophagosome (autophagic vacuole).<br>
<b>The latter can then fuse with lysosomes,</b><br>
which result in the release of lysosomal<br>
acid enzymes to degrade the contents of<br>
the autophagosome. The products of the<br>
degradation include amino acids, among<br>
other molecules and all of them are<br>
released back into the cytoplasm and re-<br>
used in metabolism as well as in building<br>
macromolecules.<br>
<b>Autolysis</b><br>
<b>Autolysis is more commonly known</b><br>
as self-digestion, which refers to the<br>
destruction of a cell through the action of<br>
its own enzymes. Lysosomes break down<br>
to release its contents (enzymes) which<br>
digest various organelles and ﬁnally the<br>
entire cell in certain pathological conditions.<br>
<b>This process is known as autolysis or self-</b><br>
destruction of the cell.<br>
<b>Exocytosis or extracellular digestion</b><br>
<b>Exocytosis is the process responsible</b><br>
for breaking down damaged cellular<br>
components and some unwanted proteins<br>
which are expelled out of the cell. One<br>
of the causes of aging is that, in long-<br>
lived cells the types of metabolic waste<br>
that cannot be broken down accumulates<br>
and lysosomes may release them outside<br>
the cell by exocytosis. For example; the<br>
enzymes of lysosome are released during<br>
the replacement of cartilage by bone during<br>
development. Also lysosomes of certain<br>
cells such as spermatozoa discharge their<br>
enzymes outside the cell during fertilization<br>
process.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   25</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
26<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Lysosomes are adapted to their functions</b><br>
due to the presence of hydrolytic enzymes<br>
which catalyse degradation of unwanted<br>
cellular substances. In addition, lysosomes<br>
have the membranes that isolate hydrolytic<br>
enzymes from the rest of the cytoplasm.<br>
This prevents unnecessary autolysis.<br>

of lysosomes.<br>
3.	 Giving reasons, mention the parts<br>
of your body which are expected to<br>
have large number of lysosomes.<br>
<b>Microbodies</b><br>
<b>Microbodies are small, spherical organelles</b><br>
bounded by a single phospholipid bilayer<br>
membrane which contain a matrix of<br>
intracellular materials such as catalase<br>
(peroxidase) enzyme and other proteins<br>
(Figure1.15).  They are present in almost<br>
all eukaryotic cells and are mostly seen near<br>
the ER, and sometimes near mitochondria<br>
and plastids. They can be distinguished<br>
from other cell organelles by their contents.<br>
<b>Microbodies include peroxisomes,</b><br>
glyoxysomes, and glycosomes. Peroxisomes<br>
are particularly predominant in the liver and<br>
kidney cells of vertebrates, while in plants<br>
they are found mostly in plant cells where<br>
photorespiration occurs. Glyoxosomes<br>
are specialized microbodies found in<br>
plants, particularly in fat storage tissues<br>
of germinating seeds and in filamentous<br>

<b>Functions of microbodies</b><br>
a)	 Microbodies such as peroxisomes<br>
found in plant and animal cells<br>
are involved in the breakdown of<br>
poisonous hydrogen peroxide to<br>
water and oxygen in the presence of<br>
peroxidase enzyme.<br>
b)	 The peroxisomes contain enzyme<br>
glycolic acid oxidase that oxidises<br>
glycolic<br>
acid,<br>
a<br>
product<br>
of<br>
photosynthesis, to glyoxylic acid, by<br>
the process called photorespiration.<br>
c)	 In plants, there are special microbody<br>
called glyoxysomes. These are the<br>
centre for the glyoxylate cycle,<br>
which involves conversion of fats<br>
into carbohydrate, especially in<br>
germinating seeds.<br>
d)	 Microbodies also help in conversion<br>
of stored lipid in germination oil<br>
seeds and liver cells into glucose in<br>
the process called gluconeogenesis.<br>
<b>Golgi apparatus</b><br>
<b>The name of this organelle was derived</b><br>
from its founder, Camillo Golgi, in 1898.<br>
<b>He identified it in the nerve cells of the</b><br>
owl and cat. This happened long before the<br>
discovery of the endoplasmic reticulum.<br>
<b>Golgi apparati are involved in important</b><br>
cellular functions, such as biosynthesis<br>
of polysaccharides, packaging of cellular<br>
synthetic<br>
products,<br>
production<br>
of<br>
exocytotic vesicles, and differentiation of<br>
cellular membranes. It occurs in all cells,<br>
except in the prokaryotic cells and in<br>
cells of certain eukaryotes, such as fungi<br>
and bryophytes. It also does not occur<br>
in cells of mature sieve tubes of plants,<br>
spermatozoa, and in red blood cells.<br>

10/10/2019   14:05<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>

<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Structure of the Golgi bodies</b><br>
<b>It is disc-shaped; consisting of central,</b><br>
ﬂattened, plate-like, compartments or<br>
cisternae (Figure 1.16). It is a modiﬁed<br>
smooth endoplasmic reticulum. The<br>
<b>Golgi apparatus is associated with small,</b><br>
spherical sac-like structures called Golgi<br>
vesicles. These contain various secretions<br>
such as hormones, mucus or enzymes, and<br>
they are future lysosomes.<br>
<b>Functions of the Golgi bodies</b><br>
<b>The functions of Golgi bodies include the</b><br>
following:<br>
a)	 They form lysosomes.<br>
b)	 They concentrate and pack<br>
secretions, such as enzymes.<br>
c)	 They are involved in transformation<br>
of spermatids into mature<br>
spermatozoa.<br>
d)	 They are involved in formation of<br>
primary cell walls in plants.<br>
<b>Cytoskeleton</b><br>
<b>These are complex network of protein</b><br>
ﬁlaments and microtubules which exist<br>
in the cytoplasm of eukaryotic cells.<br>
<b>It anchors proteins or organelles, such</b><br>
as nucleus to their ﬁxed location. The<br>
cytoskeleton consists of microtubules,<br>
microﬁlaments and intermediate ﬁlaments<br>
(Figure 1.17).<br>

<b>Figure 1.17 Structures of the cytoskeletons</b><br>
<b>Microtubules</b><br>
<b>These are tubular structures made up of</b><br>
arranged globular tubulin. They are found<br>
in the cytoplasm of animal and plant cells.<br>
<b>They occur in cilia, ﬂagella, centrioles,</b><br>
the cortex of meristematic cells, and basal<br>
bodies.<br>
<b>Functions of microtubules</b><br>
a)	 They determine the shape of the cell.<br>
b)	 They form a framework along which<br>
the plant cell wall is laid down.<br>
c)	 In cilia and ﬂagella, they help beating<br>
of rhythmic movements.<br>
d)	 They bring about movement of<br>
chromosomes during anaphase in<br>
nuclear division.<br>
e)	 Since they are tubular, they transport<br>
materials from one part of the<br>
cytoplasm to another.<br>
f)	 They are involved in the movements<br>
of other cell organelles such as Golgi<br>
vesicles, lysosomes, and mitochondria.<br>
<b>For example, the movement of Golgi</b><br>
vesicles towards the center to form cell<br>
plate during the formation of a primary<br>
cell wall in plant cells is brought about<br>
by microtubules.<br>
<b>Microﬁlaments</b><br>
<b>These are much narrower than microtubules,</b><br>
being only about 5-7 nm in diameter.<br>
<b>They are thread-like structures, arranged</b><br>
in sheets or in bundles beneath the cell<br>
surface membrane. They are chemically<br>
composed of a large amount of actin,<br>
hence their name actin filaments. Actin<br>
filaments, usually in association with<br>
myosin, bring about many types of cell<br>
movements. For example the contractile<br>
proteins (actin and myosin) lead to<br>
contraction and relaxation of muscles.<br>
<b>Functions of microﬁlaments</b><br>
a)	 They determine the shape of cells;<br>
since they offer a cytoskeletal support.<br>
b)	 The cleavage of animal cells during<br>
cytokinesis is brought about by the<br>
constriction of a ring of microﬁlaments<br>
after nuclear division.<br>
c)	 They<br>
are<br>
responsible<br>
for<br>
any<br>
movement that the cell makes; due to<br>
the presence of actin and myosin that<br>
influence muscle cells contraction and<br>
relaxation.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   29</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
30<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Intermediate filaments</b><br>
<b>These structures are found between</b><br>
microtubules<br>
and<br>
microﬁlaments.<br>
<b>They provide shapes of cells and act as</b><br>
intercellular tendons, preventing excessive<br>
stretching of the cells.<br>
<b>Centrioles</b><br>
<b>A centriole is a cylindrical organelle</b><br>
found in animal cells,   algal cells and<br>
fungal cells, but not in cells of higher<br>
plants. Animal cells contain a pair of<br>
centrioles in the cytoplasm which usually<br>
lie at right angles to each other close to<br>
the nuclear membrane. In cross section,<br>
each centriole is seen to contain nine<br>
groups of microtubules with three tubules<br>
in each group (Figure 1.18). During cell<br>
division, the centrioles divide and migrate<br>
chromosome in opposite poles of the<br>
cell where they act as a focus for spindle<br>
formation. Centrioles also produce the<br>
basal bodies from which cilia and flagella<br>
develop.<br>
(a)<br>
(b)<br>
Figure 1.18 Structure of centriole (a) side view and (b) cross section<br>
<b>Exercise 1.9</b><br>
1.	 What do you understand by the<br>
term cytoskeleton?<br>
2.	 Explain the functions of microtu­<br>
bules and microﬁlaments in plant<br>
and animal cells.<br>
3.	 Describe the structure, location and<br>
functions of centrioles in animal<br>

<b>Comparison of prokaryotic and</b><br>
eukaryotic cells<br>
<b>Common features which can be found in</b><br>
prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells are as<br>
follows:<br>
a)	 Both have cell membranes that<br>
separate the cell’s interior from its<br>
surrounding environment.<br>
b)	 Both  have a cytoplasm that consists<br>
of a jelly-like region within the cell in<br>
which other cellular components are<br>
floating.<br>
c)	 Both have ribosome particles that<br>
synthesize proteins, and the genetic<br>
material of the cell.<br>
<b>The prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells also</b><br>
differ in many aspects as elaborated in the<br>
Table 1.1.<br>

<b>Cytology</b><br>

<b>Prokaryotic cells</b><br>
<b>Eukaryotic cells</b><br>

<b>Mainly unicellular (some</b><br>
cyanobacteria may be<br>
multicellular).<br>

<b>Protoctista which have many</b><br>
unicellular organisms).<br>
<b>Nucleus</b><br>
Have no true nucleus.<br>
<b>Have a true nucleus, bound by a</b><br>
double membrane.<br>
<b>Double membrane</b><br>
bound organelles<br>
<b>Have no double</b><br>
membrane bound<br>
organelles.<br>
<b>Have many double membrane bound</b><br>
organelles, such as mitochondria,<br>
chloroplasts, and nucleus.<br>
<b>DNA</b><br>
Have circular DNA.<br>
Have helical or linear DNA.<br>
Have naked DNA.<br>
<b>Have DNA associated with proteins</b><br>
known as histones and is organised<br>
into chromosomes.<br>
<b>Ribosomes</b><br>
<b>Have small and</b><br>
simple ribosomes (70s<br>
ribosomes).<br>
<b>Have larger and more complex</b><br>
ribosomes (80s ribosomes).<br>
<b>Cytoplasm</b><br>
<b>Have only one type of</b><br>
organelles, that is, the<br>
ribosomes.<br>
<b>Filled with a large and complex</b><br>
collection of organelles, many<br>
of them enclosed in their own<br>
membranes.<br>
<b>Cell size</b><br>
<b>Small in size</b><br>
Relatively large in size.<br>
<b>Functional</b><br>
compartments<br>
<b>Have only one membrane</b><br>
(the plasma membrane)<br>
enclosing all of the cell’s<br>
internal contents.<br>
<b>Have many different functional</b><br>
compartments divided by membranes.<br>
<b>Cell wall</b><br>
Mureinic cell wall.<br>
Cellulose or chitinous cell wall.<br>

<b>Cell division</b><br>
<b>Involves binary fission or</b><br>

<b>No meiosis. Transfer</b><br>
of DNA is done by<br>

<b>Involves meiosis during gamete</b><br>

1.3.3 Cell differentiation<br>
<b>Cellular differentiation is the process</b><br>
of cell transformation from one form to<br>
another. It leads to the development of<br>
specialised types of cells for carrying out<br>
speciﬁc functions. This process involves<br>
biochemical<br>
and<br>
structural<br>
changes.<br>
<b>The differentiated cells become more</b><br>
specialised than the undifferentiated ones.<br>
<b>In most cases differentiation occurs</b><br>
during the development of a multicellular<br>
organism, as it changes from a simple<br>
zygote to a complex system of cell types<br>
or tissues. The process continues to<br>
adulthood; as adult stem cells divide and<br>
create fully differentiated daughter cells<br>
during tissue repair. Differentiated cells<br>
change in size, shape, membrane potential,<br>
metabolic activity, and responsiveness<br>
to signals. Cell differentiation leads to<br>
cell specialisation, which in turn leads to<br>
division of labour that improves efﬁciency<br>
of the organisms. Examples of specialised<br>
cells in animals are sex cells, nerve cells,<br>
red blood cells, and epithelial cells; and<br>
in plants are xylem, phloem, root hair,<br>
and parenchyma cells. Figure 1.19 shows<br>
root hair cell as an example of specialised<br>
plant cell.<br>
<b>Cell wall</b><br>
<b>Cytoplasm</b><br>
<b>Vacuole</b><br>
<b>Nucleus</b><br>
<b>Figure 1.19 Root hair cell</b><br>
<b>Signiﬁcance of cell differentiation</b><br>
<b>Cell differentiation has the following</b><br>
signiﬁcance:<br>
a)	 It modiﬁes cells to suit their functions<br>
more efﬁciently. This means that,<br>
during differentiation the cell becomes<br>
equipped<br>
with<br>
structural<br>
and/or<br>
chemical changes to enhance its<br>
efﬁcient.<br>
<b>Examples include:</b><br>
i)	 Spermatozoa are packed with<br>
numerous<br>
mitochondria<br>
and<br>
acrosomal enzymes. They also have<br>
ﬂagella. All these features facilitate<br>
efﬁcient fertilisation of egg cells.<br>
ii)	 Female gametes (egg cells) have<br>
numerous microvilli for absorption<br>
of food from follicular cells. They<br>
also have a large proportion of<br>
cytoplasm which contains food<br>
reserve for the developing embryo.<br>
iii)	 Nerve cells have features like<br>
myelin sheath and nodes of Ranvier,<br>
which facilitate rapid transmission<br>
of impulses. The synaptic vesicles<br>
contain neurotransmitters that aid in<br>
synaptic transmission of impulses.<br>
iv)	 The cells in xylem vessels and<br>
tracheids are hollow, and have<br>
ligniﬁed cell walls for efﬁcient<br>
carriage of water and dissolved<br>
mineral salts.<br>
b)	 It enables cellular organization, as a<br>
way of forming different tissues and<br>
organs (organogenesis) through the<br>
expression of a specific set of genes<br>
within the body to perform more<br>
specialized and complex tasks.<br>
c)	 It forms the basis for embryonic stem<br>
cell research, whereby researchers<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   32</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
33<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
can identify stem cells, which can<br>
be used in the future to deal with the<br>
conditions that require transplanting,<br>
such as kidney transplanting.<br>
d)	 It helps in the future treatment of cancer<br>
patients by enabling measurement of<br>
the cancer progress at cellular level,<br>
where<br>
the<br>
cytopathologists<br>
term<br>
‘grade’ is used as a marker to determine<br>
how differentiated cell in a tumor is.<br>
