1.3<br>
<b>Observation</b><br>
of<br>
a<br>
specialised root hair cell<br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>Onion roots, surgical blade, iodine</b><br>
solution, petri dish, dropper, light<br>
microscope, and microscope slides.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Take the onion root, and use a surgical<br>
blade to cut it transversely into very<br>
thin slices.<br>
b)	 Put a slice on the petri dish, and add<br>
one drop of iodine solution using a<br>
dropper.<br>
c)	 Transfer the stained slice to the<br>
microscope slide.<br>
d)	 Observe the stained slice under a<br>
light microscope.<br>
<b>Questions</b><br>
1.	 What conclusion can you make<br>
from what you have observed?<br>
2.	 What<br>
are<br>
the<br>
features<br>
that<br>
characterise a specialised onion<br>
cell?<br>
<b>Safety precautions</b><br>
<b>Observe safety precautions when working</b><br>
with sharp objects such as knife and<br>
surgical blade.<br>
<b>Exercise 1.10</b><br>
1.	 Explain features which are found<br>
in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic<br>
cells.<br>
2.	 Using examples, explain the concept<br>
of cell differentiation.<br>
3.	 Explain why cell differentiation is<br>
generally referred to as a cellular<br>
division of labour.<br>
4.	 What is the importance of cell<br>
differentiation in animals and plants?<br>
<b>1.4 The organic constituents of cells</b><br>
(Biochemistry)<br>
<b>The cells are composed of water, inorganic</b><br>
ions and organic molecules (carbon-<br>
containing compounds). Water is the most<br>
abundant molecule which constitutes large<br>
part of cells, constituting about 70% of<br>
the total mass. It also interacts with other<br>
constituents in the biochemistry of life. The<br>
organic components of the cells include:<br>
<b>Carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, enzymes,</b><br>
nucleic acids and regulatory substances<br>
such as hormones and vitamins.<br>
1.4.1 Carbohydrates<br>
<b>Carbohydrates are among of the fundamental</b><br>
classes of macromolecules found in living<br>
organisms. These are molecules which<br>
contain the carbonyl compound (aldehyde<br>
and ketone derivatives). They are primary<br>
products of photosynthesis, and energy<br>
providing substrates for various organisms<br>
including mammals. Carbohydrates contain<br>
three elements, namely carbon, hydrogen,<br>
and oxygen, in which hydrogen and oxygen<br>
are in the ratio of 2:1 (two hydrogen atom<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   33</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
34<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
and one oxygen atom).  This ratio or<br>
proportion of hydrogen to carbon is the<br>
same as in water, hence the name hydrate<br>
of carbon. The general empirical formula<br>
for carbohydrates is Cx (H2O)y where by x<br>
and y are variables.<br>
<b>Properties of carbohydrates</b><br>
<b>They are either simple sugars or compound</b><br>
sugars. The latter are formed by condensation<br>
of the former. For example; starch is formed<br>
by condensation of several glucose units.<br>
<b>They have one hydrated carbon (CH2O),</b><br>
hence the name carbohydrates. The ratio<br>
of hydrogen to oxygen in a carbohydrate<br>
molecule is always 2:1. They are derivatives<br>
of polyhydroxyl alcohols and can be<br>
polyhydroxy aldehydes with an aldehyde<br>
group  H-C=O,<br>
-<br>
examples are glyceraldehyde,<br>
ribose, glucose, and galactose sugars.<br>
<b>They can also be polyhydroxy ketones</b><br>
with a ketone group (C=O), examples are<br>
dihydroxyacetone, ribulose, and fructose<br>
sugars. Carbohydrates can be oxidised to<br>
yield energy. For example, the oxidation of<br>
glucose during respiration.<br>
<b>C6H12O6 + 6O6</b><br>
6CO2 + 6H2O + Energy<br>
<b>Aldoses</b><br>
and<br>
ketoses<br>
are<br>
reducing<br>
compounds; they have a tendency of<br>
reducing Copper (II) in Benedict’s solution<br>
into Copper (I), which precipitates as a red<br>
solid substance of Copper (I) oxide.<br>
<b>Classes of carbohydrates</b><br>
<b>Carbohydrate can be classiﬁed according</b><br>
to the number of basic sugars or saccharide<br>
units present in a molecule. These classes<br>
include the following:<br>
a)	 Monosaccharides<br>
b)	 Disaccharides<br>
c)	 Polysaccharides<br>
<b>Table 1.2 Major types of carbohydrates and their sources</b><br>
<b>Type</b><br>
<b>Examples</b><br>
<b>Sources</b><br>
<b>Monosaccharides</b><br>
<b>Fructose</b><br>
Sweet fruits and honey.<br>
<b>Glucose</b><br>
Fruits, such as grapes, coconut water, and sweet potatoes.<br>
<b>Galactose</b><br>
Milk and dairy product.<br>
<b>Disaccharides</b><br>
<b>Sucrose</b><br>
Sugar cane and carrots.<br>
<b>Lactose</b><br>
Milk.<br>
<b>Maltose</b><br>
Malt, such as radicles of germinating cereals.<br>
<b>Polysaccharides</b><br>
<b>Starch</b><br>
Yam, irish potatoes, sweet potatoes, and green banana.<br>
<b>Cellulose</b><br>
Cell walls of various plant cells.<br>
<b>Chitin</b><br>
<b>Cell walls of fungal hyphae, and the exoskeleton of</b><br>
arthropods such as insects, crabs, and prawns.<br>

35<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
<b>Monosaccharides (Single sugars)</b><br>
<b>These are the simplest carbohydrates,</b><br>
having only one sugar or saccharide<br>
molecule, which cannot be hydrolysed<br>
into small molecules. They are classiﬁed<br>
according to two different characteristics;<br>
the placement of its carbonyl group and<br>
the number of carbon atoms it contains. As<br>
for the ﬁrst characteristic, if the carbonyl<br>
group is an aldehyde, the monosaccharide<br>
is an aldose and if the carbonyl group is<br>
a ketone, the monosaccharide is a ketose.<br>
<b>Monosaccharide are further classified</b><br>
according to the number of carbon atoms<br>
they contain, therefore, monosaccharides<br>
with three carbon atoms are called trioses;<br>
those with four carbon atoms are called<br>
tetroses; those with ﬁve carbon atoms<br>
are called pentoses; and those with<br>
six carbon atoms are called hexoses.<br>
<b>These two systems of classiﬁcation of</b><br>
monosaccharides are often combined.<br>
<b>For</b><br>
example,<br>
glyceraldehyde<br>
an<br>
aldotriose (a three-carbon aldehyde),<br>
ribose is an aldopentose (a ﬁve-carbon<br>
aldehyde), glucose is an aldohexose (a<br>
six-carbon aldehyde), dihydroxyacetone<br>
is a ketotriose (a three-carbon ketone),<br>
ribulose is a ketopentose (a five-carbon<br>
ketone) and fructose is a ketohexose (a<br>
six-carbon ketone).<br>
<b>Trioses</b><br>
<b>These</b><br>
are<br>
the<br>
smallest<br>
molecules of monosaccharides<br>
which contain three carbon<br>
atoms<br>
in<br>
their<br>
molecules.<br>
<b>Examples are glyceraldehyde</b><br>
and dihydroxyacetone. Their<br>
empirical formula is C3H6O3.<br>
<b>Tetroses	 They</b><br>
are<br>
monosaccharides<br>
containing four carbon atoms<br>
such as erythrose and threose.<br>
<b>Their empirical formula is</b><br>
<b>C4H8O4.</b><br>
<b>Pentoses	 They contain ﬁve carbon atoms</b><br>
in their molecules, examples<br>
are<br>
ribose,<br>
deoxyribose,<br>
ribulose and arabinose. Their<br>
empirical formula is C5H10O5.<br>
<b>Hexoses	 These are monosaccharides</b><br>
containing six carbon atoms in<br>
their molecules, such as glucose,<br>
fructose, and galactose. Their<br>
empirical formula is C6H12O6.<br>
<b>They are the most common</b><br>
monosaccharides.<br>
<b>Heptoses	 They contain seven carbon</b><br>
atoms in their molecules. Their<br>
empirical formula is C7H14O7.<br>
<b>Properties of monosaccharides</b><br>
<b>Most of the monosaccharides have a sweet</b><br>
taste. They exist in a crystalline solid form at<br>
room temperature and are extremely soluble<br>
in water; despite their high molecular<br>
weights. The presence of large number of<br>
<b>OH groups makes the monosaccharides</b><br>
much more water soluble than most other<br>
molecules of similar molecular weight.<br>
<b>Moreover, all monosaccharides are reducing</b><br>
sugars; as they have free aldehyde or ketone<br>
group. They reduce mild oxidizing agents,<br>
such as Tollens’, Fehling or Benedict’s<br>
reagents.<br>
<b>Open chain and ring forms of pentose</b><br>
and hexose sugars<br>
<b>Pentose and hexose sugars can exist in both</b><br>
open chains (straight structures) and ring<br>
structures. Example furanose or furan ring<br>
has five membered ring, and pyranose or<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   35</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
36<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
pyran ring has six membered ring structures.<br>
<b>These aromatic ring forms are the most</b><br>
stable and used to form disaccharides and<br>
polysaccharides. The ring form occurs in<br>
aqueous solution and can form two isomers<br>
which are either alpha (α) or beta (β) isomers.<br>
<b>The α form is when the hydroxyl (OH)</b><br>
group on carbon atom number 1 projects<br>
below the ring while β form is when the OH<br>
group on carbon atom number 1projects<br>
above the ring. Pentose sugars may form<br>
the five membered ring when their carbon<br>
atom number 1 joins with the oxygen atom<br>
of carbon number 4, an example is as shown<br>
in ribose and deoxyribose sugars (Figure<br>
<b>1.20 a and b). The only difference that exists</b><br>
between ribose and deoxyribose sugar is<br>
that deoxyribose sugar lacks oxygen in<br>
carbon number 2. Hexoses can form both<br>
six and five membered ring. For example<br>
glucose can exist in two isomers of six<br>
membered rings (α- glucose or β-glucose).<br>
<b>The formation of ring form is when the</b><br>
oxygen atom of carbon number 5 joins to<br>
the carbon number 1 bearing the aldehyde<br>
and transfer its hydrogen to it and break the<br>
bond to form the OH either above or below<br>
the ring. The oxygen is part of the ring<br>
and carbon number 6 sticks up out of the<br>
ring. The same process occurs in galactose.<br>
<b>The only difference between galactose and</b><br>
glucose is the different orientation of their<br>
hydroxyl group in carbon number 4 (Figure<br>
<b>1.20 c and d). In fructose, the carbonyl group</b><br>
is in carbon number 2, so the ring is formed<br>
by the oxygen atom of carbon number 5<br>
joining with carbon number 2, leading to<br>
the formation of furan ring. When the OH<br>
in carbon atom number 1 is projected below<br>
the ring, it becomes α-fructose while when<br>
<b>OH in carbon atom number 2 is projected</b><br>
above the ring is β-fructose (Figure 1.20<br>
e). Fructose can also form the pyronose as<br>
in glucose.<br>

<b>Disaccharides (double sugars)</b><br>
<b>They are formed through condensation of</b><br>
two monosaccharides; examples include<br>
sucrose, maltose, and lactose. They are<br>
composed of two monosaccharide units<br>
bound together by a covalent bond known<br>
as a glycosidic bond. They are formed<br>
via dehydration (condensation) reaction<br>
resulting in the loss of a hydrogen atom<br>
from one monosaccharide and a hydroxyl<br>
group from the other. The formula of<br>
disaccharides is C12H22O11. Although there<br>
are numerous kinds of disaccharides, a<br>
handful of disaccharides are particularly<br>
notable. Sucrose is the most abundant<br>
disaccharide, and the main form in which<br>
carbohydrates are transported in plants.<br>
<b>It is composed of one glucose molecule</b><br>
and one fructose molecule. Lactose, a<br>
disaccharide composed of one galactose<br>
molecule and one glucose molecule,<br>
occurs naturally in mammalian milk.<br>
<b>Other</b><br>
notable<br>
disaccharides<br>
include<br>
maltose which is made up of two glucose<br>
molecules linked by 1, 4 –glycosidic bond.<br>
<b>Properties of disaccharides</b><br>
<b>Disaccharides are composed of two</b><br>
molecules of monosaccharides linked<br>
to each other by glycosidic bond. Like<br>
monosaccharides, disaccharides are sweet<br>
in taste and crystalline water soluble<br>
compound. All disaccharides cannot pass<br>
through the plasma membrane of the cell,<br>
since there is no carrier enzyme that can<br>
carry disaccharides to move across the<br>
plasma membrane. Among disaccharides,<br>
maltose and lactose are reducing sugars,<br>
while sucrose is a non-reducing sugar.<br>
<b>Maltose (malt sugar)</b><br>
<b>Maltose is a double sugar which occurs</b><br>
naturally in roots and radicles of germinating<br>
cereals, such as maize, sorghum and ﬁnger<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   37</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
38<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
millet. Artiﬁcially, it is made up by chemical<br>
combination of two a-glucose units. During<br>
this combination -OH group at carbons<br>
1 and 4 of the two glucose residues, are<br>
involved in formation of oxygen covalent<br>
bond called glycosidic bond. Since it is<br>
formed between carbons 1 and 4, then<br>
it is termed a 1, 4-glycosidic bond. This<br>
process involves condensation, therefore,<br>
a molecule of water is lost (Figure 1.21).<br>
<b>Maltose is a reducing sugar since it has</b><br>
a free aldehyde group in its molecule. In<br>
one of the glucose units, the aldehyde at<br>
carbon 1 has been used in the formation of<br>
the bond, while in the second glucose unit,<br>
the aldehyde at carbon 1 remained intact,<br>
since the carbon involved in the formation<br>
of the bond is that at position 4.<br>
Figure 1.21 Chemical combination of two a-glucose units to form maltose<br>
<b>Sucrose (cane sugar)</b><br>
<b>This is a double sugar that naturally</b><br>
occurs in stems of sugar cane plants. It<br>
is made up by chemical combination of<br>
glucose (an aldose sugar) and fructose (a<br>
ketose sugar). During the reaction, the –<br>
<b>OH group at carbon 1 of glucose and that</b><br>
at carbon 2 of fructose contribute to the<br>
formation of the 1,2-glycosidic bond. The<br>
reaction is also condensation; therefore,<br>
a molecule of water is lost (Figure 1.22).<br>
<b>Sucrose is a non-reducing sugar because</b><br>
it lacks any active reducing group. The<br>
aldehyde group at carbon 1 of glucose<br>
and ketone group at carbon 2 of fructose<br>
have their –OH group contributing to the<br>
formation of the glycosidic bond.<br>
Figure 1.22 Chemical combination between glucose and fructose to form sucrose<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   38</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
39<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
<b>Lactose (milk sugar)</b><br>
<b>This is found exclusively in the milk of</b><br>
mammals and in milk products. Lactose<br>
is the only carbohydrate of milk which<br>
is synthesized by mammary gland<br>
during lactation. It is derived from the<br>
condensation of galactose and glucose<br>
linked by 1, 4 - glycosidic bond (Figure<br>
1.23).<br>
<b>Polysaccharides (multi-sugars)</b><br>
<b>Polysaccharides are polymer carbohydrate</b><br>
molecules, composed of long chains of<br>
monosaccharide units bound together by<br>
glycosidic bonds. On hydrolysis, they<br>
give their constituent monosaccharides.<br>
<b>They range in structure, from linear to</b><br>
highly branched molecules, examples<br>
include storage polysaccharides such as<br>
starch and glycogen as well as structural<br>
polysaccharides such as cellulose and<br>
chitin. Starch is the main storage of<br>
polysaccharides in plants.  It is made up of<br>
two polymers, amylose and amylopectin<br>
while in animals and fungi, glycogen is<br>
the main storage form of polysaccharides.<br>
<b>In plants, cellulose is a structural</b><br>
constituent of their cell walls while in<br>
fungi and most arthropods, chitin is the<br>
structural constituent of the cell wall and<br>
exoskeleton respectively.<br>
<b>Properties of polysaccharides</b><br>
<b>Polysaccharides are complex carbohydrate</b><br>
polymers, consisting of several<br>
monosaccharides, linked together by<br>
glycosidic bonds. They are large molecules<br>
which are often insoluble in water and exist<br>
in non-crystal form.<br>
<b>Starch</b><br>
<b>Starch is a polymer of hexose sugar (α</b><br>
glucose) that can be extracted as a white<br>
powder. It is a major storage carbohydrate of<br>
plants, and it is a product of photosynthesis.<br>
<b>Chemically, starch is a polymer of repeated</b><br>
a-glucose units that are bonded together<br>
by glycosidic bonds. This polysaccharide<br>
is a mixture of two substances; amylose<br>
and amylopectin. Amylose unit is a linear,<br>
helical chain which consists of around 500<br>
to 20,000 monosaccharides connected by a<br>
(1-4) glycosidic bonds between the glucose<br>
units. Amylopectin differs from amylose in<br>
being highly branched, and has short side<br>
chains of about 30 glucose units linked<br>
with α (1-6) glycosidic bonds in addition<br>
to 1,4-glycosidic bonds (Figure 1.24).<br>
<b>Amylopectin molecules may contain up</b><br>
to two million glucose units.<br>
Figure 1.23 Chemical combination between galactose and glucose to form lactose<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   39</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>

<b>Glycogen</b><br>
<b>Glycogen</b><br>
is<br>
the<br>
form<br>
in<br>
which<br>
polysaccharide is stored in animals and<br>
fungi. It is often called an animal starch,<br>
and it is stored mainly in liver and skeletal<br>
muscles. Like starch, it is made up of<br>
a-glucose molecules and exists as granules.<br>
<b>Structurally, it resembles amylopectin,</b><br>
except that it is more branched, and its<br>
chains are shorter (Figure 1.25). The<br>
glucose chains are organized globularly<br>
like branches of a tree. They originate<br>
from a pair of molecules of glycogen in a<br>
protein which acts as a primer at the core of<br>
the structure. Glycogen can be converted<br>
back into glucose when energy is needed<br>
through the process called glycogenolysis.<br>
<b>Glycogen is easily converted back to</b><br>
glucose in order to provide energy for<br>
body activities. When glucose cannot be<br>
stored as glycogen or used immediately<br>
for relese of energy, it is converted into<br>
fat.<br>

<b>Cellulose</b><br>
<b>Cellulose is an important structural</b><br>
polysaccharide of plants, which largely<br>
constitutes the chemistry of the cell wall.<br>
<b>It is chemically composed of several</b><br>
thousands of b-glucose units, joined<br>
together by 1, 4-glycosidic bonds, to form<br>
long, unbranched chains. Many chains run<br>
parallel to each other and have cross linkages<br>
between them (Figure 1.26). These help to<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   40</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>

41<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
give cellulose its considerable stability to<br>
make it a valuable structural material. It is<br>
the major component of the plant cell walls,<br>
where it plays structural role.<br>
<b>Cellulose’s structural strength has made it</b><br>
a valuable raw material in manufacturing<br>
various industrial products, such as cotton,<br>
which is a pure form of cellulose. It is also<br>
used for manufacturing of fabrics.  Rayon,<br>
a product of cellulose extract is used in<br>
manufacturing of industrial belts, tyre cods<br>
and clothing.  Cellulose derivatives have<br>
various uses, for example; cellophane is<br>
used in packaging, and celluloid is used<br>
for manufacturing of photographic ﬁlm.<br>
<b>Other cellulose products include paper</b><br>
and explosives.<br>
<b>Figure 1.26  Structure of cellulose</b><br>
<b>Chitin</b><br>
<b>Chitin is an extracellular structural</b><br>
polysaccharide found in large quantities<br>
in the body covering cuticle of insects<br>
and exoskeletons of crustaceans. Chitin is<br>
also found in smaller amounts in sponges,<br>
molluscs, annelids and cell walls of fungi.<br>
<b>Also, been identified in the cell walls of</b><br>
some green algae. Its structure and chemistry<br>
are similar to those of cellulose. Chitin, like<br>
cellulose, has its repeating units joined in β<br>
(1,4) linkages. However, the two differ in<br>
that, the hydroxyl group (-OH) of chitin at<br>
carbon atom number two (C-2), is replaced by<br>
–NH.CO.CH3 (acetyl-amino) group. It is<br>
thus a result of an amino sugar, glucose<br>
amine, combining with an acetyl group<br>
(CH CO-). Chitin, is therefore a polymer<br>
of acetyl, glucose and amine (Figure 1.27).<br>

<b>Chitin has also been proven to be useful</b><br>
for several medical and industrial<br>
purposes.Chitin is used as a flocculating<br>
agent for waste water, wound-healing<br>
agent, a thickener and stabilizer for foods<br>
and pharmaceuticals. Also, it used as a<br>
binder for dyes, fabrics, and adhesives,<br>
and a sizing and strengthening agent for<br>
paper. In butterfly wing scales, chitin is<br>
often organized into stacks of nano-layers<br>
or nano-sticks made of chitin nanocrystals<br>
that produce various iridescent colours by<br>
thin-film interference.<br>
<b>Biochemical test for carbohydrates</b><br>
<b>Carbohydrates are carbon compound</b><br>
that have polyhydroxy aldehydes and<br>
polyhydroxy ketones, with reducing<br>
properties. Carbohydrates may be present<br>
as isolated molecules or they may be<br>
physically associated or chemically bound<br>
to other molecules. The specific test for a<br>
particular type of carbohydrate is based<br>
on the presence of specific component of<br>
carbohydrate or the reducing properties<br>
resulting from aldehyde or ketone groups.<br>
<b>Different methods can be used to test for</b><br>
the presence of starch, reducing sugars and<br>
non-reducing sugars (Table 1.3).<br>
a)	 Biochemical test for starch<br>
<b>The presence of starch in biological materials</b><br>
can be tested by using Iodine solution. The<br>
use of iodine to test the presence of starch<br>
is one of common experiments. A solution<br>
of iodine (I2) and potassium iodide (KI) in<br>
water has a light orange-brown colour. If is<br>
added to a sample that contains starch, the<br>
color changes to a dark blue (or blue black).<br>
<b>In the absence of starch, the orange-brown</b><br>
colour of the aqueous solution remains.<br>
<b>The basis of the test</b><br>
<b>The Iodine bound inside the helical structure</b><br>
of the amylose forms a dark blue colour.<br>
<b>The reaction is due to the formation of</b><br>
polyiodide chains (complex) from the<br>
reaction of starch and iodine. The amylose in<br>
starch forms helices where iodine molecules<br>
are assembled, forming a dark blue colour<br>
(Figure 1. 28).<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   42</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>

<b>Cytology</b><br>
<b>Amylose</b><br>
<b>Iodine complex (iodide ions)</b><br>
Figure 1.28 A helical structure of amylose bound the iodine molecule<br>
b)	 Biochemical test for reducing sugars<br>
<b>All monosaccharides and some disaccharides,</b><br>
including maltose and lactose (with<br>
exception of sucrose), are reducing sugars;<br>
as one of the two units may have an open-<br>
chain form with an aldehyde group.<br>
<b>This means that they carry out a type of</b><br>
chemical reaction known as reduction.<br>
<b>The characteristic property of reducing</b><br>
sugars is that, in aqueous medium, they<br>
generate one or more compounds containing<br>
an aldehyde group. An aldehyde can be<br>
the source of electrons that reduces ions.<br>
<b>The oxidizing agent must be capable of</b><br>
oxidizing aldehydes, but not alcohols. Such<br>
oxidising agents include Benedict’s solution<br>
(CuSO4/citrate), Fehling's reagents (CuSO4/<br>
tatrate), and Tollen's reagent [2[Ag(NH3)2]+].<br>
<b>However, sucrose, in which the anomeric</b><br>
carbons of the two units are linked together,<br>
are non-reducing disaccharides since neither<br>
of the rings is capable of opening. Such<br>
disaccharides do not have a free reducing<br>
group and are therefore non-reducing<br>
sugars.<br>
<b>The common test for reducing sugar is</b><br>
<b>Benedict’s test. Fehling’s reagent is not</b><br>
commonly used because it is corrosive and<br>
toxic. In this test, Benedict’s solution makes<br>
use of the ability of these sugars to reduce<br>
copper (II) into copper (I). The test involves<br>
the use of an alkaline solution of Copper<br>
(II) Sulphate (CuSO4) which is reduced to<br>
insoluble Copper (I) oxide (Cu2O). In the<br>
absence of reducing sugar, the blue colour<br>
of the Benedict’s solution remains.<br>
<b>The basis of the test</b><br>
<b>Benedict’s</b><br>
solution<br>
contains<br>
copper<br>
sulphate. Reducing sugars reduce soluble<br>
blue copper sulphate; containing copper<br>
(II) ions (Cu2+) to insoluble red-brown<br>
copper oxide containing copper (1).<br>
<b>Cu2+ + e-</b><br>
<b>Cu+</b><br>
<b>Blue solution</b><br>
<b>Brick red ppt</b><br>
c) Biochemical test for non-reducing<br>
sugar<br>
<b>A non-reducing sugar is a carbohydrate that</b><br>
is not oxidized by a weak oxidizing agent<br>
in basic aqueous solution.<br>

solution (No heating is<br>

solution to the solid form of<br>
starch.<br>

<b>Benedict’s test</b><br>

<b>Add 2 ml of Benedict’s</b><br>
solution, and heat gently to<br>

colouration of the<br>
mixture turns green,<br>
then yellowish to<br>
orange and may ﬁnally<br>
form a brick- red<br>
precipitates.<br>

sugar present.<br>
<b>Non-</b><br>
reducing<br>
sugar<br>

sugar (such as sucrose)<br>
solution in a clean and dry<br>
test-tube.<br>
<b>Add 1ml dilute hydrochloric</b><br>
acid, heat gently, then allow to<br>
cool.<br>
<b>Carefully, neutralize with 1 ml</b><br>
of sodium hydroxide solution.<br>
<b>Add 2 ml of Benedict’s</b><br>
solution and heat to boil.<br>
<b>The initial blue</b><br>
colouration of the<br>
mixture turns green, then<br>
yellowish to orange and<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
45<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
<b>In basic aqueous medium, non-reducing</b><br>
sugars do not generate any compounds<br>
containing an aldehyde group. Due to<br>
that, it cannot donate electrons to other<br>
molecules; hence, cannot act as a reducing<br>
agent. Sucrose is the most common non-<br>
reducing sugar. The linkage between the<br>
glucose and fructose units in sucrose, which<br>
involves aldehyde and ketone groups, is<br>
responsible for the inability of sucrose to<br>
act as a reducing sugar. A non-reducing<br>
sugar does not reduce copper (II) sulphate;<br>
therefore, there is no direct test for it.<br>
<b>However, if it is first hydrolysed to its</b><br>
constituent monosaccharides, it will then<br>
give a positive Benedict’s test results.<br>
<b>The basis of the test</b><br>
<b>A non-reducing sugar (sucrose) can</b><br>
be hydrolysed by heating with dilute<br>
hydrochloric acid to give glucose and<br>
fructose, both of which are reducing sugars.<br>
<b>The solution is neutralized with dilute</b><br>
sodium hydroxide or potassium hydroxide<br>
so as to give the reducing sugar results with<br>
the Benedict’s test on heat.<br>
<b>Activity 1.4 Biochemical test for</b><br>
carbohydrates<br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>Sweet potatoes, coconut seed, apparati</b><br>
(test tubes, test tube racks, test tube<br>
holder and brush, beaker, scalpel or<br>
surgical blade, measuring cylinder,<br>
droppers, mortar and pestle), reagents<br>
(Benedict’s solution, iodine solution,<br>
dilute hydrochloric acid, dilute sodium<br>
hydroxide solution,  and distilled water),<br>
and a source of heat.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Prepare two solutions, one from<br>
sweet potatoes and another one<br>
from coconut pulp.<br>
b)	 Carry out biochemical test using<br>
the given reagents and apparati to<br>
investigate food substances present<br>
in each of the solutions.<br>
<b>Question</b><br>
<b>State roles of each food substance found</b><br>
in the tested solutions.<br>
<b>Functions of carbohydrates</b><br>
a)	 Monosaccharides, such as hexoses<br>
are used to form disaccharides and<br>
polysaccharides which are other types/<br>
forms of carbohydrates.<br>
b)	 Carbohydrates are the chief energy<br>
sources in living organisms because<br>
they are oxidised to give energy. For<br>
example, glucose is the most common<br>
respiratory substrate which when<br>
completely oxidised, yields about 38<br>
<b>ATP.</b><br>
c)	 Trioses<br>
such<br>
as<br>
glyceraldehyde<br>
and<br>
dihydroxyacetone<br>
are<br>
used<br>
as<br>
intermediates<br>
in<br>
respiration,<br>
photosynthesis and other carbohydrate<br>
metabolic<br>
processes.<br>
<b>During</b><br>
respiration<br>
(during<br>
glycolysis),<br>
six carbon sugar splits to form<br>
dihydroxyacetone<br>
phosphate<br>
and<br>
phosphoglyceraldehyde which enters<br>
the cycle, whereas during dark<br>
reaction of photosynthesis the formed<br>
phosphoglyceraldehyde becomes a<br>
source of lipids and proteins.<br>

10/10/2019   14:05<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
46<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
d)	 They are the sources of other food<br>
substances, such as a triose sugar in<br>
plants.<br>
e)	 Pentose sugar, such as ribose and<br>
deoxyribose sugars used in the<br>
synthesis of nucleic acids.  Ribose is a<br>
constituent of Ribonucleic acid (RNA)<br>
and deoxyribose is a constituent of<br>
Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA).<br>
f)	 Ribose sugar is also used in the<br>
synthesis of ATP and coenzymes, such<br>
as NAD and NADP.<br>
g)	 Carbohydrate such as ribulose (a<br>
five carbon sugar) forms Ribulose<br>
biphosphate,<br>
which<br>
is<br>
used<br>
as<br>
carbon dioxide acceptor during light<br>
independent stage of photosynthesis.<br>
h)	 In association with proteins and<br>
phospholipids, they form structural<br>
parts of a membrane.<br>
i)	 They<br>
form<br>
structural<br>
parts<br>
of<br>
organisms, examples: cellulose in<br>
plant cells and chitin in exoskeleton of<br>
arthropods.<br>
j)	 They are important constituents of<br>
connective tissues in animals.<br>
k)	 Cellulose ﬁbres in edible fruits and<br>
otherfoods help to prevent constipation<br>
in humans.<br>
l)	 Flower nectar contains sugar, which is<br>
important in the process of pollination.<br>

the signiﬁcance of this?<br>
3.	 Comment on the ﬁnal colour change<br>

5.	 Show how the structure of α-glucose<br>
is maneuvered to produce β-glucose,<br>
galactose and fructose.<br>

<b>Cytology</b><br>
1.4.2 Lipids<br>
<b>Lipids are made up of carbon, hydrogen,</b><br>
and oxygen in which the content of oxygen<br>
is always smaller compared to carbon and<br>
hydrogen. Lipids are important constituents<br>
of the diet, because they are the source of<br>
high energy value. Natural fats and oils are<br>
compounds of glycerol and fatty acids. They<br>
are esters which are formed as the result<br>
of the reaction between organic acids and<br>
alcohols.<br>
<b>Formation of lipids</b><br>
<b>Lipids are esters formed by the reaction</b><br>
between fatty acids and alcohols. In simple<br>
lipids such as fats and oils, the alcohol<br>
involved in the reaction is glycerol. The<br>
formation of lipids is by condensation<br>
reaction; therefore, a molecule of water<br>
is lost. This forms a covalent bond called<br>
ester bond.<br>
<b>Types of lipids</b><br>
<b>In the year 1943, Bloor proposed the</b><br>
classification of lipids based on their<br>
chemical composition as simple lipids,<br>
compound lipids, and derived lipids.<br>
a)	 Simple lipids or homolipids<br>
<b>These are esters of fatty acids and various</b><br>
alcohols. Such lipids include fats and<br>
oils (whose alcohol is glycerol) and<br>
waxes which contain alcohols higher<br>
than glycerol. Fats differ from oils in that<br>
they contain fatty acids with saturated<br>
hydrocarbon chain. In oils, fatty acids<br>
contain unsaturated hydrocarbon chains.<br>
<b>At room temperature, oil is liquid, while</b><br>
fat is solid. Fatty acids have a general<br>
formula of CH3 (CH2)nCOOH where 'n' is<br>
a whole number. Stearic acid is a common<br>
fatty acid in animals’ adipose tissue. The<br>
fats and oils are triglycerides, formed<br>
by the combination of one trihydroxyl<br>
alcohol, (glycerol), and three fatty acid<br>
molecules. In this process, three molecules<br>
of water are lost and the ester bond is<br>
formed (Figure 1.29).<br>
<b>H - C - O - H      H - O  - C- R</b><br>
<b>H - C - O - H      H - O  - C- R</b><br>
<b>H - C - O - H      H - O  - C- R</b><br>

b)	 Compound lipids or heterolipids<br>
<b>These are esters of fatty acids with</b><br>
alcohols<br>
that<br>
possess<br>
additional<br>
groups which may be derived from<br>
other acids other than fatty acids. The<br>
compound lipids contain fatty acids,<br>
alcohols, and other compounds, such<br>
as phosphorous, amino-nitrogen and<br>
carbohydrates as shown in table (1.4).<br>
<b>Table 1.4 Compound lipids</b><br>
<b>Phospholipids or</b><br>
phosphatides<br>
These contain a phosphate group. Their glycerol forms ester bond with<br>
phosphoric acid and two fatty acids (Figure 1.30). They usually contain<br>
one hydrophilic head and two hydrophobic tails. They are called polar<br>
lipids and are amphipathic in nature.<br>
<b>Glycolipids</b><br>
Glycolipids are compound lipids with carbohydrates. These lipids include<br>
also certain structurally related compounds comprising of gangliosides,<br>
sulpholipids, and sulfatides groups.<br>
<b>Sphingolipids or</b><br>
phosphosphingosides<br>
Sphingolipids occur mostly in the cells of the brain. They do not<br>
contain glycerol in their molecules; instead, they contain amine alcohol<br>
(sphingosine or sphingol). For instance, the myelin sheath of the<br>
nerve ﬁbres contains a lipid known as sphingomyelin, which contains<br>
sphingosine and phospholipids in its molecules. Terpenes are mostly<br>
found in plants. Examples of terpenes are natural rubber and gern oil.<br>

c)	 Derived lipids<br>
<b>Derived lipids are substances derived from</b><br>
simple and compound lipids by hydrolysis.<br>
<b>The most common derived lipids are</b><br>
steroids, terpenes, and carotenoids.<br>
Steroids. These do not contain fatty acids.<br>
and are non saponiﬁable and not hydrolysed<br>
on heating. They are widely distributed<br>
in animals, where they are associated<br>
with physiological processes. Examples:<br>
hormones, oestrogen, progesterone and<br>
testosterone.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   48</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
49<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
<b>Terpenes. They are essential oils in plants</b><br>
found particularly in conifers, citrus<br>
trees and some insects. They are used for<br>
formation of aroma into medicine, such as<br>
aromatherapy, perfume and food additives.<br>
<b>These include certain fat soluble vitamins,</b><br>
such as vitamins A, E, and K.<br>
<b>Carotenoids.  These are widely distributed</b><br>
in both plants and animals. They are<br>
exclusively of plant origin. Due to the<br>
presence of many conjugated double bonds,<br>
they are coloured red or yellow. Examples<br>
are lycopene, carotenes, and xanthophyll<br>
(oxygenated derivatives of carotenes).<br>
<b>Properties of lipids</b><br>
<b>Lipids are either liquids or non-crystalline</b><br>
solids at room temperature. Pure fats or oils<br>
are colourless, odourless, and tasteless.<br>
<b>They are less dense than water and are</b><br>
esters of alcohols and acids. Simple lipids,<br>
such as fats are esters of fatty acids and<br>
glycerol. Lipids are insoluble in water,<br>
but soluble in organic solvents such as<br>
ether, toluene and chloroform. They can<br>
be hydrolysed by alkaline compounds<br>
into their constituents’ components by the<br>
process known as saponiﬁcation. Simple<br>
lipids such as fats and oils are hydrolysed<br>
by the lipase enzyme into fatty acids and<br>
glycerol. Lipids contain either saturated or<br>
unsaturated hydrocarbon chains and have<br>
a high caloriﬁc value due to the presence<br>
of large number of hydrogen atoms in their<br>
hydrocarbon chains. They are poor heat<br>
and electric conductors. Forthis reason,<br>
they are functional parts of nerve cells and<br>
skin sub-cutaneous layer.<br>
<b>Biochemical test for lipids</b><br>
<b>Lipids are nonpolar and do not dissolve</b><br>
in polar solvents, such as water. They<br>
only dissolve in nonpolar solvents, such<br>
as benzene, ether, absolute alcohols, and<br>
chloroform. This property makes the lipids<br>
to be tested effectively by adding nonpolar<br>
dye, which can easily be absorbed by a<br>
nonpolar lipids. Lipids can be tested using<br>
several methods, including that which<br>
involves the use of Sudan III solution, the<br>
grease spot test and emulsification test<br>
(Table 1.5).<br>
<b>The basis of the tests</b><br>
<b>Fat globules are stained red with Sudan III</b><br>
solution. Being less dense than water and<br>
insoluble in water, a red stained oil layer<br>
floats on the surface of water. In addition,<br>
with grease spot test, fats or oil droplets<br>
create a translucent spots on the paper on<br>
warming. As already known that, lipids are<br>
immiscible with water, therefore addition<br>
of water to a solution mixture of lipids<br>
and ethanol results into emulsion of tiny<br>
droplets in the water, which reflect light,<br>
giving a white opalescent appearance.<br>
<b>Emulsification process is permanent and</b><br>
complete in the presence of emulsifying<br>
agents, such as bile salts, soap and protein.<br>
<b>This process is important in fat digestion</b><br>
in the intestine, as the emulsifying agents<br>
(bile salts) lower surface tension of the<br>
lipids and increase the surface area, hence<br>
easily acted by digestive enzymes.<br>

and shake vigorously. Then allows the<br>

<b>A red- stained oil</b><br>
layer separates<br>
on the surface of<br>
solution.<br>
<b>Lipids</b><br>
present.<br>
<b>Grease spot test</b><br>
<b>Rub a drop of the sample on to a</b><br>
piece of paper.<br>
<b>Allow time for any water to</b><br>

<b>Shake vigorously to dissolve the</b><br>

cold water.<br>

finely divided<br>
and is dispersed<br>
in water when<br>
shaken with water<br>
it forms a cloudy<br>
white suspension<br>
(emulsification).<br>
<b>Lipids</b><br>
present.<br>
<b>Functions of lipids</b><br>
a)	 They are structural component of<br>
membranes. Examples: phospholipids<br>
and glycolipids.<br>
b)	 They insulate the body against heat<br>
loss. For example, fats found in the<br>
sub- cutaneous layer of the skin are an<br>
insulating blanket.<br>
c)	 They are sources of energy; therefore,<br>
they are used as alternative respiratory<br>
substrates when carbohydrates are<br>
completely exhausted.<br>
d)	 They are sources of metabolic water.<br>
<b>This is an important source of water</b><br>
for animals found in arid and semi-<br>
arid areas such as camels.<br>
e)	 Since lipids are less dense than water,<br>
they aid in buoyancy in the aquatic<br>
animals such as whales.<br>
f)	 They are precursors of important body<br>
requirements such as vitamin D and<br>
sex hormones.<br>
g)	 They protect internal vital organs such<br>
as heart and kidneys.<br>
h)	 They form protective layers against<br>
water loss for example waxes in plants.<br>
i)	 They facilitate fast conduction of<br>
nerve impulses, as they are integral<br>
parts of myelinated nerve ﬁbres.<br>
j)	 They are components of some enzyme<br>
systems.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   50</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>

51<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
k)	 They form plasma proteins, such<br>
as<br>
lipoproteins<br>
which<br>
facilitate<br>
transportation of lipids in the aqueous<br>
environment of the body.<br>

<b>Exercise 1.12</b><br>
1.	 Give an account on the properties<br>
of lipids.<br>
2.	 Classify lipids based on their<br>
chemical composition.<br>
3.	 Describe the structural functions<br>
of lipids in living organisms.<br>
1.4.3  Proteins<br>
<b>Proteins are large biomolecules or</b><br>
macromolecules, consisting of one or<br>
more long chains of amino acids. A<br>
molecule contains carbon, hydrogen,<br>
oxygen, nitrogen, sulphur and sometimes<br>
phosphorous. The amino group (NH2)<br>
provides protein’s basic nature, and the<br>
carboxyl group (-COOH) gives the acidic<br>
nature of the amino acid. The R-group is<br>
known as the side chain which represents<br>
the hydrogen atom or any other group such<br>
as the alkyl group (Figure 1.31). Proteins<br>
differ from one another, primarily in their<br>
sequence of amino acids, which is dictated<br>
by the nucleotide sequence of their genes<br>
and which usually results in protein folding<br>
into speciﬁc three-dimension structure that<br>
determines its activity.<br>
<b>H</b><br>
<b>Amino group</b><br>
<b>Carboxylic acid group</b><br>
<b>Side chain</b><br>
<b>H N</b><br>
<b>H</b><br>
<b>C</b><br>
<b>R</b><br>
<b>C</b><br>
<b>OH</b><br>
<b>O</b><br>
<b>Figure 1.31 Structure of the amino acids</b><br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   51</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
52<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Properties of the amino acids</b><br>
<b>They are colourless, crystalline solids which</b><br>
are soluble in water but insoluble in organic<br>
solvent such as ether, chloroform, and<br>
acetone. They are amphoteric compounds as<br>
they have both acidic and basic properties.<br>
<b>In neutral aqueous solutions, they exist as</b><br>
dipolar or zwitterions. On one side, the<br>
acidic carboxyl group has a tendency of<br>
donating hydrogen ion (proton); therefore,<br>
it dissociates, to release hydrogen ion and<br>
becomes negatively charged. On the other<br>
side, the basic amino group has a high afﬁnity<br>
to hydrogen; therefore, it accepts hydrogen<br>
ion and becomes positively charged. Each<br>
amino acid has its own pH value at which it<br>
exists in its neutral zwitterionic form. This<br>
pH at which the amino acids are electrically<br>
neutral is termed as an Isoelectric point<br>
(I.E.P). In the alkaline medium, when the<br>
pH of the I.E.P increases, the amino group<br>
dissociates, releasing hydrogen ions, (H+),<br>
the amino acid thus becomes negatively<br>
charged. In the acidic medium, (when the<br>
pH is lowered), the carboxyl group accepts<br>
hydrogen ions, and the whole structure<br>
becomes positively charged (Figure 1.32).<br>
<b>Formation of proteins</b><br>
<b>All proteins are formed by condensation</b><br>
of amino acids to give peptide chains. The<br>
condensation reaction occurs between<br>
the amino group of one amino acid and<br>
the carboxylic group of other amino<br>
acid to form a dipeptide molecule linked<br>
by peptide bond (Figure 1.33). Further<br>
combination of this type extends the<br>
length of the chain to form a polypeptide<br>
chain, which usually contains hundreds of<br>
amino acids. Polypeptides may be linked<br>
by other forces such as disulphide bridges,<br>
hydrogen bond, hydrophobic interaction<br>
and ionic bonds.<br>
Figure 1.33 Formation of dipeptide molecule linked by the peptide bond<br>
<b>Figure 1.32 Structure of the zwitterion</b><br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   52</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
53<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
<b>Structure of polypeptide</b><br>
<b>The structure of polypeptide molecule is</b><br>
determined by amino acids sequences and<br>
its configuration. For each particular type<br>
of protein, the chain of amino acid makes<br>
up a polypeptide molecule of a specific<br>
shape. This shape is very important in<br>
the functioning of the protein, especially<br>
enzymes. The type and the three-<br>
dimension configuration of a polypeptide<br>
molecule, is stabilised by the interactions<br>
of five different types of bonds (Figure<br>
1.35).<br>
<b>Peptide bond</b><br>
<b>This is a bond formed between carboxyl</b><br>
group (-COOH) of one amino acid and<br>
amino group (-NH2) of another amino<br>
acid. A polypeptide molecule has a free<br>
amino group at one end, and a carboxyl<br>
group at the other end.<br>
<b>Hydrogen bond</b><br>
<b>Hydrogen bond is a bond that is formed</b><br>
in<br>
the<br>
polypeptide<br>
chain<br>
between<br>
amino acid and side (R) groups. The<br>
bond<br>
is<br>
an<br>
electrostatic<br>
attraction<br>
between the hydrogen atom and another<br>
electronegative atom. Hydrogen bond<br>
forms between the carbonyl (C=O) of one<br>
amino acid or the amino group (N-H) of<br>
another amino acid. Hydrogen present in<br>
hydroxyl (-OH) group or amino group<br>
(-NH2) of amino acids, become slightly<br>
electropositive.<br>
<b>Therefore,</b><br>
hydrogen<br>
bond is a partially electrostatic attraction<br>
between the hydrogen (H) atom which is<br>
covalently bound to a more electronegative<br>
atom or group, such as nitrogen (N) and<br>
oxygen (O). Although hydrogen bonds<br>
are very weak, the absolute number of<br>
bonds plays a considerable role in shape<br>
and stability of the polypeptide molecule.<br>
<b>Disulphide bond</b><br>
<b>This is formed between side chains of</b><br>
cysteine (amino acid containing sulphur)<br>
and keeps parts of polypeptide intact and<br>
maintains its stability. If two molecules<br>
of cysteine line up alongside with each<br>
other, the neighbouring sulphur can be<br>
oxidized to form a disulphide bridge.<br>
<b>A disulphide linkage may be formed</b><br>
between the cysteine residues of the same<br>
polypeptide chain or different polypeptide<br>
chains of a functional protein. Disulphide<br>
bonds stabilize the tertiary or quaternary<br>
structures of the protein.<br>
<b>Ionic bond</b><br>
<b>The bond occurs between the positively</b><br>
and negatively charged side chains of<br>
amino acids that come in contact with<br>
each other. Normally ionic bond is<br>
formed between ions of opposite charges<br>
from ionized acid (-COO-) and basic<br>
<b>(-NH3</b><br>
+) groups of the amino acids. The<br>
availability of ionized carboxylic group<br>
(COO-) and amino group (-NH3<br>
+) at<br>
the side chain of amino acid, and at the<br>
terminal of polypeptide chain may form<br>
ionic interactions, which help to make<br>
a polypeptide molecule of a particular<br>
shape.<br>
<b>Hydrophobic interaction</b><br>
<b>This is considered to be among the major</b><br>
driving force for the folding of globular<br>
proteins in aqueous environment. Some<br>
<b>R groups of amino acids are non-polar as</b><br>
they have equal number of charges from<br>
amino and carboxyl groups. The non-polar<br>
<b>R groups are hydrophobic and they repel</b><br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   53</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
54<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
from water. In a long polypeptide chain,<br>
there are many such non-polar amino acids<br>
which may occur adjacent to each other.<br>
<b>In an aqueous environment such as inside</b><br>
the cell, the linear polypeptide will fold<br>
into a particular shape that hydrophobic<br>
amino acids come in contact with each<br>
other, while excluding water due to its<br>
hydrophobicity.<br>
<b>Hydrogen</b><br>
bond<br>
<b>Disulphide</b><br>
link<br>
<b>Ionic bond</b><br>
<b>Van der Waals interactions</b><br>
(dispersion forces) act between<br>
hydrophobic side branches<br>
<b>Polypeptide backbone</b><br>
<b>Figure1.34 Bonds in a polypeptide molecule</b><br>
<b>Categories of proteins</b><br>
<b>Due to their complexity, it is difﬁcult to</b><br>
classify protein molecules into a single,<br>
well deﬁned category. They can be classiﬁed<br>
according to their structure, composition,<br>
level of organisation, and functions.<br>
a)	 Classiﬁcation of proteins based on<br>
their structure<br>
<b>Based on their structure, proteins are</b><br>
categorized as ﬁbrous, globular, and<br>
intermediate proteins.<br>
<b>Fibrous proteins. These proteins form</b><br>
long polypeptide chains, cross linked at<br>
intervals, forming long ﬁbres or sheets<br>
(Figure 1.35). Their shapes resemble<br>
long ribbons or ﬁbres. Fibrous proteins<br>
are mostly found in animals, and are<br>
usually insoluble in water as well as in<br>
other aqueous media. Fibrous proteins<br>
aid in protection and structural support.<br>
<b>Examples of fibrous proteins include</b><br>
collagen (tendons, bones, and other<br>
connective tissues), myosin (found in<br>
muscles) and keratin (found in nails,<br>
horns, hairs, fur, and feathers).<br>
<b>Figure 1.35 Structure of fibrous proteins</b><br>
(α-keratin)<br>
<b>Globular proteins. These proteins have</b><br>
tertiary structure, in which the polypeptide<br>
chains are tightly folded to form the<br>
spherical shape (Figure 1.36). They<br>
are soluble in water. Examples of such<br>
proteins include enzymes, antibodies, and<br>
some hormones, such as insulin.<br>

<b>Intermediate proteins. These are ﬁbrous,</b><br>
soluble proteins. A good example of<br>
intermediate proteins is ﬁbrinogen, which<br>
forms an insoluble ﬁbrin during blood<br>
clotting.<br>
b)	 Classiﬁcation of proteins based on<br>
their composition<br>
<b>Based on their composition, proteins can</b><br>
be classified as simple and conjugated or<br>
complex proteins.<br>
<b>Simple proteins. These are proteins made</b><br>
up of only amino acids, and are mostly<br>
globular. When decomposed by acids,<br>
these proteins liberate their constituent<br>
amino acids. Examples of simple proteins<br>
are albumins, globulins, and histones.<br>
<b>Conjugated proteins. These are proteins</b><br>
made up of amino acids and other organic<br>
compounds. They have a non-amino<br>
acid group termed as prosthetic group.<br>
<b>Examples of conjugate proteins and their</b><br>
prosthetic groups are shown in Table 1.6.<br>

c)	 Classiﬁcation of proteins based on<br>
their level of organisation<br>
<b>There are four types of proteins based</b><br>
on the level of structural organization,<br>
namely primary, secondary, tertiary, and<br>
quaternary structures of proteins.<br>
<b>Primary structure of proteins. The</b><br>
primary structure of protein is a linear<br>
sequence of amino acids that make up<br>
the polypeptide chain (Figure 1.37). Its<br>
sequence is determined by the sequence of<br>
nucleotide bases of the DNA in the genetic<br>
code. The amino acid sequence determines<br>
the positioning of different R-groups<br>
relative to each other. The positioning also<br>
determines the way the protein folds and<br>
the ﬁnal structure of the molecule.<br>

<b>Secondary structure of proteins. The</b><br>
secondary structure of proteins refers to<br>
the regular folding pattern of β-sheets and<br>
α helix of the polypeptide chain (Figure<br>
1.38). The linear unfolded structure of the<br>
polypeptide chain assumes a helical shape<br>
to produce the secondary structure. The<br>
regular pattern is due to the hydrogen bond<br>
formation between atoms of the amino acid<br>
and backbone of the polypeptide chain. It<br>
includes components of hairs, claws, nails,<br>
and the skin.<br>

bending and twisting of the polypeptide<br>
chain (Figure 1.39). The linear sequence<br>
of the polypeptide chain is folded into a<br>
compact globular structure. The folding<br>
of the polypeptide chain is stabilized by<br>
weak, non-covalent interactions. These<br>
interactions are hydrogen bonds and<br>

covalent bonds, also contribute to the<br>
formation of the tertiary structure.<br>

enzymes and antibodies.<br>

d) Classification of proteins according<br>
to their functions<br>
<b>Proteins can be classified into different</b><br>
categories depending on their physical and<br>
chemical structures as well as their location<br>
in the cell. They can also be grouped based<br>
on the metabolic functions they perform in<br>
the body as follows:<br>
<b>Enzymes proteins. These are biological</b><br>
catalysts which are mostly protein in<br>
nature. They are the most varied and highly<br>
specialised proteins with catalytic activity.<br>
<b>Virtually, all the chemical reactions of</b><br>
organic biomolecules in cells are catalysed<br>
by enzymes, resulting into increased reaction<br>
rate. Enzymes such as urease, catalase, and<br>
lactase catalyse a variety of body reactions.<br>
<b>Structural proteins. These are proteins</b><br>
which aid in strengthening or protecting<br>
biological structures. These protein act<br>
as supporting filaments, cables or sheets<br>
to give biological structure, strength, and<br>
protection. Collagen is an example of the<br>
structural protein of the bone and connective<br>
tissue. Collagen and elastin provide a fibrous<br>
framework in animal connective tissues,<br>
such as tendons and ligaments. Keratin is<br>
the structural protein of hairs, horns, finger<br>
nails, feathers and other skin appendages<br>
of animals.<br>
<b>Transport or carrier proteins. These</b><br>
proteins are involved in transportation of<br>
ions and other substances. Examples of such<br>
proteins include haemoglobin, the iron-<br>
containing protein of blood, which transports<br>
oxygen from the lungs to other parts of<br>
the body, serum albumin which transports<br>
fatty acids in the blood, lipoproteins, which<br>
carry lipids from the site of absorption<br>
or synthesis to the site of utilization or<br>
storage and membrane transport proteins<br>
which transport substances across the cell<br>
membranes.<br>
<b>Nutrient and storage proteins. These</b><br>
are proteins which provide reservoirs of<br>
essential nutrients to a growing embryo.<br>
<b>A good example is the albumin of an egg</b><br>
white, used as an amino acid source for the<br>
developing embryo. Casein, the protein of<br>
milk, is the major source of amino acids<br>
for baby mammals. Ferritin is an iron-<br>
binding protein; which stores iron in the<br>
liver. Moreover, plants store proteins in<br>
seeds, where seeds of many plants store<br>
nutrient protein for growth of embryonic<br>
plants such as wheat, rice, maize, and bean.<br>
<b>Contractile or motile proteins. These</b><br>
proteins function in the contractile tissues.<br>
<b>They include actin, myosin, and tubulin</b><br>
proteins. They are important for movement<br>
of body parts. Contractile proteins are<br>
responsible for undulation of the cilia and<br>
flagella, which propel many cells.<br>
<b>Defence proteins. These are proteins which</b><br>
defend the body against invaders, such<br>
as antibodies, ﬁbrinogen and thrombin.<br>
<b>These are highly specialized proteins</b><br>
that recognize and combine with foreign<br>
substances like viruses, bacteria and destroy<br>
them. Fibrinogen and thrombin are blood<br>
clotting proteins which protect the body<br>
against excessive bleeding.<br>
<b>Regulatory proteins. They regulate</b><br>
gene expression and cellular or metabolic<br>
activities of an organism. These include<br>
hormones, such as, insulin hormone which<br>
is secreted by the pancreas, helps in the<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   58</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
59<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
regulation of the blood sugar concentration<br>
that regulates the metabolism of cells.<br>
<b>Transcription factors are proteins that</b><br>
regulate gene expression. Some repress gene<br>
expression by binding to target genes and<br>
activating their transcription. The examples<br>
of such protein include lac repressor and<br>
catabolite activator protein (CAP). Lac<br>
repressor is a DNA-binding protein which<br>
inhibits the expression of genes coding<br>
for proteins involved in the metabolism of<br>
lactose in bacteria, whereas CAP promotes<br>
transcription at several sites as it affects<br>
the metabolism of sugars and amino acids,<br>
protein folding, toxin production and pilus<br>
synthesis.<br>
<b>Receptor proteins. Receptor proteins are</b><br>
built into the membrane of a nerve cell<br>
and they detect chemical signals released<br>
by other nerve cells. Other proteins act<br>
as chemical messengers within the brain<br>
throughout the body. They are involved<br>
in the cell’s response to chemical stimuli.<br>
<b>Properties of proteins</b><br>
<b>Proteins have the following properties:</b><br>
a)	 They are colourless, tasteless, and<br>
amphoteric in nature as they have both<br>
acidic and basic properties derived from<br>
–COOH and –NH2 groups respectively.<br>
b)	 They exhibit characteristic isoelectric<br>
points and have buffering properties.<br>
c)	 They are large molecules; hence,<br>
they have high molecular weight.<br>
<b>For instance, haemoglobin has the</b><br>
molecular weight of about 68,000 g/mol.<br>
d)	 Proteins differ in their solubility in<br>
water, some are insoluble, example<br>
keratin while others are highly soluble<br>
such as albumin. Soluble protein<br>
can be precipitated from solution by<br>
addition of certain salts example NaCl<br>
and (NH4)2SO4. Decrease in solubility<br>
occurs due to competition between salt<br>
and protein molecules for water as well<br>
as a decrease in charge on the protein<br>
molecule.<br>
e)	 Proteins are also coagulated by heat and<br>
agents like strong acids, alkali, alcohol,<br>
acetone, urea and salts of heavy metal.<br>
f)	 Proteins are speciﬁc in their reactions<br>
and substrates they act upon. This<br>
property is clearly illustrated by<br>
enzymes.<br>
g)	 Proteins are colloidal in nature, that is,<br>
they can be hydrolysed into their amino<br>
acid constituents.<br>
h)	 They can be denatured or changed from<br>
their natural state by heat or chemicals.<br>
<b>Denaturation of proteins</b><br>
<b>A protein is said to be denatured, if it loses</b><br>
its natural three-dimension conformation<br>
or shape. This change may be temporary<br>
or permanent, but the amino acid sequence<br>
of the protein remains intact because<br>
the peptide bonds are not cleaved.<br>
<b>Denaturation results in the alteration of</b><br>
physical properties of a protein in terms of<br>
solubility and other criteria. However, the<br>
protein in this situation can no longer carry<br>
out its normal biological functions. The<br>
causes of this condition include heat and<br>
radiation, organic solvents and detergents,<br>
highly concentrated salts and strong acids<br>
and alkalis. Denaturation is also caused by<br>
heavy metals, urea solution and mechanical<br>
forces.<br>
<b>Heat and radiations, such as ultra violet</b><br>
rays and infra-red. These supply kinetic<br>
energy which causes strong vibrations<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   59</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
60<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
of protein molecules. These result into<br>
breaking down of ionic and hydrogen<br>
bonds that are weaker than peptide bonds,<br>
resulting into coagulation of such protein<br>
molecules.<br>
<b>Organic solvents and detergents. These</b><br>
reagents disrupt hydrophobic interactions<br>
and form bonds with hydrophobic (non-<br>
polar) groups. Consequently, this disrupts<br>
hydrogen bonds resulting into denaturation.<br>
<b>An example of such chemical substances</b><br>
is methyl alcohol, which is used as a<br>
disinfectant to clean the skin before<br>
injection. The alcohol denatures proteins<br>
in bacterial cell walls.<br>
<b>Highly concentrated salts, strong acids,</b><br>
and alkalis. These compounds disrupt the<br>
ionic bonds, resulting into coagulation of<br>
proteins. If such proteins remain mixed<br>
with these reagents for a long time, peptide<br>
bonds may also break down.<br>
<b>Presence of heavy metals such as</b><br>
mercury (Hg), silver (Ag), and lead<br>
(Pb). Cations of such metals tend to form<br>
strong bonds with the negatively charged<br>
carboxyl groups on the proteins, leading to<br>
the disruption of ionic bonds. The protein’s<br>
polarity is reduced and its insolubility<br>
becomes high, hence precipitated as an<br>
insoluble metal protein.<br>
<b>Urea solution. Urea tends to disrupt</b><br>
hydrogen bonds. Being amide-like, it<br>
forms hydrogen bonds of its own via<br>
distorting the unique conﬁguration of the<br>
protein molecule.<br>
<b>Mechanical forces. Physical movement</b><br>
of proteins may break hydrogen bonds.<br>
<b>For example, stretching of hair breaks</b><br>
the hydrogen bonds in the keratin helix.<br>
<b>The latter is extended and the hair is</b><br>
stretched. If released, the hair returns to<br>
its normal length. However, if it is wetted<br>
and then dried under tension, it keeps<br>
its new length. This is the basis for hair<br>
styling. Many liquid proteins denature<br>
and precipitate when they are vigorously<br>
agitated because of incorporation of<br>
air bubbles and adsorption of protein<br>
molecules to the air-liquid interface.<br>
<b>General functions of proteins</b><br>
a)	 They are essential building materials<br>
of the body. For that reason, they<br>
are needed for proper growth and<br>
development. This explains why<br>
kwashiorkor<br>
victims<br>
experience<br>
stunted growth.<br>
b)	 They are used as alternative respiratory<br>
substrates when both carbohydrates<br>
and lipids are completely exhausted.<br>
c)	 They absorb excess ﬂuids in the body.<br>
<b>For example, kwashiorkor victims</b><br>
have swollen lower parts of their<br>
legs and some parts of hands due to<br>
accumulation of excess ﬂuids, this<br>
condition is called oedema.<br>
d)	 They form structural components<br>
of body parts. For instance, keratin<br>
is a structural protein of hair, horns,<br>
hooves, and nails.<br>
e)	 They are structural components of<br>
membranes. For example, globular<br>
protein and glycoprotein which form<br>
the cell surface membrane.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   60</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:05<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
61<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
f)	 They take part in various metabolic<br>
processes in the body, for example<br>
enzymes<br>
that<br>
catalyse<br>
different<br>
physiological activities in the body.<br>
g)	 Proteins such as hormones have<br>
the following roles: act as chemical<br>
messengers<br>
and<br>
regulate<br>
body<br>
metabolites.<br>
<b>Examples</b><br>
of<br>
such<br>
proteins are insulin and glucagon,<br>
which regulate the level of blood<br>
sugar in mammals.<br>
h)	 Proteins such as anti-bodies protect<br>
the body against infections.<br>
i)	 Proteins such as myosin and actin are<br>
contractile; therefore, they interact to<br>
bring about contraction and relaxation<br>
of muscles, hence, the movement of<br>
body parts and locomotion of animals.<br>
j)	 Proteins such as haemoglobin and<br>
myoglobin transport oxygen in the<br>
blood.<br>
k)	 Proteins such as ﬁbrinogen are<br>
important for blood clotting, hence,<br>
they help in healing wounds by<br>
forming ﬁbres over injured parts to<br>
prevent excessive loss of blood and<br>
water, and entry of germs.<br>
l)	 Proteins such as ovalbumin of egg<br>
white and casein of milk are storage<br>
in function. For example albumin<br>
supplies food to a developing embryo.<br>
m)	Proteins help to build and improve<br>
the body immune system. This<br>
explains why children suffering from<br>
kwashiorkor are vulnerable to various<br>
opportunistic diseases.<br>
<b>Biochemical test for proteins</b><br>
<b>Proteins are complex organic structures</b><br>
made up of polypeptide chains of amino<br>
acids, which have different amino<br>
acid sequences and three dimensional<br>
configuration to define a particular type<br>
of protein. An amino acid is a class of<br>
organic compounds with a carboxyl<br>
group (-COOH), an amino group (NH2)<br>
and a side group, all attached to a central<br>
carbon atom. Proteins differ from each<br>
other in terms of their  type, number and<br>
sequence of amino acids that make up<br>
the polypeptide backbone. Therefore,<br>
different proteins have different molecular<br>
structures,<br>
nutritional<br>
attributes<br>
and<br>
physiochemical properties. There are<br>
several methods used to test for proteins.<br>
<b>The most common method is chemical</b><br>
method. Chemical method relies on the<br>
properties of amino acids or peptide bonds<br>
that are common to all proteins. The biuret<br>
test is a chemical assay that is widely used<br>
to detect the presence of proteins and<br>
amino acids in a sample. The test relies<br>
on a color change to confirm the presence<br>
of proteins. If proteins are present, the<br>
sample will turn violet (Table 1.7).<br>
<b>The basis of tests</b><br>
<b>Biuret test is the chemical test used to</b><br>
detect the presence of peptide bond as a<br>
general test for detection of proteins. In<br>
the presence of dilute copper (II) sulphate<br>
(CuSO4) in alkaline solution (medium),<br>
nitrogen atoms in the peptide chain form<br>
a purple complex with Copper (II) ions<br>
(Cu2+).<br>

food sample.<br>
<b>Exercise 1.13</b><br>
1.	 Proteins are said to be amphoteric<br>
in nature, what is the biological<br>
importance of this?<br>
2.	 Explain the medical importance of<br>
protein denaturation.<br>
3.	 Classify proteins on the basis of<br>
structure and function.<br>
1.4.4 Enzymes<br>
<b>Enzymes are complex, three dimension</b><br>
globular proteins that are made up by living<br>
cells. They are biological catalysts, since<br>
they alter the rate of different physiological<br>
processes in living organisms. They are<br>
also found in natural secretions, such as<br>
plant juices, milk and the digestive juices<br>
where they catalyse several metabolic<br>
reactions or processes taking place in the<br>

<b>Cytology</b><br>
<b>Mechanisms for enzyme action</b><br>
<b>Several steps of enzymic action result in</b><br>
the formation of products. There are two<br>
theories which explains the mechanism of<br>
enzymic action. These are the lock and key<br>
hypothesis and the induced fit hypothesis.<br>
<b>In the lock and key hypothesis, an enzyme</b><br>
holds the substrate as a lock holds the<br>
key, while the induced ﬁt hypothesis, the<br>
active site expands and contracts to form<br>
enzyme - substrate interaction. The two<br>
molecules form a temporary structure<br>
called an enzyme-substrate complex as<br>
an intermediate product of substrate to<br>
be converted into product. The products<br>
have different shapes from the substrates;<br>
therefore, once the product is formed, it<br>
escape from the active site, leaving it<br>
free for accommodating other substrate<br>
molecules.<br>
living cells. A compound with which the<br>
enzyme combines is called a substrate.<br>
<b>The substrate ﬁxes itself in an active</b><br>
site of the enzyme whose shape is ideal<br>
for accommodating it. The active site<br>
is a group of amino acids comprising<br>
the region of the enzyme into which<br>
the substrate ﬁts in order to catalyse the<br>
reaction. By acting as catalysts, enzymes<br>
lower the activation energy. Activation<br>
energy is the minimum amount of energy<br>
that is required to activate a substrate<br>
molecule to a condition in which they can<br>
undergo biological transformation (Figure<br>
1.41).<br>

<b>Lock and Key theory</b><br>
<b>The theory states that, as the key ﬁts in</b><br>
one lock, the same happens to a substrate<br>
which ﬁts only in one active site of the<br>
enzyme to form an enzyme-substrate<br>
complex (Figure 1.42). The speciﬁc action<br>
of an enzyme with a single substrate can be<br>
explained using a Lock and Key analogy<br>
ﬁrst postulated in 1894 by Emil Fischer.<br>
<b>In this analogy, the lock is the enzyme and</b><br>
the key is the substrate. Only the correctly<br>
sized key (substrate) ﬁts into the key hole<br>
(active site) of the lock (enzyme). Smaller<br>
keys, larger keys, or incorrectly positioned<br>
teeth on keys (incorrectly shaped or sized<br>
substrate molecules) do not ﬁt into the<br>
lock (enzyme). Only the correctly shaped<br>
key opens a particular lock.<br>
<b>Key (substrate)</b><br>
<b>Lock (enzyme)</b><br>
<b>Lock-key</b><br>
complex<br>
<b>Enzyme substrate</b><br>
complex<br>
<b>Enzyme</b><br>
<b>Active site</b><br>
<b>Substrate</b><br>
<b>Figure 1.42 A model explaining the lock and key hypothesis</b><br>
<b>Induced fit theory</b><br>
<b>The induced-ﬁt theory assumes that the</b><br>
substrate plays a role in determining<br>
the ﬁnal shape of the enzyme; thus, the<br>
enzyme is partially ﬂexible (Figure 1.43).<br>
<b>This explains why certain compounds</b><br>
can bind to the enzyme but do not react<br>
because the enzyme has been distorted too<br>
much. Other molecules may be too small<br>
to induce the proper alignment. Therefore,<br>
they cannot react, since only the specific<br>
substrate is capable of inducing the proper<br>
alignment of the active site.<br>

65<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
<b>Factors governing rate of enzyme</b><br>
activity<br>
Several factors affect enzyme activities.<br>
<b>These include substrate concentration,</b><br>
enzyme concentration, temperature, and<br>
pH level.<br>
a)	 Substrate concentration<br>
<b>At fixed concentration of enzymes, an</b><br>
increase in substrate concentration will<br>
increase the rate of reaction. This is<br>
because more substrate molecules will<br>
be colliding with enzyme molecules.<br>
<b>Therefore, more products will be formed</b><br>
at a time. However, after a certain<br>
concentration, any increase will have<br>
no effect on the rate of reaction, since<br>
all enzyme’s active sites have bound to<br>
substrate, and the remaining substrate will<br>
be unable to bind to enzymes.<br>
<b>This point is called enzyme saturation</b><br>
point. Thus, extra substrates have to wait<br>
for the enzyme to release the product<br>
and become free to accommodate other<br>
substrate (Figure 1.44).<br>
<b>Maximum reaction rate</b><br>
<b>Rate approaching</b><br>
maximum rate<br>
<b>Rate of reaction</b><br>
<b>Rate increases directly with</b><br>
substrate concentration<br>
<b>Substrate concentration</b><br>
<b>Figure 1.44 The effect of substrate</b><br>
concentration on the reaction rate of enzyme<br>
b)	 Enzyme concentration<br>
<b>The rate of any enzyme catalysed reaction</b><br>
directly depends on the concentration of the<br>
enzymes. Provided that, the temperature and<br>
other conditions are suitable for reaction and<br>
there are excess substrate molecules, the rate<br>
of the reaction is directly proportional to the<br>
concentration of enzymes. If the amount<br>
of the substrate is restricted, it may limit<br>
the rate of the reaction. Likewise, addition<br>
of more enzymes cannot increase the rate.<br>
<b>Therefore, the graph tails off as shown in</b><br>
Figure1.45.<br>

on the reaction rate of enzyme controlled reaction<br>
c)	 Temperature<br>
<b>Generally,</b><br>
increase<br>
in<br>
temperature<br>
increases the kinetic energy that molecules<br>
possess, implying more random collisions<br>
between molecules per unit time. Since<br>
enzymes catalyse reactions by randomly<br>
colliding<br>
with<br>
substrate<br>
molecules,<br>
increasing temperature will increase the<br>
rate of reaction, leading to more products.<br>
<b>However, increasing temperature also</b><br>
increases the vibration energy that<br>
molecules have; speciﬁcally in case<br>
of enzyme molecules which put strain<br>
on the bonds that hold them together.<br>
<b>As temperature increases, more bonds,</b><br>
especially the weaker hydrogen and ionic<br>
bonds, will break because of this strain.<br>
<b>Breaking of bonds within the enzyme will</b><br>
cause the active site to change in shape.<br>
<b>This change in shape means that the active</b><br>
site is less complementary to the shape of<br>
the substrate; therefore, it is less likely<br>
to catalyse the reaction. The change in<br>
shape prevents the enzyme and substrate<br>
from fitting together exactly. Finally, the<br>
enzyme will become denatured, and it will<br>
no longer be functional, hence decreasing<br>
the rate of reaction.<br>
<b>However, below normal temperature,</b><br>
enzymes become less active due to<br>
reduction in speed of molecular movement.<br>
<b>When the temperature is lowered below</b><br>
or near freezing point, the enzymes are<br>
said to be inactive. This condition will last<br>
when the higher temperature above the<br>
freezing point is restored.<br>
<b>Therefore, as the temperature increases,</b><br>
the rate of reaction also increases due<br>
to increased kinetic energy. There is a<br>
temperature range at which the rate of<br>
enzyme’s action is maximum. This is<br>
called an optimum temperature. Below<br>
this temperature, enzymes are less active.<br>
<b>Contrary, above the optimum temperature,</b><br>
enzymes are denatured. All enzymes work<br>
within a range of temperature specific to<br>
the organism. In human, the optimum<br>
temperature for many enzymes lies around<br>
40 °C and denaturation occurs at about 60 °C<br>
(Figure 1.46). However, enzymes of some<br>
other organisms have different optimum<br>
temperatures. For example thermophilic<br>
bacteria such as Thermus aquaticus have<br>
optimum enzymatic temperature ranges<br>
from 51 °C to 80 °C and in Archaea the<br>
optimum enzymic temperature ranges from<br>
41 °C to 122 °C.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   66</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
67<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
<b>The effect of temperature on the rate of</b><br>
reaction can be expressed as temperature<br>
coefﬁcient, Q10. According to the Q10 rule,<br>
the rate of a physiological process or<br>
reaction doubles for every 10 °C rise in<br>
temperature, if the temperature is within<br>
the range that can be tolerated by a living<br>
system. A mathematical expression is<br>
presented below:<br>
rate of reaction at (X+10) °C<br>
rate of reaction at X °C<br>
<b>Q10=</b><br>
<b>Where: X is the initial temperature</b><br>
<b>Thus, within the range of 0 - 40 °C, Q10 of</b><br>
an enzyme controlled reaction is 2.<br>
<b>This means that the rate of the enzyme’s</b><br>
action doubles for every 10 °C rise in<br>
temperature.<br>
d)	 pH<br>
<b>The pH scale measures the acidity and</b><br>
basicity of a solution. pH is a measure<br>
of hydrogen ion (H+) concentration.<br>
<b>It is therefore a good indicator of the</b><br>
hydroxy ion (OH-) concentration. Its<br>
values ranges from 1 to 14. Lower pH<br>
values implies higher H+ concentration<br>
and lower OH- concentration. Acidic<br>
solutions have pH values below 7, and<br>
basic solutions (alkalis) have pH values<br>
above 7 while the pH of 7 is termed as<br>
‘neutral’. Hydrogen (H+) and hydroxyl<br>
(OH-) ions are charged, therefore, they<br>
interfere with hydrogen and ionic bonds<br>
that hold together an enzyme, since they<br>
will be attracted or repelled by the charges<br>
created by the bonds. This interference<br>
causes a change in shape of the enzyme<br>
and, consequently, its active site. Most<br>
enzymes are sensitive to pH and have<br>
specific ranges of activity. Different<br>
enzymes have different optimum pH<br>
values; hence, the bonds within them are<br>
influenced by H+ and OH- ions at different<br>
pH value. Thus, the shape of their active<br>
site is most complementary to the shape<br>
of their substrate.<br>
<b>At optimum pH, the rate of reaction is</b><br>
maximum. Any change in pH, above<br>
or below the optimum, will quickly<br>
cause a decrease in the rate of reaction,<br>
since more enzyme molecules will have<br>
active sites whose shapes are not less<br>
complementary to the shape of their<br>
substrates (Figure 1.47). Slight changes<br>
in pH above or below the optimum<br>
do not cause a permanent change to<br>
<b>Temperature °C</b><br>
0            10            20	          30<br>
40           50           60<br>
<b>Enzymes</b><br>
denature at high<br>
temperatures so<br>
rate falls rapidly<br>
<b>Optimum temperature is usually</b><br>
around 40 °C<br>
<b>Rate of reaction</b><br>
Figure 1.46 Effect of temperature on the rate of an enzyme-controlled reaction<br>

the enzyme, since the bonds can be<br>
reformed. However, extreme changes in<br>
pH can cause enzymes to be denatured<br>
and permanently lose their functions.<br>
<b>Enzymes in different locations have</b><br>
different optimum pH values, since their<br>
environmental conditions may differ.<br>
<b>For example, the enzyme “pepsin”</b><br>
functions best at pH value of around<br>
2 and is found in the stomach which<br>
contains hydrochloric acid. “Carbonic<br>
anhydrase” which is a key enzyme in all<br>
living organisms works best at  pH value<br>
of around  7, and “chymotrypsin” which<br>
is found in small intestine works best at pH<br>
value of around 9.<br>
