<b>Enzyme cofactors</b><br>
<b>Many enzyme-catalysed reactions require</b><br>
more than just an enzyme and its substrate.<br>
<b>Some enzymes require a particular</b><br>
ion or even a small molecule known as<br>
cofactor. Cofactors are small, non-protein<br>
components of enzymes that are needed for<br>
their efﬁcient activity. These substances are<br>
stable at high temperatures and vary from<br>
simple inorganic ions to complex organic<br>
compounds. In such enzymes, the protein<br>
part is called an apoenzyme and the non-<br>
protein groups that are associated with the<br>
enzyme are known as prosthetic groups.<br>
<b>Only apoenzyme and cofactor together are</b><br>
active as a catalyst. The cofactor is either<br>
inorganic ions, usually metal ions such<br>
as Fe2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Cl- or small organic<br>
molecules such as haem, biotin, Flavine<br>
adenine dinucleotide (FAD), Nicotineamide<br>
adenine dinucleotide (NAD). They may<br>
also be vitamins called coenzymes. Some<br>
enzymes need both, a metal ions and a<br>
coenzyme to become active.<br>
<b>Metal ion and/or coenzyme</b><br>
<b>Apoenzyme + Cofactor = Active enzyme</b><br>
<b>Enzymes inhibitors</b><br>
<b>There are many molecules that can interfere</b><br>
with  enzymes activity either by reducing or<br>
destroying their actions. These molecules<br>
are called enzyme inhibitors. There are<br>
two main groups of inhibitors: competitive<br>
and non-competitive inhibitors. In either<br>
type, inhibitors are either reversible or<br>
irreversible. Reversible inhibitors generally<br>
bind to an enzyme with weak bonds, such<br>
as hydrogen bonds, which are easily<br>
broken. The effect of these inhibitors to<br>
the enzyme is temporary, such that, when<br>
they are detached, the enzyme regains its<br>
normal functioning.  Contrary, irreversible<br>
inhibitors are strongly bonded to the<br>
enzyme in such a way that an enzyme and<br>
the inhibitor cannot be separated without<br>
damaging the enzyme.<br>
<b>Competitive inhibitors resemble substrates,</b><br>
therefore, they compete with the substrates<br>
for the active site of an enzyme molecule<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   68</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
69<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
(Figure 1.48a). When the inhibitor remains<br>
ﬁxed to the active site, the enzyme is<br>
prevented from working normally, as the<br>
substrate cannot move into the active<br>
site. The substrate is then prevented<br>
from binding to the same active site and<br>
therefore, decreases the enzyme affinity to<br>
other substrates. The effects of competitive<br>
inhibitors decrease with increased<br>
concentration of substrates. A good example<br>
of the competitive inhibitors is malonic<br>
acid, which competes with succinic acid<br>
for the active site of a respiratory enzyme,<br>
succinic dehydrogenase.<br>
<b>Non-competitive inhibitors are substances</b><br>
that do not resemble substrates. Therefore,<br>
they neither compete for nor attach to<br>
the active site of the enzyme. They ﬁx<br>
themselves elsewhere on the enzyme<br>
molecule. In so doing, they completely<br>
alter the shape of the enzyme molecule<br>
in such a way that the active site cannot<br>
accommodate the substrate. Since the<br>
inhibitor does not compete with the<br>
substrates for the active site of the enzyme,<br>
an increase in substrate concentration does<br>
not reduce the effect of the non competitive<br>
inhibitor. An example of non-competitive<br>
inhibitor is cyanide, which attaches itself<br>
to copper prosthetic group of cytochrome<br>
oxidases, thereby inhibiting respiration<br>
process (Figure 1. 48b).<br>

<b>Enzyme regulation</b><br>
<b>Regulation of enzymatic activity is an</b><br>
important biological activity in controlling<br>
different metabolic processes. Enzymes<br>
are important for regulation of different<br>
metabolic activities, such as biochemical<br>
pathways, homeostasis, gastro intestinal<br>
digestion and growth. The common way<br>
in which metabolic pathways in the cells<br>
are regulated involve allosteric enzymes<br>
which are designed to change the shape<br>
and are regulated by the compounds<br>
which act as non-competitive inhibitors.<br>
<b>The regulation of these enzymatic</b><br>
activities includes; end-product inhibition,<br>
zymogens and genetic control.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   69</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
70<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>End-product inhibition control. If a given</b><br>
physiological process involves several steps<br>
and various intermediates, the end product<br>
of the pathway may inhibit the enzyme at<br>
the start (Figure 1.49). In this example, the<br>
product “Y” acts as an inhibitor to enzyme<br>
“a” (ea). If the level of product Y falls, the<br>
inhibition is reduced. If the level of Y rises<br>
above normal, inhibition of “ea” increases;<br>
therefore, the level of  Y is reduced. In this<br>
way, homeostatic control of Y is achieved.<br>
<b>This mechanism is termed as negative</b><br>
feedback, because the information from<br>
the end of the pathway which is feedback<br>
to the start has a negative effect; that is the<br>
high concentration of Y reduces its own<br>
production rate. The enzymes that are<br>
inhibited in this way are called allosteric<br>
enzymes. These enzymes can have more<br>
than one shape. One shape renders the<br>
enzyme active (by allosteric activator),<br>
another renders it inactive (by allosteric<br>
inhibitor).<br>
(Substrate)   U       V       W        X        Y   (Product)<br>
ae<br>
eb<br>
ed<br>
ed<br>
<b>Inhibition (negative feedback)</b><br>
<b>Intermediate compounds</b><br>
<b>Figure 1.49 End product inhibition of enzyme</b><br>
<b>Zymogens (proenzymes) control. Some</b><br>
enzymes are synthesized in inactive forms<br>
that differ in composition from the active<br>
forms. Activation of such enzymes, known<br>
as zymogens or proenzymes, requires<br>
a chemical reaction that either adds or<br>
splits off part of the molecule. Some<br>
enzymes that digest proteins, examples:<br>
trypsinogen,<br>
chemotrypsinogen,<br>
and<br>
proelastase are produced in the pancreas.<br>
<b>These enzymes must be inactive when</b><br>
they are synthesized, so that they cannot<br>
attack the pancreas.<br>
<b>Genetic control. This is the regulation of</b><br>
enzymatic activity by control of the synthesis<br>
of the enzyme. The synthesis of enzymes is<br>
regulated by genes. Genes carry the code for<br>
making enzymes. These mechanisms, which<br>
are controlled by hormones can accelerate<br>
or decelerate enzyme synthesis. The genetic<br>
control strategy is particularly useful for<br>
enzymes which are needed only at certain<br>
stages of development. Depending on the<br>
genetics and other environmental factors,<br>
the body can produce many enzymes during<br>
a lifetime. As body ageing commences, the<br>
body tries to automatically conserve its<br>
decreasing resources and produces fewer<br>
enzymes of all types. When this lifetime<br>
enzyme potential is reached, the body can<br>
no longer sustain life. Death occurs because<br>
nothing takes place in the body without<br>
enzymes. Enzymes constitute the ‘`life<br>
force” which powers the whole system in<br>
the organism’s life expectance.<br>
<b>Properties of enzymes</b><br>
<b>They are proteins (globular proteins) in</b><br>
nature and biocatalysts, as they lower<br>
the activation energy of the reaction they<br>
catalyse.  They are never used up, never<br>
affected by the reaction they catalyse,<br>
meaning that it remains unchanged at the<br>
end of reaction. Since they are proteins,<br>
enzymes are coded for by DNA. They<br>
have active sites where the substrates are<br>
accommodated; and the sites have speciﬁc<br>
shapes which make them to be speciﬁc to<br>
not only the substrates they act upon, but<br>
also the reactions they catalyse. They are<br>
sensitive to temperature and pH changes,<br>
and they can also be affected by enzyme<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   70</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
71<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
and substrate concentration. They are very<br>
efﬁcient, thus, they are needed in a very<br>
small amount; this means that a small<br>
amount of enzymes acts on large quantities<br>
of substrates. Enzymes work reversibly;<br>
meaning that they catalyse reversible<br>
reactions. For example, an enzyme carbonic<br>
anhydrase catalyses the combination of<br>
carbondioxide and water to form carbonic<br>
acid in tissues where the concentration of<br>
carbon dioxide is high. In the lungs, where<br>
the concentration of carbon dioxide is low,<br>
the same carbonic anhydrase, catalyses the<br>
dissociation of carbonic acid into carbon<br>
dioxide and water.<br>
<b>Classiﬁcation of enzymes</b><br>
In 1964, the International Union of Biochemistry (IUB) introduced a system of classifying<br>
enzymes based on the type of reactions they catalyse. This system recognised six major<br>
functional classes of enzymes.<br>
a)	 Oxidoreductases which catalyse redox reactions (biological oxidation and<br>
reduction reactions) by the transfer of hydrogen, oxygen, or electrons from one<br>
molecule to another. Example;<br>
<b>Ethanal + NADH + H+  dehydrogenase   Ethanol + NAD+</b><br>
Hydrogen is simultaneously lost from NADH and gained by ethanal. NADH is<br>
oxidised to NAD+ and ethanal is reduced to ethanol.<br>
b)  Transferases which catalyse the transfer of a group from one compound to another.<br>
<b>Example;</b><br>
<b>Glutamic asid + Pyruvic</b><br>
aminotransferase<br>
α-ketoglutaric acid + alanine<br>
The R-group on the amino acid, glutamic acid, is exchanged with the R-group on a<br>
keto acid, pyruvic acid. A new amino acid, alanine is formed along with a new keto<br>
acid, α-ketoglutaric acid.<br>
c) 	 Hydrolases which catalyse the splitting of a large substrate molecule into two<br>
smaller products in the presence of water (hydrolysis process).<br>
<b>Example;</b><br>
<b>Lactose + Water</b><br>
<b>Lactase</b><br>
<b>Glucose + Galactose</b><br>
The disaccharide, lactose, is broken down into two monosaccharide residues by<br>
the lactase enzyme in the presence of water. All digestive enzymes fall under this<br>
category.<br>
d)   Lyases which catalyse the removal of a chemical group by the process other than<br>
hydrolysis.<br>
<b>Example;</b><br>
<b>Pyruvic acid  decarboxylase</b><br>
<b>Ethanal + Carbon dioxide</b><br>

6-phosphate in the presence of the phospho-hexo-isomerase enzyme. The position<br>
of the phosphate group in the glucose-1-phosphate molecule is changed to form the<br>
isomer glucose-6-phosphate.<br>

1.4.5 Adenosine Triphosphate (ATP)<br>
<b>ATP is a nucleoside triphosphate used in</b><br>
cells, often called the “molecular unit of<br>
currency” of intracellular energy transfer.<br>
<b>It belongs to a category of high energy</b><br>
compounds that release energy when<br>
the bond between the second and third<br>
phosphate is broken. The presence of<br>
these high energy bonds makes it possible<br>
for ATP to store and release energy for<br>
cellular reactions.<br>
<b>Chemical composition</b><br>
<b>ATP consists of adenosine (composed of</b><br>
an adenine ring and a five carbon sugar,<br>
ribose sugar) and three phosphate groups<br>
(triphosphate) (Figure 1.50). ATP is<br>
highly soluble in water and is quite stable<br>
in solutions with pH ranging between 6.8<br>
and 7.4, but easily hydrolysed at extreme<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   72</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
73<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
pH. Therefore, ATP is best stored as an<br>
anhydrous salt. It is also considered as<br>
the energy currency of the cell and life,<br>
because all cells need this energy molecule<br>
in order to perform their functions in the<br>
human body.<br>
<b>Formation of ATP</b><br>
<b>One molecule of ATP contains three</b><br>
phosphate groups, and is produced by a<br>
wide variety of enzymes, including ATP<br>
synthase, from adenosine diphosphate<br>
(ADP) or adenosine monophosphate<br>
(AMP) and various phosphate group<br>
donors.<br>
<b>Phosphate groups</b><br>
<b>Adenine</b><br>
<b>Ribose</b><br>
<b>Figure 1.50 The chemical structure of</b><br>
adenosine triphosphate<br>
<b>Synthesis of ATP</b><br>
<b>ATP is synthesised when an inorganic</b><br>
phosphate, Pi, is energetically bonded to<br>
adenosine diphosphate, ADP. The amount<br>
of energy required in this process is<br>
30.6kJ.<br>
<b>30.6 kJ + ADP + Pi</b><br>
<b>Condensation</b><br>
<b>ATP + H2O</b><br>
<b>The processes that yield ATP, therefore,</b><br>
involve phosphorylation. They are of two<br>
types:<br>
a)	 Photosynthetic phosphorylation, ATP<br>
is synthesised during light dependent<br>
phase of photosynthesis. In this case,<br>
when light of appropriate wavelength<br>
strikes on the chlorophyll, its electrons<br>
get excited, as they gain excess<br>
energy. In this state, they are boosted<br>
to high energy levels. As the electrons<br>
move downhill back to ground state,<br>
they lose the gained excess energy,<br>
which is used to combine ADP and<br>
<b>Pi to form ATP. This process is called</b><br>
photophosphorylation (refer section<br>
5.2).<br>
b)	 Respiratory phosphorylation, which<br>
is the cellular process involving two<br>
stages. The ﬁrst stage is substrate<br>
level phosphorylation in which ATP<br>
is synthesised in the cytoplasm, by<br>
glycolytic pathway. The second stage<br>
is oxidative phosphorylation, which<br>
occurs in the electron transport system<br>
in the inner mitochondrial membranes.<br>
<b>Roles of ATP</b><br>
<b>ATP provides energy for the following:</b><br>
a)	 Synthesis of macromolecules, such as<br>
polysaccharides from monosaccharides,<br>
proteins from amino acids and DNA<br>
replication.<br>
b)	 Active transport across the plasma<br>
membranes in the cell, example:<br>
sodium-potassium pump.<br>
c)	 Cellular movements, such as cilia<br>
action in trachea and fallopian tubes,<br>
spindle ﬁbers during cell division and<br>
muscle contraction.<br>
d)	 Production of useful secretions by<br>
vesicles.<br>
e)	 Activation of important molecules,<br>
such as glucose, which is activated into<br>
glucose-6-phosphate during respiration.<br>

<b>1.5  Water as a constituent of the cell</b><br>
<b>Water is the most abundant molecule in</b><br>
a cell, constituting about 80% of total<br>
volume of cell. The liquid part of the cell<br>
is called cytosol. Generally, without water,<br>
life would not exist on this planet as water<br>
supports metabolic reactions.<br>
<b>Properties of water</b><br>
<b>Water is an excellent solvent for polar</b><br>
compounds such as ionic substances, like<br>
salts, and non-ionic substances like sugars.<br>
<b>It has a high heat of vaporization due to</b><br>
hydrogen bonds which hold molecules<br>
together. Latent heat of vaporization means<br>
a measure of the heat energy required to<br>
overcome the attractive forces between<br>
molecules and make them to escape in<br>
form of a gas (vaporize). This property is<br>
significant in minimizing water loss from<br>
the body and cooling it.<br>
<b>Water has high heat capacity which is the</b><br>
heat required to raise the temperature of<br>
1 kilogram of water by   10C. By having<br>
high heat capacity means that, increase in<br>
heat energy can lead to relatively small<br>
rise in temperature. Also water has a high<br>
heat of fusion. It requires a relatively large<br>
amount of energy to melt its solid state (the<br>
ice). Conversely, liquid water must lose a<br>
relatively large amount of heat energy to<br>
freeze. Thus, this property is important in<br>
maintaining natural states of cell contents.<br>
<b>Water has a density of 1000 kgm-3 or 1 gcm-3 at</b><br>
standard temperature and pressure. However,<br>
the density decreases with a decrease in<br>
temperature. Thus, frozen water (ice) ﬂoats<br>
on liquid water. Water has a high surface<br>
tension and cohesive force.  Cohesive force<br>
is the force by which individual molecules<br>
stick together.<br>
<b>Water being liquid at room temperature,</b><br>
provides a liquid environment inside the<br>
cells. Example glycolysis and synthesis of<br>
proteins that take place in the cytoplasm.<br>
<b>Additionally, water has a high latent</b><br>
heat of vaporisation. This means that<br>
water needs a lot of energy to evaporate<br>
hence, helps in cooling the body through<br>
sweating.<br>
<b>Roles of water</b><br>
<b>The roles of water in living organisms can</b><br>
be categorized as follows:<br>
<b>The metabolic role of water</b><br>
a)	 It is used for hydrolysis of many<br>
substances, such as proteins into<br>
amino acids, fats into fatty acids and<br>
glycerol. In the hydrolytic processes,<br>
water is brought in by enzymes or<br>
hydrolytic reagents such as mineral<br>
acids.<br>
b)	 It is the medium for all cellular<br>
metabolic processes, for instance,<br>
water is an important raw material for<br>
photosynthesis.<br>
c)	 It facilitates diffusion of materials<br>
across surfaces; for example, for<br>
passage of food solutions into blood<br>
stream across the walls of the ileum.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   74</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
75<br>
<b>Cytology</b><br>
<b>Water as solvent</b><br>
<b>Water is a universal solvent; it readily</b><br>
dissolves other substances, hence it is<br>
used for the following purposes:<br>
a)	 For<br>
transportation<br>
of<br>
various<br>
substances from one part of the body<br>
to another. These are carried by blood<br>
and other ﬂuids in solution.<br>
b)	 For removal of metabolic wastes, such<br>
as nitrogenous waste products. These<br>
excretory wastes are removed from the<br>
body in the form of solution in water.<br>
<b>Water as a lubricant</b><br>
<b>Due to its viscosity property, water takes</b><br>
part in lubricating body parts which<br>
slide past each other. It makes various<br>
lubricating ﬂuids in the body; example:<br>
mucus, which aids movements in animals<br>
such as snails. It also lubricates internal<br>
parts, such as gut walls in animals,<br>
the synovial ﬂuid (which lubricates<br>
movements in joints of vertebrates) and<br>
pleural ﬂuid (which lubricates movements<br>
of lungs during breathing).<br>
<b>Supporting role of water</b><br>
<b>The supporting role of water is made clear</b><br>
in the following examples:<br>
a)	 In animals such as annelids and<br>
nematodes, water exerts a hydrostatic<br>
pressure which helps to support and<br>
maintain their structure. This is known<br>
as the hydrostatic skeleton.<br>
b)	 Osmotic inﬂux of water into plant<br>
cells generates turgor pressure which<br>
supports<br>
herbaceous<br>
(non-woody)<br>
plants. The turgor pressure also<br>
supports primary growth in woody<br>
plants.<br>
c)	 Water found in humours of the eye<br>
helps to maintain the eye’s shape.<br>
d)	 The mammalian foetus is supported<br>
and protected by an amniotic ﬂuid,<br>
which is largely water.<br>
e)	 Water provides support (habitat) to<br>
aquatic organisms such as ﬁsh.<br>
<b>Other functions</b><br>
<b>Other functions of water include the</b><br>
following:<br>
a)	 It controls body temperature in<br>
mammals. Evaporation of sweat from<br>
the body surface has the cooling effect.<br>
b)	 It is an agent for dispersal of seeds,<br>
larvae, and male gametes of animals<br>
and lower plants such as Bryophytes.<br>
c)	 In mammals, ﬂuids in the inner ear are<br>
important for hearing and balance.<br>

<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Two</b><br>
<b>Principles of</b><br>
classification<br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
Planet Earth contains different kinds of organisms, ranging in size from microorganisms<br>
to macroorganisms. Due to the existing diversity of organisms, it is difficult to clearly<br>
understand their characteristics and how they are related in evolutionary terms.<br>
Classification helps in identification of organisms that share common characteristics<br>
and traits. In this chapter, you will learn about the concept of classification, classification<br>
systems, categories of classification, taxonomic ranks, nomenclature and taxonomic keys.<br>
<b>2.1  The concept of classification</b><br>
<b>The science of classification of living</b><br>
organisms dates back to 384-322 BC,<br>
when the ﬁrst taxonomist, Aristotle<br>
initially classified organisms into two<br>
major groups, namely animals and plants.<br>
<b>With advances in science and technology,</b><br>
classification has constantly been dynamic<br>
and more reﬁned. For example, by using<br>
electron microscope and molecular<br>
techniques, taxonomists have classified<br>
organisms into five kingdoms namely<br>
<b>Monera, Protoctista, Fungi, Plantae, and</b><br>
<b>Animalia. Considering the fact that the</b><br>
number of organisms occupying the earth<br>
is large, it has been difficult for scientists<br>
to study each individual organism. Since<br>
related organisms share some common<br>
features, scientists group related organisms<br>
together so that they can easily study their<br>
characteristics.<br>
<b>The process of grouping organisms based</b><br>
on their common or shared characteristics<br>
is called classification. For organisms to be<br>
classified uniformly across the world, a set<br>
of principles, procedures, and rules must be<br>
observed. The principles of classiﬁcation<br>
were pioneered by Carolus Linnaeus and<br>
published in the 18th century in his book<br>
“Systema Naturae”. In that publication<br>
a simple way of naming organisms was<br>
also explained. The science of classifying<br>
organisms is termed taxonomy and the<br>
scientist who classifies organisms is known<br>
as a taxonomist. Taxonomy involves<br>
placing organisms into their respective<br>
groups and sub groups called taxa without<br>
considering evolution. The science of<br>
placing organisms in their respective taxa<br>
that reflect their evolutionary history is<br>
called systematics.<br>
77<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   77</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
78<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Importance of classiﬁcation of</b><br>
organisms<br>
<b>The importance of classiﬁcation of</b><br>
organisms is summarised as follows:<br>
a)	 It helps to reduce large number of<br>
groups of dissimilar organisms into<br>
small number of manageable groups<br>
of organisms with more features in<br>
common. For example, all species of<br>
ﬂower producing plants are placed<br>
under division Angiospermophyta.<br>
<b>Similarly, all species of non ﬂowering</b><br>
plants<br>
are<br>
placed<br>
in<br>
division<br>
<b>Coniferophyta and all spore producing</b><br>
plant species with conductive tissues<br>
are placed in division Filicinophyta.<br>
<b>Once plant species are grouped in this</b><br>
way, it makes it easy to understand<br>
their life cycle.<br>
b)	 It makes easy to classify all known<br>
organisms and predict the placement<br>
of the yet to be discovered based on<br>
already known features. For example,<br>
if a new plant species with ﬂowers<br>
is discovered today it will be placed<br>
under division Angiospermophyta.<br>
<b>Similarly, if it produces ﬂowers, leaves,</b><br>
berries and stems with stipules as<br>
those of coffee plants; it will be placed<br>
under the coffee family Rubiaceae.<br>
<b>Furthermore, if it has more features</b><br>
in common with Coffea arabica it<br>
will deﬁnitely be placed under genus<br>
<b>Coffea,</b><br>
making<br>
its<br>
classiﬁcation<br>
simple and less time consuming.<br>
c)	 Classiﬁcation simpliﬁes communication<br>
among taxonomists worldwide. This is<br>
because, taxonomically, an organism<br>
is assigned only one name, unlike<br>
the use of common names in which<br>
one species is named differently in<br>
different places. Zea mays is a scientiﬁc<br>
name for maize. In America, maize is<br>
also known as corn but in Britain corn<br>
is wheat which sounds confusing.<br>
<b>However, Zea mays worldwide will</b><br>
only refer to different names of what<br>
we call maize in Tanzania.<br>
d)	 It<br>
helps<br>
to<br>
show<br>
evolutionary<br>
relationship between organisms. The<br>
classiﬁcation of organisms is mostly<br>
based on evolutionary history and<br>
relationship among them. This makes it<br>
easy to understand how organisms are<br>
related interms of their evolutionary<br>
history. A Leopard (Panthera pardus)<br>
and Lion (Panthera leo) which are<br>
both classiﬁed under family Felidae<br>
and genus Panthera have a lot of<br>
features in common. These are hairs,<br>
mammary glands, and other various<br>
morphological features. It is therefore<br>
most likely that they share a common<br>
ancestor.<br>
e)	 Organisms grouped in the same taxon,<br>
such as at genus level, normally have<br>
many common features. For example,<br>
all plant species under genus Coffea<br>
resemble coffee plants in many aspects,<br>
such as leaf type, leaf arrangement<br>
on stems, ﬂower type, and stipules.<br>
<b>They all differ from members of</b><br>
cotton plants, genus Gossypium on the<br>
same features. This kind of grouping<br>
therefore simpliﬁes description of<br>
organisms because understanding of<br>
features of just one individual within a<br>
certain group/taxon can give an insight<br>
into all other members within the same<br>
taxon.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   78</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
79<br>
<b>Principles of classification</b><br>
f)	 It paves a way towards understanding<br>
other disciplines, such as ecology,<br>
medicine and pharmacy. For example,<br>
plants of the same genus may<br>
have similar ecological or growth<br>
requirements.<br>
<b>Ecologically,</b><br>
all<br>
plants under the bean/legume family<br>
(Leguminosae/Fabaceae)<br>
have<br>
the<br>
ability to harbour Nitrogen ﬁxing<br>
bacteria. Likewise, members under the<br>
same genus may produce secondary<br>
metabolites or biochemicals with<br>
similar medicinal or pharmacological<br>
properties.<br>
<b>2.2   Classiﬁcation systems</b><br>
<b>Organisms can be grouped in many ways</b><br>
for convenience of studying them and<br>
to fully appreciate their comparative<br>
evolutionary relatedness.  Traditionally,<br>
all organisms were grouped by Aristotle<br>
as either animals or plants with just<br>
a few sub groups in each group. This<br>
classiﬁcation had shortfalls as it did not<br>
consider the evolutionary relationship<br>
among organisms grouped together. For<br>
example, grouping into one or single<br>
group of all ﬂying animals such as birds,<br>
bats, moths and butterﬂies, as done by<br>
Aristotle was taxonomically misleading.<br>
<b>This is because bats are actually mammals</b><br>
with hairs on their bodies and they differ<br>
from birds which have feathers. They<br>
also differ from the butterﬂies, which are<br>
insects. A penguin which has feathers<br>
would be difﬁcult to classify it with birds<br>
into the same group as it lacks the ability<br>
to ﬂy.<br>
<b>The controversy of classiﬁcation was</b><br>
partly resolved by the taxonomist Carolus<br>
<b>Linnaeus in the 1700s. This taxonomist</b><br>
retained the two major groups or kingdoms<br>
of organisms, Animalia and Plantae.<br>
<b>Linnaeus introduced several taxa under</b><br>
each kingdom to accommodate groups<br>
of organisms sharing more common<br>
characteristics in which resemblance among<br>
organisms increased hierarchically down<br>
the groups. He classiﬁed organisms based<br>
on their shared natural features.<br>
<b>Since then, classiﬁcation has been very</b><br>
dynamic, and has gone through five systems.<br>
<b>These are artificial, natural, phyletic,</b><br>
phenetics and phylogenetics or cladistics.<br>
<b>In artificial approach, only one or a few</b><br>
observable characteristics were used.<br>
<b>Natural approach grouped together</b><br>
individuals reflecting how they occur<br>
in nature.  This approach did not reflect<br>
evolutionary history though it used many<br>
characteristics.  Natural approach was<br>
followed by phyletic approach based on<br>
<b>Charles Darwin’s publication; “The origin</b><br>
of species by means of natural selection”<br>
and the development of modern theory<br>
of chromosomes.  In phyletic system<br>
organisms were classified from simple to<br>
complex, reflecting evolutionary history.<br>
<b>However, assigning of ancestral or derived</b><br>
status was done subjectively.  Phyletic was<br>
followed by phenetic approach.  Phenetics<br>
uses many characteristics and mathematical<br>
algorithms to group similar organisms in<br>
same cluster and different organisms in<br>
different clusters.  As in natural system,<br>
phenetics does not consider evolutionary<br>
history.  The most recent approach is<br>
known as phylogenetic.  This approach<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   79</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
80<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
bring together organisms that share derived<br>
characters in groups called clades.  Clades<br>
thus are made up of organisms that share<br>
the most recent ancestor.<br>
<b>The change from one approach to the next</b><br>
was influenced by advances in science and<br>
technology leading to use of new source<br>
of taxonomic data, methods of analysis<br>
and weaknesses observed in previous<br>
approach.  Phylogenetic approach use DNA<br>
markers and different computer software<br>
and analysis programs.<br>
<b>Types of classification systems</b><br>
<b>There</b><br>
are<br>
two<br>
major<br>
systems<br>
of<br>
classification; artificial and natural system<br>
of classification.<br>
<b>Artificial system of classiﬁcation</b><br>
<b>This system of classification uses a few</b><br>
easily observable characteristics to classify<br>
organisms for easy and quick comparison or<br>
study. Examples of artiﬁcial classiﬁcation<br>
could be when grouping all animals as fauna<br>
and all plants as ﬂora, or when grouping all<br>
organisms on the basis of their body size as<br>
microorganisms and macroorganisms. Other<br>
examples are when grouping animals as<br>
ﬂying and non-ﬂying animals, edible versus<br>
non-edible plants, spices versus non-spices<br>
plants, predators versus prey animals, and<br>
medicinal versus non-medicinal plants. As<br>
noted from the above examples, this system<br>
of classiﬁcation neglects natural relationship<br>
existing among these groups of organisms.<br>
<b>For instance, birds and bats are all ﬂying</b><br>
animals, but the former have feathers while<br>
the latter have hairs and mammary glands.<br>
<b>Similarly, medicinal plants producing</b><br>
ﬂowers, seeds, and fruits are different<br>
from spore producing medicinal plants.<br>
<b>Thus, the medicinal value attached to these</b><br>
plants cannot sufﬁce their placement in one<br>
group because they have no shared natural<br>
features. This limits the application of this<br>
classiﬁcation system despite its significant<br>
merits. Artificial system cannot group<br>
individuals that are evolutionary related.<br>
<b>As a result all classfication systems that</b><br>
does not reflect evolutionary history are<br>
considered to be artificial.<br>
<b>Merits of artificial system of</b><br>
classiﬁcation<br>
a)	 It is simple to identify and classify<br>
organisms since newly discovered<br>
organisms with just a few known<br>
information can be easily ﬁtted in. It<br>
therefore takes short time in placing an<br>
organism into its group.<br>
b)	 It is stable; it does not change with time<br>
or discovery of new organisms given its<br>
broad nature of classiﬁcation.<br>
c)	 It is less expensive; since it uses<br>
few observable features to group the<br>
organism.<br>
d)	 This system does not require special<br>
classiﬁcation techniques or skilled<br>
personnel.<br>
<b>Demerits of artificial system of</b><br>
classiﬁcation<br>
a)	 It does not consider evolutionary<br>
or phylogenetic relationship among<br>
organisms. Closely related organisms<br>
under the artiﬁcial system are most<br>
likely to be placed in different groups<br>
while unrelated organisms such as bats<br>
and birds may be grouped together<br>
because both have wings. This makes<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   80</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
81<br>
<b>Principles of classification</b><br>
the system somewhat misleading<br>
because, phylogenetically, organisms<br>
belonging to one group would be<br>
expected to have originated from a<br>
common ancestor.<br>
b)	 It provides only a limited characteristics<br>
or information about each member. For<br>
example, organisms such as viruses,<br>
bacteria and some fungi are grouped<br>
as microorganisms because they<br>
are microscopic. This classiﬁcation<br>
has ignored other features like their<br>
modes of feeding, reproduction,<br>
and cell structure. Use of these<br>
features necessitate grouping of these<br>
microorganisms in different groups.<br>
c)	 It does not allow the prediction of<br>
information, hence it limits recent<br>
advancements in taxonomy.<br>
d)	 It does not incorporate new discoveries.<br>
<b>New species cannot be easily added to</b><br>
the existing groups.<br>
<b>The natural system of classiﬁcation</b><br>
<b>The natural system of classiﬁcation</b><br>
is the system of grouping organisms<br>
based on natural features they have in<br>
common, particularly those that reflects<br>
the evolutionary relationship. Characters<br>
that show evolutionary relationship are<br>
shared derived ones which are many and<br>
are shared aross members of one group<br>
<b>With advances in science and technology,</b><br>
the natural system of classification has<br>
incorporated anatomical, embryological,<br>
serology, physiological, and molecular<br>
characteristics and techniques in grouping<br>
organisms.<br>
<b>Merits of natural system of</b><br>
classiﬁcation<br>
a)	It<br>
allows<br>
organisms<br>
which<br>
are<br>
genetically and evolutionarily related<br>
to be grouped in the same taxon, the<br>
evolutionary relatedness increases down<br>
the hierarchy. This clearly implies that<br>
organisms grouped together at genus<br>
level will be more evolutionarily related<br>
than those grouped at family level.<br>
b)	This classiﬁcation system considers<br>
homology of all characters of organisms<br>
which makes it easy to predict<br>
information about missing links in<br>
the course of evolution of organisms<br>
even though there is no fossil evidence<br>
to substantiate the link. For example,<br>
some taxonomists have predicted<br>
that ﬂowering plants evolved from<br>
<b>Pteridosperms, a seeded fern which is</b><br>
not existing.<br>
c)	It<br>
allows<br>
critical<br>
thinking<br>
and<br>
development<br>
of<br>
inquiry<br>
minds.<br>
<b>Although</b><br>
description<br>
based<br>
on<br>
morphological, physiological, anatomical,<br>
and embryological features of a newly<br>
discovered species has to be done before<br>
a decision on its proper placement to a<br>
taxon is reached.<br>
d)	It is accurate, since it involves plenty<br>
of scientiﬁc research to gather enough<br>
information before the actual placement<br>
of an organism to a particular taxon is<br>
decided.<br>
<b>Demerits of natural system of</b><br>
classiﬁcation<br>
a)	 It makes classiﬁcation of organisms<br>
tedious,<br>
expensive<br>
and<br>
time<br>
consuming. It is very difﬁcult, since it<br>

requires much information to place the<br>
organisms into their respective groups.<br>
<b>Newly identified organisms are thus</b><br>
not easily classiﬁed.<br>
b)	 It is not stable; it changes with the<br>
increasing diversity of organisms and<br>
new discoveries based on expanding<br>
knowledge, science and technology.<br>
<b>It was the discovery of the light</b><br>
microscope that led to the discovery<br>
of kingdom Protista. Later, with<br>
the invention of the scan electron<br>

<b>Archaeae and Eubacteria into different</b><br>
groups within the same kingdom<br>
Monera.<br>
c)	 Given the wealth of information,<br>
knowledge, techniques, and skills<br>
required to classify organisms, natural<br>
classiﬁcation is limited to taxonomic<br>
experts.<br>

<b>Principles of classification</b><br>
<b>2.3   Taxonomic ranks</b><br>
<b>The classiﬁcation system by Carolus</b><br>
<b>Linnaeus in 1700s introduced a systematic</b><br>
way of grouping organisms stepwise, from<br>
a very broad group of organisms called<br>
<b>Kingdom to narrow level of individual</b><br>
organism<br>
called<br>
species.<br>
<b>Linnaeus,</b><br>
recognizing<br>
the<br>
natural<br>
variations<br>
existing among organisms divided them<br>
into ﬁve broad groups. He arranged<br>
them hierarchically from the broadest to<br>
the smallest in terms of the number of<br>
organisms in each. Originally, these groups<br>
were Kingdom, Class, Order, Genus<br>
(singular) Genera (plural), and Species.<br>
<b>However, later on, two more groups were</b><br>
introduced. The ﬁrst introduced group<br>
was called Family, which was created<br>
by Michael Adanson in 1763, and it was<br>
allocated between order and genus. The<br>
second group termed Division or Phylum<br>
(singular) phyla (plural) was introduced<br>
by Ernest Haeckel in 1866 and it was<br>
assigned between kingdom and class,<br>
making seven hierarchical groups namely<br>
<b>Kingdom, Phylum, Class, Order, Family,</b><br>
<b>Genus, and Species (Figure 2.1), which</b><br>
are still being used by taxonomists today.<br>
<b>Phylum is used in animal classification</b><br>
and Division in plant classification.  The<br>
system in which organisms are classified<br>
hierarchically from kingdom to species<br>
level is called a taxonomic hierarchy or<br>
taxonomic ranking. Any named group<br>
or rank within the hierarchy such as<br>
kingdom or phylum is referred to as taxon<br>
(singular) taxa (plural). A taxonomic rank<br>
is therefore a level at which an organism<br>
is placed within the hierarchy.<br>
<b>Genus: Homo</b><br>
<b>Species: Homo sapiens</b><br>
<b>Family: Hominidae</b><br>
<b>Order: Primates</b><br>
<b>Class: Mammalia</b><br>
<b>Kingdom: Animalia</b><br>
<b>Phylum: Chordata</b><br>
<b>Figure 2.1 Classfication of human being</b><br>
<b>Organisms placed under the same taxon</b><br>
have features unique to that group; thus,<br>
they can be used to describe the entire group.<br>
<b>For example, members of same species have</b><br>
the largest number of characters that are<br>
more similar and often reproduce fertile<br>
offspring.  Lions fall in the species Panthera<br>
leo, leopard in Panthera pardus, and tiger<br>
<b>Panthera tigris.  Thus  lion, leopard and</b><br>
tiger are different species  in the same<br>
genus Panthera.  The genus comprises of<br>
relatively more closely related organisms<br>
with similar morphology, structure,<br>
reproductive organs and most importantly,<br>
evolutionary history compared to those<br>
at the higher ranks.  However, they can<br>
not interbred to produce fertile offspring.<br>
<b>Members of different related genera fall in</b><br>
one family, families is one order and related<br>
orders into one class. Related classes are<br>
placed in one phylum/division and related<br>
phyla in a kingdom. Kingdom Plantae,<br>
for example, contains four divisions,<br>
namely Angiospermophyta (Angiosperm),<br>
<b>Coniferophyta (Gymnosperm), Pteridophyta</b><br>
or Filicinophyta (Ferns and their relatives),<br>
and Bryophyta (Moss).<br>

10/10/2019   14:06<br>

84<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Kingdom  Animalia is made up of Polifera,</b><br>
<b>Cnidaria, Platyhelminthes, Nematoda and</b><br>
its relatives (the round worms), Annelida,<br>
<b>Arthropoda, Molusca, Echnodermata,</b><br>
Chordata and several other smaller phyla.<br>
<b>Organisms at the rank of kingdom have a</b><br>
few features in common compared to the<br>
organisms at lower ranks. This book will<br>
focus on five selected phyla of Kingdom<br>
<b>Animalia, namely, Platyhelminthes,</b><br>
<b>Nematoda, Annelida, Arthropoda, and</b><br>
Chordata.<br>
<b>Importance of taxonomic hierarchy in</b><br>
classification<br>
a)	 It provides a standard method to be<br>
followed by taxonomists in assigning<br>
new scientific names to a newly<br>
discovered species.  This makes it easy<br>
to classify or group organisms.<br>
b)	 It provides a classification system<br>
of living organisms which expresses<br>
natural or evolutionary relationship<br>
among the members of the same group<br>
and between various organisms.<br>
c)	 It provides smooth communication<br>
among the taxonomists, while avoiding<br>
confusion and repetition of species<br>
names.<br>
d)	 It simplifies access to information on<br>
various organisms across the taxa.<br>
<b>Exercises 2.1</b><br>
1.   Differentiate between artificial and<br>
natural systems of classification.<br>
2.  Explain the concept of “ranks” as<br>
used in classification.<br>
3.  Explain what would be the problem<br>
if organisms were not grouped into<br>
ranks.<br>
<b>2.4  Nomenclature</b><br>
<b>The process of assigning scientiﬁc names</b><br>
to organisms is called nomenclature. The<br>
biological nomenclature uses two names<br>
that is the generic name representing the<br>
name of the genus and specific name<br>
representing the species name. The Swedish<br>
taxonomist Carolus Linnaeus (1707-<br>
1778) is acknowledged for using binomial<br>
system consistenty. The word binomial<br>
is a combination of two Greek words bi-<br>
nomina which literally means ‘two-names.’<br>
<b>Binomial nomenclature follows a set of</b><br>
agreed rules and principles.<br>
2.4.1 Rules used in binomial<br>
nomenclature<br>
<b>Binomial nomenclature is governed by</b><br>
nomenclatural codes, which provide<br>
details of rules necessary in giving<br>
scientiﬁc names for various broad groups<br>
of organisms, such as animals, plants,<br>
fungi algae, bacteria, and virus. For<br>
example, the code of nomenclature for<br>
wild plants is called the International Code<br>
of Botanical Nomenclature (ICBN) while<br>
for animals is called the International Code<br>
of Zoological Nomenclature (ICZN). The<br>
rules used in binomial nomenclature are<br>
as follows:<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   84</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
85<br>
<b>Principles of classification</b><br>
a)	 The name of an organism should come<br>
from its generic and speciﬁc name.<br>
<b>For example human being belongs</b><br>
to genus Homo and species sapiens;<br>
hence, its scientiﬁc name should be<br>
<b>Homo sapiens. Sweet potato belongs</b><br>
to genus Ipomoea and species batatas;<br>
its scientiﬁc name should therefore be<br>
Ipomoea batatas.<br>
b)	 The full scientific name should<br>
include name of the author; the<br>
person who was the first to publish<br>
the name effectively. For example,<br>
<b>Rana temporaria L, where L stands</b><br>
for Linnaeus means Linnaeus was the<br>
person who effectively published this<br>
name.<br>
c)	 A speciﬁc epithet may be taken from<br>
any source and may even be composed<br>
arbitrarily.<br>
d)	 An organism can bear only one correct<br>
scientific name, which is the earliest<br>
effectively assigned to it, if several<br>
names had been given to it earlier.<br>
e)	 The ﬁrst name of the binomial, that is<br>
the generic name, always begins with<br>
a capital letter and the entire species<br>
name is written in small letters as in<br>
Pennisetum mezianum.<br>
f)	 All binomial names should be written<br>
in the Latin language. If the name is<br>
hand written, it should be underlined<br>
separately or italised in case of a print<br>
form. For instance, the scientiﬁc name<br>
of a leopard can be written as Panthera<br>
pardus when hand written or Panthera<br>
pardus when printed.<br>
g)	 Any scientiﬁc name is not legitimate,<br>
unless it is accompanied by a suitable<br>
description and a diagnosis which<br>
reflects only the unique characteristics<br>
of the species.<br>
h)	 If the binomial name is cited more<br>
than once in a paper or report, the<br>
genus name can be abbreviated the<br>
second time it is written in the same<br>
document. In this case, the ﬁrst letter<br>
of the genus is written in a capital<br>
letter and separated from species<br>
name by a dot. For instance, Panthera<br>
lupus can be written as P. lupus or the<br>
bacteria Escherichia coli as E. coli.<br>
<b>The abbreviation “spp.” is used to</b><br>
represent several species. In this case,<br>
the genus, but not the abbreviation, is<br>
italicized or underlined. For instance,<br>
<b>Hyparrhenia spp. implies several</b><br>
species of the genus Hyparrhenia such<br>
as Hyparrhenia rufa, Hyparrhenia<br>
collina, and Hyparrhenia variabilis.<br>
<b>On the other hand, if the actual</b><br>
speciﬁc name cannot be ascertained<br>
or speciﬁed, an abbreviation “sp”<br>
followed by a dot is placed after the<br>
generic name, but it is not italicised<br>
while the generic name is italicised or<br>
underlined, for example, Hyparrhenia<br>
sp.<br>

2.4.2  Signiﬁcance of scientiﬁc names<br>
<b>The use of scientiﬁc names is very</b><br>
important for the following reasons:<br>
a)	 They<br>
simplify<br>
communication<br>
worldwide. This is because only one<br>
valid name is used for each living<br>
organism.<br>
b)	 The<br>
system<br>
provides<br>
uniformity<br>
in naming organisms worldwide.<br>
<b>This helps to avoid confusion and</b><br>
ambiguity.<br>
c)	 Scientific names are not influenced by<br>
language barrier or region as common<br>
names.<br>

of<br>

the<br>
significance<br>
of<br>

<b>2.5    Taxonomic keys</b><br>
<b>There are several ways of identifying an</b><br>
unidentiﬁed organism. Identification is<br>
naming an organism based on existing<br>
classfication. The easiest way is by<br>
consulting and directly asking an expert such<br>
as a taxonomist with profound knowledge<br>
and experience in identification. Also, by<br>
comparing the unidentiﬁed organism with<br>
authentically identified specimen stored<br>
in museums for animals or herbaria for<br>
plant specimens. The other way is to use<br>
a local ﬁeld guide in which pictures and/<br>
or drawings of organisms coupled with<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   86</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
87<br>
<b>Principles of classification</b><br>
explanations are available for comparison.<br>
<b>Moreover, taxonomic keys may be used for</b><br>
identification of unidentiﬁed organisms.<br>
<b>Taxonomic keys are tools or devices used</b><br>
by taxonomists for a quick identiﬁcation<br>
of organisms. The identification is based<br>
on permanent contrasting phenotypic<br>
features. Phenotypic features, also known<br>
as morphological features, include features<br>
such as hairs, stipules, shape, number of<br>
appendages, and segments. In order to<br>
identify an organism using taxonomic<br>
keys, all readily observable features of<br>
the unknown organisms are recorded ﬁrst.<br>
<b>These are later matched with diagnostic</b><br>
features of a named taxon.<br>
<b>The diagnostic features are characteristics</b><br>
unique to a particular taxon or rank. They<br>
differ from those of an organism belonging<br>
to a different taxa. Diagnostic features<br>
therefore depict natural phylogenetic or<br>
evolutionary relationship, and they are<br>
reﬂective of features used in both artiﬁcial<br>
and natural systems of classiﬁcation.<br>
<b>Taxonomic keys enable organisms to be</b><br>
identiﬁed into their appropriate taxon and<br>
it increases familiarity of taxonomist with<br>
the identiﬁed organisms; since it involves<br>
careful investigation of the presence<br>
or absence of particular structures and<br>
manifestation of present characteristics.<br>
<b>Procedures for construction of</b><br>
taxonomic keys<br>
a)	 The organisms to be identiﬁed<br>
are collected and displayed for a<br>
thorough observation of identiﬁable<br>
features. The collected organisms,<br>
such as, animals and plants are called<br>
specimens.<br>
b)	 Each of the collected specimens is<br>
carefully examined, and its easily<br>
observable features are identiﬁed and<br>
recorded in a notebook.<br>
c)	 A table listing the specimens in one<br>
column and characters to be studied<br>
in the other is created.<br>
d)	 From the table, study the recorded<br>
specimen features carefully and<br>
determine a general pair of exclusion<br>
character which can be used to<br>
divide the entire group of collected<br>
specimens into two.<br>
e)	 Subdivide each of the obtained<br>
groups into two more groups using<br>
shared or common features.<br>
f)	 The second group is subdivided<br>
further into two other smaller groups<br>
based on their common features.<br>
g)	 The smaller groups are continuously<br>
subdivided into two groups using<br>
contrasting pairs of statements or<br>
couplets until all of the specimens are<br>
identified.<br>
h)	 If the constructed key is numbered,<br>
allocate the number of couplet to be<br>
considered next after each step to the<br>
last step in which the specimen is<br>
identiﬁed.<br>
i)	 Write down the couplets in a special<br>
pattern to get a desired framework for<br>
a particular key.<br>
<b>Types of taxonomic keys</b><br>
<b>There are various types of diagnostic keys</b><br>
used in taxonomy. The most common<br>
and simple key used for identification of<br>
organisms is called dichotomous key. This<br>
type of key was pioneered by a French<br>
taxonomist, Jean Baptiste Lamark in<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   87</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
88<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
1778. Dichotomous is a Greek word which<br>
literally means ‘divided into 2 branches’ or<br>
‘cut in half.’ This kind of key is constructed<br>
using two statements with contrasting<br>
features and mutually exclusive choices at<br>
a branching point. The pair of statements is<br>
called leads or couplet, and they provide two<br>
alternatives at each branching, each leading<br>
to a subsequent statement until an organism<br>
is identiﬁed. As one moves down the key,<br>
a large group of organisms with diverse<br>
features is reduced to a smaller group and<br>
ﬁnally to an individual organism or taxon.<br>
<b>The couplets can be organised using</b><br>
numbers as in numeric keys or letters. It is<br>
worth noting that the characteristics used<br>
in dichotomous keys may be quantitative or<br>
qualitative. The former can be exampliﬁed<br>
by features such as number of petals, leaf<br>
size, and petiole size, while the latter<br>
could be leaf shape, stem texture such as<br>
woody or herbaceous and ﬂower-colour.<br>
<b>In constructing dichotomous keys, features</b><br>
used in describing organisms must be<br>
permanent but not temporal or transitional<br>
features, such as those emanating from<br>
seasonal variations or growth of an organism<br>
based on how couplets are organised in<br>
directing the user from one choice to the<br>
next. Two types of dichotomous keys are<br>
recognised. These are indented key and<br>
bracketed or simple numbered key.<br>
<b>Indented dichotomous key</b><br>
<b>In indented dichotomous key pairs of</b><br>
contrasting statements are successively<br>
indented, with equal distance from the left.<br>
<b>Each statement (or lead) starts with the same</b><br>
character being described, and they both<br>
begin with the same noun, such as leaves,<br>
corolla, and petiole.<br>
<b>Example: Indented dichotomous key for five plant species</b><br>
1a. Flowers present<br>
2a. Leaves small<br>
<b>Abelia grandiflora</b><br>
2b. Leaves large<br>
3a. Flowers red, petals free<br>
<b>Hibiscus rosa-sinensis</b><br>
3b. Flowers red, petals connate into tube<br>
<b>Delonix regia</b><br>
1b. Flowers absent<br>
4a. Tree with needle-like leaves<br>
<b>Pinus sylvestris</b><br>
4b. Shrub with leafy fronds<br>
<b>Dryopteris filix-mas</b><br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   88</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
89<br>
<b>Principles of classification</b><br>
<b>Bracketed dichotomous keys</b><br>
<b>In bracketed dichotomous keys, sets of</b><br>
couplets are kept together (bracketed),<br>
and they start at the same level near the<br>
margin. The couplets must be assigned<br>
numbers or letters. The user is provided<br>
with a pair of lead for identiﬁcation of<br>
unidentiﬁed organism or taxon and is<br>
directed by numbers to a subsequent<br>
lead, if the selected lead does not provide<br>
the answer.<br>
Example 1:  Bracketed dichotomous keys for five plant species<br>
1a. Flowers present...................................................2<br>
1b. Flowers absent....................................................3<br>
2a. Leaves small.......................................................Abelia grandiﬂora<br>
2b. Leaves large........................................................4<br>
3a. Tree with needle-like leaves...............................Pinus sylvestris<br>
3b. Shrub with leafy fronds......................................Dryopteris filix-mas<br>
4a. Flowers red, petals free.......................................Hibiscus rosa-sinensis<br>
4b. Flowers red, petals connate into tube.................Delonix regia<br>
Example 2: Bracketed dichotomous keys for seven animal species (Figure 2.2)<br>
1a. Organism has a backbone……………………….2<br>
1b. Organism does not have a backbone……………3<br>
2a. Organism has wings…………………………….4<br>
2b. Organism does not have wings………………….5<br>
3a. Organism has antennae ……………………….. Grasshopper<br>
3b. Organism does not have antennae………………Spider<br>
4a. Organism has feathers…………………………..Bird<br>
4b. Organism has hair……………………………….Bat<br>
5a. Organism has legs………………………………6<br>
5b. Organism does not have legs……………………Snake<br>
6a. Organism has a shell…………………………….Turtle<br>
6b. Organism does not have a shell………………….Frog<br>

<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Three</b><br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of</b><br>
organisms<br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
In comparative studies of natural groups, organisms are classified on the basis of<br>
their shared characteristics and common ancestry. The grouping is done in relation to<br>
physiology, embryonic development, and phylogenetic relationship. In this chapter, you<br>
will learn the six groups of organisms, namely Viruses,  Monera, Protoctista, Fungi,<br>
Plantae, and Animalia.<br>
<b>3.1  General overview of classification</b><br>
<b>The natural groups of organisms are the</b><br>
broad categories of organisms formed<br>
by placing together organisms which<br>
are naturally or evolutionarily related<br>
for comparative purposes. This enables<br>
taxonomists across the world to compare<br>
for various reasons how individual groups<br>
of organisms are related. The number of<br>
natural groups of organisms, for example<br>
at kingdom level has been steadily<br>
increasing since the 18th century, when<br>
<b>Carolus Linnaeus introduced the natural</b><br>
classiﬁcation system from which kingdom<br>
<b>Animalia (multicellular heterotrophs) and</b><br>
<b>Plantae (multicellular autotrophs) were</b><br>
conceived. This system was adopted by<br>
many taxonomists and gained a wider<br>
popularity over the artiﬁcial system of<br>
classiﬁcation.<br>
<b>In 1866, the third kingdom of unicellular</b><br>
organisms, Protista was put forward by<br>
<b>Ernest Haeckel after its long placement</b><br>
under kingdom Animalia since it was ﬁrst<br>
discovered by Antoine van Leeuwenhoek<br>
in 1674. The discovery of the scanning<br>
electron microscope facilitated microscopic<br>
studies, and it made it possible to discern<br>
<b>Eukaryotes (organisms with distinct</b><br>
nucleus) from Prokaryotes (organisms<br>
lacking a clearly deﬁned nucleus but have<br>
their DNA lying free in the cytoplasm).<br>
<b>This led to the establishment of kingdom</b><br>
<b>Monera, in which all types of bacteria were</b><br>
grouped. The placement of multicellular<br>
eukaryotic saprophytic organisms such as<br>
mushrooms, yeast, and Rhizopus was still<br>
contradicting, because they were erratically<br>
classiﬁed under kingdom Plantae or Protista.<br>
<b>This prompted Robert Whitaker to propose</b><br>
kingdom Fungi in 1969 under which all<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   92</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
93<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
multicellular saprophytes were grouped,<br>
making ﬁve kingdoms. However, molecular<br>
studies by Carl Woese in the 1970s revealed<br>
cellular structural differences among<br>
prokaryotes based on membrane structure,<br>
ribosomal RNA, lipid compounds, among<br>
others. This necessitated the splitting of<br>
kingdom Monera also called Prokaryota<br>
into Archaebacteria and Eubacteria. In<br>
1982, about a decade later, Margulis and<br>
<b>Shwartz proposed a classiﬁcation system</b><br>
which adopted the ﬁve kingdoms, namely<br>
the Prokaryotae and four Eukaryote<br>
kingdoms. However, that was not the end,<br>
because recent molecular work has further<br>
revealed phylogenetic relatedness among<br>
protists which lead to yet another kingdom,<br>
Chromista.<br>
<b>In  2015, Cavalier Smith and others introduced</b><br>
biological taxonomy of 8 kingdoms namely;<br>
<b>Eubacteria, Archaebacteria (Archae),</b><br>
<b>Archezoa, Protozoa, Chromista, Fungi,</b><br>
<b>Plantae, and Animalia. Biological taxonomy</b><br>
of 8 kingdoms has introduced a taxonomic<br>
rank called domain above the kingdom.<br>
<b>Three domains of life, namely domain</b><br>
<b>Archaea, Bacteria and Eukarya, are thus</b><br>
recognised. Domain Archaea includes<br>
<b>Archaea bacteria which are the most</b><br>
primitive bacteria with cell wall containing<br>
pseudomurein but lacking a peptidoglycan<br>
cell wall. Domain bacteria consist of all<br>
true bacteria, while Eukarya encompasses<br>
all Eukaryotic organisms thus bringing<br>
together the kingdoms Protoctista, Fungi,<br>
Plantae, and Animalia.<br>
<b>3.2   Viruses</b><br>
<b>The ﬁrst viruses were isolated in 1852</b><br>
as infectious extract from tobacco plants<br>
suffering from tobacco mosaic. In 1898,<br>
<b>Beijerink named the isolated extract ‘virus’</b><br>
a Latin word, meaning toxic or infectious.<br>
<b>Viruses are therefore infectious particles,</b><br>
thought to have genomes detached out<br>
of eukaryotic or prokaryotic cells. Thus,<br>
a virus can be deﬁned as a fragment of<br>
nucleic acid (single or double stranded<br>
<b>DNA or RNA), surrounded by a protein</b><br>
coat and capable of replicating once<br>
they are inside a living cell. Generally,<br>
viruses are too minute; they are smaller<br>
than bacteria and cannot be seen by a<br>
light microscope, but they can be viewed<br>
under the electron microscope. The latter<br>
has simpliﬁed an understanding on the<br>
characteristics of viruses, including their<br>
shapes and ways in which they interact<br>
with their hosts.<br>
<b>Characteristics of viruses</b><br>
a)	 They are the smallest organisms with<br>
size ranging from 17 nm to 300 nm. On<br>
average, they are 50 times smaller than<br>
bacteria, which range in size between<br>
0.1–10 µm.<br>
b)	 They are simple in structure with a<br>
fragment of nucleic acid which is either<br>
<b>DNA or RNA, enclosed in a protein</b><br>
or lipoprotein layer. Most animal and<br>
bacterial viruses have DNA, but other<br>
animal and plant viruses have RNA.<br>
c)	 Viruses<br>
lack<br>
cellular<br>
structure<br>
organisation, hence they are acellular.<br>
d)	 Viruses can reproduce in a living cell<br>
only, that is, inside the cell of a living<br>
host.<br>
e)	 Most viruses are infectious; they can<br>
cause diseases to their hosts. They show<br>
a high degree of host cell speciﬁcity, as<br>
they can recognise and infect speciﬁc<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   93</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
94<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
types of host cells. For example, a<br>
virus causing disease in plants will<br>
not cause disease in animals and vice-<br>
versa. However, the same viruses can<br>
cause disease to different species in the<br>
same group of organisms. A virus called<br>
<b>H5-N1 that causes a fatal bird’s ﬂu was</b><br>
recently discovered to cause infection<br>
and death in humans.<br>
f)	 In the absence of a host, viruses can<br>
crystallize outside a living medium<br>
and assume features of non-living<br>
organisms.<br>
g)	 The ability of a virus to reproduce inside<br>
the cell and crystallize in the absence of<br>
a living host places them between living<br>
and non-living organisms.<br>
h)	 Viruses are obligate endoparasites<br>
because they can live and replicate<br>
inside their host cell only.<br>
3.2.1 Classiﬁcation of viruses<br>
<b>Generally, classiﬁcation of viruses has been</b><br>
challenging due to their characteristics<br>
while inside or outside the host cell. Several<br>
other attributes of viruses intensify the<br>
difﬁculties experienced by taxonomists<br>
in classifying them because they are very<br>
microscopic nucleocapsids (they are smaller<br>
than a molecule), lack cellular organisation<br>
(acellular) and lack certainty in evolutionary<br>
history despite having fragments of<br>
genetic materials such as DNA or RNA.<br>
<b>Thus, they do not fit precisely into the</b><br>
established biological classification system<br>
as they possess both living and non-living<br>
characteristics.<br>
<b>Living characteristics of viruses</b><br>
<b>While inside the host cell, viruses possess</b><br>
a number of characteristics similar to those<br>
of living organisms as follows:<br>
a)	 They possess nucleic acid; either DNA<br>
or RNA, which is a genetic material<br>
helping the virus to produce identical<br>
copies of itself.<br>
b)	 They penetrate through a host cell by<br>
the help of enzymes derived from the<br>
protein coat on the cell surface of the<br>
host cell.<br>
c)	 They are able to multiply or reproduce<br>
inside the host cell.<br>
d)	 They are able to undergo mutation in<br>
response to host internal changes, and<br>
they respond rapidly.<br>
e)	 They possess capsid, which is a protein<br>
coat embracing nucleic acids.<br>
f)	 They show speciﬁcity to hosts and<br>
have the ability to infect the host<br>
cell and take control of its metabolic<br>
activity.<br>
<b>Non-living characteristics of viruses</b><br>
<b>As non-living organisms, viruses have the</b><br>
following characteristics;<br>
a)	 They do not replicate on their own and<br>
lack cellular structures hence unable<br>
to carry out any life processes when<br>
outside the host cell.<br>
b)	 They do not have any enzyme system<br>
such as respiratory enzyme, hence<br>
they lack energy releasing processes.<br>
c)	 While outside the host cell, viruses<br>
are inert, but retain their ability to<br>
replicate, if they invade a host cell.<br>
<b>Viruses are therefore crystalline in the</b><br>
absence of a host cell. When they exist<br>
as individual particles outside the host<br>
cell, they are called virions.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   94</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
95<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
d)	 They are ﬁlterable; therefore, they can<br>
pass through a bacterial proof ﬁlter<br>
paper.<br>
e)	 They are resistant to very high<br>
temperatures due to lack of enzymes.<br>
<b>Normally,</b><br>
at<br>
high<br>
temperatures,<br>
enzymes are denatured and thus render<br>
them useless.<br>
<b>Viruses can be classified based on a</b><br>
number of factors including; phenotypic<br>
characteristics, such as type of nucleic acid,<br>
host organisms, type of disease they cause,<br>
mode of replication, and morphology. In the<br>
classification according to type of nucleic<br>
acid, there are RNA and DNA viruses.<br>
<b>RNA virus is a virus that has RNA as its</b><br>
genetic material, normally single-stranded<br>
<b>RNA, but some viruses may form double</b><br>
helix intra-stranded complimentary base<br>
pair of RNA. Viruses infect both plants<br>
and animals, examples are Tobacco Mosaic<br>
<b>Virus (TMV) which infects plants, influenza</b><br>
virus, hepatitis virus, and polio virus which<br>
infect humans. A type of RNA virus known<br>
as Retrovirus has a peculiar character of<br>
inserting a copy of its RNA into DNA of<br>
the host cell by reverse transcription process<br>
and changing the genetic material of that<br>
cell, example is Human Immunodeficiency<br>
<b>Virus (HIV). On the other hand, DNA virus</b><br>
is a type of virus whose genetic material<br>
is DNA rather than RNA. The DNA may<br>
be either double or single stranded. These<br>
viruses infect animals and plants. Examples<br>
include herpes virus, smallpox virus,<br>
chickenpox virus; which infect animals,<br>
and tomato yellow leaf curl virus; which<br>
infect plants.<br>
<b>Classification according to the host range</b><br>
includes plant viruses which infect plants<br>
only. Examples include; Tomato leaf curl<br>
virus that infect tomatoes and Tobacco<br>
mosaic virus which infect plants like tobacco<br>
and other plants including tomato, potato,<br>
orchid and blackcurrant, and cauliflower<br>
mosaic virus which infect cauliflower plant.<br>
<b>In this type also there are animal viruses</b><br>
which infect animals only, examples include<br>
influenza virus and Herpes simplex virus<br>
which infect human. Also, there are bacteria<br>
viruses that infect bacteria, for example;<br>
bacteriophage such as lambda (l) phage<br>
(a lysogenic virus which is less virulent<br>
whereby the host and a phage can exist<br>
together for many years), enterobacteria<br>
<b>T2, and T4 phages (lytic viruses which</b><br>
infect the bacteria known as Escherichia<br>
coli). Likewise, there are insect viruses<br>
which affect insects only, and these include;<br>
<b>Baculovirus, Sacbrood virus, Entomopox</b><br>
virus and Granulosis virus.<br>
<b>Based on the type of disease they cause,</b><br>
viruses can be classified as; measles virus;<br>
cause measles, polio virus; cause polio,<br>
and rabies virus; cause rabies. According to<br>
morphology there are different categories<br>
based on the symmetry of capsid which<br>
include helical and icosahedral viruses,<br>
the helical morphology consists of helical<br>
array of capsid proteins wrapped around a<br>
helical filament of genetic material, example<br>
<b>TMV, whereas in icosahedral the protein</b><br>
subunits are arranged in a form of hollow,<br>
quasi spherical structure, example; Herpes<br>
simplex virus.<br>

3.2.2  General structure of viruses<br>
<b>Viruses are composed of fragments of</b><br>
genetic material (DNA or RNA), which<br>
forms a nucleic acid core. The DNA<br>
contains a few genes and can either be<br>
single or double stranded. The nucleic acid<br>
core is enclosed by a protein coat called<br>
capsid as in bacteriophage. The capsid is<br>
made up of small identical protein sub units<br>
called capsomeres which are structures that<br>
enable viruses to crystallize and assume<br>
a non-living matter. The capsid, together<br>
with the genetic material (DNA or RNA),<br>
constitute a nucleocapsid. Some viruses<br>
may have additional envelope of lipoprotein<br>
(lipid rich protein) around the capsid. The<br>
lipoprotein layer is usually derived from the<br>
cell membrane of the host cell. The viruses<br>
with this layer are called enveloped viruses,<br>
examples are inﬂuenza and herpes viruses.<br>
<b>Those with no envelope are referred to as</b><br>
naked viruses, for instance, bacteriophage or<br>
simply a phage; viruses that attack bacteria<br>
(Figure 3.1 a, b and c).<br>
<b>Capsid</b><br>
<b>Nucleic acid</b><br>
<b>(DNA)</b><br>
<b>Sheath</b><br>
<b>Base</b><br>
(a)<br>
(b)<br>
(c)<br>
<b>Tail</b><br>

<b>Tobacco mosaic virus (TMV)</b><br>
3.2.3   Viral replication<br>
<b>Viral replication involves formation of</b><br>
viruses during the infection process, and they<br>
replicate only when in living cells; that is the<br>
viruses must first get into the host cell before<br>
viral replication can occur. Replication<br>
between viruses differ significantly and<br>
depends on the type of genes in them. For<br>
example, most DNA viruses gather in the<br>
nucleus while most RNA viruses develop<br>
merely in the cytoplasm.<br>
<b>The life cycle of a bacteriophage</b><br>
<b>In principle, viral life cycle is the same</b><br>
in all bacteriophages. Retroviruses such<br>
as Human Immunodeﬁciency Viruses<br>
(HIV) show uniqueness at a certain stage<br>
of their replication due to ability to convert<br>
their RNA back into a DNA copy. Some<br>
viruses called lytic phages kill host cells<br>
immediately after they enter. On the other<br>
hand, other viruses such as lambda phages<br>
may remain dormant for a long time after<br>
inserting their DNA into the host DNA,<br>
but they may eventually be activated to<br>
complete their life cycle. These types of<br>
viruses are described as lysogenic phage and<br>
the dormant stage is called prophage. The<br>
viral replication therefore, occurs in two<br>
major stages or phases, namely; lysogenic<br>
and lytic phases or cycles as summarised<br>
in Figure 3.2, and Figure 3.3 respectively.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   96</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>

97<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
a)  Lysogenic cycle<br>
<b>When a phage contacts a bacterium, its tail</b><br>
ﬁbers attach to receptor sites on the bacteria<br>
and it sheds its protein coat outside the<br>
host cell. The viral DNA is incorporated in<br>
the bacteria chromosomes (simple circles<br>
of DNA) and is replicated along with it.<br>
<b>Therefore, replicated copies of viral DNA</b><br>
will be produced each time the bacteria cell<br>
divides. However, the phage is not virulent<br>
at this stage but dormant, and bacterial cells<br>
may exist together with the dormant phage<br>
<b>DNA for many generations. This dormant</b><br>
or latent stage of the phage is also termed as<br>
prophage and the host’s cell as a lysogenic<br>
cell. The viral DNA released may enter<br>
a virulent pathway called lytic cycle, if<br>
activated (Figure 3.2).<br>
<b>Phage</b><br>
<b>Bacterial cell</b><br>
<b>Phage DNA</b><br>
<b>Bacterial</b><br>
cell DNA<br>
<b>The DNA of phage is injected into</b><br>
the bacterial cell<br>
<b>The DNA of phage becomes incorporated</b><br>
into bacterial DNA<br>
<b>The bacterial cells divided to produce</b><br>
two cells whose DNA still incorporates<br>
the DNA of the phage<br>
Each cell continues to divide many times  to give large numbers of cells which may, under special<br>
circumstances, enter a lytic phase whereby phage replication takes place. Then, cell bursts (lysis)<br>
and the phage particles escape.<br>
<b>Figure 3.2 The life cycle of a lysogenic phage</b><br>
b) Lytic cycle<br>
<b>When a phage particle approaches the host</b><br>
cell, it attaches to the host wall and injects<br>
its genetic material. Other lysogenic viruses<br>
may be activated to enter the lytic cycle;<br>
this occurs when their genetic materials are<br>
already inside the host cell. They undergo<br>
replication and produce more phages,<br>
disrupting the host’s DNA as a result, the<br>
phage DNA takes control of the bacterial<br>
cell machinery, replicates more repeatedly,<br>
and codes for its new coat proteins. New<br>
viral proteins and new virus particles are<br>
assembled. As a large number of new<br>
viral particles is produced, the phage also<br>
produces lysozymes containing digestive<br>
enzymes which digest the host cell. This<br>
makes the host cell to burst and release<br>
phages. Due to the digestion of the host<br>
cell caused by the phage, this phase is<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   97</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
98<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
termed as lytic phase from the word lysis<br>
meaning ‘digest’. The host cell lysis and<br>
release of phages mark the culmination<br>
of the lytic phase (Figure 3.3). The newly<br>
produced phages are capable of infecting a<br>
new bacteria and the cycle starts over again<br>
as lysogenic cycle with or without a break<br>
or dormant stage.<br>
<b>The lysogenic and lytic cycles can be</b><br>
distinguished due to the fact that, in<br>
lysogenic cycles, the spread of the viral<br>
<b>DNA occurs through the usual bacteria</b><br>
reproduction as their DNA has incorporated<br>
with the bacteria DNA while in a lytic cycle,<br>
the viral DNA and the host DNA replicate<br>
separately within the host cell resulting in<br>
many copies of the virus being produced<br>
very quickly. Also, the lysogenic cycle does<br>
not lyse the host cell straight away while<br>
in the lytic cycle, the host cell is lysed or<br>
destroyed.<br>
<b>Phages that replicate using both lytic and</b><br>
lysogenic cycles are called temperate phages<br>
while phages that replicate only through the<br>
lytic cycle are called virulent phages.<br>

<b>Bacterial cell</b><br>
<b>Empty protein coat</b><br>
<b>Phage DNA</b><br>
<b>Phage particle approaches bacterial cell</b><br>
<b>Phage attached to bacterial wall. DNA is</b><br>
injected into bacterial cell and protein ‘ghost’<br>
is left outside<br>
<b>Phage DNA inside bacterial cell multiplies</b><br>
using bacterial nucleic acid and other substances<br>
absorbed by bacterium<br>
<b>Phage DNA induces formation of protein</b><br>
coat. Eventually bacterial cell burst (lysis)<br>
releasing about 200 exact copies of original<br>
phage<br>
