<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
<b>Retroviruses</b><br>
<b>These are special groups of viruses</b><br>
that contain an enzyme named reverse<br>
transcriptase, which is capable of<br>
synthesizing a DNA copy from the virus<br>
<b>RNA. This is unlike the usual transcription</b><br>
in which RNA is synthesized from a<br>
fragment of DNA. Literally, the term retro<br>
means ‘reverse or going back.’ Hence,<br>
retroviruses were so named due to their<br>
ability to undergo this reverse transcription<br>
process in producing DNA copies from<br>
<b>RNA. These copies can be incorporated</b><br>
randomly into chromosomes of the host<br>
<b>DNA. It is in this group where the Human</b><br>
<b>Immunodeﬁciency Virus (HIV), Hepatitis</b><br>
<b>B viruses and Herpes viruses belong. The</b><br>
retrovirus DNA is known as provirus.<br>
<b>Provirus can be injected in the host DNA</b><br>
and remain dormant for a long time prior to<br>
expression and formation of its new RNA. At<br>
this stage, the host does not portray any sign<br>
of infection. This explains why it sometimes<br>
takes a long time for people infected with<br>
<b>HIV to develop AIDS symptoms. However,</b><br>
when provirus is incorporated into the host<br>
cell DNA, it may activate host genes to<br>
produce RNA which will be packaged<br>
inside the retrovirus particles and delivered<br>
together with retroviral RNA to the next<br>
infected cell. The retrovirus binds on T-<br>
helper lymphocytes and destroys them and<br>
cripples the immune system.<br>
3.2.4   Economic importance of viruses<br>
<b>Though viruses have some disadvantages;</b><br>
as they cause diseases to organisms by being<br>
endoparasites and infectious pathogens,<br>
yet some have significant beneficial in<br>
medical industry, biotechnology, research,<br>
agriculture and ecology. Furthermore,<br>
viruses have been used as biological<br>
warfare (biological weapons) and cause<br>
devastating epidemics in human societies,<br>
as they are highly infectious and relatively<br>
easy to produce.<br>
<b>Advantages of viruses</b><br>
a)	 Viruses<br>
are<br>
widely<br>
applied<br>
in<br>
preparation<br>
of<br>
vaccines<br>
against<br>
animal diseases. Currently, there is a<br>
number of vaccines against deadly<br>
human diseases such as poliomyelitis,<br>
measles, and yellow fever.<br>
b)	 Viruses such as bacteriophages play<br>
a great role in marine ecology and<br>
carbon cycling.<br>
c)	 Viruses are important in the field of<br>
molecular and cell biology; they help<br>
in manipulating and investigation<br>
of cells, hence providing valuable<br>
information about the aspects of cell<br>
biology.<br>
d)	 Some viruses are used in biological<br>
studies. Example, the enzymes reverse<br>
transcriptase discovered in retroviruses<br>
are currently widely used in various<br>
aspects in genetic engineering.<br>
e)	 They stimulate the synthesis and<br>
release of interferon in the body. This<br>
suppresses the viral replication and<br>
stimulates the activity of cytotoxic<br>
cells.<br>
f)	 They are used in gene therapy through<br>
genetic manipulation of somatic cells<br>
of individuals as well as in production<br>
of transgenic plants and animals.<br>
g)	 Certain pathogenic viruses can be used<br>
as a pests control agent.<br>
<b>Disadvantages of viruses</b><br>
a)	 Viruses are known to cause various<br>
diseases in living organisms, for<br>
example influenza, measles, ebola,<br>
<b>AIDS, chickenpox, and herpes in</b><br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   99</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
100<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
human, potato mosaic and tobacco<br>
mosaic in plants, and newcastle in<br>
poultry.<br>
b)	 Some viruses can cause chronic<br>
infections; for example, the virus can<br>
replicate the entire life of the host,<br>
regardless of the presence of the host’s<br>
defense mechanisms. This is common<br>
in Hepatitis viral infection and in HIV.<br>
c)	 A host with chronic viral infections<br>
can be a carrier of an infectious virus<br>
for lifetime. It can sometimes kill<br>
many cells, causing the organism to<br>
suffer ill effects.<br>

<b>3.3   Kingdom Monera</b><br>
<b>The kingdom Monera consists of unicellular</b><br>
prokaryotes. Literally, the term prokaryote<br>
is a combination of two Greek words,<br>
pro meaning ‘before’ and karyo meaning<br>
‘nucleus,’ which simply means before<br>
nucleus or without a true nucleus. Although<br>
prokaryotes have no true nucleus, they still<br>
have diffuse area(s) of nucleoplasm called<br>
nucleoid, containing genetic materials with<br>
no distinct nuclear membrane or envelope.<br>
<b>This is a very unique feature unifying all</b><br>
prokaryotes. It makes them distinct from<br>
species of the other four kingdoms which<br>
have membrane enclosed nuclei. Lack<br>
of membrane bounded nucleus makes<br>
prokaryotic organisms evolutionarily the<br>
most primitive life forms. It is believed<br>
that the oldest prokaryote evolved about<br>
<b>3.5 billion years ago. Moreover, most</b><br>
organisms under this kingdom have a cell<br>
wall containing peptidoglycan (Refer to<br>
Figure 1.3).<br>
<b>Prokaryotic organisms are diverse, and</b><br>
they constitute the largest group interms<br>
of abundance of organisms it encompasses.<br>
<b>Studies show that a gram of soil may contain</b><br>
approximately 2.5 billion bacteria, while<br>
a cubic centimeter of milk has more than<br>
3,000 million bacteria. Organisms belonging<br>
to this kingdom are very minute (1- 10 µm)<br>
to be seen without the aid of a microscope<br>
and are thus termed as microscopic. This<br>
is the reason why kingdom Monera was<br>
not among the two traditional kingdoms;<br>
<b>Plantae and Animalia, as it was still difﬁcult</b><br>
to see, identify, and characterise them. The<br>
discovery of the Monerans, therefore, came<br>
following the discovery of the microscope,<br>
particularly the electron microscope and<br>
computer applications which revealed<br>
the ﬁner details of the Monerans. This<br>
simpliﬁed the understanding of differences<br>
across members of this kingdom, leading to<br>
the recognition of the three major divisions,<br>
namely; division Archaea, Eubacteria,<br>
and Cyanobacteria. Earlier classiﬁcation<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   100</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
101<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
system splited Monera into two groups,<br>
in this case, all Archaea bacteria were<br>
grouped under kingdom Archaebacteria<br>
while Cyanobacteria and Eubacteria were<br>
classiﬁed under kingdom Eubacteria or<br>
Prokaryota.<br>
<b>The members of kingdom Monera</b><br>
are cosmopolitan in distribution and<br>
predominant in all habitats, ranging from<br>
aquatic; that is marine, brackish and fresh<br>
waters to terrestrial environments; such<br>
as in the air, dust, soils, on plants and<br>
animals. They also range from parasitic,<br>
free living and symbiotic bacteria to animals<br>
and plants. Some bacteria are saprophytes,<br>
especially decomposers, which are very<br>
important in nutrient cycling. Nitrogen<br>
ﬁxing bacteria are found in symbiotic<br>
association with leguminous plant roots<br>
while the cyanobacteria form symbiotic<br>
relationships called lichens with fungi.<br>
<b>Parasitic prokaryotes include infectious</b><br>
bacteria such as Salmonella typhi that<br>
causes typhoid and Vibrio cholerae which<br>
causes cholera, spend part or their entire<br>
life in their hosts as facultative parasites<br>
or obligate parasites respectively. The free<br>
living bacteria are capable of synthesizing<br>
their own food using energy obtained<br>
from light or chemicals. The former is<br>
called photoautotrophic bacteria such as<br>
<b>Purple sulphur bacteria, while the latter</b><br>
is called chemoautotrophic bacteria such<br>
as Iron bacteria (Ferrobacillus). Some<br>
prokaryotes such as Archaebacteria have<br>
interesting features such as the ability to<br>
survive in extremely harsh environments,<br>
such as very low oxygen concentration<br>
(anoxic condition), high alkaline or<br>
acidic media, high salt concentration, and<br>
extreme temperatures (in hot springs and<br>
volcanic domes) where other organism have<br>
completely failed to inhabit. For example,<br>
methanogens (Methanobrevibacter<br>
ruminantium) which produce methane gas<br>
in anoxic conditions in ruminant’s digestive<br>
systems. These similar bacteria produce<br>
methane gas from cow dung which is used<br>
as biogas, which is one of the renewable<br>
sources of energy.<br>
3.3.1  Characteristics of monerans<br>
a)	 They are very microscopic unicellular<br>
organisms, with an average diameter<br>
of 0.1-10 mm.<br>
b)	 Their cells lack a well organised<br>
nucleus, since they have no nuclear<br>
membrane, and the nuclear materials<br>
are freely suspended in the cytoplasm.<br>
c)	 They<br>
lack<br>
cytoskeletons<br>
and<br>
membrane bound organelles, such as<br>
mitochondria, plastids, and chloroplast.<br>
<b>The photosynthetic Moneras have</b><br>
photosynthetic lamella instead of<br>
chloroplasts.<br>
d)	 They have few and smaller ribosomes,<br>
70s ribosomes.<br>
e)	 They have small, circular DNA that<br>
lacks the histone protein coat.<br>
f)	 Most of physiological processes, such<br>
as respiration and food synthesis (for<br>
the autotrophs), occur in membrane<br>
systems.<br>
g)	 The cell wall is composed of a<br>
carbohydrate-protein complex called<br>
murein or peptidoglycan.<br>
h)	 Most prokaryotes move using a beating<br>
ﬂagella, gas vesicles, and gliding<br>
mechanisms. Flagella, if present, lack<br>
an internal 9+2 ﬁbril arrangement<br>
pattern.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   101</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
102<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
i)	 They reproduce asexually by binary<br>
fission and sexually by conjugation, no<br>
mitotic and meiotic divisions, hence no<br>
spindle formation.<br>
3.3.2 Classiﬁcation of Monera<br>
<b>The microorganisms in kingdom Monera</b><br>
are considered as primitive organisms and<br>
the most ancient living forms on earth. This<br>
kingdom is divided into two main groups<br>
which are Archaebacteria (Extremophiles)<br>
and Eubacteria (cyanobacteria and true<br>
bacteria). In this text, much emphasize<br>
will be devoted to Eubacteria.<br>
<b>Division Archaebacteria</b><br>
<b>Under this group, bacteria are the most</b><br>
primitive and ancient members of kingdom<br>
<b>Monera. Their cell wall chemistry differs</b><br>
from eubacteria as they lack peptidoglycan.<br>
<b>Instead, they have lipids which are different</b><br>
in composition from other bacteria due to<br>
the presence of long chains of fatty acids<br>
with glycerol attached to it by ester linkage.<br>
<b>Another unique feature of these bacteria is</b><br>
their ability to inhabit extreme environments<br>
in which other organisms cannot survive.<br>
<b>These habitats include environments</b><br>
with extreme temperatures, saline, and<br>
acidic conditions. Others can survive in<br>
oxygen deprived or anoxic conditions.<br>
<b>Because of their ability to survive in</b><br>
extreme conditions, Archaebacteria<br>
are grouped into methanogens, which<br>
produce methane by reducing carbon-<br>
dioxide, for example, methanogens<br>
(Methanobrevibacter ruminantium)<br>
found in ruminants’ digestive system. The<br>
other group is hyperthermophiles which<br>
are temperature-loving and acid-loving<br>
archaeans (acidophils). They are found in<br>
hot springs and acidic conditions such as in<br>
hot springs and phosphoric or arsenic ponds.<br>
<b>The third group is the extremohalophiles</b><br>
which are salt loving archaeans found in<br>
salt lakes. Other classiﬁcation systems<br>
treat Archaebacteria as a kingdom and<br>
place the groups of archaea into distinct<br>
phyla or divisions while the entire group<br>
of Archaebacteria in this text is regarded<br>
as a division.<br>
<b>Division Eubacteria</b><br>
<b>These include the true bacteria and the</b><br>
cyanobacteria, also known as blue-green<br>
bacteria or   blue-green algae, which are<br>
photosynthetic. Members of this division<br>
lack true nuclei and they have a strong and<br>
rigid cell wall containing a polysaccharide<br>
called murein cross-linked by short chains<br>
of amino acids. They have a variety of<br>
shapes ranging from spherical, rod-like,<br>
and spiral or comma shaped. Additionally,<br>
some are heterotrophs, living as pathogenic<br>
parasites while others are free living<br>
autotrophs. Furthermore, eubacteria have<br>
simple circular DNA which is not associated<br>
with proteins to form chromosomes and<br>
they use mesosomes for respiration.<br>
<b>Cyanobacteria are grouped in the same</b><br>
division with other true bacteria, because<br>
they share many features. For example,<br>
both possess prokaryotic cell structures,<br>
reproduction by binary ﬁssion, their cell<br>
wall contains peptidoglycan (murein), and<br>
have mucilaginous sheet.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   102</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
103<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
<b>In other classiﬁcation systems, cyanobacteria</b><br>
form a division of their own called Division<br>
<b>Cyanobacteria. This is because, unlike</b><br>
bacteria, they have chlorophyll a for<br>
photosynthesis and they produce oxygen<br>
during photosynthesis which resembles<br>
algae more than bacteria. In addition, their<br>
chlorophyll lamellae and DNA lie free in<br>
the cytoplasm. Cyanophytes can occur as<br>
free living cells or as colonies. They have<br>
specialised cells called heterocyst which are<br>
capable of ﬁxing Nitrogen. These bacteria<br>
form blooms in ponds. Sometimes, they<br>
are found in symbiotic association called<br>
lichen with fungi.<br>
<b>Classiﬁcation of bacteria</b><br>
<b>Bacteria are single celled microbes,</b><br>
with a simple cell structure that has no<br>
nucleus or membrane bound organelles.<br>
<b>Although genetic divergence specifies</b><br>
the evolutionary relationships of bacteria,<br>
reaction to Gram stain, mode of nutrition,<br>
morphological, motility, and biochemical<br>
features of bacteria remain important in<br>
the identification and classification of these<br>
organisms. Three major ways can be used<br>
to identify bacteria; based on their cell wall<br>
chemistry or composition which makes<br>
them respond differently to Gram stain,<br>
difference in morphology of their cells, and<br>
mode of nutrition.<br>
<b>Classiﬁcation of bacteria based on</b><br>
their staining properties<br>
<b>In this case, the bacterial cell is smeared on</b><br>
a microscope slide and stained with crystal<br>
violet solution followed by iodine solution,<br>
and later washed with organic solvent such as<br>
acetone or ethanol and counterstained with<br>
a red dye called safranin or carbolfuschin.<br>
<b>This staining based classiﬁcation of bacteria</b><br>
was invented by Hans Christian Gram, a<br>
<b>Danish microbiologist in 1884, and was</b><br>
therefore named “Gram stain’s test.” This<br>
simple test classiﬁes bacteria into two broad<br>
groups namely; Gram negative and Gram-<br>
positive (3.4 a and b). The Gram-positive<br>
bacteria appear blue to purple, because they<br>
retain the crystal violet colour of the dye<br>
used to stain them. In contrast, the Gram-<br>
negative bacteria do not retain the colour<br>
of the stain (crystal violet).<br>
<b>The staining differences are based on</b><br>
variation in the cell wall chemistry,<br>
particularly in the amount of peptidoglycan.<br>
<b>The Gram-positive bacteria have a</b><br>
simple cell wall with a thick layer of<br>
peptidoglycan<br>
(polysaccharides<br>
and<br>
protein). Moreover, due to differences<br>
in cell wall constituents, the Gram-<br>
positive bacteria cell wall is susceptible<br>
to lysozymes and antibiotics. The Gram-<br>
negative bacteria are resistant to antibiotics<br>
and lysosomes. This is because, although<br>
they have thinner cell walls, they are more<br>
complex, by having additional outer layer<br>
on the outside of murein layer which is<br>
thin, smooth membrane-like containing<br>
lipid and polysaccharides that protect the<br>
bacteria from host’s lysozyme, as well<br>
as antibacterial enzymes found in tears,<br>
saliva and other body fluids. Examples of<br>
<b>Gram-positive bacteria include Bacillus</b><br>
sp., Clostridium sp., Streptococcus sp.,<br>
and Staphylococcus sp., while Gram-<br>
negative bacteria include Escherichia coli,<br>
<b>Chlamydia sp., Neisseria sp., Salmonella</b><br>
sp., Treponema sp., and Azotobacter sp<br>
(Figure 3.4).<br>

<b>The framework of the protein that makes</b><br>
the peptidoglycan materials of the cell<br>
wall is a porous network that allows<br>
diffusion of the staining solution (see the<br>
three dimensional detailed view of the<br>
Gram-positive bacteria in Figure 3.5).<br>
<b>Activity 3.1  Identification of Gram-</b><br>
positive and Gram-negative bacteria<br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>Dried smears of bacteria, gentian violet</b><br>
or iodine solution stain, alcohol, red<br>
dye (safranin/ carbolfuschin), and<br>
watch glass.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Place an air dried smear on a watch<br>
glass.<br>
b)	 Gently flood the smear with iodine<br>
solution or gentian violet, and let it<br>
stand for one minute.<br>
c)	 Decolourise by adding alcohol, drop<br>
by drop until no further stains come<br>
out.<br>
d)	 Counterstain by flooding the smear<br>
with a red dye such as safranin or<br>
carbolfuschin and let it stand for<br>
about 40-45 seconds.<br>
e)	 Observe the colour change and<br>
explain your results.<br>

<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
<b>Classiﬁcation of bacteria based on</b><br>
their morphology<br>
<b>Bacteria can be classiﬁed according to</b><br>
their morphology or shapes, which are<br>
diverse, ranging from rod-like to comma-<br>
like shapes. They can exist singly or<br>
in a series of interconnected individual<br>
cells called colonies. This should not be<br>
confused with multicellular organisms,<br>
because each cell is capable of carrying out<br>
all its essential life processes, and each can<br>
survive independently if separated from<br>
the colony. The cells normally associate to<br>
form a colony after binary ﬁssion, as the<br>
two resulting cells adhere to each other.<br>
<b>Some bacteria cells associate in tubular</b><br>
sheaths, forming ﬁlaments which undergo<br>
binary ﬁssion simultaneously. Four shapes<br>
of bacteria exist, namely cocci, bacilli,<br>
spirilla, and vibrio or comma-shape.<br>
<b>Cocci (singular coccus)</b><br>
These are spherical-shaped bacteria.<br>
<b>They may exist as a single cell, while</b><br>
others may exist in pairs, forming a<br>
double-celled (diplococcus) organism.<br>
<b>The diplococus may be surrounded by a</b><br>
capsule, for example the pneumococcus<br>
bacteria (Diplococcus pneumoniae) a<br>
causative agent of pneumonia. They may<br>
also occur in chains of interconnected<br>
individuals or in bunches (cluster cells)<br>
such as Streptococcus and Staphylococcus<br>
respectively (Figure 3.6 a, b, c, and d).<br>
(a)<br>
(b)<br>
(c)<br>
(d)<br>
Figure 3.6 Structures of cocci bacteria (a) single-celled (b) double-celled (c) colon and (d) chain<br>
<b>Bacilli (singular bacillus)</b><br>
<b>These are rod-shaped bacteria which may</b><br>
occur singly as in Escherichia coli; a common<br>
gut-living symbiont and Salmonella typhi<br>
which causes typhoid fever. The bacilli may<br>
occur in chains as seen in Nitrogen ﬁxing<br>
bacteria (Azotobacter) and the anthrax<br>
causing bacteria (Bacillus anthracis).<br>
<b>The bacilli usually have a tendency of</b><br>
forming endospores with various shapes,<br>
position and size, examples include oval<br>
spore without swelling at the center like<br>
in Bacillus anthracis, and spherical spore<br>
with a swollen terminal like in Clostridium<br>
tetani; the causative of tetanus or with sub-<br>
terminal swollen example Clostridium<br>
botulinum the causative of botulism (Figure<br>
<b>3.7 a, b and c).</b><br>

<b>Spirilla (singular spirillum)</b><br>
<b>Bacteria in this group have a long cylinder</b><br>
cell, coiled into a spiral or helical body.<br>
<b>The individuals under this group are also</b><br>
called spirochaetes; their name came<br>
from the fact that they contain distinctive<br>
double membrane, and most of which<br>
have long helically coiled structure<br>
(corkscrew-shaped or spiraled). They are<br>
also characterised by presence of unique<br>
axial ﬁlaments composed of ﬂagella,<br>
running from one end of the cell, where<br>
they are directly attached to the cell wall.<br>
The ﬂagella are used for locomotion.<br>
<b>Bacteria under this group include free</b><br>
living bacteria found in water and muds<br>
as well as parasitic spirochaetes such as<br>
<b>Treponema pallidum that causes syphilis</b><br>
in humans (Figure 3.8).<br>
<b>Figure 3.8 The structure of a Spirillum bacterium</b><br>
<b>Vibrio</b><br>
<b>This group of bacteria includes various</b><br>
curved bacteria which appear superﬁcially<br>
like a comma. That is why they are<br>
sometimes named as comma-shaped<br>
bacteria. These bacteria possess a single<br>
ﬂagellum which is used in locomotion as<br>
seen in Vibrio cholerae (Figure 3.9).<br>
<b>Figure 3.9 The structure of a comma-shaped</b><br>
bacterium<br>
<b>Classification of bacteria based on</b><br>
their mode of nutrition<br>
<b>Bacteria obtain their food from their</b><br>
environment in different ways. Some of<br>
them can synthesise their own food using<br>
energy from the sunlight, while others<br>
use chemicals as their energy source.<br>
<b>Some of the bacteria cannot synthesize</b><br>
their own food and they rely on other<br>
organisms as their source of food. Thus,<br>
in this regard, bacteria are classified into<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   106</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
107<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
photoautotrophs, chemoautotrophs, and<br>
chemoheterotrophs.<br>
<b>Photoautotrophs</b><br>
<b>Photoautotrophs bacteria need light as</b><br>
their source of energy in manufacturing<br>
their food. The light is trapped with the<br>
aid of chlorophyll 'a' and carotenoid<br>
pigments. This type of photosynthesis<br>
is much simpliﬁed compared to that of<br>
plants. Examples include blue-green<br>
bacteria.<br>
<b>Chemoautotrophs</b><br>
<b>These bacteria acquire their energy by</b><br>
oxidising simple inorganic substances, but<br>
not from sunlight as in photoautotrophs.<br>
<b>The obtained energy is used to synthesise</b><br>
food in the presence of carbon dioxide.<br>
<b>Important nitrogen ﬁxing bacteria such as</b><br>
<b>Nitrosomonas and Nitrobacter belong to</b><br>
this group. Others include Iron bacteria<br>
which oxidise Iron two (Ferrous Iron) to<br>
<b>Iron three (Ferric Iron), and the energy</b><br>
released from oxidation is utilised during<br>
food synthesis.<br>
<b>Chemoheterotrophs</b><br>
<b>All bacteria which cannot manufacture</b><br>
food on their own as they lose the ability to<br>
photosynthesize in the course of evolution<br>
are heterotrophs (heterotrophic bacteria).<br>
<b>They are named “chemo” heterotrophs,</b><br>
when they obtain energy for their growth<br>
from organic compounds such as proteins<br>
and glucose. Chemoheterotrophs can<br>
be further categorised into saprotrophs,<br>
parasites and mutualists. The saprotrophs<br>
obtain their food from dead and decaying<br>
organic matter, majority of them are<br>
decomposers, which are very important<br>
in nutrient recycling. Parasitic bacteria<br>
are pathogenic as they obtain their foods<br>
by living in or on other organisms thereby<br>
causing diseases and even death of their<br>
hosts. Some bacteria live permanently<br>
on or in their host (obligate parasitic<br>
bacteria), while other bacteria’s life cycles<br>
depend on multiple host (facultative<br>
parasitic bacteria). Other bacteria live in<br>
a mutual (symbiotic) relationship with<br>
other organisms where by both partners<br>
benefit from the association. Examples of<br>
symbiotic association include; Rhizobium<br>
bacteria that live in the root nodules of<br>
leguminous plants, and also have ability<br>
to fix nitrogen into useful compounds<br>
like nitrates which are needed by these<br>
plants, and Escherichia coli which live<br>
symbiotically in the human gut and<br>
contribute to the formation of vitamin B<br>
and K.<br>
3.3.3 Bacterial reproduction<br>
<b>Bacteria reproduce both sexually and</b><br>
asexually; sexual reproduction involves<br>
two organisms/cells exchanging their<br>
genetic materials (not gametes, as they<br>
have no reproductive structures) and<br>
produce daughter cells which differ in their<br>
genetic materials from that of the parental<br>
cells. Sexual reproduction involves the<br>
process of genetic recombination through<br>
cell to cell contact (Conjugation), while in<br>
asexual reproduction the cell divides by<br>
binary fission to form two daughter cells<br>
with similar genetic materials as that of<br>
the parental cell.<br>
<b>Asexual reproduction in bacteria</b><br>
<b>Most bacteria reproduce asexually. A</b><br>
single bacterium can reproduce asexually<br>
by binary ﬁssion which involves division<br>
of one bacterium into two bacteria (Figure<br>

3.10). In this process, the dividing cell<br>
elongates and if the cell is spherical, the<br>
elongation is very much restricted. A rod-<br>
shaped bacterium elongates to almost<br>
double its size. Then, the protoplasmic<br>
mass divides into two equal halves by a<br>
transverse wall or constriction. The cell<br>
division is preceded by the replication of<br>
<b>DNA. The two daughter cells soon grow</b><br>
to maturity and divide further.<br>

<b>Sexual reproduction in bacteria</b><br>
<b>Sexual reproduction in bacteria is primitive</b><br>
in the sense that it does not involve union<br>
of gametes as commonly seen in other<br>
organisms, particularly the eukaryotes.<br>
<b>The</b><br>
reproduction<br>
simply<br>
involves<br>
combination of genetic material by the<br>
process called genetic recombination.<br>
<b>This process involves the primitive form</b><br>
of sexual reproduction called conjugation<br>
(the transfer of DNA between two cells<br>
which are in direct contact). One of the<br>
two cells (male) donates while the other<br>
(female) receives genetic material. The<br>
ability to donate the genetic material is<br>
controlled by the bacterial gene found<br>
in a special type of plasmid called sex<br>
factor which also codes for the formation<br>
of a small tubular structure called pilus,<br>
connecting the two bacterial cells. Through<br>
this pilus, the genetic material is injected<br>
to one or more recipient(s) at a time. This<br>
kind of exchange is essential especially<br>
during unfavorable condition, and they<br>
produce more resistant cells (Figure 3.11).<br>
1. Formation of pilus<br>
4.  Fragment of donor DNA<br>
incorporated into recipient<br>
3.  Donor DNA replicated by rolling<br>
cycle method and transferred<br>

3.3.4 Economic importance of monerans<br>
<b>Bacteria have a wide range of economic</b><br>
importance; ranging from beneﬁcial to<br>
detrimental effects to humans and other<br>
organisms. Furthermore, many bacteria<br>
serve important roles in both industries<br>
and agriculture.<br>
<b>Advantages of monerans</b><br>
a)	 Production of vinegar<br>
<b>Some bacteria, such as those of genera</b><br>
<b>Acetomonas and Acetobacter are used in</b><br>
making vinegar (for vinegar fermentation),<br>
whereby ethanol is partially oxidised into<br>
ethanoic acid (vinegar).<br>
b)	 Manufacturing of dairy products<br>
<b>In this case, bacteria such as Streptococcus</b><br>
lactis are employed in preparation and<br>
preservation of commercial butter, cultured<br>
milk, and cheese from cream milk. The<br>
bacteria species Streptococcus thermophilus<br>
and Lactobacillus bulgaricus are frequently<br>
used in preparation of yoghurt by inoculating<br>
fresh milk with a starter culture containing<br>
<b>Brevibacterium linens in order to produce</b><br>
the appealing smell in cheese.<br>
c)	 Manufacturing of amino acids,<br>
proteins, and starch<br>
<b>In the manufacture of amino acids, some</b><br>
bacteria such as Monococcus glutamis are<br>
used. Industrial proteins such as amylase<br>
are produced by bacteria such as Bacillus<br>
polymysa.<br>
d)	 Decomposition of organic matters<br>
<b>Most bacteria are chemoheterotrophs</b><br>
which obtain their food from dead or<br>
decaying organic matter. In so doing, such<br>
bacteria reduce heaps of dead substrates<br>
such as leaf litter, dead logs and animals<br>
into simple substances like nutrients which<br>
can be easily absorbed by other plants.<br>
<b>This process is called decomposition and</b><br>
it ensures nutrients recycling. It is vital to<br>
nature because it unlocks nutrients locked<br>
in dead bodies of other organisms and<br>
makes them available to others.<br>
e)	 Treatment and purification of<br>
waste water or sewage<br>
<b>Bacteria can be used in treating and</b><br>
purification of water or sewage in<br>
oxidation pond, by reducing the bulky of<br>
wastes and converting them into simpler<br>
forms which can be easily handled in the<br>
subsequent stages in a stabilisation ponds<br>
(lagoon). Examples of decomposers<br>
include Streptomyces sp. and Bacillus sp.<br>
f)	 Making  flavour, aroma and curing<br>
of agricultural products<br>
<b>Bacteria are used for production of different</b><br>
dairy flavour compounds, such as butyric<br>
acid, lactic acid and diacetyl in mixed<br>
cultures of Lactobacillus acidophilus and<br>
<b>Pediococcus pentosaceus. Some bacteria</b><br>
such as Bacillus subtilis have been used to<br>
bring about several physio-chemicals and<br>
sensory changes in soybean foods to make<br>
it highly digestible and nutritious. Bacillus<br>
subtilis which dominate traditionally<br>
fermented soy foods have typical taste,<br>
texture and aroma which is popular in<br>
<b>Asian and African countries. Certain</b><br>
bacteria such as Bacillus megaterium<br>
can be used in curing off the bitterness in<br>
leaves of tea and tobacco.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   109</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>

<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
g)	 Nitrogen fixation and nitrification<br>
in the soil<br>
<b>Some bacteria, besides being decomposers</b><br>
are important in ﬁxing atmospheric nitrogen<br>
into a form that can be utilised by plants<br>
by the process known as nitrogen fixation.<br>
<b>Examples of nitrogen fixing bacteria are</b><br>
Azotobacter, Clostridium, and Rhizobium.<br>
<b>Some bacteria convert ammonia into</b><br>
nitrates in the soil by the process known<br>
as nitrification.  Nitrosomonas convert<br>
ammonia into nitrite which later gets<br>
oxidised to nitrates by Nitrobacter. The<br>
two processes are important in agriculture,<br>
because nitrogen is one of the elements<br>
required by plants in large quantity for<br>
growth and other physiological processes.<br>
<b>Nitrogen contributes largely in improving</b><br>
agricultural yields.<br>
h)	 Bioindicator in detecting water<br>
pollution<br>
<b>Cyanobacteria blooms in ponds can</b><br>
be used as bioindicator, since they are<br>
sensitive to water pollution. Changes in<br>
abundance of cyanobacteria in aquatic<br>
ecosystems can serve as indicators of<br>
water pollution.<br>
i)	 Biotechnology and genetic<br>
engineering<br>
<b>Bacteria can be used to alter and replicate</b><br>
genes that are then introduced into<br>
plant or animals. Bacterial systems lend<br>
themselves to genetic manipulation in part<br>
because of their rapid reproduction rates.<br>
<b>Thermus aquaticus (Taq) is a thermal stable</b><br>
bacterium which is used to produce DNA<br>
polymerase. DNA polymerase enzyme is<br>
used in amplification of short segments of<br>
<b>DNA through polymerase chain reaction</b><br>
(PCR). Some genetically engineered<br>
bacteria of genus Pseudomonas are used<br>
in cleaning oil-contaminated water as they<br>
feed on oil spills.<br>
j)	 Biodiversity studies<br>
<b>Bacteria may be one of the most abundant</b><br>
and species-rich groups of organisms,<br>
that mediate many critical ecosystem<br>
processes. They contribute signiﬁcantly<br>
to the global biodiversity, given the<br>
existence of numerous species of bacteria.<br>
<b>The position and role of each species</b><br>
in the ecosystem cannot be underrated.<br>
<b>Cyanobacteria initiated early life on land</b><br>
as they were the ﬁrst organism to produce<br>
oxygen from photosynthesis. Such oxygen<br>
was used by early aerobic organisms.<br>
k)	 Medical application<br>
<b>Some bacteria such as Streptococcus are</b><br>
used in the process of manufacturing<br>
antibiotics,<br>
such<br>
as<br>
Streptomycin.<br>
<b>Streptococcus lactis ferments milk to</b><br>
produce lactic acid which prevents growth<br>
of harmful bacteria in the stomach, by<br>
maintaining the acidic environment in the<br>
stomach. Lactic acid prevents bacterial<br>
vaginosis, thus preventing urinary tract<br>
infection (UTI). Starch, in the form of<br>
amylose, is produced for industrial uses<br>
by bacteria. For example Escherichia coli<br>
or E. coli are used in the mass production<br>
of asparaginase enzyme which has also<br>
a medical application in chemotherapy<br>
against lymphoblastic leukemia.<br>
l)	 Symbiotic association with other<br>
organisms<br>
<b>Some bacteria such as cyanobacteria live in</b><br>
association with fungi in which the former<br>
synthesise food through photosynthesis<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   110</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>

111<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
and supply it to fungi, while fungi provide<br>
support and protection from dehydration<br>
to cyanobacteria. Some non-pathogenic<br>
bacteria live on human skin and are<br>
important in destroying the bad bacteria<br>
that live in symbiotic association with<br>
human. For example, Staphylococcus is<br>
among skin microbiota (skin flora) that live<br>
symbiotically with human skin, protecting<br>
the host from pathogenic bacteria.In<br>
addition, Ruminococcus bacteria that live<br>
symbiotically with ruminant animals,<br>
breakdown the plant fibres (cellulose) into<br>
monosaccharides, and E.coli in human<br>
intestines synthesises vitamin K.<br>
m)	Biological control<br>
<b>Bacteria are potentially used in several</b><br>
biological control methods in agriculture<br>
and public health programs as bio<br>
pesticides. For example, some bacteria are<br>
soil dwelling Gram-positive, commonly<br>
used as biological pesticides. They<br>
infect and kill the destructive organisms,<br>
including caterpillar of some butterﬂies<br>
and larvae of some insects. An example of<br>
a soil dwelling Gram-positive bacterium<br>
is Bacillus thuringiensis.<br>
<b>Disadvantages of monerans</b><br>
a)	 Pathogenic bacteria cause diseases in<br>
humans. For instance; Vibrio cholerae<br>
cause cholera, Treponema pallidum;<br>
syphilis,<br>
<b>Shigella</b><br>
dysenteriae;<br>
shigellosis,<br>
<b>Salmonella</b><br>
typhi;<br>
typhoid fever, Entamoeba histolytica;<br>
dysentery, Mycobacterium tuberculosis;<br>
tuberculosis, and Escherichia coli cause<br>
urinary tract infection (UTI).<br>
b)	 In farm animals, some diseases such as<br>
anthrax in cattle, avian tuberculosis in<br>
poultry, and black leg in sheep, goat and<br>
cattle are caused by bacteria including<br>
<b>Bacillus anthracis, Mycobacterium</b><br>
avium, and Clostridium chauvoel<br>
respectively.<br>
c)	 Some bacteria cause diseases in<br>
plants, resulting into crop destruction,<br>
for example; corky root in lettuce,<br>
halo blight in beans, and bacterial<br>
pith necrosis in tomatoes are caused<br>
by bacteria such as Rhizomonas<br>
suberifaciens, Pseudomonas syringae<br>
<b>Pv. Phaseolicola, and Pseudomonas</b><br>
corrugate respectively.<br>
d)	 Foods with high protein content are<br>
often decomposed by bacteria leading<br>
into food spoilage. For example, the<br>
smell coming from rotten eggs or any<br>
other protein-containing foods results<br>
from decomposition of protein by<br>
proteolytic bacteria.<br>
e)	 During the process of bioleaching,<br>
some bacteria such as Thiobacillus<br>
oxidise sulphides to form sulphuric<br>
acid and Hydrogen ions (H+) which<br>
can leak into the ground and turn<br>
surface and ground water into acidic,<br>
hence water pollution; which is an<br>
environmental destruction.<br>
f)	 Bacteria called Clostridium botulinum<br>
can release toxin in imperfectly canned<br>
food.<br>

<b>3.4 Kingdom Protoctista</b><br>
<b>In the early classiﬁcation of living organisms</b><br>
into four kingdoms, all organisms which<br>
could not ﬁt into kingdoms Fungi, Plantae<br>
and Animalia were placed under kingdom<br>
<b>Protista. This made kingdom Protista very</b><br>
diverse, as it accommodated both unicellular<br>
prokaryotes and eukaryotes. Later, with the<br>
use of molecular taxonomy, prokaryotes<br>
were separated from Protista to form a<br>
group of unicellular organisms lacking a<br>
well organised nucleus. These were placed<br>
under kingdom Monera, while all single-<br>
celled eukaryotes constituted kingdom<br>
Protoctista.<br>
<b>Recent phylogenetic studies have shown</b><br>
that even Protoctista is no longer a group<br>
of naturally related organisms. This makes<br>
it difﬁcult to clearly deﬁne kingdom<br>
<b>Protoctista. For example, protoctists such</b><br>
as algae have chloroplasts; hence, they are<br>
photoautotrophs. Amoeba is a heterotroph,<br>
while slime molds and water molds<br>
are saprophytes sharing many features<br>
in common with fungi than with other<br>
protoctists. It is for this reason, kingdom<br>
<b>Protoctista is deemed controversial by</b><br>
contemporary taxonomists. This is why the<br>
classiﬁcation system has advanced from<br>
ﬁve to eight kingdoms. Members under<br>
kingdom Protoctista have been separated<br>
into two kingdoms, namely Protozoa and<br>
<b>Chromista. More recently, they have been</b><br>
separated into three kingdoms: Protozoa,<br>
<b>Chromista, and Archezoa. However, this</b><br>
text will focus on Protoctista as a kingdom<br>
for convenience.<br>
<b>Protoctists can be deﬁned as unicellular</b><br>
eukaryotic organisms other than fungi,<br>
plants, and animals. Evolutionarily,<br>
members in high kingdoms such as Fungi,<br>
<b>Plantae and Animalia, have their ancestors</b><br>
in the Protoctista kingdom. Therefore,<br>
protoctists are eukaryotes consisting of<br>
unicellular and multicellular members.<br>
<b>The multicellular protoctists consist of an</b><br>
assembly of similar cells such as Spirogyra.<br>
<b>The major difference between protists and</b><br>
protoctists is that the former consists of<br>
only unicellular microscopic organisms<br>
(protozoans) while the later is the mixture<br>
of unicellular and multicellular organisms.<br>
<b>Studies based on the base sequence of</b><br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   112</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
113<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
mitochondrial and chloroplast DNA indicate<br>
that members of kingdoms Fungi, Plantae<br>
and Animalia have their ancestors in the<br>
kingdom Protoctista.<br>
<b>Characteristics of protoctists</b><br>
a)	 Organisms under this kingdom are<br>
nucleated; that is, all are eukaryotes.<br>
b)	 Some protoctists are unicellular while<br>
others are multicellular.<br>
c)	 They lack tissue differentiation.<br>
d)	 Protoctists are adapted to both, aquatic<br>
and terrestrial habitats.<br>
e)	 They have various types of vesicles<br>
that<br>
perform<br>
different<br>
functions.<br>
<b>These include their increased surface</b><br>
area to facilitate exchange of materials<br>
needed for their survival. For instance,<br>
contractile vacuole helps protoctists<br>
to discharge excess water taken by<br>
osmosis. They also have food vacuole,<br>
as in Paramecium, which helps them<br>
in digestion of engulfed food particles.<br>
f)	 Their cell surfaces are diverse, ranging<br>
from just a plasma membrane as in<br>
<b>Amoeba, to a stiffer surface as in</b><br>
<b>Euglena to ensure the integrity of the</b><br>
cell.<br>
g)	 Many protoctists are involved in<br>
endo-symbiotic<br>
relationship<br>
with<br>
other organisms. A good example<br>
is a radiolarian which harbors other<br>
photosynthetic<br>
protoctists.<br>
<b>These</b><br>
protoctists, through photosynthesis,<br>
synthesise food that is shared by<br>
both, while the radiolarian in turn<br>
confers protection and provide some<br>
metabolites to the other symbiont.<br>
h)	 Both asexual and sexual types of<br>
reproduction are common in the<br>
majority of protoctists, although some<br>
members lack sexual reproduction.<br>
<b>Asexual reproduction occurs by binary</b><br>
ﬁssion (in which one nucleus divides),<br>
multiple ﬁssion (in which many nuclei<br>
divides into multiple daughter cells),<br>
or budding (in which a new cell grows<br>
on the surface of the mother cell).<br>
<b>During all these types of asexual</b><br>
reproduction, an organism replicates<br>
its nucleus and divides to form new<br>
organisms. Sexual reproduction in<br>
protoctists is still primitive, given that<br>
it is mainly a recombination of genetic<br>
material.<br>
i)	 Some protoctists are parasites, while<br>
others are free living organisms.<br>
<b>The free living members are either</b><br>
autotrophs<br>
(such<br>
as<br>
<b>Spirogyra)</b><br>
or heterotrophs (such as Amoeba<br>
proteus).<br>
<b>Classiﬁcation of protoctists</b><br>
<b>Classifying</b><br>
protoctists<br>
has<br>
been<br>
a<br>
difficult task due to their high diversity.<br>
<b>Traditionally, protoctists were subdivided</b><br>
into several groups based on their physical<br>
similarities to higher kingdoms of<br>
<b>Animals, Plants and Fungi. This text will</b><br>
deal with six selected phyla of Protoctista<br>
namely;<br>
<b>Rhizopoda,</b><br>
<b>Zoomastigina,</b><br>
<b>Apicomplexa, Euglenophyta, Oomycota,</b><br>
and Chlorophyta.<br>
<b>Phylum Rhizopoda</b><br>
<b>Rhizopoda is a broad group of protozoan</b><br>
amoeboid organisms placed in kingdom<br>
<b>Protoctista. The shape and organization</b><br>
of pseudopodia are among the main<br>
characteristics that are used to classify<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   113</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
114<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
members of phylum Rhizopoda. This<br>
phylum comprises of all forms of amoeba<br>
including parasitic and free living amoeba.<br>
<b>Parasitic amoeba include Entamoeba</b><br>
hystolytica which feeds on cells  of<br>
the human colon, and cause amoebic<br>
dysentery also (amoebiasis). The disease<br>
is characterised by abdominal pains,<br>
nausea, vomiting, erosion of blood vessels<br>
of the gut and diarrhoea containing blood.<br>
<b>General characteristics of phylum</b><br>
<b>Rhizopoda</b><br>
<b>They are found in sea water, fresh water,</b><br>
and in the soil. They can also be found<br>
in a mud shallow pond and slow ﬂowing<br>
streams containing plenty of decaying<br>
organic matter.<br>
a)	They are single-celled eukaryotes and<br>
their cells have no deﬁnite shape.<br>
b)	They are unicellular whose bodies<br>
are surrounded by membranes. Their<br>
cytoplasms have two distinct regions,<br>
the plasmasol (inner) and plasmagel<br>
(outer).<br>
c)	Most of them are free living, forming<br>
important links in the food chains. They<br>
feed on plant and animal materials.<br>
<b>In contrast, others are parasitic or</b><br>
infectious to animals, including human<br>
being; some human pathogens such as<br>
<b>Entamoeba histolytica cause amoebic</b><br>
dysentery.<br>
d)	They reproduce asexually by binary<br>
ﬁssion.<br>
e)	They move by using pseudopodia;<br>
hence, amoeba and other protoctists<br>
using this mode of locomotion are<br>
called pseudopods.<br>
f)	 They possess contractile vacuole that<br>
carries out osmoregulation. Normally,<br>
ﬂuids in the amoeba cell are relatively<br>
more concentrated which allow more<br>
water to ﬂow to their body in response<br>
to osmotic gradient. Amoeba in turn<br>
pumps out this water using their<br>
contractile vacuole.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of phylum</b><br>
<b>Rhizopoda</b><br>
<b>Members of the group Rhizopoda have</b><br>
the following features which distinguish<br>
them from the rest of the protoctists.<br>
a)	 They have pseudopodia, which are<br>
used for both locomotion and feeding.<br>
<b>Such pseudopodia are constantly</b><br>
changing as amoeba moves and feeds.<br>
<b>Food particles and small organisms</b><br>
are engulfed using pseudopodia and<br>
digested in the food vacuoles. The<br>
digested food is assimilated into the<br>
rest of the body. This type of feeding<br>
is called phagocytosis. Ingestion is<br>
by endocytosis and egestion is by<br>
exocytosis.<br>
b)	 The food vacuole and the oil droplets of<br>
amoeba confer a granular appearance<br>
to the endoplasm.<br>
c)	 They possess a contractile vacuole,<br>
which carries out osmoregulation.<br>
<b>When the contractile vacuole reaches</b><br>
a certain size, it fuses with the cell<br>
membrane where water is released<br>
outside the cell.<br>
d)	 Their cytoplasm has two distinct<br>
regions; viscous outer layer ectoplasm<br>
(plasmagel) and more ﬂuid internal<br>
endoplasm (plasmasol).<br>

<b>Structure of Entamoeba histolytica</b><br>
<b>Entamoeba histolytica has a regular body</b><br>
which changes constantly. The body<br>
is covered by a thin semi-permeable<br>
membrane called plasmallema or plasma<br>
membrane; and it is differentiated into<br>
two distinct portions; an outer ectoplasm<br>
(plasmagel) and an inner endoplasm<br>
(plasmasol). In the endoplasm, there is<br>
a prominent nucleus which is enclosed<br>
by a nuclear membrane. They possess<br>
pseudopodia for locomotion and feeding<br>
by engulfing food substances (Figure<br>
3.12).<br>

<b>Pseudopodia</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.12 Structure of Entamoeba histolytica</b><br>
<b>Adaptations of Entamoeba to its mode</b><br>
of life<br>
<b>Some amoeba such as Naegleria are free</b><br>
living; they can live in different habitats<br>
such as on the bottom of ponds and lakes,<br>
whereas others like Entamoeba exhibit a<br>
parasitic mode of life. Parasitic amoeba<br>
have the following adaptations to their<br>
mode of life.<br>
a)	 They can form cysts which resist<br>
digestive agents in the stomach.<br>
b)	 They lack contractile vacuoles, since<br>
they live in isotonic state with the<br>
host’s gut ﬂuid.<br>
c)	 They live where there is a plenty of<br>
food supply from their host.<br>
d)	 They are physiologically tolerant to<br>
low oxygen concentration, since they<br>
live in areas with low oxygen supply<br>
in the large intestine.<br>
e)	 They have rapid binary ﬁssion and<br>
production of a large number of<br>
cysts to ensure their existence and<br>
perpetuation in alternative hosts.<br>
f)	 They can feed on various foods,<br>
ranging from bacteria to blood cells,<br>
and digest them with their enzymes.<br>

taxonomists consider Protoctista<br>

of protoctists.<br>

of Entamoeba?<br>
<b>Phylum Zoomastigina</b><br>
<b>This phylum consists of ﬂagellated</b><br>
parasitic protoctists. It contains free-<br>
living organisms, some are symbionts,<br>
such as protozoans which live in the gut<br>
of termites and digest cellulose in the<br>
wood eaten by the termites, and some<br>
are parasites. Examples of parasitic<br>
organisms found in this phylum include<br>
<b>Trypanosoma gambiense; a parasite</b><br>
causing African sleeping sickness.<br>

<b>General characteristics of phylum</b><br>
<b>Zoomastigina</b><br>
a)	 The phylum Zoomastigina contains<br>
organisms such as Trichonympha and<br>
<b>Trypanosoma, which have one or two</b><br>
ﬂagella.<br>
b)	 The members of this phylum are<br>
heterotrophic; single-celled organisms.<br>
c)	 It comprises of both free-living and<br>
parasitic<br>
organisms.<br>
<b>Free-living</b><br>
<b>Zoomastigina are found in ponds and</b><br>
puddles with plenty of organic matter<br>
while a few such as Trypanosoma are<br>
parasites.<br>
d)	 Some have undulating membrane.<br>
e)	 Most of them reproduce by a simple<br>
binary ﬁssion.<br>
f)	 The body is overlaid by a semi-rigid<br>
pellicle.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of phylum</b><br>
<b>Zoomastigina</b><br>
<b>Zoomastigina species differ from the other</b><br>
members of the kingdom Protoctista by<br>
being the only heterotrophic unicellular<br>
eukaryotes with one or more flagella.<br>
<b>Structure of Trypanosoma</b><br>
<b>The genus Trypanosoma contains a large</b><br>
number of parasitic species which infect<br>
wild animals, domesticated animals and<br>
humans. Trypanosoma is divided into<br>
several sub-genera based on morphological<br>
differences.<br>
<b>Different</b><br>
species<br>
of<br>
trypanosomes are transmitted by insects.<br>
<b>Examples include; Trypanosoma cruzi,</b><br>
<b>Trypanasoma brucei and Trypanosoma</b><br>
gambiense which is a causative agent of<br>
sleeping sickness in humans. This parasite<br>
is common in west and central Africa, its<br>
vector is a tsetse ﬂy, Glossina palpalis. It is<br>
about 15µm long, and 1µm wide, pointed<br>
at both ends and bears a prominent ovoid<br>
nucleus in the central region. It is enclosed<br>
in a strong pellicle which maintains the<br>
body shape. The entire body is covered<br>
by a wavy undulating membrane. Along<br>
the edge of the membrane is a ﬂagellum<br>
attached posteriorly to a small granule<br>
known as a blepharoplast (basal body).<br>
<b>Anteriorly, the ﬂagellum projects in front</b><br>
of the body as a short, ﬁne slash, and<br>
immediately posterior to the basal body,<br>
there is a prominent granule, a parabasal<br>
body (Figure. 3.13).<br>

<b>Adaptations of Trypanosoma spp. to</b><br>
their mode of life<br>
<b>Trypanosoma spp. are highly adapted to</b><br>
parasitic mode of life in their hosts due<br>
to possession of the following adaptive<br>
features:<br>
a)	 They have large surface areas to<br>
volume ratio. This is important for<br>
absorption of oxygen and food from<br>
their hosts.<br>
b)	 They live isotonically within the<br>
blood plasma of their hosts. They<br>
lack<br>
contractile<br>
vacuoles<br>
and<br>
osmoregulation does not take place.<br>
c)	 They have rapid reproductive rate by<br>
binary ﬁssion which ensures that large<br>
number of parasites are produced.<br>
<b>Large number of these parasites</b><br>
is potentially important especially<br>
in adverse conditions where some<br>
parasite formed die, but some will<br>
remain.<br>
d)	 They have a hard pellicle that protects<br>
cytoplasmic structures and restricts<br>
action of digestive agents of the host.<br>
e)	 They are able to remain dormant in<br>
their host’s cells of liver and spleen<br>
during<br>
adverse<br>
conditions.<br>
<b>This</b><br>
ensures existence of the species.<br>

<b>Phylum Apicomplexa</b><br>
<b>This phylum consists of eukaryotic</b><br>
unicellular organisms, which are spore<br>
forming parasites of animals. They are<br>
also known as sporozoans; an example<br>
is the parasite Plasmodium which cause<br>
malaria in humans.<br>
<b>General characteristics of phylum</b><br>
<b>Apicomplexa</b><br>
a)	 Most of them possess a unique type of<br>
plastid called an apicoplast, used for<br>
piercing host cells.<br>
b)	 They are unicellular and spore<br>
forming organisms. Almost all species<br>
are obligate endoparasites of animals,<br>
except<br>
nephromyces<br>
which<br>
live<br>
symbiotically in marine animals.<br>
c)	 They have an infectious stage known<br>
as sporozoite.<br>
d)	 They<br>
reproduce<br>
asexually<br>
by<br>
schizogony and sexually by sporogony.<br>
e)	 The<br>
parasite<br>
changes<br>
its<br>
shape<br>
depending on the host it inhabits.<br>
<b>For instance, it is sickle shaped in</b><br>
salivary glands of mosquito, while it<br>
is spherical or amoeboid in liver cells<br>
of humans.<br>
f)	 They are non-motile.<br>
g)	 They form resistant spores after<br>
fertilisation.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of phylum</b><br>
<b>Apicomplexa</b><br>
<b>Organisms in the phylum Apicomplexa</b><br>
differ from other groups by the following<br>
features:<br>
a)	 They have a plastid called apicoplast,<br>
which is used for piercing host cells.<br>
b)	 They<br>
reproduce<br>
asexually<br>
by<br>
schizogony in the human body and<br>

sexually by sporogony in the mosquito.<br>
c)	 They change their shapes depending<br>
on the host it inhabits. For instance,<br>
plasmodium is sickle shaped in<br>
salivary glands of mosquito, while it is<br>
amoeboid in liver cells of human.<br>
<b>Structure of Plasmodium</b><br>
<b>The Plasmodium is oval shaped, and</b><br>
lacks contractile vacuoles and locomotory<br>
organs. The apical end is a bridged cone-<br>
shaped projection demarcated by the polar<br>
rings. A single mitochondrion is generally<br>
present at the posterior end and the Golgi<br>
apparatus is unremarkable. Just beneath<br>
the inner membrane, there is a row of<br>
microtubules which originate from the<br>
polar end of the apical end and continue to<br>
the posterior end. Attached to the nucleus,<br>
there is an endoplasmic reticulum, a<br>
network of membranous tubules within<br>
the cytoplasm (Figure 3.14). Additionally,<br>
there are the rhoptries, which are the<br>
specialized secretory organelles.<br>
<b>Golgi apparatus</b><br>
<b>Nucleus</b><br>
<b>Endoplasmic reticulum</b><br>
<b>Mitochondrion</b><br>
<b>Rhoptries</b><br>
<b>Microtubules</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.14  Structure of a Plasmodium</b><br>
<b>Life cycle of Plasmodium</b><br>
<b>The parasite in the form of sporozoites</b><br>
enters the blood stream of the human<br>
being after a mosquito bite. It then travels<br>
to the liver and invades it (Figure 3.15).<br>
<b>The sporozoites grow, divide and produce</b><br>
many haploid forms called merozoites or<br>
schizozoites in liver cells. The merozoites<br>
in the liver cells exit and re-enter blood<br>
stream, where red blood cells are invaded.<br>
<b>The merozoites multiply in the red blood</b><br>
cells by asexual reproduction. In the red<br>
blood cells, they develop into schizonts<br>
(feeding stage), which rupture the cells,<br>
releasing newly formed merozoites which<br>
then invade other red blood cells. Some<br>
of the merozoites in infected blood cells,<br>
leave a cycle of asexual replication.<br>
<b>Instead of replicating, the merozoites</b><br>
in these cells develop into sexual forms<br>
of the parasite called male and female<br>
gametocytes. The cycle in mosquito<br>
starts when the mosquito bites an infected<br>
human and ingests gametocytes. The male<br>
and female gametes fuse to form diploid<br>
zygote, which develops into moving<br>
ookinete. The ookinete burrows in the<br>
midgut wall of mosquito, forming oocyst<br>
on the other side. Growth and division<br>
of each oocyst produce numerous active<br>
haploid forms called sporozoites. After 8<br>
to 15 days (depending on the species), the<br>
oocyst bursts, thus releasing sporozoites<br>
into the body cavity of the mosquito,<br>
from where they travel to, and invade<br>
the mosquito salivary glands. The cycle<br>
of human infection re-starts when the<br>
mosquito taking a blood meal injects the<br>
sporozoites from its salivary glands into<br>
the human blood stream.<br>

<b>Adaptations of Plasmodium to its mode</b><br>
of life<br>
<b>Plasmodium, a parasite that causes malaria</b><br>
invades the human red blood cells as an<br>
essential step of its complex life cycle; it<br>
has the following adaptive features to its<br>
mode of life:<br>
a)	 Presence of a well developed chemotactic<br>
responses which enable them  ﬁnd<br>
their way to the liver cells, then to the<br>
human red blood cells, gut epithelium,<br>
and eventually, to the salivary glands<br>
of mosquitoes.<br>
b)	 They have enzymes which enable them<br>
to penetrate through the host cells such<br>
as liver and red blood cells of human<br>
as well as crop and salivary glands of<br>
mosquitoes.<br>
c)	  Plasmodium has an extremely simple<br>
structure, which enhances its sheltered<br>
life within the bodies of the two hosts.<br>
d)	 The ability to adjust their osmotic<br>
pressure in relation to the mammalian<br>
blood, mosquito’s crop, haemocoel and<br>
salivary glands assures their existence<br>
in the mentioned parts.<br>
e)	 They have gametocytes which can resist<br>
mosquito’s digestive enzymes, and they<br>
have adapted to high reproductive rate<br>
by schizogony in liver cells, repeated<br>
schizogony in red blood cells, and<br>
sporogony in the mosquito salivary<br>
glands, to ensure their survival.<br>

and other protoctists.<br>
2.	 Describe the reproductive cycle of<br>
a Plasmodium.<br>
3.	 Plasmodium is able to survive in<br>
its two different hosts. Justify.<br>

<b>Phylum Euglenophyta</b><br>
<b>This phylum comprises of unicellular aquatic</b><br>
algae, most of them live in freshwater;<br>
many possess flagella and are motile. The<br>
outer part of the cell consists of firm but<br>
flexible layer called a pellicle or periplast,<br>
which cannot properly be considered a cell<br>
wall. Some euglenoids contain chloroplasts<br>
that contain chlorophyll 'a' and 'b'; the<br>
photosynthetic pigments, as in the phylum<br>
chlorophyta. Others are heterotrophic and<br>
can ingest or absorb their food. Food is<br>
stored as paramylon. Reproduction takes<br>
place by longitudinal cell division. The<br>
most common organism in this phylum is<br>
photosynthetic protozoan such as Euglena,<br>
usually found in water bodies like ponds.<br>
<b>General characteristics of phylum</b><br>
<b>Euglenophyta</b><br>
a)	 They have chloroplasts which contain<br>
chlorophyll for photosynthesis.<br>
b)	 Most of them are found in fresh water;<br>
only few are marine dwellers.<br>
c)	 They are protected by pellicle which<br>
surrounds the cytoplasm, since they<br>
lack cell wall. This enables the cells<br>
to change shape because they move<br>
around by euglenoid movement by the<br>
aid of myonemes.<br>
d)	 They have two ﬂagella. One of them<br>
is short, while the other is long; these<br>
arise from the bottom of a reservoir.<br>
e)	 They have eye spot (photoreceptor)<br>
for detection of light intensity.<br>
f)	 They have pyrenoids for storage of<br>
starch.<br>
g)	 They sometimes feed heterotrophically<br>
by using the gullet.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of phylum</b><br>
<b>Euglenophyta</b><br>
<b>Members of the phylum Euglenophyta</b><br>
are distinguished from other phyla by the<br>
following features:<br>
a)	 They have pyrenoids for storage of<br>
starch.<br>
b)	 They have eye spot (photoreceptor)<br>
for detection of light intensity.<br>
c)	 They possess both plant and animal<br>
characteristics (Figure 3.16). Plant<br>
characteristics include; presence of<br>
chloroplasts containing chlorophyll,<br>
pyrenoids, and utilization of nitrites<br>
or ammonia as sources of nitrogen.<br>
<b>Animal</b><br>
characteristics<br>
include;<br>
possession of myonemes (muscle- like<br>
strands), and ﬂagella for locomotion<br>
(euglenoid movement), utilization of<br>
amino acids, peptones or polypeptide<br>
as a source of nitrogen, presence of<br>
gullet with sphincter and reservoir for<br>
ingestion of food.<br>
<b>Flagellum</b><br>
<b>Eye spot</b><br>
<b>Reservoir</b><br>
<b>Contractile</b><br>
vacuole<br>
<b>Chloroplast</b><br>
<b>Nucleus</b><br>
<b>Pellicle</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.16 Structure of the Euglena</b><br>

<b>Adaptations of Euglena</b><br>
<b>Euglena is able to survive freely in its</b><br>
environment due to presence of the<br>
following adaptive features:<br>
a)	 It has chloroplast which contains<br>
chlorophyll for photosynthesis.<br>
b)	 It sometimes behaves as heterotroph<br>
due to possession of a gullet with<br>
sphincter and reservoir.<br>
c)	 It can swim using ﬂagella.<br>
d)	 It has a ﬂexible pellicle for maintenance<br>
of shape and aiding in euglenoid<br>
movement.<br>
e)	 It possesses contractile vacuole for<br>
osmoregulation.<br>
f)	 It has a high (rapid) rate of multiplication<br>
during favorable conditions and forms<br>
cysts under unfavorable conditions to<br>
ensure survival.<br>
g)	 It has a photoreceptor for detection of<br>
light conditions.<br>

<b>Phylum Oomycota</b><br>
<b>The</b><br>
phylum<br>
includes<br>
fungus-like<br>
organisms, which are also referred to as<br>
“water molds”. Normally oomycetes may<br>
occur as saprotrophs; living on decayed<br>
matter or parasites living on higher<br>
plants and can be aquatic, terrestrial or<br>
amphibious. Oomycetes play an important<br>
role in the decomposition and recycling<br>
of decaying matter. Members of this<br>
phylum are fungi-like protoctists such as<br>
Phytophthora, Puccinia and Pythium.<br>
<b>General characteristics of phylum</b><br>
<b>Oomycota</b><br>
a)	 They<br>
are<br>
filamentous<br>
protoctists<br>
which must absorb their food from<br>
the surrounding water or soil, or may<br>
invade the body of another organism<br>
to feed.<br>
b)	 They are mostly parasites of plants,<br>
example<br>
<b>Phytophthora</b><br>
infestans<br>
which causes serious diseases such as<br>
potato blight disease.<br>
c)	 They reproduce sexually by oogamy<br>
in which male and female gametes<br>
fuse to form an oospore.<br>
d)	 The general body is organised into<br>
mycelia with aseptate or coenocytic<br>
hyphae.<br>
e)	 They have cell walls made up of<br>
cellulose.<br>
f)	 They have a tubular structure called<br>
a haustorium used for absorption of<br>
nutrients from the host (Figure 3.17).<br>
<b>Distinctive features of phylum Oomycota</b><br>
<b>Oomycota can be distinguished from other</b><br>
members of the group by the following<br>
features:<br>
a)	 They have sporangia which produce<br>
zoospores.<br>
b)	 Zoospore have two flagella attached to<br>
a ventral groove; the anterior flagellum<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   121</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
122<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
is a tinsel while the posterior one is a<br>
whiplash type.<br>
<b>Structure of Phytophthora</b><br>
<b>The vegetative body of Phytophthora</b><br>
consists of mycelium which is abundantly<br>
branched where septa may develop in the<br>
older parts and at the base of sex organs.<br>
<b>The mycelium branches arise at right</b><br>
angles. The mycelium grows both intra- and<br>
intercellularly. During intercellular growth,<br>
it develops finger like haustoria inside the<br>
neighbouring host cells, which are normally<br>
the mesophyll cells of the plant leaves.<br>
<b>The haustoria absorb nutrients from the</b><br>
leaf cells. The hyphae possess sporangium<br>
which produces spores (Figure 3.17).<br>

<b>Sporangium</b><br>
<b>Stoma</b><br>
<b>Guard cell</b><br>
<b>Lower epidermis</b><br>
<b>Leaf mesophyll cell</b><br>
Figure 3.17 Structure of Phytophthora infestans, growing on an infected potato leaf<br>
<b>Adaptations of Phytophthora to its mode</b><br>
of life<br>
<b>The Phytophthora have the following</b><br>
adaptations to their mode of life:<br>
a)	 Formation of cysts during unfavourable<br>
conditions ensures its survival.<br>
b)	 Ability to reproduce both sexually and<br>
asexually, with a high reproductive<br>
output, increases its chances of<br>
survival.<br>
c)	 It has haustoria with a large surface<br>
area for penetration into the plant cells<br>
and absorption of nutrients from them.<br>
d)	 The haustoria secrete enzymes which<br>
help the parasite to penetrate its host<br>
cell.<br>
e)	 It has spores which withstand the<br>
adverse conditions, such that, under<br>
favourable conditions, they germinate<br>
to Phytophthora infestans.<br>

<b>Phylum Chlorophyta</b><br>
<b>Members of this phylum were formerly</b><br>
classiﬁed as plants. They include<br>
unicellular non motile alga (Chlorella<br>
and Acetabularia), a unicellular motile<br>
alga (Chlamydomonas), ﬁlamentous alga<br>
(Spirogyra) and a thalloid marine alga,<br>
(Ulva). In other classiﬁcation, chlorophytes<br>
are placed under kingdom plantae, since<br>
they are more phylogenetically related to<br>
plants. They are regarded as ancestors of<br>
plants, because they have photosynthetic<br>
pigments such as chlorophyll 'a', 'b' and 'β'<br>
carotenoids as well as xanthophylls, which<br>
are characteristics of plants. Additionally,<br>
they possess photosynthetic apparatus called<br>
pyrenoid for condensation of glucose to<br>
starch, which is the same as photosynthetic<br>
product in plants. Under this phylum there<br>
are important species of high economic<br>
importance including sources of agar and<br>
phycocolloids, which are widely used in<br>
textile, pharmaceutical, and food industries.<br>
<b>Chlorophytes are primarily aquatic, and are</b><br>
the primary source of energy and oxygen<br>
to marine heterotrophs. Few are found in<br>
special habitats in terrestrial environments.<br>
<b>Chlorophytes are heterogeneous in their</b><br>
pigmentation, shapes, and size. They range<br>
from microscopic, simple and unicellular<br>
such as Chlorella and Chlamydomonas to<br>
giant multicellular macroalgae. Although<br>
they resemble eukaryotic plants in many<br>
ways, they have no true roots, stem or leaves<br>
and do not produce seeds. The simplest<br>
structure is unicellular, but they may exist<br>
in colonies or in ﬁlaments of several distinct<br>
cells. Others may be multinucleated with<br>
interconnected cells that lack cross walls<br>
as in Volvox and Oedogonium respectively.<br>
<b>This section will focus on the characteristics</b><br>
of chlorophytes, structure, and adaptations<br>
of Spirogyra.<br>
<b>General characteristics of phylum</b><br>
<b>Chlorophyta</b><br>
a)	 They<br>
are<br>
adapted<br>
to<br>
aquatic<br>
environments ranging from fresh to<br>
marine water, for example, their sexual<br>
reproduction relies on water as they<br>
produce motile sperms which have to<br>
move in water medium to fertilise the<br>
egg.<br>
b)	 They are photoautotrophs and have<br>
chlorophyll 'a' and 'b' and other<br>
photosynthetic pigments such as<br>
carotenoids used in photosynthesis.<br>
c)	 They have cell walls made up of<br>
cellulose.<br>
d)	 They store carbohydrates in the form<br>
of starch.<br>
e)	 They possess large vacuole for<br>
osmoregulation.<br>
f)	 They occur in a great range of sizes<br>
and forms, including unicellular,<br>
ﬁlamentous, colonial and thalloid<br>
forms.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   123</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
124<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
g)	 They reproduce both sexually and<br>
asexually; sexual reproduction involves<br>
specialised cells called sporocytes<br>
which undergo meiosis to produce<br>
haploid motile or ﬂagellated cells called<br>
zoospores. These grow mitotically to<br>
form a gamete producing body called<br>
gametophyte, capable of producing<br>
either male or female reproductive<br>
cells (gametes). Female gametes<br>
can be either larger than male ones<br>
(anisogamous) or similar (isogamous)<br>
morphologically. Female and male<br>
gametes are ﬂagellated, they can swim<br>
and unite to form a diploid zygote which<br>
settles and grows mitotically to form a<br>
multicellular body called sporophyte<br>
which can again produce sporocytes.<br>
<b>The gametes, if not fertilised, lose their</b><br>
ﬂagella and grow into a new gamete-<br>
producing body. Asexual reproduction<br>
occurs by vegetative fragmentation<br>
where individual cell or short chains<br>
of cells separated from the main body<br>
are capable of growing into a new body.<br>
h)  Life cycle of chlorophytes is diverse.<br>
<b>For</b><br>
instance,<br>
<b>Ulva</b><br>
shows<br>
an<br>
isomorphic alternation of generations<br>
in which sporocyte-producing body<br>
called<br>
sporophyte<br>
generation<br>
is<br>
morphologically identical to gamete-<br>
producing body called gametophyte<br>
generation. Ulothrix shows haplontic<br>
life cycle, in which sporophyte<br>
and<br>
gametophyte<br>
generation<br>
are<br>
morphologically different.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of phylum</b><br>
<b>Chlorophyta</b><br>
<b>Chlorophytes are distinguished from other</b><br>
species of Protoctists by the following<br>
features<br>
a)	 They have spiral chloroplasts with<br>
pyrenoids.<br>
b)	 They have central suspended nucleus<br>
with cytoplasmic strands.<br>
c)	 They<br>
show<br>
an<br>
isomorphic<br>
and<br>
haplontic alternation of generations.<br>
<b>Sturcture of Spirogyra</b><br>
<b>Spirogyra is a genus of filamentous green</b><br>
algae which have helical or spirally<br>
arranged chloroplast as a characteristic<br>
feature of this genus. They have thin strands<br>
of cytoplasm in which the prominent<br>
nucleus is suspended, and their spiral<br>
chloroplasts embedded in the cytoplasm<br>
bear the structure called pyrenoid for starch<br>
storage. The cell wall contains cellulose<br>
at the inner layer, and the outer layer<br>
contains pectin, which is responsible for<br>
the slippery surface of algae. Surrounding<br>
the cell wall, there is mucilage, which<br>
thickens the cell membrane, store water<br>
and food. Moreover, Spirogyra has long,<br>
unbranched filaments with cylindrical<br>
cells that are joined end to end. Each cell<br>
has a central vacuole. The cells are long<br>
and thin filaments, and sometimes these<br>
filaments develop root-like structures for<br>
attachment to the substrate (Figure 3.18).<br>

<b>Adaptations of Spirogyra to its mode of</b><br>
life<br>
<b>Spirogyra is able to live and thrive in its</b><br>
environment due to the following adaptive<br>
features:<br>
a)	 It has pyrenoids for storage of starch.<br>
b)	 It has a large vacuole for osmo<br>
regulation.<br>
c)	 It has chlorophyll 'a' and 'b' for<br>
photosynthesis, hence it is an autotroph.<br>
d)	 It has mucilage layer to protect it from<br>
desiccation and infection.<br>
e)	 It has a cellulose cell wall for strength<br>
and protection.<br>
<b>Activity 3.2 Observation of Spirogyra</b><br>
under a light microscope<br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>Fresh Spirogyra, microscope, slides,</b><br>
slide cover, dropper, office pin, watch<br>
glass, and beaker containing water.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Collect fresh Spirogyra from fresh<br>
water ponds or slow moving water<br>
streams, and put it into a watch<br>
glass.<br>
b)	 Add a drop of water on a clean slide<br>
using a dropper, and using an ofﬁce<br>
pin pick a few threads of Spirogyra<br>
and mount them on the slide.<br>
c)	 Separate the strands using a pin to<br>
remain with a few threads. Cover<br>
your specimen with a coverslip.<br>
d)	 Place the slide under a light<br>
microscope for observation.<br>
e)	 Notice the wide variety of<br>
chloroplast types and the small,<br>
round, colorless pyrenoids on some<br>
or all the larger chloroplasts.<br>
f)	 Based on your observation, draw<br>
and label the diagram of Spirogyra<br>
showing<br>
the<br>
pyrenoids,<br>
spiral<br>
chloroplast, mucilage, cytoplasm,<br>
and nucleus.<br>

2.	 Describe ways in which Spirogyra<br>
is adapted to its mode of life.<br>
3.	 Explain the role of Spirogyra in<br>
oxygen balance in fresh water<br>
habitat.<br>
4.	 With examples, explain why<br>
taxonomists sometimes regard<br>
Spirogyra as a plant.<br>

<b>Economic importance of kingdom</b><br>
<b>Protoctista</b><br>
<b>Protoctista are mostly aquatic organisms</b><br>
found in sea, fresh water and moist soil.<br>
<b>Many are also found in the form of</b><br>
plankton and some live in the bodies of<br>
animals as parasites. Some members of<br>
the kingdom Protoctista plays an essential<br>
role in the ecosystem that can benefit<br>
humans and other creatures. However,<br>
some members are important pathogens<br>
and can bring harm to living organisms. In<br>
some cases, Protoctista might be a source<br>
of pollution in lakes and coastal shores.<br>
<b>Advantages of kingdom Protoctista</b><br>
<b>Protoctists have the following economic</b><br>
importance:<br>
a)	 Algae are edible and nutritious to<br>
humans and other animals. Some<br>
species are cultivated and harvested<br>
for consumption by human being. Red<br>
algae are rich in vitamins and minerals.<br>
<b>Carrageenan,</b><br>
a<br>
polysaccharide<br>
extracted from red algae, is used as<br>
thick agent in ice cream and other<br>
foods.<br>
b)	 Giant kelp forests (Large brown algae)<br>
are rich ecosystems, providing food<br>
and shelter for many organisms.<br>
c)	 Trichonymphs are flagellates that live<br>
in the intestines of termites. These<br>
protozoans break down cellulose in<br>
wood into carbohydrates that termites<br>
can digest.<br>
d)	 Algae and Euglena are primary<br>
producers in aquatic ecosystems. Most<br>
species are primary sources of food for<br>
aquatic organisms such as ﬁshes and<br>
zooplanktons.<br>
e)	 Some Protoctists are used as ﬁsh baits.<br>
f)	 Algae species produces oxygen useful<br>
for aerobic respiration, particularly<br>
in the aquatic environment which is<br>
utilised by other aquatic organisms.<br>
g)	 Some are used in agriculture as good<br>
sources or fertilizer. For example,<br>
liquid extracts from the brown algae<br>
supply potassium and trace elements<br>
such as zinc.<br>
h)	 Some algae serve as a source of agar,<br>
used in the pharmaceutical industry<br>
in preparation of culture media for<br>
growing microbes, such as bacteria<br>
and fungi.<br>
i)	 Some protoctists such as algae are<br>
milled or ground to obtain powder<br>
which is used as a thickener, binder,<br>
gelling or stabilising agents in the<br>
manufacture of various products such<br>
as cosmetics, paints, tooth paste, and<br>
ice cream.<br>
j)	 Some protoctists such as Entamoeba<br>
coli; a non-pathogenic species of genus<br>
<b>Entamoeba, can exist as a commensal</b><br>
parasite in the human gastrointestinal<br>
tract and feed on pathogenic bacteria.<br>
<b>Disadvantages of kingdom Protoctista</b><br>
a)	 They cause pollution; the red algae<br>
cause water pollution called the algal<br>
bloom which may hinder boating and<br>
recreation.<br>
b)	 They cause diseases to human beings;<br>
for instance, Amoebiasis is caused<br>
by Entamoeba histolytica, malaria is<br>
caused by Plasmodium species (like<br>
<b>Plasmodium malariae, Plasmodium</b><br>
vivax, and Plasmodium falciparum).<br>
c)	 Some members of the kingdom<br>
cause<br>
diseases<br>
to<br>
animals;<br>
for<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   126</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
