<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
example Trypanosoma brucei causes<br>
<b>Trypanosomiasis (sleeping sickness)</b><br>
to humans, Trypanosoma vivax causes<br>
Nagana disease in cattle.<br>
d)	 Some<br>
members<br>
cause<br>
diseases<br>
to plants. A good example is<br>
<b>Phytophthora infectans which infect</b><br>
tomato and potato plants.<br>
3. 5  Kingdom Fungi<br>
<b>Organisms belonging to this kingdom are</b><br>
those originally placed in kingdom Plantae<br>
under the two kingdom classiﬁcation<br>
system. The early placement of fungi<br>
under kingdom Plantae was due to the<br>
morphological appearance of some fungi,<br>
particularly the mushrooms which resemble<br>
plants. The microscopic fungi were not<br>
yet known by then, since the microscopes<br>
were not yet invented. It was discovered<br>
later that fungi differ from plants in several<br>
ways, hence they were placed in their own<br>
kingdom, and most of them had economic<br>
importance. Through mycology (a study of<br>
fungi), over 100,000 species of Fungi have<br>
been described. It is estimated that there are<br>
over 1million species of Fungi waiting for<br>
identiﬁcation.<br>
<b>Fungi are eukaryotic, unicellular or</b><br>
multicellular multinucleate organisms,<br>
made up of a mass of branching and delicate<br>
thread-like structures called hyphae, which<br>
collectively constitute fungal bodies called<br>
mycelium. Depending on the species, some<br>
hyphae may have cross walls called septa,<br>
dividing hyphae into many cells with one or<br>
more nuclei. In some species, the cytoplasm<br>
is continuous without cross walls. Fungi<br>
are thallophytes, lacking chlorophyll,<br>
hence nutrition in these organisms is either<br>
parasitic or saprophytic. Fungi produce<br>
extracellular enzymes which digest almost<br>
everything including protein and starch. The<br>
end products of digestion are absorbed by<br>
special structures called haustoria (plural)<br>
or haustorium (singular). Some fungi are<br>
parasites, as they obtain nutrients directly<br>
from other living organisms such as plants<br>
and animals. They have cell walls made up<br>
of chitin, unlike plant cell walls which are<br>
made up of cellulose. Organisms under this<br>
kingdom store carbohydrate in the form of<br>
glycogen like animals, but not starch, as in<br>
chlorophytes and plants.<br>
<b>They have a variety of shapes and sizes</b><br>
extending from microscopic to macroscopic.<br>
<b>Sexual reproduction in fungi involves two</b><br>
haploid nuclei of compatible mating hyphae<br>
strains that unite to form a zygote which<br>
later grows into a new fungal body. Asexual<br>
reproduction is accomplished by production<br>
of asexual spore which, under favorable<br>
conditions, germinates and grows to form<br>
new haploid fungal hyphae. Examples<br>
of organisms in this kingdom include<br>
mushrooms, yeasts, Penicillium, bread<br>
mould, and toadstool.<br>
<b>Position of kingdom Fungi</b><br>
<b>As explained earlier, the position of fungi in</b><br>
classiﬁcation was one of the controversial<br>
arguments. It was previously not clear<br>
whether fungi should be classiﬁed as an<br>
animal or as a plant, because they have<br>
some features in common for both plant<br>
and animal groups.<br>
<b>Similarities between fungi and animals</b><br>
<b>Similar to animals, fungi have the</b><br>
following features:<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   127</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
128<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
a)	 They have chitin as a structural<br>
carbohydrate in the cell wall. This is<br>
a feature typical of animals such as<br>
arthropods; example insects.<br>
b)	 They store carbohydrates in the form<br>
of glycogen.<br>
c)	 They are heterotrophs. Thus, they<br>
cannot manufacture their own food<br>
since they are either saprophytes or<br>
parasites. The parasitic fungi can be<br>
facultative or obligate.<br>
<b>Similarities between fungi and plants</b><br>
<b>Similar to plants, fungi have the following</b><br>
features:<br>
a)	 Their cells have cell wall.<br>
b)	 Some fungi have vegetative bodies<br>
that are superﬁcially differentiated<br>
into shoot-like and root-like systems.<br>
c)	 Most fungi’s growth is restricted to<br>
apical cells.<br>
d)	 They are non-motile.<br>
e)	 They reproduce sexually by formation<br>
of spores such as ascospore in yeast<br>
and basidiospores in mushrooms. Some<br>
fungi reproduce asexually by producing<br>
spores such as sporangiospore.<br>
f)	 They lack centrioles in their cells.<br>
<b>Fungi are heterogeneous kingdom</b><br>
consisting of several phyla. However,<br>
despite many ways in which fungal<br>
organisms are distinct, the major criterion<br>
used in grouping them into their respective<br>
groups is their reproductive structures.<br>
<b>Within the kingdom Fungi there are three</b><br>
phyla, namely; Zygomycota, Ascomycota,<br>
and Basidiomycota.<br>
<b>Phylum Zygomycota</b><br>
<b>Fungi belonging to this phylum produce</b><br>
asexual resting spore called zygospore,<br>
which is produced when two opposite mating<br>
strains come close together and their haploid<br>
nuclei unite. The zygospores are resistant<br>
to breakage but they are light, hence they<br>
can be dispersed by wind and water. The<br>
structure of zygospore is capable of keeping<br>
the fungus over a long period of dormancy.<br>
<b>Cells within the zygospore undergo meiosis</b><br>
to form haploid spores and when released<br>
in favorable conditions they germinate<br>
to form a new hyphae or mycelium. This<br>
phylum comprises of saprophytic fungi<br>
such as Mucor and Rhizopus stolonifer; a<br>
common bread mould. Zygomycetes can<br>
also reproduce asexually by spores born<br>
out of sporangia. Some zygomycetes are<br>
parasites in plants, insects, and animals.<br>
<b>General characteristics of phylum</b><br>
<b>Zygomycota</b><br>
a)	 They produce a characteristic sexual<br>
resting spore called zygospore.<br>
b)	 They<br>
are<br>
eukaryotic<br>
organisms,<br>
with aseptate hyphae that have<br>
well developed branching mycelia.<br>
<b>Aseptate means without septa or</b><br>
cross walls, and are sometimes called<br>
coenocytic.<br>
c)	 They undergo both sexual and asexual<br>
reproduction. Sexual reproduction is<br>
by conjugation; this involves fusion<br>
of two haploid nuclei from the two<br>
mating hyphae or strains to produce<br>
zygospores.<br>
<b>Asexual</b><br>
reproduction<br>
occurs via haploid spores released<br>
from sporangia.<br>
d)	 They are saprophytes since they feed<br>
on dead decaying organic matter by<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   128</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
129<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
undergoing extracellular digestion.<br>
<b>However, some zygomycetes are</b><br>
parasitic on plants, animals, and<br>
insects.<br>
e)	 Their hyphae have three distinct parts,<br>
namely sporangiophore, stolon, and<br>
rhizoids.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of phylum</b><br>
<b>Zygomycota</b><br>
<b>Members of the phylum Zygomycota have</b><br>
the following features that differentiate<br>
them from other phyla:<br>
a)	 They have aseptate hyphae which<br>
lack cross walls between adjacent<br>
cells. They are therefore coenocytic in<br>
structure.<br>
b)	 Their cytoplasm is continuous and<br>
multinucleate.<br>
c)	 They undergo sexual reproduction<br>
involving two gametangia to produce<br>
a resting spore known as zygospore.<br>
<b>Structure of Rhizopus</b><br>
<b>The body of Rhizopus consists of branching</b><br>
mycelia composed of three types of hyphae;<br>
stolons, rhizoids, and normally branching<br>
sporangiophores (a stalk that arises from the<br>
vegetative hypha), and sporangia (asexual<br>
spore-forming structures) arise from stolons<br>
opposite to rhizoids (Figure 3.19). Stolons<br>
are the horizontal creeping hyphae that<br>
interconnect the upright growing hyphae.<br>
<b>The sporangiospores are produced inside</b><br>
the spherical structure called sporangium<br>
which is supported by the columella.<br>
<b>Sporangium</b><br>
<b>Collumela</b><br>
<b>Apophysis</b><br>
<b>Sporangiophore</b><br>
<b>Mycellium</b><br>
<b>Rhizoids</b><br>
<b>Stolon</b><br>
<b>Sporangiospores</b><br>

<b>Adaptations of Rhizopus to their mode</b><br>
of life<br>
<b>Members of Rhizopus are saprophytic, and</b><br>
can grow and survive on various organic<br>
substrates including bread, mature fruits,<br>
and vegetables. They are able to acclimatize<br>
themselves to their environment due to the<br>
presence of the following adaptive features:<br>
a)	Pressure in columella makes the<br>
sporangium burst to release haploid<br>
spores. Each individual spore upon<br>
falling on conducive environment can<br>
germinate to a new hyphae body. These<br>
asexual spores are produced in large<br>
quantities; even in harsh conditions<br>
when the environment is dry, they ensure<br>
a quick spread of the species.<br>
b)	They have rhizoids for anchorage on the<br>
substrate and absorption of nutrients.<br>
c)	Their hyphae show chemotropism; in<br>
response to the digested food substances.<br>
d)	They produce thick-walled resistant,<br>
dormant zygospore. This enables the<br>
spores to withstand unfavourable and<br>
adverse conditions.<br>
e)	The wall of sporangium is so brittle that<br>
it easily breaks off to release the spores<br>
and ensure their multiplication.<br>
f)	 Under asexual reproduction, they<br>
produce large number of spores to<br>
ensure survival of the species.<br>
g)	The spores are small and very light,<br>
and the sporangia are raised up to aid<br>
dispersal of spores by wind.<br>
<b>Activity 3.3 Observation of Rhizopus</b><br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>Bread, petri or dissecting dishes, hand</b><br>
lens or light microscope, and slides.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)		 Take one slice of bread in a dish.<br>
b)	 Leave it exposed on a bench for ﬁve<br>
to seven days, until black hairy-like<br>
structures appear on the slice.<br>
c)	 Use a hand lens or a light microscope<br>
to observe the specimen on the slice.<br>
<b>Carefully observe the upright hyphae</b><br>
with black spherical structures at<br>
their tips.<br>
<b>Questions</b><br>
1.	 Draw a well labelled diagram of<br>
the observed specimen.<br>
2.	 State the functions of each<br>
structure in the diagram you have<br>
drawn.<br>
3.	 Identify the specimen by its<br>
common name.<br>
<b>Phylum Ascomycota</b><br>
<b>These are ascocarp forming fungi such as</b><br>
yeasts (Saccharomyces), Aspergillus, and<br>
<b>Penicillium. The ascocarp are cup-like</b><br>
structures containing small sacs which<br>
produce spores known as ascospores,<br>
resulting from sexual reproduction. Besides<br>
sexual spores, ascomycetes produce asexual<br>
spores called conidia, which develop at<br>
the tips of specialised hyphae called<br>
conidiophores. This is the most diverse<br>
group with about 30,000 described species.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   130</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
131<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
<b>Organisms in this phylum form many</b><br>
colourful cup shaped growths called morels<br>
on decaying logs of trees, fruits, crops,<br>
and foods. Most ascomycetes have highly<br>
branched hyphae. They are thus mycelia<br>
except yeasts, which are unicellular.<br>
<b>Yeast shows a number of differences in</b><br>
morphology, reproduction, and cell structure,<br>
when compared to other ascomycetes.<br>
<b>Besides lacking hyphae, yeast does not</b><br>
have ascocarp, hence they are sometimes<br>
referred to as hemiascomycetes; meaning<br>
“half ascomycetes,” to distinguish them<br>
from euascomycetes or true ascomycetes<br>
that have hyphae and ascocarp. They<br>
extracellularly secrete and produce enzymes<br>
such as protease (protein digesting enzymes)<br>
and cellulase (cellulose digesting enzyme).<br>
<b>These kinds of enzymes make this group</b><br>
very destructive to animals, and plants.<br>
<b>However, some ascomycetes such as yeast</b><br>
are widely used in brewing industries for<br>
production of alcohol through fermentation,<br>
while others are used in bakery and textile<br>
industries.<br>
<b>General characteristics of phylum</b><br>
<b>Ascomycota</b><br>
a)	 Their mycelium is made up of tightly<br>
woven septate hyphae except yeasts,<br>
which are unicellular.<br>
b)	 They are heterotrophic saprophytes; in<br>
contrast to other members are parasites.<br>
<b>For example, Candida albicans are</b><br>
infectious ascomycetes which cause<br>
mouth thrush. In plants, they are<br>
responsible for powdery mildew which<br>
infects cereal grains.<br>
c)	 They have a fruiting body containing<br>
several sac-like structures called asci<br>
(singular ascus) which produce haploid<br>
spores called ascospores. These spores<br>
can germinate to form new haploid<br>
hyphae.<br>
d)	 They possess conidia which are<br>
found at the apex of hyphae called<br>
conidiophores which produce millions<br>
of resistant spores used in asexual<br>
reproduction.<br>
e)	 Sexual reproduction is accomplished by<br>
mating of compatible hyphae forming a<br>
dikaryotic hyphae; that is hyphae with<br>
two nuclei in their cell. The nuclei will<br>
fuse only after the formation of ascus.<br>
<b>The diploid nucleus will later undergo</b><br>
meiotic and mitotic divisions to form<br>
ascospores.<br>
f)	 Some ascomycetes such as yeasts are<br>
unicellular and reproduce asexually by<br>
budding (new cells form on the surface<br>
of the old ones).<br>
<b>Distinctive features of phylum</b><br>
<b>Ascomycota</b><br>
<b>The following are the distinctive features</b><br>
of the phylum Ascomycota:<br>
a)	 They have specialised spore producing<br>
structures called ascocarps.<br>
b)	 They reproduce asexually using conidia<br>
formed on the tips of conidiophores; in<br>
some members asexual reproduction is<br>
through budding.<br>
c)	 Some are unicellular heterotrophs,<br>
and lack typical hyphae, for example<br>
Saccharomyces.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   131</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>

132<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Structure of Saccharomyces</b><br>
<b>Saccharomyces are flat, smooth, and</b><br>
moist. They are unicellular, and they lack<br>
hypha. They have eukaryotic organelles<br>
such as mitochondria, ribosomes, Golgi<br>
apparatus, vacuoles and ribosomes which<br>
are found within the cytoplasm (Figure<br>
3.20). The cell walls of Saccharomyces<br>
are elastic, determine the shape of the<br>
cell, and provide osmotic and physical<br>
protection.<br>

<b>Nucleus</b><br>

<b>Vacuole</b><br>
<b>Food reserves such</b><br>
as glucose and oil<br>

to parent cell<br>
Figure 3.20 Structure of Saccharomyces (a) a single cell (b) a budding cell<br>
<b>Adaptations of Saccharomyces to its</b><br>
mode of life<br>
<b>Saccharomyces species have the following</b><br>
adaptive features that enable them suit to<br>
their mode of life:<br>
a)	 They store carbohydrates in the form<br>
of glycogen for use during shortage of<br>
food supply.<br>
b)	 They have permeable cell walls to allow<br>
entry of nutrients that are obtained<br>
from external digestion. Thus, they<br>
can absorb simple monosaccharides<br>
and vitamins directly from their<br>
environment.<br>
c)	 They secrete extracellular enzymes<br>
such as sucrase and cellulase for<br>
digestion<br>
of<br>
carbohydrate,<br>
and<br>
protease for digestion of protein.<br>
d)	 They have high reproductive rate<br>
through budding, to produce new cells<br>
hence increase in number.<br>
e)	 Spores’ ability to remain dormant in<br>
unfavourable conditions ensures their<br>
existence.<br>
f)	 Some Saccharomyces are facultative<br>
anaerobes. They have an ability to<br>
respire anaerobically or aerobically,<br>
which ensures survival in both aerobic<br>
and anaerobic conditions.<br>

dropper, slides, and microscope.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Put some yeast in a beaker contain­<br>
ing small amount of water, then stir<br>
to get a suspension.<br>

<b>Exercise 3.9</b><br>
1.	 In which ways are Ascomycetes<br>
similar to and yet different from<br>
<b>Zygomycetes?</b><br>
2.	 With examples, explain why yeast<br>
(Saccharomyces cerevisiae) is not<br>

<b>Ascomyota, but it is still classiﬁed</b><br>

its mode of life.<br>
4.	 In which ways are Ascomycetes<br>
economically important in our daily<br>
life?<br>
<b>Phylum Basidiomycota</b><br>
<b>This phylum consists of basidiomycetes</b><br>
which contains about 25,000 species.  It<br>
is the most common and widely known<br>
phylum of kingdom fungi. Organisms in<br>
this kingdom include mushrooms, bracket<br>
fungi, puffballs, smuts, rust and toadstools.<br>
<b>Some basidiomycetes are parasites, while</b><br>
others are saprophytes. Bracket fungi cause<br>
damage to plants. For example Puccinia<br>
graminis causes wheat rust; smuts infest<br>
cereals, among other plant species.<br>
<b>Saprophytic basidiomycetes include the</b><br>
edible mushroom Agaricus campestris while<br>
others like Amanita virosa are poisonous.<br>
<b>In addition, symbiotic association called</b><br>
mycorrhizae are common between<br>
basidiomycetes and roots of higher plants.<br>
<b>This phylum got its name due to the</b><br>
presence of a characteristic club shaped<br>
structure known as a basidium (plural<br>
basidia) from which basidiospores are<br>
produced. Basidiospores are characteristic<br>
sexual<br>
reproductive<br>
structures<br>
of<br>
basidiomycetes.<br>
<b>Individual</b><br>
basidia<br>
are fused to form basidiocarp, which<br>
is the most spectacular and familiar<br>
stage of most basidiomycetes such as<br>
mushrooms. Species in this phylum have<br>
septate hyphae with distinct small pores.<br>
<b>Haploid hyphae of basidiomycetes like</b><br>
mushrooms fuse to form a dikaryotic<br>
mycelium called stipe, growing upright<br>
and culminate into an umbrella-like cap<br>
called basidiocarp in which the hyphae<br>
are tightly packed. The hyphae tips are<br>
swollen forming a characteristic club-like<br>
structure (basidia) in which the dikaryotic<br>
nuclei fuse and later meiotically divide to<br>
form four haploid basidiospores. These<br>
are ejected out of the basidia through four<br>
ﬁngerlike projections at the tip of each<br>
basidium. Each spore can germinate to<br>
form new haploid hyphae. Evolutionarily,<br>
basidiomycetes are more closely related to<br>
ascomycetes than to the other phyla. The<br>
basidia function in a similar way as ascus.<br>
<b>General characteristics of phylum</b><br>
<b>Basidiomycota</b><br>
a)	 They have a characteristic cap-like<br>
structure called basidiocarp, which<br>

<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
contains numerous club-like structures<br>
known<br>
as<br>
basidia<br>
from<br>
which<br>
haploid, sexually reproduced spores<br>
(basidiospores) are produced.<br>
b)	 Sexual<br>
reproduction<br>
in<br>
most<br>
basidiomycetes<br>
results<br>
into<br>
the<br>
formation of dikaryotic hyphae<br>
c)	 They have septate hyphae.<br>
d)	 Some basidiomycetes are saprophytes<br>
while others are parasites.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of phylum</b><br>
<b>Basidiomycota</b><br>
<b>Members of phylum Basidiomycota</b><br>
possess some features which differentiate<br>
them from the other phyla. These features<br>
include:<br>
a)	 Basidium formed at the tips of hyphae is<br>
a characteristic sexual spore producing<br>
structure in which union of dikaryotic<br>
nuclei occurs, followed by meiosis to<br>
produce haploid basidiospores.<br>
b)	 Hyphae have septa with distinctive<br>
pores.<br>
<b>Structure of Agaricus</b><br>
<b>Members of Agaricus consist of a stalked</b><br>
fruiting body with pileus on its top (a<br>
fleshy cap), which in turn bears numerous<br>
radially arranged gills on its basement. The<br>
vegetative mushroom body is also called<br>
mycelium. The underground hyphae have<br>
minute threads called rhizoids, as a group<br>
are also known as mycelium threads. They<br>
are located underneath the fruiting body,<br>
and they store and supply the nutrients to<br>
the mushroom. These structures anchor<br>
the mushroom to the substrate, while the<br>
stipe (stem) make it stand upright (Figure<br>
3.21). Cup (volva) is found below the<br>
stem while the ring (annulus) surrounds<br>
the stem.<br>

<b>Ring (annulus)</b><br>
<b>Cup (volva)</b><br>
<b>Gills (lamellae)</b><br>
<b>Mycelium thread</b><br>
(rhizoid)<br>
<b>Stem (stipe)</b><br>

<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
<b>Adaptations of Agaricus to its</b><br>
saprophytic mode of life<br>
<b>Members of the genus Agaricus are</b><br>
adapted to saprophytic mode of life.  The<br>
adaptations of Agaricus to its mode of life<br>
are as follows:<br>
a)	 They have ability to secrete a variety<br>
of hydrolytic enzymes for extracellular<br>
digestion of various organic matters.<br>
b)	 They possess rhizomorphs which are<br>
responsible for absorption of nutrients<br>
from the substratum.<br>
c)	 They have stipe for upward transport<br>
of nutrients to reach cap cells.<br>
d)	 They store carbohydrates in the form<br>
of glycogen for use during shortage of<br>
food supply.<br>
e)	 They are able to grow and survive on<br>
different substrata to increase chances<br>
of survival.<br>
f)	 They produce large numbers of very<br>
tiny and resistant basidiospores which<br>
are easily dispersed and resistant to<br>
harsh conditions.<br>
g)	 They have pileus or cap made up<br>
of closely packed hyphae to confer<br>
protection to the gills.<br>
<b>Activity 3.5 Observation of a mushroom</b><br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>Fresh or preserved mushroom, hand lens</b><br>
or light microscope, petri dish, and a<br>
pair of gloves.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Wear a pair of gloves on your hands<br>
and collect a fresh or preserved<br>
specimen of mushroom and put it in<br>
a petri dish.<br>
b)	 Observe the specimen using a hand<br>
lens or under a low power of the<br>
light microscope.<br>
c)		 Take note of the gills radiating out<br>
from the stalk (stipe) like the spokes<br>
of the wheel.<br>
d)	 Examine part of the gill under high<br>
power and note the presence of tiny<br>
club-shaped basidia lining one side<br>
of the gill. Sometimes, small ﬁnger-<br>
like projections at the tips of basidia<br>
are visible in which black spots<br>
‘basidiospores’ are found.<br>
<b>Questions</b><br>
1.	 Draw and label the structure<br>
under observation.<br>
2.	 Outline the adaptive features of<br>
the specimen to its mode of life.<br>
<b>Safety precaution</b><br>
<b>Some mushroom species are poisonous</b><br>
when consumed, so be careful when<br>
dealing with mushrooms.<br>
<b>Economic importance of Fungi</b><br>
<b>Although some fungal species are</b><br>
pathogenic that may cause disease in plants<br>
and animals. Most fungi are saprophytic<br>
and not pathogenic to plants and animals<br>
are important to human life at many levels.<br>
<b>Fungi play an important role in medical</b><br>
industry, agriculture, research and in the<br>
ecosystem.<br>

<b>Advantages of kingdom Fungi</b><br>
a)	 Some members of the kingdom<br>
<b>Fungi such as Agaricus species are</b><br>
source of food to human being. For<br>
example Agaricus bisporus are the<br>
most important edible mushroom<br>
commercially cultivated world-wide.<br>
<b>They are rich source of nutrients such</b><br>
as proteins, carbohydrates, lipids,<br>
minerals, fibres, and vitamins. They<br>
can be used as nutritional supplements<br>
to humans. They can also be used for<br>
medical therapy: as antimicrobial,<br>
anticancer, and antioxidant roles. Other<br>
animals such as ants and millipedes<br>
feed on fungi.<br>
b)	 Yeasts are used in fermentation to<br>
produce alcohol in brewing industries.<br>
c)	 Fungi are important in production of<br>
organic acids and organic solvents<br>
such as acetic acid, lactic acid, amyl,<br>
isoamyl alcohol, and glycerol.<br>
d)	 Saprophytic fungal communities are<br>
important in the soil as they decompose<br>
dead organic matter and recycle<br>
nutrients locked in dead plants and<br>
animals thereby improving soil fertility.<br>
e)	 Some members of the kingdom<br>
<b>Fungi are used in producing medicine</b><br>
(antibiotics) such as penicillin from<br>
<b>Penicillium sp., and ephedrine extracted</b><br>
from yeast.<br>
f)	 They are sources of important hormones<br>
such as gibberellins obtained from<br>
<b>Gibberella fujikuroi. This hormone</b><br>
regulates vegetative and fruit growth<br>
in plants.<br>
g)	 They are important in cheese industry<br>
and in production of some enzymes<br>
such as amylase.<br>
h)	 They are used in research such as<br>
biochemical genetics; a good example<br>
is Neurospora crassa (ascomycetes)<br>
which is used in many aspects of<br>
cell biology and biochemistry to<br>
elucidate various molecular events<br>
involved in epigenesis, cell fusion and<br>
development.<br>
i)	 Fungi, particularly the genetically<br>
engineered ones, can be used in<br>
bioremediation to degrade some<br>
pollutants<br>
in<br>
the<br>
environment.<br>
<b>They are useful in detoxiﬁcation of</b><br>
poisonous substances such as cyanide<br>
which is removed from cassava by<br>
moulds, such as Rhizopus.<br>
j)	 Saprophytic fungi are used as<br>
scavengers in sewage treatment to<br>
clean the environment. Some fungi<br>
with the ability to digest cellulose are<br>
used in waste papers’ disposal.<br>
k)	 Some fungi are grown for commercial<br>
production in order to extract pigments<br>
which are used in the preparation of<br>
various dye materials.<br>
l)	 Some fungi can be used in biological<br>
control as they obtain their food<br>
by destroying other organisms like<br>
amoeba, rotifers, and nematodes. In<br>
addition, entomogenous fungi are<br>
parasitic on insects and other small<br>
anthropods such as mites and spiders.<br>
m)	They are involved in symbiotic<br>
mycorrhizal association with roots<br>
of vascular plants in which they<br>
increase the plant roots’ surface area<br>
for absorption of nutrients of nutrients,<br>
while, in turn, the fungi get some of<br>
the photosynthesized food from plants.<br>
n)	 They are used in biological studies<br>
as specimens, for instance Rhizopus,<br>
Saccharamyces, and Agaricus.<br>
<b>Disadvantages of kingdom Fungi</b><br>
a)	 Some fungi produce toxins that can<br>
affect plants and animals, for example<br>

<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
some fungi are so poisonous that<br>
when consumed by mammals they can<br>
cause death. An example of poisonous<br>
mushrooms is Amanita.<br>
b)	 Some fungi cause diseases, for example,<br>
ringworms (dermatophytosis or tinea)<br>
are fungal skin infection in animals and<br>
smuts in plants.<br>
c)	 Foods such as grains, tubers, and fruits<br>
can be destroyed by saprophytic fungi<br>
such as Mucor and Rhizopus if not well<br>
stored.<br>
d)	 Saprophytic fungi deteriorate organic<br>
materials such as leather, natural<br>
fabrics, and damp timber, example<br>
mold leather, mildew and wet rot fungi.<br>
e)	 When accidentally consumed in food,<br>
some fungal species affect the nervous<br>
system and may cause hallucinations,<br>
example an invasive fungus called<br>
<b>Cryptococcus may cause a serious</b><br>
inflamation of brain and spinal cord,<br>
the condition known as cryptococcal<br>
meningitis.<br>
f)	 Some fungi such as the Penicillium,<br>
which are used in pharmaceutical and<br>
cheese industries, cause various types<br>
of allergic conditions to some people.<br>

1.	 With examples, explain how<br>
mycology and its application<br>
can be an important step towards<br>
industrial revolution in Tanzania.<br>
2.	 Give reasons to justify the position<br>
of fungi in their kingdom.<br>
3.	 What is the single important factor<br>
considered in classifying fungi<br>
into different phyla? Give an<br>
example for each phylum.<br>
4.	 Explain why the placement of<br>
fungi under the kingdoms Plantae<br>
and Animalia was confusing early<br>
taxonomists.<br>

and basidiomycetes are evolutionarily<br>
related.<br>
<b>3.6   Kingdom Plantae</b><br>
<b>Plants can be broadly deﬁned as</b><br>
multicellular eukaryotic photoautotrophs.<br>
<b>They contain chloroplasts which have</b><br>
chlorophylls 'a' and 'b'. Some plants contain<br>
photosynthetic pigment called carotenoid<br>
which is used for photosynthesis. Their<br>
cells have cell walls made up of cellulose<br>
and have large permanent vacuoles. Plants<br>
store carbohydrates in the form of starch.<br>
<b>They reproduce sexually by production of</b><br>
spores and vary from primitive to advanced<br>
plants. The most primitive plants such as<br>
<b>Bryophytes produce spores which are of</b><br>
the same kind and size (homospores) and<br>
the plants are termed homosporous. On the<br>
other hand the advanced plants, including a<br>
few species of ferns and all seed producing<br>
plants are heterosporous, as they produce<br>
two types of spores: micro and macro spores.<br>
<b>Plants exhibit alternation of generations in</b><br>
which a haploid gamete producing phase<br>
called gametophyte generation alternate<br>
with the diploid spore producing phase<br>
called sporophyte generation. Plants are<br>
sessile, meaning that they have limited<br>
locomotion but show curvature movements.<br>
<b>Their vegetative body is divided into root</b><br>
and shoot systems.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   137</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
138<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Plants are believed to have evolved from</b><br>
an aquatic ancestor which was probably<br>
<b>Ulotricales algae stocks. Life on land started</b><br>
about 0.5 billion years ago when the earliest<br>
plants started to establish their life on land.<br>
<b>The earliest plants were very tiny and were</b><br>
not well differentiated in roots, stem, and<br>
leaves. In addition, they had no vascular<br>
tissues. Water was absorbed to their bodies<br>
by simple diffusion. They produced motile<br>
sperms which restricted their habitats to wet<br>
areas or seasonally wet areas.<br>
<b>Early plants slowly adapted to terrestrial</b><br>
habitat by developing features such as<br>
cuticle to protect them from desiccation,<br>
development of roots and vascular<br>
system for water uptake and movement,<br>
and translocation of food. Furthermore,<br>
in sexual reproduction, fertilisation was<br>
taking place inside the female reproductive<br>
structure called archegonia, within which<br>
zygote development occurs. This was<br>
important to protect the delicate zygote<br>
from desiccation. The most advanced<br>
plants overcome dependence on water in<br>
reproduction by producing special tubes<br>
called pollen tube to carry sperms to the<br>
female reproductive organs for fertilisation.<br>
<b>Plants can reproduce asexually in various</b><br>
ways, including through the use of gemmae<br>
as in bryophytes, and using various other<br>
types of vegetative reproduction such as<br>
fragmentation and cuttings.<br>
<b>Members of the kingdom Plantae are</b><br>
heterogeneous in many features. Among<br>
key aspects used in the classiﬁcation of<br>
plants is alternation of generation. The<br>
features characterising each phase of<br>
generation are: types of  spores, spore<br>
producing features, vascular tissue, ability<br>
to produce seeds, and ﬂower formation.<br>
<b>Among others, these features have led to the</b><br>
classiﬁcation of plants into four divisions,<br>
namely Bryophyta, Filicinophyta (or<br>
<b>Pteridophyta), Coniferophyta (or Pinophyta)</b><br>
and Angiospermophyta.<br>
3.6.1  Division Bryophyta<br>
<b>Bryophytes are the most primitive terrestrial</b><br>
plants. They resemble the most advanced<br>
algae species in certain ways especially their<br>
dependence on water in sexual reproduction<br>
and lack of conducting tissues. Unlike algae,<br>
they form a zygote which is protected in<br>
the female reproductive structure called<br>
archegonium that safeguards the zygote<br>
against physical damage and desiccation.<br>
<b>Most bryophytes have poor vegetative</b><br>
differentiation because they are thallose<br>
(or thalloid). The lack of conducting tissues<br>
(xylem and phloem) in bryophytes, unlike<br>
in other divisions of plants, is a challenge<br>
in terrestrial environments where water is<br>
limited.<br>
<b>However, water can simply be absorbed over</b><br>
their surface as they have various structural<br>
features to ensure the absorption of water<br>
falling on their surface. For instance, their<br>
leaf-like structures are overlapping, and<br>
have small warts on them to delay escaping<br>
water. In addition, they do not grow tall,<br>
hence, water can rise by capillarity, and<br>
they also have rhizoids for anchorage and<br>
easy absorption of water and mineral salts<br>
from the soil surface. Bryophytes include<br>
mosses, hornworts, and liverworts.<br>
<b>Traditionally, bryophytes were classified</b><br>
into two classes, namely Musci and<br>
<b>Hepaticae. Members of class Musci include</b><br>
moss plants (Funaria sp.), while members<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   138</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
139<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
of class Hepaticae include liverworts (Pellia<br>
sp). A recent classiﬁcation splits bryophytes<br>
into three classes, namely Hepaticopsida<br>
(Liverworts), Bryopsida (Mosses), and<br>
Anthoceropsida (Hornworts).<br>
<b>General characteristics of division</b><br>
<b>Bryophyta</b><br>
a)	 They show alternation of generations<br>
in which the haploid gametophyte<br>
generation is dominant over the diploid<br>
sporophyte generation.<br>
b)	 The sporophyte is attached on the<br>
gametophyte generation, and it depends<br>
upon it for support and nutrition.<br>
c)	 The<br>
gametophyte<br>
generation<br>
is<br>
anchored by ﬁlamentous rhizoids<br>
which provide support and used for<br>
absorption of water and mineral salts.<br>
d)	 They lack vascular tissue, meaning<br>
that they have no xylem and phloem.<br>
e)	 They have a thallus body which shows<br>
low level of differentiation; hence,<br>
they lack true leaves, stems, and roots.<br>
f)	 They have a sporophyte generation,<br>
which produces homospores. Upon<br>
landing on a conducive environment,<br>
the spores can germinate to form<br>
the<br>
gametophyte<br>
generation<br>
on<br>
which male and female reproductive<br>
structures develop. They undergo<br>
sexual reproduction which involves<br>
multicellular<br>
sex<br>
organs<br>
called<br>
antheridia (male sex organs) and<br>
archegonia (female sex organs).<br>
g)	 They are found mainly in damp and<br>
shady environment because their sexual<br>
reproduction depends on water, which<br>
facilitates mobility of their sperms.<br>
h)	 Asexual<br>
reproduction<br>
is<br>
by<br>
fragmentation in which a small part<br>
can detach from the mother plant to<br>
form green multicellular reproductive<br>
bodies of different shapes called<br>
gemmae. Gemmae is formed in gemma<br>
cups on the leaf surfaces, stem apex<br>
or inside the cells. Each gemmae can<br>
germinate to form a new gametophyte<br>
upon falling on a suitable substrate.<br>
i)	 They contain photosynthetic pigments<br>
(chlorophyll) as that of higher plants.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of division</b><br>
<b>Bryophyta</b><br>
<b>Bryophytes possess the following features</b><br>
which differentiate them from members<br>
of other divisions:<br>
a)	 They lack conducting tissue such as<br>
xylem and phloem.<br>
b)	 They do not possess true stem,<br>
leaves, and roots. They have rhizoids<br>
instead of roots and they also lack<br>
cuticle; therefore, absorption of water<br>
and mineral salts in species such as<br>
liverworts takes place over the whole<br>
surface of the plant by diffusion.<br>
c)	 They have a dominant gametophyte<br>
generation, on which sporophyte<br>
generation is attached and depends on<br>
gametophyte for food and support.<br>
d)	 They are homosporous and their<br>
haploid<br>
spores<br>
germinate<br>
into<br>
a<br>
characteristic<br>
structure<br>
called<br>
protonema, which are ﬁlament of cells<br>
that later grow to form gametophytes.<br>

10/10/2019   14:06<br>

Structure of Funaria sp.<br>
<b>A  mature Funaria plant is structurally</b><br>
erect, measuring only a few centimetres<br>
above<br>
the<br>
ground.<br>
<b>It</b><br>
is<br>
clearly<br>
differentiated into stem-like and root-like<br>
systems. The stem-like structure bears<br>
leaf-like structures that are arranged<br>
spirally along it. At the base of the “stem”<br>
are tuft of adventitious rhizoids that are<br>
used for anchorage and absorption of<br>
water and mineral salts. The greenish part<br>
of the plant (that is the stem-like structure<br>
and leaf-like structure), and the rhizoids<br>
constitute a gametophyte body (Figure<br>
3.22). The sporophyte has a capsule which<br>
encloses the spores and their sex organs<br>
(antheridia and archegonia) which are<br>
borne at the tips of stem-like structures.<br>
<b>Funaria hygrometrica is the most common</b><br>
moss species.<br>

<b>Figure 3.22 Structure of a Funaria</b><br>
<b>Reproduction of Funaria</b><br>
<b>A mature gametophyte of genus Funaria</b><br>
consists of short stem-like structure in<br>
which antheridia and archegonia are<br>
borne at the tips of the male and female<br>
stems respectively. Sexual reproduction<br>
requires water which is an essential<br>
medium for sperm swimming from the<br>
male gametangium (antheridium) to<br>
female<br>
gametangium<br>
(archegonium).<br>
<b>Normally, when it rains, these small stem-</b><br>
like structures become ﬂooded, saturated,<br>
swollen, and they burst to release sperms<br>
on the surface. The sperms produced have<br>
two ﬂagella (bi-ﬂagellate spermatozoids);<br>
hence, they can swim towards the egg<br>
(oogonium) located in the archegonium.<br>
<b>Fertilisation takes place inside the</b><br>
archegonium to form a diploid zygote.<br>
<b>It keeps growing while still inside the</b><br>
archegonium to form a stalk-like structure<br>
called seta which is a young sporophyte.<br>
<b>This structure later matures and produces</b><br>
capsule on its tip. This means the entire<br>
sporophyte (seta and capsule) grows on<br>
the gametophyte, where it is supported and<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   140</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
141<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
supplied with food. The capsule produces spores which, once released, can germinate<br>
to form another gametophyte. The cycle repeats over again (Figure 3.23).<br>

a)	 Collect fresh moss plants from damp<br>
areas, wet walls, or tree barks.<br>
b)	 Take a single moss plant and put it<br>
on a petri dish.<br>
c)	 Observe the specimen using hand<br>
lens or light microscope. Note the<br>
greenish part and a small stalk<br>
growing on it; bearing a small club-<br>
like structure at the top.<br>

of Figure 3.22.<br>

<b>Adaptations of Funaria to its mode of</b><br>
life<br>
<b>Members of Funaria have the following</b><br>
adaptive features:<br>
a)	 They have chlorophyllous “leaf-like<br>
structures” for photosynthesis.<br>
b)	 They have limited heights to overcome<br>
problems associated with lack of<br>
vascular tissues. Water and mineral<br>
salts can move up by capillarity in<br>
their short stems.<br>
c)	 They possess rhizoids for anchorage<br>
on soil as well as absorption of water<br>
and mineral salts.<br>
d)	 Male<br>
gametes,<br>
antherozoids,<br>
are<br>
biﬂagellate<br>
for<br>
swimming<br>
into<br>
archegonia.<br>
e)	 They produce small and light spores<br>
that are easily dispersed to allow<br>
colonization of new areas.<br>
f)	 The spores are tolerant to long<br>
periods of unfavorable conditions<br>
due to the presence of a thick wall<br>
with sporopollen in one of the major<br>
chemical component (polymers) on<br>
the outer wall of the spores.<br>
g)	 They have elongated seta to expose<br>
the capsule to air for easy dispersal of<br>
spores.<br>
h)	 Archegonia<br>
secrete<br>
chemical<br>
attractants which attract antherozoids<br>
to swim towards the egg during<br>
fertilisation.<br>

1.	 Brieﬂy explain how bryophytes<br>
have<br>
managed<br>
to<br>
overcome<br>
various challenges in terrestrial<br>
habitats.<br>

why<br>

are<br>

division Bryophyta.<br>
3.6.2 	 Division Filicinophyta<br>
(Pteridophyta)<br>
<b>Members of division Filicinophyta</b><br>
are called   pteridophytes. Examples of<br>
pteridophytes include ferns. Pteridophytes<br>
inhabit damp shady environments, such<br>
as on the ﬂoor of moist forests and river<br>
banks. About 1100 species of ferns are<br>
currently identiﬁed. Ferns are related to<br>
bryophytes in several ways, indicating<br>
that they might have probably descended<br>
from bryophytes. For example sexual<br>
reproduction in pteridophytes depends on<br>
water like in bryophytes. Moreover, like<br>
bryophytes, the pteridophytes’ zygotes are<br>
retained and develop inside the multicellular<br>
archegonium. However, unlike bryophytes,<br>
pteridophytes exhibit alternation of<br>
generation in which the sporophyte<br>
generation is dominant over gametophyte<br>
generation which is relatively reduced and<br>
dependent on sporophyte. Ferns are also<br>
heterogenous in size and shape, ranging<br>
from ﬁliform ferns to tree like ferns which<br>
can grow to approximately 20 metres tall<br>
and with broad leaves known as fronds. A<br>
fern tree called Cyathea manniana is very<br>
common in most parts of the eastern arch<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   142</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
143<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
mountain forests of Tanzania such as the<br>
<b>Udzungwa scarp forest reserve. This fern</b><br>
can grow to a height of about 5 metres.<br>
<b>Ferns have conducting tissues (xylem and</b><br>
phloem) which are not well developed and<br>
are sometimes termed as tracheophytes.<br>
<b>While most pteridophytes are homosporous,</b><br>
some of the fern species produce two types<br>
of spores (heterosporous). Besides the<br>
pteridophytes, which are regarded as true<br>
ferns, other plant species called fern allies<br>
are not true ferns, though they relatively<br>
resemble pteridophytes in various ways. For<br>
example they disperse by shedding spores<br>
to initiate an alternation of generation. An<br>
example of a common fern is Dryopteris<br>
filix-mas, found in damp woods and other<br>
shady places.<br>
Structure of Dryopteris sp.<br>
<b>A mature fern plant has an underground</b><br>
creeping stem called rhizome. This bears<br>
adventitious true roots for anchorage and<br>
absorption of water and mineral salts from<br>
the soil. It has broad leaves called fronds<br>
attached to the rhizome by a long stalk<br>
or petiole. Characteristically, the young<br>
leaves show a tightly rolled structure<br>
called circinate leaves or croziers which<br>
later unroll to reveal fronds with a stalk<br>
or petiole at the base. The bases of the<br>
fronds are covered with dry brown scales<br>
called ramenta for protection of young<br>
leaves against drought. The frond has a<br>
midrib called rachis, which bear leaﬂets<br>
called pinnae on both sides and small<br>
rounded sub-divisions of pinnae called<br>
pinnules (Figure 3.24).<br>
<b>The mature frond bears specialised</b><br>
reproductive structures called sori (clusters<br>
of sporangia) on the lower surface of the<br>
frond. The sporangia contain spores;<br>
therefore, sporangia bearing leaves are<br>
called sporophyll (sporo meaning spore<br>
and phyll meaning leaves). The Dryopteris<br>
species have poorly developed vascular<br>
tissues with simple xylem elements for<br>
the mechanical support and transport of<br>
water and mineral salts, while phloem<br>
elements are mainly for the translocation<br>
of synthesized food.<br>

<b>Reproduction in Dryopteris</b><br>
<b>The diploid sporophyte generation have</b><br>
a cluster of sporangia (sori) in which<br>
the spore mother cell undergoes meiotic<br>
division to produce haploid spores. When<br>
the sporangia mature they break and release<br>
the spores. In favorable conditions, the<br>
spores germinate into a small green heart-<br>
shaped structure called prothallus forming<br>
a haploid gametophyte generation, bearing<br>
male and female reproductive structures<br>
(antheridia and archegonia), and rhizoids.<br>
<b>The archegonia produce female gametes</b><br>
while the antheridia produce ﬂagellated<br>
male gametes which swim to the archegonia<br>
in the presence of water to fertilise the egg,<br>
forming a zygote. The zygote develops<br>
to form a sporophyte generation with a<br>
horizontal stem (rhizome) and leaves. The<br>
gametophytes shrink and degenerate (Figure<br>
3.25).<br>

<b>Adaptations of Dryopteris to its mode of</b><br>
life<br>
<b>Dryopteris possess the following features</b><br>
which enable them to adapt to their<br>
environment:<br>
a)	They have chloroplasts containing<br>
chlorophyll for capturing light energy<br>
needed for photosynthesis.<br>
b)	They have roots for anchorage and<br>
absorption of water and mineral salts.<br>
c)	They have stomata which facilitate<br>
gaseous exchange.<br>
d)	They have xylem responsible for<br>
transportation of water and dissolved<br>
minerals and also they have phloem for<br>
translocation of manufactured food.<br>
e)	Rhizomes play part in storing food<br>
and propagating new plants, and can<br>
remain viable in the soil for a long time<br>
to ensure survival.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   144</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
145<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
f)	 They have a well-developed and<br>
independent sporophyte generation,<br>
since the gametophyte withers and<br>
dies as the young leaves of sporophyte<br>
grow.<br>
g)	They have cuticle in their leaves to<br>
prevent excessive water loss.<br>
h)	Archegonia secrete chemical which<br>
attracts antherozoids to swim towards<br>
the egg during fertilization.<br>
<b>Activity 3.7 Observation of fern plants</b><br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>Mature fern plant (Dryopteris sp.) and</b><br>
hand lens or light microscope.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	Collect<br>
a<br>
mature<br>
fern<br>
plant<br>
(Dryopteris sp.) from a damp<br>
shaded area such as in the forest,<br>
ﬂoor or water canal or river bank.<br>
b)	Uproot the plant with a small part<br>
of the rhizome and roots.<br>
c)	Carefully examine the collected<br>
fern plant. Take note of the fronds<br>
(leaves) arising from the horizontal<br>
stem (rhizome).<br>
d)	Turn the frond and observe its lower<br>
side with the aid of a hand lens or a<br>
light microscope. Note small dark<br>
or brown patches on the lower side<br>
of mature fronds. Each discrete<br>
patch is called sorus.<br>

and rhizoids.<br>

appropriate measures he should take<br>
to rescue his fern plants, that appear to<br>
have a lot of dust brown spots on the<br>
lower side of the leaves, which were<br>
not there when they were young. Based<br>
on your knowledge of ferns, advice him<br>
accordingly.<br>
2.	Give reasons as to why ferns are<br>
considered to have evolved from<br>

to<br>

than<br>
bryophytes?<br>
3.6.3  Division Coniferophyta<br>
<b>Coniferophyta is a division of kingdom</b><br>
<b>Plantae which belongs to a broad group of</b><br>
non-ﬂowering seed bearing plants, referred<br>
to as gymnosperms. The word gymnosperms<br>
originated from a combination of two Greek<br>
words Gymno meaning ‘naked’ and sperma<br>
meaning ‘seed.’ Theophrastus was the ﬁrst<br>
person to use this term in his book “Enquiry<br>
into plants” referring to plants producing<br>
naked seeds. Under gymnosperms, there<br>
are four groups namely:<br>
a)	 Conifers; the cone bearing plants such<br>
as cypresses; example: Cupressus<br>
species (Figure 3.26), pines; example:<br>
<b>Pinus sylvestris, and Spruce; Picea</b><br>
species, which are the most abundant<br>
Gymnospermous species.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   145</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
146<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
b)	 Cycads, the palm like plants.<br>
c)	 Gingkos, exampliﬁed by only one<br>
extant species, the Gingko biloba,<br>
which is regarded as a living fossil,<br>
because the majority of species in this<br>
division are extinct.<br>
d)	 Gnetos such as Welwitschia sp.<br>
restricted to deserts in Namibia and<br>
Angola.<br>
<b>Figure 3.26 A photo of cypress tree among the</b><br>
popular Christmas tree<br>
<b>Source: TAFORI, 2019</b><br>
<b>Species belonging to this division are</b><br>
evolutionarily, more advanced than<br>
pteridophytes. They have more advanced<br>
vascular tissues, and they do not require<br>
water for fertilization. The presence of<br>
a highly reduced gametophyte and seed<br>
formation also makes them evolutionarily<br>
more advanced than ferns. Coniferophytes<br>
are very abundant in the cold and moist<br>
regions such as the southern highlands and<br>
along the slopes of Mount Kilimanjaro in<br>
<b>Tanzania. Globally, coniferophytes are</b><br>
common in the North-western part of the<br>
<b>United States, the northern hemisphere,</b><br>
and China.<br>
<b>Characteristics of division</b><br>
<b>Coniferophyta</b><br>
a)	 They are non-ﬂowering, seed bearing<br>
plants, producing naked seeds which<br>
are not enclosed in ovaries or fruit<br>
tissues.<br>
b)	 Sexual<br>
reproduction<br>
involves<br>
microspores (male gametophyte) and<br>
megaspores (female gametophyte)<br>
which are found in male and female<br>
cones or strobili respectively.<br>
c)	 Fertilisation does not require water;<br>
instead, they develop pollen tubes<br>
which carry sperms to the ovule for<br>
fertilisation.<br>
d)	 They have poor xylem with only<br>
tracheids as conducting elements but<br>
no vessel elements. This is the reason<br>
why most coniferophytes produce soft<br>
wood.<br>
e)	 Their phloem tissues lack companion<br>
cells; instead, they are associated with<br>
albuminous cells.<br>
f)	 Leaves are reduced into spiny or<br>
needle-like leaves to minimize water<br>
loss through transpiration. Exceptions<br>
are Gingkos and Cycads.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of division</b><br>
<b>Coniferophyta</b><br>
<b>Presence of the following features in</b><br>
members of division Coniferophyta<br>
differentiate them from members of other<br>
divisions:<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   146</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
147<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
a)	 The plants bear cones from which<br>
microspores<br>
(pollen<br>
grains)<br>
and<br>
megaspores (ovules) are produced.<br>
b)	 The pollen grains are winged to<br>
provide buoyance, hence they are<br>
wind pollinated.<br>
c)	 They have seeds which are not<br>
enclosed within the ovary; thus, no<br>
ovaries, and no formation of fruits.<br>
d)	 Their xylem lacks vessel elements but<br>
has only tracheids as the conducting<br>
elements.<br>
e)	 Their phloem tissues are associated<br>
with albuminous cells instead of<br>
companion cells.<br>
f)	 The majority produce resin in special<br>
ducts called resin canals. Such resin is<br>
useful in wound healing and deterring<br>
browsers.<br>
g)	 Most are evergreen plants with needle-<br>
like shaped leaves.<br>
<b>Structure of Pinus</b><br>
<b>The sporophyte consists of roots, stem and</b><br>
branches, bearing needle-shaped leaves<br>
which appear in clusters of two to ﬁve.<br>
<b>These clusters are called fascicle. Their</b><br>
leaves are dark green with cuticle, sunken<br>
stomata, and resin canals in the mesophyll.<br>
<b>Their stems develop relatively wider</b><br>
annual rings of the xylem. The constituent<br>
tissues of these species have resin canals<br>
which run vertically and laterally along<br>
the stem. The bark, has secondary phloem,<br>
which is relatively thick and does not have<br>
companion cells, but albuminous cells that<br>
perform the same function as the companion<br>
cells.<br>
<b>The trunk has a tap root system with</b><br>
lateral roots in different directions. Roots<br>
of some Pinus form symbiotic association<br>
(mycorrhizae) with fungi. Young shoots<br>
have two types of leaves; small scaly<br>
leaves on the main stem and leaves on the<br>
dwarf shoots. In addition, the plant has<br>
foliar spars which develop into normal<br>
branches. The fertile plant bears female<br>
and male reproductive cones which are<br>
distinct when mature. The male cones<br>
are relatively soft or herbaceous and<br>
small while female cones are woody and<br>
relatively larger. Usually, male cones are<br>
borne on the lower branches while female<br>
cones are borne on the upper branches<br>
(Figure 3.27(b)). The leaves enclosing the<br>
cones are called sporophylls. For instance,<br>
microsporophylls are associated with<br>
male cones, whereas megasporophylls are<br>
associated with female cones.<br>
<b>Female cone</b><br>
<b>Seed</b><br>
<b>Female cone</b><br>
<b>Male cone</b><br>
(a)<br>
(b)<br>
Figure 3.27 General structure of (a) female cone and seeds and (b) Pinus plant<br>

10/10/2019   14:06<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
148<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Reproduction of Pinus</b><br>
<b>The sporophyte generation is dominant,</b><br>
more pronounced, and varies in form<br>
or habit. They include shrubs and trees,<br>
consisting of roots, stems and leaves-<br>
bearing<br>
branches.<br>
<b>The</b><br>
gametophyte<br>
is highly reduced (only a few cells).<br>
<b>They</b><br>
are<br>
heterosporous,<br>
producing<br>
microspores which are pollen grains<br>
(male gametophyte) and megaspores<br>
(female gametophyte). Male cones bear<br>
microsporangium with microsporocyte<br>
which produce microspores by meiotic<br>
division, which give rise to light winged<br>
pollen grains (microspores). The nucleus<br>
within the pollen grain divides mitotically<br>
to form a pollen tube nucleus and a<br>
generative nucleus which later divides by<br>
mitosis to form two sperm nuclei. These<br>
pollen grains are transferred to mature<br>
female cones, mainly through the process<br>
called pollination.<br>
<b>Once the pollen grain has landed on the</b><br>
female cones, the pollen tube is formed<br>
and grows, carrying two male sperms to<br>
the archegonium and penetrate the ovule<br>
via a small hole called micropyle. In female<br>
cones inside the ovule, the megasporocyte<br>
produces four haploid nuclei through<br>
meiotic division. One of these cells develops<br>
into megaspore. The nucleus of megaspore<br>
undergoes mitotic division to form a small<br>
female gametophyte where by two or three<br>
archegonia are present; each with one egg<br>
cell. During fertilisation one of the sperms<br>
fertilises the egg to form a zygote while the<br>
other sperm degenerates. The zygote later<br>
develops to form a seed embryo which is<br>
a sporophyte in resting condition (Figure<br>
3.28).<br>
<b>Sporophyte</b><br>
<b>Ovule</b><br>
<b>Seed cone scale</b><br>
<b>Megasporangium</b><br>
<b>Megasporocyte</b><br>
<b>Integument</b><br>
<b>Meiosis</b><br>
<b>Microsporangium</b><br>
<b>Microsporocyte</b><br>
<b>Megagametophyte</b><br>

<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
<b>Adaptations of Pinus to its mode of life</b><br>
<b>Pinus are able to adapt to their environment</b><br>
due to presence of the following features:<br>
a)	 The root and shoot systems are<br>
well developed to provide the plant<br>
with a good contact to the soil and<br>
atmosphere.<br>
b)	 They have roots for absorption of<br>
water and nutrients from the soil.<br>
c)	 The plant has mechanical tissues<br>
for support and vascular tissues for<br>
transportation of water and food.<br>
d)	 They have an elaborate mechanism<br>
for reducing water loss through<br>
transpiration. This becomes possible<br>
due to presence of thick cuticle,<br>
needle-like leaves to reduce their<br>
surface area and a reduced number of<br>
stomata pores. Additionally, their bark<br>
is coated with waxy material called<br>
suberin to reduce water loss.<br>
e)	 They produce lighter pollen grains,<br>
each with two wing-like structures<br>
which make them to float in air,<br>
hence easily to be carried by wind for<br>
pollination.<br>
f)	 They can reproduce sexually without<br>
necessity of using water, because the<br>
transfer of male gametes to female<br>
gametes is through pollen tube, which<br>
ensures reproduction in terrestrial<br>
environment where water is limited.<br>
g)	 The seeds are winged; hence, they can<br>
be easily dispersed by wind.<br>
<b>Activity 3.8 Observation of Pinus</b><br>
sporophyte and reproductive<br>
structures<br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>Branches of pine (Pinus) plants with</b><br>
male and female cones, hand lens or<br>
microscope.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 You are provided with branches of the<br>
<b>Pinus species, with male and female</b><br>
cones. Examine the mature female<br>
cones which are relatively woody.<br>
<b>At the base of each cone are two</b><br>
winged seeds; however, they might<br>
sometimes be missing, because they<br>
fall off when mature, especially if<br>
the cones are disturbed by wind or<br>
any other mechanical force. If seeds<br>
are missing, two small depressions,<br>
showing the mark of where the seeds<br>
were attached should be seen.<br>
b)	 Carefully examine the male cones<br>
which are relatively small and much<br>
herbaceous compared to the woody<br>
female cones. Normally, male cones<br>
are borne in clusters at the axils of<br>
lower branches. Take some pollen<br>
grains from the male cones and<br>
mount a few in a drop of water on a<br>
slide for examination under the light<br>
microscope to observe the wings on<br>
each.<br>
<b>Questions</b><br>
1.	 Draw a diagram of the male and<br>
female cones.<br>
2.	 Draw a diagram of the pine pollen<br>
grain and show the wings.<br>

3.6.4  Division Angiospermophyta<br>
<b>Angiospermophytes are ﬂowering plants</b><br>
which are the most advanced and adapted<br>
to terrestrial life. The word angiosperm<br>
is a combination of two Greek words;<br>
<b>Angeion which means ‘vessels’ or ‘carpels’</b><br>
and sperma which means ‘seeds.’ They<br>
produce seeds enclosed in the ovary, which<br>
later matures to become a fruit. Thus, their<br>
fruit is a mature ovary and the seeds in it<br>
are fertilized ovules. Angiosperms resemble<br>
coniferophyta in many aspects such as the<br>
ability to produce seeds. However, the<br>
presence of ﬂowers and seeds enclosed in<br>
the fruit makes them distinct. Flowers and<br>
fruits have contributed signiﬁcantly to the<br>
success of angiosperms in their ecosystems.<br>
<b>Flowers are diverse in colour, aroma and</b><br>
morphology. These have made it easy to<br>
attract different pollinators, instead of<br>
relying solely on the wind. The ability to<br>
produce seeds enclosed in the fruit is also<br>
an adaptation to seeds’ dispersal by animals<br>
which feed on such fruits.<br>
<b>The number of angiosperm species is over</b><br>
257,000, existing in various sizes, shapes<br>
and forms, such as grasses, herbs, shrubs,<br>
vines, liana,  suffrutescents and trees. Some<br>
of these plant species such as Cassytha<br>
ﬁliformis are parasitic, hence they rely on<br>
other plants. Others grow as epiphytes on<br>
other plants without causing any harm.<br>
<b>Angiosperms are the dominant plants in all</b><br>
habitats, ranging from terrestrial to aquatic<br>
environments, where plants such as water<br>
lilies and sea grasses are abundant. Based on<br>
duration, flowering plants are classified as<br>
annual, biannual or perennial. Annual plants<br>
are normally herbs and grasses that live only<br>
in one season. Biannual plant live for two<br>
seasons growing vegetatively in the first<br>
season and flowering in the second season.<br>
<b>Perennial plants live for three or more</b><br>
seasons. Example of perennial plants<br>
are trees. The angiosperm sporophyte<br>
is dominant with gametophytes entirely<br>
conﬁned in it. The sporophyte consists of<br>
roots, the stem, branches, and leaves. The<br>
gametophyte generation is represented only<br>
by ﬂowers in which the male and female<br>
gametophytes are conﬁned. They have well<br>
developed vascular tissues with xylem<br>
containing tracheids and vessels which<br>
make the angiosperm wood harder than<br>
those of coniferophytes. The phloem on<br>
the other hand contains companion cells<br>
and sieve tubes.<br>
<b>Angiosperms are heterosporous. They</b><br>
produce microspores (male gametophyte)<br>
and megaspores (female gametophyte).<br>
<b>Actually, the male gametophyte is the</b><br>
germinated pollen grain which is highly<br>
reduced into three nuclei, namely two male<br>
nuclei and a pollen tube nucleus. Whereas,<br>
the female gametophyte is found in the<br>
ovule’s embryo sac; the ovary is reduced into<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   150</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
151<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
eight nuclei, namely three antipodal nuclei,<br>
two polar nuclei, and an egg contained<br>
between two nuclei called synergids (Figure<br>
3.29). Following pollination, the pollen<br>
grain on the stigma develops a pollen  tube<br>
carrying two sperm nuclei which grow<br>
down to the embryo sac via micropyle. One<br>
of the sperms fertilizes a haploid egg to form<br>
a zygote which later develops to form an<br>
embryo. The second sperm fertilizes the two<br>
polar nuclei forming triploid nutritive tissue<br>
called endosperm. This process is called<br>
double fertilization, because fertilization<br>
occurs twice. A fertilized ovule becomes a<br>
seed surrounded by two integuments (seed<br>
coat) and the ovary develops into a fruit.<br>

<b>Classes of division Angiospermophyta</b><br>
<b>Old system of classification recognised</b><br>
two classes based on the number<br>
of<br>
cotyledons;<br>
<b>Monocotyledon</b><br>
and<br>
Dicotyledon.<br>
<b>Class Monocotyledoneae, such as maize,</b><br>
grass, millet, and sugar cane plants. Class<br>
<b>Dicotyledoneae, such as bean plant,</b><br>
mango, and orange plants.<br>
<b>A recent estimate of the number of</b><br>
ﬂowering plant species globally is<br>
approximately 400,000, distributed into<br>
about 393 families. When partitioned<br>
into their old two broad classes, a larger<br>
number of species are dicots, represented<br>
by 336 families. In contrast, monocots are<br>
represented by about 57 families.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of class</b><br>
<b>Monocotyledoneae</b><br>
<b>The following features possessed by</b><br>
members of the class Monocotyledoneae<br>
(Figure 3.30) differentiate them from<br>
those of class Dicotyledoneae.<br>
a)	 Leaf venation is parallel in monocots<br>
and the leaf blade or lamina is elongated<br>
with dorsal and ventral surfaces more<br>
or less identical.<br>
b)	 The stem anatomy shows vascular<br>
bundles scattered in the ground tissue<br>
and closed as they lack vascular<br>
cambium, hence no secondary growth.<br>
c)	 They have a ﬁbrous root system which<br>
arises at the base of the stem replacing<br>
the primary root.<br>
d)	 The monocot seed’s embryo bears one<br>
cotyledon.<br>
e)	 Flower parts are usually trimerous (in<br>
threes or multiple of threes).<br>
f)	 Pollen grains in the monocots mostly<br>
have one aperture.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   151</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
152<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Distinctive features of class</b><br>
<b>Dicotyledoneae</b><br>
<b>Members of the class Dicotyledoneae</b><br>
(Figure 3.31) differ from those of class<br>
<b>Monocotyledoneae due to presence of the</b><br>
following features:<br>
a)	 Dicot leaves have net like or reticulate<br>
venation with the dorsal and ventral<br>
surfaces of their leaf blade being<br>
distinct.<br>
b)	 Their stems have vascular bundles<br>
which appear in a ring form. The<br>
vascular bundles are open, that is, they<br>
have a strip of cambium which gives<br>
rise to secondary growth.<br>
c)	 The primary root system is a persistent<br>
and becomes thick tap root which<br>
develops lateral roots.<br>
d)	 Their seed embryo has two cotyledons.<br>
e)	 Floral parts are normally tetramerous<br>
or pentamerous (in four or ﬁve or<br>
multiples of four or ﬁve).<br>
f)	 Pollen grains in dicots mostly have<br>
three apertures.<br>

<b>Activity 3.9 Observation of cross</b><br>
section of the stem and morphology of<br>
monocot and dicot plants<br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>Monocot plant (grass or maize), dicot</b><br>
plant (beans or Hibiscus) plants with<br>
their flowers intact, light microscope,<br>
surgical or razor blade, and slides.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Collect some monocot and dicot<br>
plants with their ﬂowers from the<br>
school environment. Make sure that<br>
you uproot the entire plant carefully<br>
in order to display their root system<br>
clearly.<br>
b)	 Observe the morphological structure<br>
of the two classes of plants collected.<br>
c)	 Make a thin cross section of the stem<br>
of monocot and dicot plants. Place<br>
them on slides and observe one at a<br>
time using light microscope.<br>
<b>Questions</b><br>
1.	 Summarise in a tabular form<br>
the<br>
morphological<br>
differences<br>
between the two classes of the<br>
collected plants.<br>
2.	 Draw and label each of the two<br>
plants to display their root system,<br>
stem, leaf venation and ﬂower(s).<br>
3.	 Draw diagrams of the observed<br>
stem sections, indicating the<br>
vascular bundles.<br>
4.	 What are the differences between<br>
the stem sections observed in (c)?<br>
<b>Safety precautions</b><br>
1.	 Handle with care sharp objects like<br>
surgical or razor blade to avoid<br>
cutting your fingers or hands.<br>
2.	 Be aware of some dangerous<br>
animals such as piercing insects<br>
and<br>
snakes<br>
while<br>
collecting<br>
monocot and dicot plants.<br>

<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>

<b>Structure of the ﬂower</b><br>
<b>A ﬂower is interpreted as a modiﬁed leaf</b><br>
of a plant which is highly specialised<br>
for reproduction (Figure 3.32). It can be<br>
bisexual or unisexual, depending on a plant<br>
species. Bisexual and unisexual ﬂowers<br>
are called complete and incomplete<br>
ﬂowers respectively. Flowers have the<br>
following major parts:<br>
a)	 Receptacle<br>
<b>This is a swollen tip of a pedicel on which</b><br>
all ﬂoral parts are borne or attached. It has<br>
limited growth, which ceases as the last<br>
ﬂoral part is formed. The receptacle varies<br>
in shapes between members of one species<br>
and another. The ﬂoral parts are arranged<br>
in whorls around the receptacle, as rings of<br>
calyx, corolla, androecium and gynoecium.<br>
<b>When all these whorls are present, the</b><br>
ﬂower is termed as a perfect or complete<br>
flower, example, and flowers of cherries,<br>
roses, and orchids. In contrast, when one<br>
of the four whorls is missing, the flower<br>
is termed as incomplete flower. Examples<br>
include; flowers of sweet corn and most<br>
grasses. The ﬂoral parts are inserted in the<br>
following order from the base to the apex of<br>
receptacle; the ﬁrst whorl is for the calyx,<br>
the second whorl is for corolla, the third<br>
whorl is for androecium, and the fourth<br>
whorl is for gynoecium.<br>
b)	 Sepals<br>
<b>These are the lowermost or outermost</b><br>
structure, which are usually green, leaf-<br>
like in structure, protecting the developing<br>
ﬂower whilst the ﬂower is still a bud. As the<br>
inner parts of the ﬂower grow and expand,<br>
sepals are pushed outwards. In some<br>
instances, they may become expanded<br>
and brightly more coloured, like petals.<br>
<b>Examples include the flowers of Aloe and</b><br>
<b>Tulipa genera. They may be separate or</b><br>
fused into a single outer sheath collectively<br>
called calyx.<br>
c)	 Petals<br>
<b>These are normally located inside and</b><br>
above the sepals, are brightly coloured<br>
and expand to form the most conspicuous<br>
part of the ﬂower. As such, they serve to<br>
attract pollinators such as insects and birds,<br>
which are essential for accomplishing the<br>
reproductive process. The petals may<br>
be separate or united in a single tube or<br>
united in several groups. The collective<br>
term for petals is corolla. In some ﬂowers,<br>
both calyx and corolla are united to give a<br>
structure termed as “Perianth.”<br>
d)	 Androecium<br>
<b>This is a collective term for the male part</b><br>
of the ﬂower, and includes anther and<br>
ﬁlament. Anthers are usually bright orange<br>
or yellow in colour. The anther and ﬁlament<br>
together is termed stamen. The transverse<br>
section of the anther shows that it is made<br>
up of  2-4 pollen sacs containing pollen<br>
grains or microspores. The whole stamen<br>
can be regarded as microsporophyll bearing<br>
microsporangia. The fertile microsporangia<br>
are born as pollen sacs at the distal end<br>
of the ﬁlament. When mature, the pollen<br>
sac ruptures to release pollen grains<br>
(microspores or male gametophyte) which<br>
develop into male gametes.<br>
e)	 Gynoecium<br>
<b>This refers to the female reproductive</b><br>
parts of the ﬂower which consists of the<br>
stigma, style, and ovary. These three parts<br>
are collectively called pistil or carpel.<br>
<b>Gynoecium shapes vary in different</b><br>
species as some have a single carpel or a<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   154</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
155<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
number of separate carpels, or a number<br>
of carpels fused in a variety of ways. The<br>
carpel is either single or united in a closed<br>
hollow container housing (ovary), with<br>
one or more ovules. The ovules which are<br>
enclosed within the ovary bear a peculiar<br>
characteristic of angiosperms. They<br>
contain an embryo sac in which the female<br>
gametophyte is found. The distal end of the<br>
ovary bears a prolonged structure known as<br>
style, which terminates in a sticky surface<br>
called stigma.<br>
<b>Stigma</b><br>
<b>Style</b><br>
<b>Carpel</b><br>
<b>Ovary</b><br>
<b>Receptacle</b><br>
<b>Pedicel</b><br>
<b>Sepal</b><br>
<b>Androecium</b><br>
<b>Petal</b><br>
<b>Filament</b><br>
<b>Anther</b><br>
Figure 3.32 A generalized longitudinal section of an angiosperm flower<br>
<b>Flowers have the basic parts mentioned</b><br>
above, but morphological variations<br>
are common across plant species. These<br>
variations are based on the following<br>
attributes:<br>
a)	 The manner of insertion of the parts.<br>
b)	 The number of ﬂoral parts: Some<br>
ﬂowers have all four whorls while<br>
others have less than four whorls.<br>
c)	 The freedom or fusion of ﬂoral parts:<br>
<b>Some parts in some ﬂowers may be</b><br>
fused while other parts may not.<br>
d)	 The relative position of the ﬂoral parts<br>
on the receptacle; may be above or<br>
below the receptacle.<br>
e)	 The symmetry of the ﬂower: some<br>
ﬂowers<br>
have<br>
radial<br>
or<br>
bilateral<br>
symmetry<br>
while<br>
others<br>
are<br>
asymmetrical.<br>
f)	 The distribution of the sexual parts<br>
within the ﬂower: Some ﬂowers have<br>
both gynoecium and androecium parts,<br>
while others have only one part.<br>
<b>Position of floral whorls at the receptacle</b><br>
<b>As pointed out earlier, the position of ﬂoral</b><br>
parts at the receptacle varies across the<br>
species. Based on the arrangement of the<br>
ﬂoral leaves at the receptacle in relation<br>
to the position of the ovary, three types of<br>
ﬂowers are recognised:<br>
a)	 Hypogynous flower<br>
<b>In this ﬂower, the calyx, corolla, and</b><br>
androecium arise from the lower position<br>
of the ovary. This means that the calyx,<br>
corolla and androecium are inserted below<br>
the ovary that is the ovary is positioned<br>
above them. The ovary is therefore said<br>
to be superior as it occupies the highest<br>
position in the receptacle (Figure 3.33).<br>
<b>Examples of hypogynous ﬂowers include</b><br>
Hibiscus, tulip and tomato ﬂowers.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   155</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
156<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Petal</b><br>
<b>Ovary</b><br>
<b>Anther</b><br>
<b>Sepal</b><br>
<b>Thalamus</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.33 Structure of a hypogynous flower</b><br>
b)	 Perigynous flower<br>
<b>In this type of a ﬂower, the margins of</b><br>
the thalamus grow upwards to form a cup<br>
shaped structure called the calyx tube that<br>
encloses the ovary. However, it remains<br>
free from it, carries with it sepals and<br>
stamens, and the ovary is said to be half<br>
inferior. Examples include cherry and rose<br>
flowers. The term “perigynous” emanates<br>
from the nature in which other ﬂoral parts<br>
appear to enclose the ovary (Figure 3.34).<br>
<b>Petal</b><br>
<b>Ovary</b><br>
<b>Anther</b><br>
<b>Sepal</b><br>
<b>Thalamus</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.34 Structure of a perigynous flower</b><br>
c)	 Epigynous flower<br>
<b>In this ﬂower, the calyx, corolla, and</b><br>
androecium arise above the ovary and<br>
completely enclose it. The ovary is therefore<br>
said to be inferior, while the rest of the<br>
ﬂoral parts are said to be superior (Figure<br>
3.35). Examples of epigynous ﬂowers<br>
include the cucumber, apple, guava, and<br>
passion ﬂowers.<br>
<b>Ovary</b><br>
<b>Anther</b><br>
<b>Petal</b><br>
<b>Sepal</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.35 Structure of an</b><br>
epigynous flower<br>
