<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
<b>Flower symmetry</b><br>
<b>A ﬂower can be dissected longitudinally</b><br>
to expose its internal parts for clear<br>
examination. One of the fundamental<br>
aspects of a ﬂower plan is its overall<br>
shape which is described as symmetry.<br>
<b>A symmetrical ﬂower is the one that</b><br>
can be divided along at least one plane<br>
in relation to perianth into two or more<br>
identical parts which are mirror image of<br>
each other. Although most ﬂowers have<br>
a certain kind of symmetry, few ﬂowers<br>
have no symmetry and are referred to as<br>
asymmetrical ﬂowers. Three categories of<br>
ﬂowers’ symmetry are therefore recognized<br>
namely, radial symmetry or actinomorphic<br>
flower, bilateral symmetry or zygomorphic<br>
flower and asymmetric flower.<br>
a)	 Radial symmetry<br>
<b>A ﬂower is said to have a radial symmetry</b><br>
when it can be split vertically through the<br>
centre in any one of several planes to produce<br>
identical parts which are mirror images of<br>
each other. This type of symmetry applies<br>
when each part of the whorl is identical<br>
with all other parts. Normally each side<br>
can have one sepal and one petal. Radially<br>
symmetrical flowers are said to be regular<br>
or polysymetric or actinomorphic (Figure<br>
3.36). Literally, actinomorphic means star<br>
like because the ﬂower can be divided into<br>
three or more identical parts. Most ﬂowers<br>
have actinomorphic symmetry, for instance<br>
the Hibiscus ﬂower. The symbol used to<br>
denote this type of symmetry is<br>
.<br>
<b>Line of</b><br>
symmetry<br>
<b>Figure 3.36 Structure of an actinomorphic</b><br>
ﬂower<br>
b)	 Bilateral symmetry<br>
<b>A ﬂower is said to have bilateral symmetry</b><br>
when it can be split into two identical halves<br>
in one plane only. This type of symmetry<br>
is caused by differences in size and shape<br>
of petals and or sepals as can be noted<br>
in pea and bean ﬂowers. Additionally, in<br>
some ﬂowers, some whorls may be fused<br>
or some parts of an individual whorl may<br>
have different sizes compared to others in<br>
the same ﬂower (Figure 3.37). Bilaterally<br>
symmetrical flowers are said to be irregular<br>
or zygomorphic. Example sweet pea. The<br>
symbol used to represent them is ●|●.<br>
<b>Figure 3.37 Zygomorphic flower</b><br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   157</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
158<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
c)	 Asymmetric ﬂowers<br>
<b>These ﬂowers have no plane of symmetry,</b><br>
they cannot be divided into two equal halves<br>
in any plane; this is because their parts are<br>
spirally arranged, and good examples are<br>
flowers of Cacti, Canna indica, and ginger.<br>
<b>Methods of recording floral structure</b><br>
<b>Flower features can be represented to</b><br>
reveal the arrangement and relationship of<br>
the parts using three methods. These are<br>
a half-ﬂower diagram, ﬂoral formula, and<br>
ﬂoral diagram.<br>
<b>A half flower diagram</b><br>
<b>This is a schematic representation of</b><br>
a ﬂower cut longitudinally along the<br>
median plane in relation to the main stem<br>
to provide an elevation view of the ﬂower.<br>
<b>Normally, in regular ﬂowers, cutting along</b><br>
this plane will result into two identical and<br>
opposite halves of the ﬂower. A half cut<br>
ﬂower diagram shows exactly how the<br>
ﬂoral parts are arranged in relation to each<br>
other (Figure 3.38a).<br>
<b>Floral diagrams</b><br>
<b>A ﬂoral diagram is a diagrammatic</b><br>
representation of the transverse section of<br>
a ﬂower. It shows a plan of the ﬂower as<br>
viewed from above with each of its ﬂoral<br>
whorls represented by a series of centric<br>
cycles showing their number, arrangement,<br>
relative position and fusion. The axis of<br>
the inﬂorescence is represented by a small<br>
cycle at the top of the diagram which is<br>
then regarded as the posterior position<br>
of the ﬂower and bracts, if present. In<br>
principle, ﬂoral diagrams are never<br>
labelled, but standard symbols are used<br>
(Figure 3.38b). Although such symbols<br>
may not be representing physical structures,<br>
they carry additional ﬂoral information<br>
such as symmetry and orientation. Floral<br>
diagrams are useful in the identiﬁcation<br>
of ﬂowers, comparison of angiosperm<br>
taxa, reconstruction of fossil ﬂowers and<br>
understanding the ontogeny and phylogeny<br>
of angiosperms.<br>

<b>Procedure for construction of a floral</b><br>
diagram<br>
a)	 In drawing a ﬂoral diagram, it should<br>
be noted that different ﬂoral whorls are<br>
always represented in concentric circles<br>
starting with sepals on the outermost<br>
circle, followed by petals, stamens and<br>
carpels towards the inner side.<br>
b)	 Examine mature ﬂoral buds and pluck<br>
them off the mother axis after noting<br>
the anterior and posterior sides.<br>
c)	 The ﬂoral parts are drawn in a ﬂoral<br>
diagram, as they would appear in their<br>
transverse sections below the mother<br>
axis. A small circle is drawn above the<br>
ﬂoral diagram to designate the mother<br>
axis; however, for the terminal ﬂowers,<br>
this circle is not shown.<br>
d)	 In ﬂowers associated with bracts<br>
(bracteate flower), a section of bract is<br>
drawn. In contrast in bracteolate flower;<br>
bracteoles are drawn in section on the<br>
left and right sides of the diagram.<br>
e)	 The number of sepals and their<br>
arrangement in relation to the mother<br>
axis or aestivation are drawn in<br>
transverse sections. The odd number<br>
of sepals and petals are drawn either<br>
posterior or anterior to the ﬂower (that<br>
is, opposite the mother axis or opposite<br>
the bract, respectively) with its sepals<br>
and petals alternating.<br>
f)	 In zygomorphic ﬂowers, sepals and<br>
petals with unequal sizes are drawn.<br>
g)	 The spurred sepals or petals are shown<br>
by drawing a loop at their respective<br>
back.<br>
h)	 The edges of fused sepals or petals are<br>
connected by lines.<br>
i)	 The two lips of bilabiate calyx or<br>
corolla are joined by bulging lines.<br>
j)	 In epipetalous ﬂowers, where stamens<br>
are fused with petals, both whorls are<br>
linked with small radial lines.<br>
k)	 Count the number of stamens; note<br>
their cohesion and adhesion to other<br>
ﬂoral parts and position in relation to<br>
petals, introse or extrose position; and<br>
draw them inside the petals in the ﬂoral<br>
diagram. Stamens are represented by<br>
transverse sections of anthers (Figure<br>
3.39). Introse stamens face towards the<br>
centre while extrose ones face towards<br>
the petals. Staminodes are represented<br>
by an asterisk (*) or by a cross (x).<br>
<b>Plant axis</b><br>
<b>Gynaecium</b><br>
<b>Androecium</b><br>

transverse<br>

symbols<br>
<b>Floral formula</b><br>
<b>This is a representation of various ﬂoral</b><br>
parts using standardized symbols and<br>
numbers. It depicts ﬂoral information<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   159</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
160<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
pertaining to symmetry; ﬂoral whorls<br>
namely calyx, corolla, androecium and<br>
gynoecium; their numbers; and how they<br>
relate to each other. Symbols used in<br>
construction of ﬂoral formulae and their<br>
respective meanings are as summarised<br>
below:<br>
<b>Represents radially symmetrical</b><br>
ﬂower (actinomorphic).<br>
●|●	 Represents a ﬂower with bilateral<br>
symmetry (zygomorphic).<br>
♂	 Represents<br>
unisexual<br>
male<br>
or<br>
staminate ﬂower.<br>
♀	 Represents unisexual female or<br>
pistillate ﬂower.<br>
<b>Represents bisexual (hermaphrodite)</b><br>
ﬂower.<br>
<b>Br	 Represents</b><br>
ﬂower<br>
with<br>
bracts<br>
(Bracteate).<br>
<b>Ebr	 Represents ﬂower without bracts</b><br>
(ebracteate).<br>
<b>Brl	 Represents flower with bracteoles</b><br>
(Bracteolate).<br>
<b>K</b><br>
<b>Represents calyx; each K is followed</b><br>
by a number indicating the number<br>
of sepals; for example K5 represents<br>
ﬁve free sepals. If this number<br>
appears within brackets as in K(5), it<br>
denotes five fused sepals.<br>
<b>C</b><br>
<b>Represents corolla; each C is followed</b><br>
by the number indicating number of<br>
petals for example C5 depicts five<br>
free petals. If this number is enclosed<br>
by brackets as in C(5) it denotes five<br>
fused petals.<br>
<b>P</b><br>
<b>Is used if sepals and petals are</b><br>
replaced by perianth. That is, calyx<br>
and corolla are fused and cannot be<br>
differentiated.<br>
<b>A</b><br>
Represents Androecium or stamens.<br>
<b>The letter A is followed by the number</b><br>
of stamens like A(5)+5 indicates a total<br>
of ten stamens in which ﬁve are free<br>
and ﬁve are fused. A<br>
5 means five free<br>
stamens. If the stamens are attached<br>
to petals, their symbols are united<br>
with an arc line above them as in C͡ A.<br>
∞<br>
<b>Represents a large and variable</b><br>
number of whorls. It is used when<br>
the number is more than twelve in a<br>
flower. Example A∞ means many free<br>
stamens and A(∞) means many fused<br>
stamens.<br>
<b>G</b><br>
<b>Denotes Gynoecium or pistil. Each G</b><br>
is followed by the number of carpels<br>
like G1 or G2, which means one carpel<br>
and two free carpels respectively. The<br>
carpels may also be fused as in G(2)<br>
or free as in G4  implying two fused<br>
and four free carpels respectively. A<br>
small line under the letter G indicates<br>
superior ovary while a small line<br>
above it, such as Ḡ, indicates an<br>
inferior ovary.<br>
<b>The following examples highlight how ﬂoral</b><br>
formula are constructed and interpreted.<br>
<b>The above formula indicates that the</b><br>
ﬂower has radial symmetry, and it is<br>
bisexual. It further implies that the ﬂower<br>
has ﬁve sepals, ﬁve petals, numerous<br>
stamens and five carpels. The ﬂower has<br>
no bracts and all whorls are free from each<br>
other that is, not fused.<br>

<b>Floral formula of Lathyrus is;</b><br>
<b>Floral formular of Ipomoea is;</b><br>
<b>The Lathyrus ﬂower is zygomorphic,</b><br>
hermaphrodite, calyx with ﬁve fused<br>
sepals, corolla with ﬁve free petals,<br>
androecium with ten stamens (nine fused<br>
stamens and one free stamen); and a<br>
superior ovary with one free carpel.<br>
<b>The Ipomoea ﬂower is actinomorphic,</b><br>
bisexual, calyx with ﬁve free sepals,<br>
corolla with ﬁve fused petals, androecium<br>
with ﬁve free stamens united with petals,<br>
and gynoecium is superior with two fused<br>
carpels.<br>
<b>Floral formulae and floral diagrams of</b><br>
a selected flower<br>
<b>The Hibiscus is a genus that belongs to</b><br>
the family Malvaceae (Figure 3.40). The<br>
genus is quite large, comprising of several<br>
hundred species that are native to warm<br>
temperate, subtropical and tropical regions<br>
throughout the world. Several species are<br>
widely cultivated as ornamental plants for<br>
their showy flower.<br>
<b>Figure 3.40 A photo of a common Hibiscus plant</b><br>
found in several places of Tanzania<br>
<b>The floral formula of Hibiscus flower</b><br>
derived from the individual with bracts<br>
(bracteate) and bracteoles (bracteolate),<br>
radial symmetry, bisexual, fused five<br>
sepals (calyx), free five petals (corolla)<br>
fused with infinite androecium, and five<br>
fused gynoecium (hypogynous).<br>

flower is presented in structular form to<br>
express the arrangement of floral parts as<br>
projected in transverse or horizontal plane<br>
(Figure 3.41).<br>

<b>Activity 3.10 Observation and</b><br>
recording ﬂower structures<br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>Hibiscus flower, common bean flower,</b><br>
maize flower and elephant grass flower,<br>
hand lens, and surgical or razor blade.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Collect two ﬂowers from each of<br>
the following plants; Hibiscus,<br>
common bean, maize, and elephant<br>
grass.<br>
b)	 Carefully observe each ﬂower using<br>
a hand lens and state with evidence<br>
the classes to which each of the four<br>
plant species belongs.<br>
c)	 Use a surgical or razor blade to<br>
make a transverse section of each<br>
flower and examine its parts.<br>
d)	 Draw a transverse section of each flower<br>
and state their symmetry.<br>
e)	 Prepare a longitudinal section of the<br>
second ﬂower for each plant and draw<br>
well labelled diagrams.<br>
f)	 Write the ﬂoral formulae of bean,<br>
elephant grass, and maize ﬂowers.<br>
g)	 From your observation, what features<br>
do the four ﬂowers have in common?<br>
h)	 Explain the main differences<br>
between Hibiscus and grass ﬂowers<br>
<b>Safety precaution</b><br>
<b>Be careful when using surgical or razor</b><br>
blades.<br>

<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
<b>Adaptation of Angiospermophytes to</b><br>
live on land<br>
<b>Angiosperms are the most recently</b><br>
evolved plants and are relatively the most<br>
adapted plants to terrestrial environment.<br>
<b>Several factors have contributed to their</b><br>
survival, hence success on land; such<br>
factors include:<br>
a)	 Flowers have contributed to the<br>
diversiﬁcation<br>
of<br>
angiosperms<br>
throughout the time with adaptations<br>
to specialised pollination conditions.<br>
<b>The adaptations exhibited by any</b><br>
given ﬂower depend on the type of<br>
pollinators the ﬂower is designed to<br>
attract, and have evolved features to<br>
enable pollinators to recognise and<br>
locate them quickly. For instance,<br>
bees can only see yellow, blue, and<br>
ultraviolet (UV) colors; therefore, bee<br>
pollinated ﬂowers are yellow, blue or<br>
<b>UV but not red. Butterﬂies detect red;</b><br>
accordingly, they can pollinate red<br>
ﬂowers. Moths, which are nocturnal,<br>
pollinate brightly white coloured<br>
ﬂowers, which can standout against<br>
darkness.<br>
b)	 Most plants have ability to make<br>
and supply some food in the form of<br>
nectar and advertise their presence to<br>
the pollinators by producing fragrant<br>
scent. They also have a way of putting<br>
pollen on the pollinators and easily<br>
transferred to the ﬂower of the next<br>
plant.<br>
c)	 To<br>
ensure<br>
pollination,<br>
angiosper-<br>
mophyte ﬂowers have evolved ways of<br>
preventing non beneﬁcial pollinators<br>
from visiting their ﬂowers while<br>
embracing the beneﬁcial pollinators.<br>
<b>For instance, some ﬂowers can</b><br>
be pollinated by insects with long<br>
proboscis or birds with long narrow<br>
beaks, only as they secrete nectar at<br>
the base of a narrow ﬂoral tube which<br>
is the right size of their corresponding<br>
pollinators.<br>
d)	 Stamens have also become modiﬁed<br>
through<br>
time<br>
to<br>
prevent<br>
self-<br>
fertilisation in order to increase<br>
genetic diversity that will eventually<br>
enable them to inhabit a wider range<br>
of habitats. Additionally, closed carpel<br>
allows adaptations to specialised<br>
pollination conditions and controls<br>
self-fertilisation.<br>
e)	 The male gametophyte in angiosperms<br>
is highly reduced into three nuclei<br>
(a tube nucleus and two sperm<br>
nuclei). This evolved to shorten the<br>
duration of time between pollination<br>
and<br>
fertilisation.<br>
<b>Thus,</b><br>
unlike<br>
<b>Coniferophytes, which takes about a</b><br>
year for fertilisation to take place after<br>
pollination,<br>
in<br>
angiospermophytes<br>
begins<br>
fertilisation<br>
soon<br>
after<br>
pollination, which translates into seed<br>
swift, seed formation, and species<br>
perpetuation.<br>
f)	 Their male gametes are transferred<br>
through a special tube (pollen tube)<br>
to the female gametes for fertilisation.<br>
<b>The ability to reproduce sexually</b><br>
without necessarily requiring water<br>
is an important feature in terrestrial<br>
environment, where water is limited.<br>
g)	 Fruits produced by angiospermophytes<br>
are adaptations for dispersal by various<br>
agents such as wind, water, animals,<br>
and<br>
bursting.<br>
<b>Animal</b><br>
dispersed<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   163</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
164<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
fruits have ﬂeshy mesocarp which<br>
is edible or produces edible seeds or<br>
have various kinds of hooks. Wind<br>
dispersed fruits have structures for<br>
buoyancy, such as wings. These have<br>
provided angiosperms an opportunity<br>
to increase their domination in the<br>
terrestrial ecosystem.<br>
h)	 Seeds produced by angiospermophytes<br>
have a highly nutritive tissue called<br>
endosperm, which provides food for<br>
the developing embryo, cotyledons,<br>
and sometimes, for the seedling when<br>
it ﬁrst appears.<br>
i)	 They have developed extensive root<br>
system for anchorage on land and<br>
absorption of water and mineral salts.<br>
<b>Some plant roots have developed</b><br>
symbiotic relationship described as<br>
mycorrhizae with Fungi to increase<br>
the root surface area for absorption of<br>
nutrients and water from the soil.<br>
j)	 They have an advanced and elaborated<br>
vascular system for transportation of<br>
materials. For example, xylem has<br>
both vessel and tracheids for efﬁcient<br>
water movement.<br>
k)	 They<br>
have<br>
stomatal<br>
pores<br>
for<br>
transpiration and gaseous exchange.<br>
<b>These are modiﬁed in various ways to</b><br>
reduce excessive water loss.<br>
l)	 Control<br>
of<br>
evapotranspiration,<br>
depending on the environment in<br>
which a particular species is adapted<br>
to live. For example, xerophytes have<br>
sunken stomata; most of them are<br>
restricted on the lower side.<br>
m)	They have cuticle, a thick waxy<br>
material covering the epidermal cells<br>
in all plant leaves. In addition, stems<br>
of monocots restrict excessive water<br>
loss.<br>
n)	 Deciduous trees shed their leaves<br>
during dry season to reduce the rate<br>
of transpiration, hence, limiting water<br>
loss.<br>
o)	 Asexual reproduction by vegetative<br>
propagation ensures rapid growth and<br>
maturity.<br>
<b>Economic importance of plants</b><br>
<b>Plants are very important to the survival</b><br>
of other organisms; without plants our day<br>
to day life would have been impossible.<br>
<b>Animals’ life is dependent on plants</b><br>
directly and or indirectly. However, there<br>
are some plants with detrimental effects<br>
to life of animals. The following are<br>
advantages and disadvantages of plants.<br>
<b>Advantages of kingdom Plantae</b><br>
a)	 Species in this kingdom are the chief<br>
source of food for heterotrophs. Primary<br>
consumers such as browsers and<br>
grazers depend on angiospermophytes<br>
as their sole source of food. Likewise,<br>
human beings obtain various types<br>
of food such as vegetables, potatoes,<br>
sugar, cereals, and fruits from plants.<br>
b)	 They are important atmospheric<br>
puriﬁers due to their ability to sequester<br>
carbon dioxide (CO2), thus reducing<br>
its concentration in the atmosphere.<br>
<b>Carbon dioxide is one of the</b><br>
greenhouse gases; hence its reduction<br>
in the atmosphere will in turn reduce<br>
the risks of global warming. Through<br>
photosynthesis, plants produce oxygen<br>
which is used by most of the organisms<br>
for respiration.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   164</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>

165<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
c)	 Plants such as legumes have symbiotic<br>
relationship with nitrogen ﬁxing bacteria<br>
in their root nodules. These bacteria are<br>
capable of ﬁxing unavailable nitrogen<br>
from the atmosphere into available<br>
forms, which can be absorbed by plants.<br>
d)	 They help to conserve water in the soil<br>
by preventing excessive evaporation.<br>
<b>For example, plant leaves and grasses</b><br>
are used as mulching materials covering<br>
the soil surface to conserve water.<br>
e)	 Grasses and some trees have important<br>
catchment value, because they have<br>
matted root networks that reduce lateral<br>
ﬂow of water, evaporation, and soil<br>
erosion.<br>
f)	 Plant communities or vegetation such<br>
as grasslands, woodland, and forests<br>
provide habitats for wild animals.<br>
<b>Additionally, bird species use plant</b><br>
materials for construction of their nests.<br>
g)	 They are used in research and biological<br>
studies.<br>
h)	 Plants are sources of traditional fuel<br>
such as charcoal and ﬁrewood. In<br>
<b>Africa, for instance, approximately</b><br>
90% of the population use charcoal as<br>
a source of domestic energy.<br>
i)	 Ferns and most Angiospermophytes are<br>
grown as ornamental plants for indoor<br>
and outdoor decoration, examples: true<br>
ferns, Hibiscus, cacti and roses.<br>
j)	 They provide raw materials for<br>
industrial purposes. Examples are<br>
cotton for making clothes, latex for<br>
making rubber, cellulose for making<br>
ﬁber products such as ropes, and wood<br>
pulp for making paper. Additionally,<br>
ﬁbres from plants such as sisal are used<br>
to manufacture mattresses and carpets.<br>
<b>White pines are extensively used for</b><br>
making boxes, furniture, ﬂoor, and<br>
panel.<br>
k)	 Resins from pines are used for making<br>
premier paints and varnish solvents<br>
ointments, wax, and shoe polish.<br>
l)	 They provide timber for various<br>
purposes such as furniture making,<br>
poles for houses construction and<br>
electric supply.<br>
m)	Forests attract tourists and thus aid in<br>
generating national income.<br>
n)	 Plant leaves such as grass, banana and<br>
coconut leaves, are used in thatching<br>
houses.<br>
o)	 Fern rhizome, root bark, and rhizome<br>
bark of certain species are used for<br>
growing Orchids.<br>
p)	 Some plants are source of medicine for<br>
example Neem trees<br>
<b>Disadvantages of kingdom Plantae</b><br>
a)	 Some plants can produce metabolites<br>
or accumulate toxic or poisonous<br>
products which are dangerous to<br>
consumers (animals). Cassava plants<br>
can<br>
accumulate<br>
cyanide,<br>
which<br>
is toxic, if consumed by humans.<br>
<b>Jatropha curcas produces a toxic</b><br>
protein called curcin. Tea and coffee<br>
produce caffeine which in high doses<br>
may cause hypertension in humans.<br>
b)	 Weed plants compete with food crops,<br>
leading into low crop yield.<br>
c)	 Some plants are parasites to other<br>

ﬁliformis is a parasitic weed in crops<br>
such as mango, orange and cashewnut<br>

d)	 Some aquatic weeds can colonise<br>
water bodies and affect ecosystem as<br>

activities.<br>

<b>3.7  Kingdom Animalia</b><br>
<b>Animals are a diverse group of organisms</b><br>
that make up the kingdom Animalia. This<br>
kingdom comprises of animals, which<br>
are multicellular eukaryotic organisms.<br>
<b>Most of them have high level of tissue</b><br>
differentiation<br>
and<br>
specialised<br>
body<br>
organs.<br>
<b>They</b><br>
undergo<br>
heterotrophic<br>
mode of nutrition; that is, they depend<br>
on other organisms directly or indirectly<br>
to obtain organic food. They digest food<br>
internally, particularly in the gut and store<br>
carbohydrate in the form of glycogen.<br>
<b>Animals are mobile, they can move from</b><br>
one place to another in search for food,<br>
shelter, mates, and safety. Mobility in<br>
animals include movement of organs.<br>
<b>For example corals are sedentary animals</b><br>
but their organs move to trap food.<br>
<b>In most animals, body activities and</b><br>
responses are coordinated by the nervous<br>
and endocrine systems. Higher animals<br>
reproduce sexually, involving haploid<br>
gametes. Most animals are triploblastic<br>
organisms, meaning they have three body<br>
layers (outer layer; an ectoderm, middle<br>
layer; mesoderm, and endoderm as an<br>
inner layer); Examples of such animals<br>
include all multicellular animals, with<br>
the exception of certain invertebrates<br>
such as the cnidarians and sponges. Some<br>
are diploblastic, consisting of two layers<br>
of cells (ectoderm and endoderm); for<br>
example cnidarians (jellyfish, corals and<br>
sea anemones). Their body symmetry<br>
is bilateral, except phylum cnidaria<br>
that have radial symmetry and phylum,<br>
<b>Porifera example sponges whose body</b><br>
regularly lack symmetry. Most animals<br>
have anterior and posterior ends, with oral<br>
and anal openings.<br>
<b>Kingdom Animalia is divided into</b><br>
about twenty eight phyla including<br>
<b>Polifera,</b><br>
<b>Cnidaria,</b><br>
<b>Platyhelminthes,</b><br>
<b>Nematoda, Annelida, Arthropoda, Mollusca,</b><br>
<b>Echnodermata, Chordata and  other smaller</b><br>
phyla.  The first eight phyla fall under the<br>
group of invertebrates (animals without<br>
a backbone) while the ninth phylum,<br>
<b>Chordata, includes some invertebrates</b><br>
and<br>
vertebrates<br>
(animals<br>
having<br>
a<br>
backbone). This book will deal with five<br>
selected phyla, namely Platyhelminthes,<br>
<b>Nematoda (Aschelminthes), Annelida,</b><br>
Arthropoda, and Chordata.<br>

<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
3.7.1  Phylum Platyhelminthes<br>
<b>This is a group of ﬂatworms. The worms</b><br>
can be free living or parasites. One of the<br>
best-known example of ﬂatworms is the<br>
tapeworm.<br>
<b>General Characteristics of phylum</b><br>
<b>Platyhelminthes</b><br>
a)	 They are dorsoventrally ﬂattened and<br>
some are unsegmented worms. Most of<br>
them have mouth and gut with no anus.<br>
b)	 They have ﬂame cells in the mesoderm<br>
for excretion and osmoregulation.<br>
c)	 They are hermaphrodites with a<br>
complex reproductive system, which<br>
prevents self-fertilisation, but favours<br>
cross-fertilization.<br>
d)	 They are triploblastic acoelomate<br>
animals, which have three body layers<br>
(ectoderm, endoderm, and mesoderm)<br>
without a body cavity or coelom.<br>
e)	 Some have cilia on their outer surface<br>
for locomotion, for example Planaria,<br>
others have cilia in larval stage, for<br>
example miracidium larva in flukes,<br>
but lack cilia in adult stage.<br>
f)	 They exhibit bilateral symmetry.<br>
g)	 They have a very simple nervous<br>
system, with two nerve cords which run<br>
down on either side of the body. They<br>
have two simple brains called ganglia,<br>
which are simple bundles of nerves.<br>
h)	 Some have two eyespots that help in<br>
sensing the presence of light.<br>
i)	 Some platyhelminths have hooks and<br>
suckers for attachment. Examples of<br>
platyhelminths are Taenia solium,<br>
<b>Taenia saginata (tapeworms), and</b><br>
<b>Fasciola hepatica (liver ﬂuke). Most</b><br>
members of this phylum are parasites<br>
while others are free living, for<br>
example: Planaria.<br>
<b>Classiﬁcation of Platyhelminthes</b><br>
<b>Common Platyhelminthes, fall into three</b><br>
classes namely; class Cestoda, Trematoda<br>
and Turbellaria. Class Cestoda consists<br>
of endoparasite animals such as the<br>
tapeworms, including Taenia solium (the<br>
pork tapeworm), Dipylidium caninum (the<br>
dog tapeworm) and Taenia saginata (beef<br>
tapeworm). Class Trematoda comprises<br>
of<br>
the<br>
ﬂukes;<br>
example,<br>
<b>Fasciola</b><br>
hepatica (Liver ﬂuke) and the blood<br>
flukes Schistosoma haematobium and<br>
<b>Schistosoma mansoni. Class Turbellaria</b><br>
consists of the free living worms such as<br>
Planaria. They are flat worms.<br>
<b>Structure of a tapeworm</b><br>
<b>An adult tapeworm for example Taenia,</b><br>
consists of a knob-like head or scolex,<br>
equipped with hooks and suckers for<br>
attachment to the intestinal wall of<br>
the host; a neck region; and a series<br>
of ﬂat, rectangular body segments (or<br>
proglottids), generated by the neck<br>
(Figure 3.42). The chain of proglottids<br>
may reach up to 15 or 20 ft (4.6–6.1 m)<br>
long. Terminal proglottids break off,<br>
and are egested in the feaces of the host.<br>
<b>Nevertheless, the new ones are constantly</b><br>
formed at the neck of the worm. As long<br>
as the scolex and the neck are intact, the<br>
worm is alive and capable of growing.<br>
<b>Rudimentary nervous and excretory</b><br>
systems run the length of the worm,<br>
through the proglottids. However, there is<br>
no digestive tract; the worm absorbs the<br>

<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
host’s digested food through its cuticle or<br>
outer covering.<br>

Adaptations of Taenia to its mode of life.<br>
<b>Taenia exhibits various special adaptations</b><br>
to its parasitic mode of life as follows:<br>
a)	 It has scolex (head) with hooks and<br>
suckers for ﬁxation and attachment on<br>
the host’s gut wall<br>
b)	 It lacks alimentary canal because it<br>
absorbs digested food materials from<br>
its hosts.<br>
c)	 It has a thin and ﬂattened body, which<br>
provides a large surface area for<br>
gaseous exchange and absorption of<br>
digested food.<br>
d)	 Its body is covered by a living<br>
epidermal layer called the tegument<br>
which produces antienzymes to protect<br>
it from hosts’ digestive enzymes.<br>
e)	 It has a large number of ploglottids,<br>
which ensures production of a large<br>
number of eggs, hence high chance of<br>
survival.<br>
f)	 It can respire anaerobically; therefore,<br>
it is able to live under low oxygen<br>
concentration, for example in the host’s<br>
gut.<br>
g)	 It is well adapted to osmotic pressure<br>
changes in the host’s body.<br>
h)	 It has ﬂame cells, which collect<br>
excretory material from all parts of<br>
the body.<br>
<b>Structure of a liver fluke</b><br>
<b>Morphologically, an adult liver ﬂuke, for</b><br>
example Fasciola, has a ﬂattened leaf<br>
shaped appearance (Figure 3.43).  In the<br>
anterior part, there is a triangular projection<br>
with a mouth surrounded by oral suckers<br>
at its apex. Ventrally, at the base of the<br>
projection, there are ventral suckers; and<br>
between the two types of suckers, there is<br>
a genital pore. Posteriorly, there is a minute<br>
excretory pore. The body is enclosed in<br>
a tough cuticle, which is extended into<br>
backward directed spines. Their muscle<br>
ﬁbres have small glands with minute ducts.<br>
<b>The mouth runs into the oesophagus which</b><br>
branches into two blind parts.<br>
<b>Oral sucker</b><br>
<b>Mouth</b><br>
<b>Genital pore</b><br>
<b>Acetabulum</b><br>
(ventral sucker)<br>
<b>Head</b><br>
<b>Flattened body</b><br>
<b>Excretory</b><br>
pore<br>

<b>Adaptations of the liver fluke to its</b><br>
mode of life<br>
<b>Liver fluke possesses the following features</b><br>
that facilitate a parasitic mode of its life in<br>
the host:<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   168</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
169<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
a)	 It has suckers that provide a means of<br>
attachment to the host.<br>
b)	 It has tegument with spines that<br>
prevent it from being washed away by<br>
bile; and also help the parasite to erode<br>
liver cells.<br>
c)	 The tough tegument and secretions<br>
from the glands prevent the worm<br>
from the effects of the host’s toxins.<br>
d)	 The parasite secretes enzymes that<br>
help it to penetrate the liver cells at<br>
various stages of its life cycle.<br>
e)	 It has mechanisms and chemicals<br>
that suppress the actions of the host’s<br>
digestive enzymes.<br>
f)	 It has a high reproductive potential<br>
and different multiplication phases<br>
that balance its high mortality rate.<br>
<b>They have two hosts, namely primary</b><br>
hosts (example: sheep and humans)<br>
and secondary host (example: fresh<br>
water snail called Lymnea sp.).<br>
<b>They have several larval stages</b><br>
(miracidium, sporocyst, redia, cercaria<br>
and metacercaria) which increase<br>
the chances of survival and more<br>
perpetuation.<br>
<b>Economic importance of phylum</b><br>
<b>Platyhelminthes</b><br>
<b>Some members of phylum Platyhelminthes</b><br>
play an important role in marine, freshwater<br>
and terrestrial ecosystems, while several<br>
species are harmful parasites to humans<br>
and other animals.<br>
<b>Advantages of phylum Platyhelminthes</b><br>
a)	 Some species of Platyhelminthes feed<br>
on dead bodies of large organisms;<br>
therefore, they decompose organic<br>
matter in the soil; for example, free<br>
living Planaria.<br>
b)	 Some members of Platyhelminthes<br>
are used in biological studies as<br>
specimens; for example, Taenia and<br>
Fasciola.<br>
<b>Disadvantages of phylum</b><br>
<b>Platyhelminthes</b><br>
<b>Some members are parasites, which cause</b><br>
diseases to man and domestic animals.<br>
<b>Examples include:</b><br>
a)   Taenia causes taeniasis; an intestinal<br>
tapeworm infection caused by eating<br>
raw or undercooked contaminated<br>
beef (Taenia saginata) or pork (Taenia<br>
solium).<br>
b)    Fasciola hepatica causes fascioliasis,<br>
which results into liver rot in sheep,<br>
cattle, and humans.<br>
c)<br>
<b>Schistosoma</b><br>
haematobium<br>
and<br>
<b>Schistosoma</b><br>
mansoni<br>
cause<br>
urinary and interstinal bilharzia<br>
(schistosomiasis) respectively.<br>
3.7.2 Phylum Nematoda<br>
(Aschelminthes)<br>
<b>Nematodes or round worms are the most</b><br>
numerous multicellular animals on earth,<br>
and inhabit a wide range of environments.<br>
<b>Many of them are parasites of animals</b><br>
(including humans) and plants. They may<br>
cause serious diseases that are deleterious to<br>
human health and agricultural productivity.<br>
<b>The free-living species inhabit marine and</b><br>
freshwater environments, as well as the soils<br>
and sediments of all of the various types of<br>
terrestrial biomes. Examples of Nematodes<br>
include: Ascaris lumbricoides (the pig and<br>
human nematodes), Wuchereria bancrofti<br>
(infecting human lymphatic system), hook<br>
worms (blood-feeding parasite of human<br>
and other animals) and Meloidogyne<br>
incognita (plant root knot nematode).<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   169</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
170<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Structure of Ascaris</b><br>
<b>Ascaris are round, slender unsegmented</b><br>
worms with tapering bodies at both ends.<br>
<b>They are characterised by being tubes</b><br>
within tubes, referring to the alimentary<br>
canal, which extends from the mouth on the<br>
anterior end to the anus, located near the<br>
tail (Figure 3.44). They possess digestive,<br>
nervous, excretory and reproductive<br>
systems, but they lack distinct circulatory<br>
and respiratory systems. The male and<br>
female Ascaris differ in morphology, as male<br>
is smaller and more curved at its posterior<br>
end than the female. They lack locomotory<br>
organs; movement is by undulating bodies<br>
in dorso-ventral waves.<br>

gonopore<br>

line<br>

<b>Straight tail</b><br>
<b>Mouth</b><br>

(a) female and (b) male<br>
<b>Adaptations of Ascaris to its mode of</b><br>
life<br>
<b>Ascaris possesses some adaptive features</b><br>
that enable it to adapt to its parasitic mode<br>
of life. Thus:<br>
a)	 It has a tough cuticle, which protects it<br>
from being digested by the enzymes of<br>
the alimentary canal of the host.<br>
b)	 It produces chemicals, which act as<br>
anti-enzymes to the digestive enzymes<br>
of the host.<br>
c)	 It has an alimentary canal, which opens<br>
at the mouth and anus. This enables the<br>
parasite to take food from the hosts’<br>
digestive system.<br>
d)	 It possesses digestive enzymes in its<br>
digestive system for the completion of<br>
partially digested food from the hosts’<br>
alimentary canal.<br>
e)	 It has sensory papillae around its<br>
mouth for detecting food present in the<br>
elementary canal of the host.<br>
f)	 It respires anaerobically, and have low<br>
metabolic rate; hence, they are able to<br>
live inside the host’s intestine.<br>
g)	 It has high reproductive potential; thus,<br>
by producing large number of eggs, it<br>
ensures its survival and existence.<br>
h)	 The pharynx has valves, which prevent<br>
regurgitation of the ingested food.<br>
<b>Economic importance of phylum</b><br>
<b>Aschelminthes</b><br>
<b>Members of Aschelminthes are associated</b><br>
with both advantages and disadvantages<br>
as follows:<br>
<b>Advantages of phylum Aschelminthes</b><br>
a)	 Nematodes, such as Ascaris are used<br>
in biological studies in different ways;<br>
for instance, in laboratory practical<br>
studies.<br>
b)	 Some nematodes are used as biological<br>
control agents for pest insects. They<br>
attack insects and kill or hinder their<br>
development.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   170</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
171<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
<b>Disadvantages of the phylum</b><br>
<b>Aschelminthes</b><br>
<b>Some Aschelminthes are parasites and cause</b><br>
diseases in plants and animals. Examples<br>
include:<br>
a) 	Wuchereria bancrofti that causes<br>
elephantiasis. This disease infects the<br>
human lymphatic system by blocking<br>
it; resulting in the accumulation of the<br>
lymph in legs, hands, and/or scrotal<br>
sacs.<br>
b) 	Ascaris lumbricoides causes ascariasis<br>
in humans that may lead to obstruction<br>
in the gut and anaemia.<br>
c) 	Meloidogyne<br>
incognita<br>
causes<br>
root knot galls in plants that drain<br>
photosynthetic products; hence, affect<br>
plant growth and reduce yield.<br>
3.7.3  Phylum Annelida<br>
<b>Members of this phylum are known as ringed</b><br>
or segmented worms. This is a large phylum<br>
comprising of lugworms, earthworms, and<br>
leeches. The species are adapted to various<br>
habitats; some members are aquatic, living<br>
in marine and fresh water, and others live<br>
in moist terrestrial environments.<br>
<b>Characteristics of phylum Annelida</b><br>
a)	 They are triploblastic, coelomate<br>
organisms.<br>
b)	 They have bilateral symmetry.<br>
c)	 They are metamerically segmented.<br>
d)	 They have a definite outer covering<br>
called cuticle<br>
e)	 They have chitinous hair-like structures<br>
called chaetae (except in leeches), the<br>
chaetae of polychaetes are found on<br>
structures known as parapodia.<br>
f)	 They have a lip like extension on<br>
the first segment above the mouth<br>
(prostomium).<br>
g)	 They have nephridia as typical<br>
excretory organs.<br>
h)	 They have central nervous system,<br>
with paired cerebral ganglia in which<br>
paired commissures around the gut<br>
lead to a double ventral nerve cord,<br>
expanded to form a ganglion in every<br>
segment.<br>
<b>Body plan of coelomates</b><br>
<b>In annelids and other coelomates there is</b><br>
an extensive internal space or body cavity<br>
called a coelom. This is the space between<br>
the body wall and the alimentary canal<br>
(Figure 3.45).<br>
<b>Functions of the coelom</b><br>
<b>The coelom has the following biological</b><br>
signiﬁcance to an organism:<br>
a)	 It contains coelomic fluid which acts<br>
as a hydrostatic skeleton. This is found<br>
in such organisms as earthworms.<br>
b)	 It separates the alimentary canal from<br>
the body wall so that the functions of<br>
the two parts take place independently.<br>
c)	 It allows the animal to grow larger.<br>
d)	 The coelomic ﬂuid may function as a<br>
circulatory medium for transportation<br>
of food, gases, and excretory wastes.<br>
e)	 It provides space for enlargement of<br>
internal organs.<br>
f)	 In<br>
some<br>
animals,<br>
it<br>
plays<br>
an<br>
osmoregulatory role.<br>

<b>Classes of phylum Annelida</b><br>
<b>The phylum has three classes namely:</b><br>
<b>Class Polychaeta, which includes the</b><br>
marine bristle worms with many chaetae<br>
and parapodia, examples are Nereis (the<br>
ragworm) and Arenicola (the lugworm).<br>
<b>Class Oligochaeta which includes; the</b><br>
earthworms and fresh water worms, that<br>
have few chaetae without parapodia,<br>
for example Lumbricus (the common<br>
earthworm), Tubifex (fresh water earthworm<br>
sometimes called sludge worm, or sewage<br>
worm). Another class of annelida is class<br>
<b>Hirudinea which includes segmented worms</b><br>
with fixed number of segments, and have<br>
neither chaetae nor parapodia; an example<br>
is Hirudo (the leeches).<br>
<b>Structure of earthworm</b><br>
<b>An earthworm is an elongated, tube-shaped,</b><br>
metamerically segmented organism (Figure<br>
3.46). Its digestive system runs through<br>
the length of its body. It has a thin wet<br>
skin through which gaseous exchange<br>
takes place. There is a double transport<br>
system composed of coelomic ﬂuid that<br>
moves within the ﬂuid-ﬁlled coelom. The<br>
circulatory system is simple and closed.<br>
<b>The worm has a central and peripheral</b><br>
nervous systems. The central nervous<br>
system consists of two ganglia above<br>
the mouth, one on either side, connected<br>
to a nerve cord running back along its<br>
length to motor neurones and sensory<br>
cells in each segment. A large number of<br>
chemoreceptors are concentrated near its<br>
mouth. Circumferential and longitudinal<br>
muscles on the periphery of each segment<br>
enable the worm to move. Similar sets of<br>
muscles line the gut and their actions move<br>
the food towards the worm’s anus.<br>
<b>Earthworm is a hermaphrodite, meaning</b><br>
that, the organism has both male and<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   172</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
173<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
female reproductive organs. Has an<br>
opening of spermotheca, which is found<br>
in segments 9, 10, and 11. Seminal ﬂuid<br>
from another worm is passed through<br>
the opening during copulation. Ventrally,<br>
on segment 14, there are very small<br>
slits, which are openings of the oviducts<br>
(female opening) and on segment 15; there<br>
are openings of the vasa differentia (male<br>
opening). Additionally, earthworm has<br>
clitella (singular clitellum) which secret<br>
a cocoon in which eggs are deposited.<br>
<b>The mouth is overhang by a ﬂap called a</b><br>
prostomium. Movement is by means of<br>
paired chaetae.<br>

(saddle)<br>

<b>Adaptations of earthworm to its mode</b><br>
of life<br>
a)	 They have ideal shapes with chaetae<br>
for burrowing.<br>
b)	 They have gizzard for breaking up<br>
plant tissues.<br>
c)	 They have clitellum, which holds<br>
together earthworms during copulation<br>
and forms an egg depositer structure<br>
called cocoon to ensure efﬁcient<br>
provision<br>
of<br>
offspring<br>
during<br>
unfavourable conditions.<br>
d)	 The mucus secreted helps to bind the<br>
walls of burrows and helps the worm<br>
to pass through smoothly.<br>
e)	 They can thrust the earth aside when it<br>
is loose and consume it when it is tight.<br>
<b>This ability ensures burrowing under</b><br>
both conditions.<br>
f)	 They are omnivorous; as they feed on<br>
live plant matters, fungi, bacteria and<br>
microscopic animals. They also feed<br>
on dead organic matter from plants and<br>
animals. This feeding habit ensures<br>
sustainable food supply.<br>
g)	 Under natural conditions, earthworms<br>
are less active; this limits oxygen<br>
consumption.<br>
h)	 They have looped surface vessels,<br>
which facilitate absorption of oxygen<br>
over a short diffusion distance.<br>
i)	 Their haemoglobin has high afﬁnity to<br>
oxygen.<br>
j)	 The head is less elaborate. The worm<br>
has tactile and photoreceptive cells for<br>
locating food at night and sensing light<br>
during the day respectively.<br>
k)	 They have chaetae that are used for<br>
movement.<br>

<b>Economic importance of Annelids</b><br>
<b>Annelids are both ecologically and</b><br>
economically important; even though on<br>
the other side, they have some drawbacks.<br>
<b>Advantages of Annelids</b><br>
a)	 They improve soil aeration, drainage<br>
channels and increase depth of the top<br>
soil through burrowing and mixing of<br>
soil layers.<br>
b)	 They are used as bait in ﬁshing<br>
industry, for example earthworms.<br>
c)	 They are used as source of nutritious<br>
food rich in proteins and vitamins for<br>
ﬁsh and carnivorous birds.<br>
d)	 They are ecological decomposers;<br>
hence, they contribute to nutrient<br>
circulation;<br>
since<br>
they<br>
feed<br>
on<br>
decaying organic matter.<br>
e)	 They are used for removing soil<br>
pollutants from the soil and clean the<br>
environment by transforming organic<br>
wastes during feeding as a way of<br>
waste management.<br>
f)	 Their excretory wastes in form of<br>
casts increase cementing effects of<br>
soil particles; hence, increase water-<br>
holding capacity.<br>
g)	 They are used as specimens in<br>
biological studies.<br>
<b>Disadvantages of Annelids</b><br>
a)	 They increase soil porosity and<br>
aeration, which is detrimental to paddy<br>
ﬁelds (rice plants in the ﬁeld).<br>
b)	 They damage young roots of the<br>
growing plants.<br>
c)	 Leeches are harmful to mammals,<br>
reptiles, and ﬁshes as they suck blood.<br>

3.7.4   Phylum Arthropoda<br>
<b>This is the largest group in the Kingdom</b><br>
Animalia, with high species diversity.<br>
<b>Arthropods represent about three-quarters</b><br>
of all known living organisms. Some of the<br>
well- known arthropods include insects,<br>
crustaceans, and arachnids. Arthropoda<br>
comes from two Greek words arthro that<br>
means ‘joint’ and podos that means ‘foot’.<br>
<b>Therefore, all arthropods have jointed</b><br>
appendages. Arthropods are found in almost<br>
every known environment including marine,<br>
freshwater, and terrestrial ecosystems.<br>
<b>They vary extremely in their habitats, life</b><br>
histories, and feeding habits.<br>
<b>Characteristics of phylum Arthropoda</b><br>
a)	 They<br>
possess<br>
a<br>
chitinous<br>
and<br>
sometimes calcareous exoskeleton,<br>
which may be rigid, stiff or flexible;<br>
shed during growth in some organisms.<br>
b)	 They have jointed, paired appendages<br>
such as legs and antennae.<br>
c)	 They have segmented bodies, which<br>
are arranged into regions, called<br>
tagmata comprising of the head,<br>
thorax, and abdomen. The head of<br>
some arthropods such as arachnids and<br>
crustaceans is fused with the thorax to<br>
form a structure called cephalothorax<br>
(prosoma).<br>
d)	 Their body plan is bilaterally symmetrical.<br>
e)	 They have a ventral nervous system<br>
and open circulatory system, which is<br>
dorsally positioned.<br>
f)	 They<br>
are<br>
triploblastic<br>
coelomate<br>
animals, which are metamerically<br>
segmented.<br>
g)	 Their coelom is reduced and confined<br>
to cavities of excretory organs and<br>
reproductive<br>
ducts<br>
during<br>
their<br>
development and form another cavity<br>
called haemocoel.<br>
h)	 They have an open circulatory system.<br>
<b>Classification of Arthropods</b><br>
<b>Arthropods are classified into five classes</b><br>
namely; class Crustacea, which include<br>
crabs, class Arachnida, which include<br>
spiders and scorpions, class Chilopoda, for<br>
example centipedes, class Diplopoda, for<br>
instance millipedes, and class Insecta, which<br>
include cockroaches and grasshoppers.<br>
<b>Class Crustacea</b><br>
<b>Crustaceans form a large group of arthropods</b><br>
that include familiar animals such as crabs,<br>
lobsters, prawns, shrimps, barnacles, and<br>
crayﬁsh. Most crustaceans are marine<br>
aquatic animals, other are found  in fresh<br>
water habitats, and a few are terrestrial,<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   175</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
176<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
for example woodlice (Isopota) found in<br>
leaf litter.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of class Crustacea</b><br>
<b>Crustacea have distinctive features that</b><br>
make them unique among other arthropods<br>
as highlighted below:<br>
a)	 They possess two body division, which<br>
are cephalothorax (the head fused with<br>
thorax) and abdomen.<br>
b)	 They have carapace or an exoskeleton<br>
hardened with calcium salts which<br>
acts as a protective shell.<br>
c)	 They have heads bearing two pairs of<br>
antennae.<br>
d)	 They have a pair of compound eyes at<br>
the ends of movable stalks.<br>
e)	 They have at least three pairs of<br>
mouthparts.<br>
f)	 Gills are used for gaseous exchange,<br>
which are the outgrowth of the body<br>
wall or limbs.<br>
g)	 They have variable number of legs,<br>
sometimes up to ten legs and can<br>
be modified for swimming. Unlike<br>
other arthopods, the legs and other<br>
appendages of Crustacea have two<br>
branches (they are biramous).<br>
<b>Structure of a crab</b><br>
<b>A crab is covered with a thick exoskeleton</b><br>
(carapace) composed of calcium carbonate.<br>
<b>This means they are well protected against</b><br>
predators (Figure 3.48). The body is divided<br>
into two parts: the cephalothorax and<br>
abdomen. The abdomen is entirely hidden<br>
under the carapace; it may not be visible at<br>
all, unless the crab is turned over. The head<br>
bears two pairs of antennae. The animal<br>
is equipped with a pair of claws (pincers)<br>
which are the most important weapons with<br>
at least three functions: seizing, eating and<br>
subduing the prey. If the food is a shellﬁsh,<br>
then the pincers can exert force to open or<br>
break the mollusc’s shell. Males also use<br>
pincers for or during ﬁghting.<br>

<b>Propodus</b><br>
<b>Cheliped</b><br>
<b>Eye</b><br>
<b>Walking legs</b><br>

<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
<b>Class Arachnida</b><br>
<b>The arachnids represent the second</b><br>
largest group of terrestrial arthropods<br>
after the insects. The class includes the<br>
animals whose bodies are organised into<br>
two tagmata called cephalothorax or<br>
prosoma (fused head with thorax) and<br>
abdomen (opisthosoma). Most of them<br>
are carnivores, except the mites, which are<br>
herbivores. Members of this class include<br>
spiders, mites, ticks and scorpions.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of class Arachnida</b><br>
<b>Arachnids can be distinguished from other</b><br>
members of the phylum Arthropoda by the<br>
following features:<br>
a)	 Their bodies are divided into two<br>
regions; prosoma and opisthosoma,<br>
separated by a narrow waist-like<br>
constriction.<br>
b)	 They lack antennae; alternatively,<br>
they use pedipalps to detect external<br>
stimuli.<br>
c)	 They lack compound eyes, they only<br>
have simple eyes.<br>
d)	 They lack true mouthparts; instead,<br>
they have two pairs of appendages born<br>
from prosoma. One pair, the chelicerae<br>
or poisonous fangs, is used for killing<br>
preys or defending themselves; the<br>
other pair  called pedipalps holds the<br>
prey in place when the animal injects<br>
poison.<br>
e)	 Usually they have four pairs of<br>
walking legs.<br>
f)	 Their respiratory structures are book<br>
lungs or book gills or trachea.<br>
<b>Structure of a spider</b><br>
<b>The spider has two main body parts: a fused</b><br>
head and thorax, called cephalothorax<br>
and abdomen (Figure 3.49). Most<br>
external appendages are attached to the<br>
cephalothorax. The appendages are legs,<br>
chelicerae, mouthparts, and pedipalps. On<br>
the ventral part of the abdomen are two<br>
hardened plates covering the book lungs.<br>
<b>The spinnerets, which produce silk, are also</b><br>
located in the abdomen.<br>
<b>Walking</b><br>
legs<br>
<b>Pedipalp</b><br>
<b>Chelicerae</b><br>
<b>Carapace</b><br>
<b>Pedicel</b><br>
<b>Abdomen</b><br>
<b>Spinneret</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.49 Structure of a spider</b><br>
<b>Adaptations of spider to its mode of</b><br>
life<br>
<b>Spiders live in almost every habitat. They</b><br>
are adapted to different environments<br>
because:<br>
a)	 They have pairs of chericerae which<br>
produce silk for capturing preys.<br>
b)	 They have pedipalps for sensation.<br>
c)	 They have four pairs of legs for<br>
locomotion.The hairy spiders have<br>
stings used to paralyse prey and defend<br>
themselves.<br>
<b>Subphylum Uniramia</b><br>
<b>The centipedes (Chilopoda), millipedes</b><br>
(Diplopoda)  and insects (Insecta) form<br>
a closely related group of arthropods.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   177</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
178<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>They have three features that distinguish</b><br>
them from other arthropods. The first is<br>
possession of a single pair of antennae<br>
(crustaceans have two pairs and arachnids<br>
have none). The second is the presence of<br>
strictly uniramus (unbranched) appendages.<br>
<b>Thirdly, the cuticle is hardened using</b><br>
tanning process involving chemicals<br>
known as hydroquinones. It is made<br>
waterproof using wax, and never has<br>
calcium carbonate. For these reasons,<br>
the three classes are nowadays grouped<br>
together under "Uniramia".<br>
<b>Class Chilopoda</b><br>
<b>This class consists of organisms found in</b><br>
terrestrial environment. They are terrestrial<br>
animals abundant in moist areas, such as<br>
leaf litters, under logs or rocks. An example<br>
of chilopoda are centipedes.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of class Chilopoda</b><br>
<b>The following features distinguish</b><br>
centipedes from other members of the<br>
phylum Arthropoda:<br>
a)	 They have a ﬂattened body with a<br>
distinct head. However, other body<br>
segments are similar, the trunk is not<br>
obviously divided into thorax and<br>
abdomen.<br>
b)	 They have one pair of legs per segment.<br>
c)	 They are carnivores, feeding mainly<br>
on insects and worms.<br>
d)	 Their ﬁrst trunk segments have<br>
appendages, which are modiﬁed into a<br>
pair of poisonous fangs.<br>
<b>Structure of centipede</b><br>
<b>Centipedes have a head bearing eyes, well-</b><br>
developed one pair of mouthparts (jaws),<br>
and one pair of antennae (Figure 3.50). The<br>
body consists of many segments, each of<br>
which bears one pair of legs. The legs of<br>
the ﬁrst body segment are modiﬁed into<br>
poisonous claws, and are used for defence<br>
as well as for capturing and paralysing<br>
preys. They have tracheae used for gaseous<br>
exchange.<br>
<b>Segment</b><br>
<b>Leg</b><br>
<b>Antennae</b><br>
<b>Eye</b><br>
<b>Poisonous jaw</b><br>
<b>Last pair of leg with</b><br>
hooks for defence<br>
<b>Figure 3.50  Structure of a centipede</b><br>
<b>Adaptations of the centipede to its</b><br>
mode of life<br>
<b>Centipedes are adapted to a wide range of</b><br>
habitats because:<br>
a)	 They are fast moving animals; this<br>
helps them to catch their prey.<br>
b)	 They feed on insects, spiders, and<br>
worms, which they hunt and paralyse<br>
with a bite of their poisonous claws.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   178</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
179<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
c)	 They have antennae for sensation.<br>
d)	 They have poisonous claws for defence<br>
and hunting.<br>
e)	 They have legs for locomotion.<br>
f)	 The last pair of legs has hooks for<br>
defence.<br>
<b>Class Diplopoda</b><br>
<b>The class Diplopoda consists of members</b><br>
with many legs; usually two pairs of legs<br>
per body segment. An example of a member<br>
of class Diplopoda is a millipede. The head<br>
bears one pair of antennae and a pair of<br>
mouthparts (jaws) while the trunk is not<br>
divided into thorax and abdomen. They are<br>
scavengers that feed on decaying organic<br>
matter, like leaves and compost.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of class Diplopoda</b><br>
<b>Diplopods have unique features, which</b><br>
differentiate them from other Arthropods.<br>
a)	 They have round bodies with clearly<br>
defined head, followed by other similar<br>
segments.<br>
b)	 They have many legs; with two pairs<br>
of legs per segment.<br>
c)	 Most millipedes are herbivorous,<br>
feeding mainly on decaying vegetation.<br>
<b>Structure of millipede</b><br>
<b>The millipede has elongated and cylindrical</b><br>
body in which each body segment bears<br>
two pairs of legs. Millipedes are made up of<br>
a series of segments, whose number varies<br>
greatly from one species of millipedes<br>
to another (Figure 3.51). Many of the<br>
segments contain special glands that secrete<br>
a noxious chemical to repel predators.<br>
<b>The ﬁrst segment behind the head does</b><br>
not have legs, but the rest of the segments<br>
have legs. Towards the end, some of the<br>
segments have no legs. The number of<br>
legless segments varies from one millipede<br>
to another. Some species of millipedes have<br>
hair or spiny appendages protruding from<br>
their bodies. Its head is on one end of its<br>
body, and is about the same diameter as<br>
the adjacent segment. Also the head has a<br>
pair of antennae that helps the millipede to<br>
sense the surrounding environment. One<br>
pair of mouthparts (jaws) is on the front<br>
part of its head and the eyes are fixed above<br>
its jaws. Most millipedes have simple eyes<br>
that provide basic vision, but not all types<br>
or species of millipedes have eyes.<br>
<b>Figure 3.51 Structure of a millipede</b><br>

<b>Adaptations of the millipede to its</b><br>
mode of life<br>
<b>Millipedes have the following features that</b><br>
make them adapt to their environment:<br>
a)	 They have many, short and strong legs<br>
that enable them to burrow into the<br>
soil.<br>
b)	 They produce special secretions,<br>
which help them to moisturize dead<br>
organic matter on which they feed<br>
upon.<br>
c)	 A millipede tends to curl up into a tight<br>
ﬂat coil for self-defence, and protect<br>
their delicate legs inside an armoured<br>
exoskeleton.<br>
d)	 Millipedes produce an offensively<br>
odorous fluid (repugnatorial fluid)<br>
when provoked, this acts as a defence<br>
against predators.<br>
<b>Class Insecta</b><br>
<b>The class contains all insects and is the</b><br>
most diverse group of organisms on<br>
earth. Members of this class are found in<br>
all environments including fresh water<br>
aquatic and terrestrial environments, but<br>
very few are found in marine habitats.<br>
<b>They differ in morphology and feeding</b><br>
habits. Although members of this group<br>
vary greatly, they have some common<br>
characteristics, which make them to<br>
belong into the same class. Members of<br>
this class have three pairs of walking legs,<br>
three distinct body parts or regions and<br>
one pair of antennae borne on the head.<br>
<b>The antennae are used as sense organs</b><br>
for detection of odour molecules in the<br>
air, changes in the concentration of water<br>
vapour, sounds, and gauging air speed.<br>
<b>Examples of insects include grasshopper,</b><br>
butterfly, house fly, cockroach, beetle,<br>
bees, ant, wasp, and termite.<br>

<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
<b>Distinctive features of class Insecta</b><br>
<b>Insects have the following features that</b><br>
differentiate them from other arthropods:<br>
a)	 Their bodies are divided into three<br>
main regions or tagmata, namely the<br>
head, thorax, and abdomen.<br>
b)	 They have three pairs of walking legs<br>
on the thorax (one pair per thoracic<br>
segment).<br>
c)	 They usually have one or two pairs of<br>
wings on the thorax, some members<br>
may lack wings.<br>
d)	 They use the tracheal system as their<br>
respiratory surface with segmental<br>
spiracles.<br>
e)	 They undergo metamorphosis during<br>
their development through the molting<br>
process.<br>
f)	 They have a pair of compound eyes<br>
and simple eyes.<br>
g)	 They usually have three pairs of<br>
mouthparts,<br>
which<br>
are<br>
maxillae,<br>
mandible, and labrum.<br>
<b>Structure of a cockroach</b><br>
<b>The body of a cockroach is elongated and</b><br>
segmented, divided into a head, thorax,<br>
and abdomen. The thorax is subdivided<br>
into three parts; prothorax, mesothorax<br>
and metathorax and each segment<br>
bears one pair of legs (prothoracic legs,<br>
mesothoracic legs and metathoracic legs<br>
respectively). The thorax also bears two<br>
pairs of wings in most adults insects. The<br>
body is covered by a rigid exoskeletons<br>
(cuticle) secreted by the epidermal layer.<br>
<b>This occurs in jointed sections or plates to</b><br>
allow body movement.  The exoskeleton<br>
is made up by a structural  protein  known<br>
as chitin. It is coated  with wax, which is<br>
impermeable to water. The exoskeleton<br>
provides attachment for body muscles.<br>
<b>Cockroaches are mostly dark brown or</b><br>
reddish in colour. Male cockroach has a<br>
narrow abdomen an expanded tergum of<br>
the last visible segment, and has a pair of<br>
styles at the end of abdomen, while the<br>
female cockroach has a wide abdomen<br>
with a large podical plates used to carry<br>
the ootheca, and lacks styles (Figure 3.53<br>
a, b and c).<br>
3.7.4 Body systems in arthropods<br>
<b>Like other animals, arthropods have</b><br>
various body systems which enable them<br>
to perform and sustain their life processes.<br>
<b>These include: respiratory, circulatory,</b><br>
digestive, nervous, reproductive and<br>
excretory systems. In this text, digestive<br>
and reproductive systems will be dealt<br>
with.<br>

<b>Digestive system of a cockroach</b><br>
<b>The alimentary canal of the cockroach is</b><br>
long and coiled tubular structure starting at<br>
the mouth opening. It is divided into three<br>
main parts, namely foregut (stomodaeum),<br>
midgut (mesenteron or ventriculus), and<br>
hindgut (proctodaeum). The foregut is<br>
differentiated into ﬁve parts: Buccal<br>
chamber, pharynx, oesophagus, crop, and<br>
gizzard. The fist two parts (buccal cavity<br>
and pharynx) are found inside the head<br>
capsule and not visible in the dissected<br>
cockroach, while  the other three part<br>
are visible. The gizzard is muscular<br>
and internally provided with six cutical<br>
pointed teeth, for crushing the food.<br>
<b>The midgut is short and tubular lined with</b><br>
glandular endoderm. At the anterior end<br>
of midgut there are eight blind glandular<br>

(digestive/mesenteric<br>

absorption. The cells lining the mesenteron<br>
secrete enzymes. Most of the food nutrients<br>
are digested in the mesenteron and the end<br>
products of the digested food substances<br>
are absorbed by the cells of mesenteron<br>
and digestive caeca. The distal end of the<br>
midgut is indicated by the presence of<br>
malpighian tubules which are excretory in<br>
function (Figure 3.54).<br>
<b>The hindgut comprises of the ileum,</b><br>
colon, and rectum. The ileum is short<br>
and narrow and receives the openings<br>
of the malpighian tubules. On the other<br>
hand, colon is wider and longer than the<br>
rectum. The wall of the rectum is provided<br>
with six rectal papillae, which help in<br>
the absorption of water and salts. The<br>
digestive  system terminates with the anal<br>
opening  (anus) through which undigested<br>
material is removed in process known as<br>
egestion.<br>

<b>Reproductive system of a male</b><br>
cockroach<br>
<b>The male cockroach has a pair of anal styles;</b><br>
the external structure which differentiate<br>
the male cockroach from female. Testes<br>
are small lobed masses (lobules) lying<br>
laterodorsally in the fourth and fifth<br>
abdominal segments. There are two testes<br>
(one on the right and the other on the left),<br>
each joined to a slender vas diferens (plural<br>
vasa diferentia) which connect to the wider<br>
ejaculatory duct.<br>
<b>Lobules of testes</b><br>
<b>Mushroom gland</b><br>
<b>Ejaculatory duct</b><br>

<b>Cercus</b><br>

<b>The testes produce sperms which transfered</b><br>
through the vasa differentia to the  seminal<br>
vesicle. All sperms from a seminal vesicle<br>
are collected together into a large bundle<br>
called spermatophore (sperm pouch).<br>
<b>There are three asymmetrical chitinous</b><br>
structures, called male gonapophyses or<br>
phallomeres; the right phallomere, the<br>

left phallomere (largest) and the ventral<br>
phallomere (smallest). These, together with<br>
the pseudo-penis, form the organ used to<br>
transfer the sperm pouch to the female<br>
during copulation (Figure 3.55).<br>
<b>Reproductive system of a female</b><br>
cockroach<br>
<b>The female organs consist of ovaries,</b><br>
oviduct, vagina, genital chamber,<br>
spermathecae, collateral glands, and female<br>
gonapophyses (ovipositor processes).<br>
<b>Ovaries of the cockroach are located</b><br>
laterally in the abdominal segments four,<br>
five and six. Each ovary consists of eight<br>
ovarioles. (Figure 3.56).<br>
<b>One oviduct from each side open into a</b><br>
genital chamber at a slit-like aperture. A pair<br>
of collateral glands also open in the genital<br>
chamber. A genital pouch or gynatrium is<br>
divisible into a genital chamber in front and<br>
the oothecal chamber behind.<br>
<b>Female gonapophyses consist of three pairs</b><br>
of chitinous rods hanging from the roof<br>
of the oothecal chamber into its cavity.<br>
<b>They help in shaping the ootheca and</b><br>
depositing eggs in it. The ootheca of the<br>
cockroach contains sixteen fertilised eggs,<br>
coated with the secretions (protein, and<br>
dihydroxyphenol) of the colleterial glands.<br>
<b>The eggs are lying in two rows, each with</b><br>
eight eggs. The female cockroach carries the<br>
ootheca for several days and finally protrude<br>
more and more until it is deposited to the<br>
dark crevice, and for about six weeks, the<br>
young cockroaches hatch out and grows<br>
into adult.<br>

<b>Dissection of a cockroach</b><br>
<b>Animals are dissected to analyse the structure</b><br>
and function of their body components.<br>
<b>Dissection provides the crucial opportunity</b><br>
for students and other scientists to develop<br>
scientific observational skills. The methods<br>
of investigating gross structure depend on<br>
careful dissection or cutting apart, of an<br>
organism and on accurate descriptions of<br>
the body parts. Dissection involves attentive<br>
isolation and removal of individual organs,<br>
accessing the area in which the organs are<br>
situated, and systematically removing the<br>
anatomical connections of organ to its<br>
surrounding.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   184</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
185<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
<b>Dissection of a cockroach allows learning</b><br>
of the location and appearance of internal<br>
organs; distinguishing among different<br>
types of tissues within an insect body<br>
and to describe the major body systems.<br>
<b>The following practical guidelines in</b><br>
dissecting a cockroach, permits the<br>
studies of internal features of the insect,<br>
including its heart, circulatory, digestive and<br>
reproductive systems. Materials necessary<br>
for the dissection of a cockroach include<br>
dissection kit, dissecting tray or board, a<br>
piece of thread, dissecting pins or office<br>
pins, scissors, fine-point forceps gloves<br>
and chloroform.<br>
<b>General procedure for dissecting a</b><br>
cockroach<br>
a)	 Melt little wax in the centre of<br>
dissecting tray.<br>
b)	 Wear a pair of gloves.<br>
c)	 Cut the insect wings, antennae and legs<br>
close to their base.<br>
d)	 Place the cockroach in the melted wax<br>
or use dissecting pins, to place the<br>
cockroach on its ventral side (sternum)<br>
while the dorsal side (tergum) is facing<br>
upwards.<br>
e)	 Allow the wax to cool, and then the<br>
insect will be fixed (Figure 3.57).<br>
<b>Figure 3.57 Cockroach fixed for dissection</b><br>
f)	 Since cockroach’s heart is on its dorsal<br>
side attached inside the terga and<br>
nervous system is on its ventral side,<br>
when dissecting, cut either on its left or<br>
right lateral side (pleural) from the last<br>
segment of the abdomen to the thorax.<br>
(a)<br>
(b)<br>
Figure 3.58 Fixed cockroach showing (a) lifted aside terga (b) pined terga<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   185</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
186<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
g)	 Carefully lift the last abdominal tergum<br>
using a forcep, then cut one side of the<br>
abdomen. Work forward by lifting<br>
aside the terga with forceps and cut<br>
around the edge with small scissor<br>
or scalpel up to the base of the head<br>
(Figure 3.58a).<br>
h)	 Place a pin to hold the terga aside (you<br>
can use an optical pin to clearly pin it<br>
and ease observation of all parts in the<br>
terga as well as those of the abdominal<br>
cavity (Figure 3.58b).<br>
i)	 Remove fats to expose the gut and<br>
other organs in the body cavity.<br>
j)	 Cover your dissection with water to<br>
bring the organs float up, prevent them<br>
from drying up and to enable them<br>
being seen clearly.<br>
k)	 Loosen the gut, deflect to one side and<br>
pin it to display all the systems (Figure<br>
3.59).<br>

l)	 Make sure the body wall is neatly<br>
pinned aside, the dissection is generally<br>
neat with all required features; avoid<br>
damaging the organs and blocking the<br>
ducts.<br>
m)	 Carefully examine all internal parts<br>
using a hand lens.<br>
<b>Safety precautions</b><br>
1.	 Care should be taken when working<br>
with live specimens and apparatus,<br>
including dissection kit tools.<br>
2.	 The working area, equipment and<br>
instruments used must be thoroughly<br>
cleaned and decontaminated after<br>
use, by using appropriate methods.<br>
3.	 Good hygiene practices should be<br>
observed all the time; hands should be<br>
kept away from the mouth, nose, eyes,<br>
and face during and after dissection.<br>

<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
<b>Hands should be thoroughly washed</b><br>
with soap or disinfectant immediately<br>
after conducting a dissection practical<br>
session.<br>
4.	 All other safety laboratory rules<br>
should be observed; the teacher or<br>
laboratory technician should guide<br>
the students.<br>
<b>Activity 3.13 Dissection of a</b><br>
cockroach to display the digestive<br>
system<br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>Fresh male or female cockroach,</b><br>
dissection kit, chloroform, dissecting<br>
tray or dish with wax intact, source of<br>
heat, and water.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Collect a fresh male or female<br>
cockroach.<br>
b)	 Prepare a dissecting tray or board<br>
and chloroform for anaesthetizing<br>
the specimen.<br>
c)	 Dissect the cockroach in a usual<br>
way; deflect the gut to your right<br>
hand side, to fully display the<br>
digestive system.<br>
<b>Questions</b><br>
1.	 Draw a diagram of your dissection<br>
and label only the parts of the<br>
digestive system. Compare your<br>
diagram with that of Figure 3.59.<br>
2.	 State the function (s) of each<br>
labelled part.<br>
3.	 Explain how the central location<br>
of a gizzard helps the organism in<br>
digestion.<br>
4.	 Differentiate the crop from the<br>
digestive caeca.<br>
<b>Note:</b><br>
1.	 The diagrams should be well drawn,<br>
large enough, generally neat and<br>
accurate.<br>
2.	 The caption should be well indicated;<br>
when written on top of the diagram it<br>
should be capitalised and underlined,<br>
while when written below the diagram,<br>
the first later should be upper case and<br>
should not be underlined.<br>
3.	 Label lines should be indicated in<br>
pencil; should be straight without<br>
arrows; and should not cross each<br>
other.<br>
4.	 Labelling words should be on the<br>
required parts and written using blue<br>
or black ink.<br>
5.	 The magnification of the diagram<br>
should correspond to the real<br>
specimen.<br>

10/10/2019   14:06<br>

<b>Activity 3:14 Dissection of a cockroach</b><br>
to display the reproductive system<br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>Fresh male or female cockroach,</b><br>
dissection kit, chloroform, dissecting<br>
tray or board with wax intact, source of<br>
heat, and water.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Collect a fresh male or female<br>
cockroach. With reasons identify<br>
the sex of your specimen.<br>
b)	 Prepare a dissecting tray or dish<br>
and chloroform for anaesthetizing<br>
the specimen.<br>
c)	 Dissect the cockroach in a usual<br>
way and deflect the gut to the left<br>
hand side of the animal, to fully<br>
display the reproductive system.<br>
<b>Questions</b><br>
1.	 Draw a well labelled diagram of<br>
your dissection. Compare your<br>
diagram with that of Figure 3.55 or<br>
Figure 3.56.<br>
2.	 State the role(s) of each labelled<br>
part.<br>
3.	 Classify the organism to the class<br>
level.<br>
<b>Adaptations of Arthropods to their</b><br>
environment<br>
<b>Arthropods are the most abundant and</b><br>
diverse group of organisms on earth. They<br>
possess the following adaptive features,<br>
which help them to be a successful diverse<br>
group:<br>
a)	 They possess a cuticle, which prevents<br>
water loss and makes them resistant<br>
to desiccation, predators, invasion<br>
by parasites, as well as physical and<br>
mechanical injury.<br>
b)	 They have modified and specialised<br>
mouthparts<br>
according<br>
to<br>
their<br>
feeding habits; their mouthparts have<br>
mandibles for cutting and chewing,<br>
piercing, and sucking as in mosquitoes.<br>
c)	 They have a well developed and<br>
efﬁcient respiratory system extending<br>
towards the individual cells in which<br>
gaseous exchange takes place.<br>
d)	 They<br>
possess<br>
ﬂexible<br>
jointed<br>
appendages which facilitate rapid<br>
movement with minimum utilisation<br>
of muscles.<br>
e)	 They possess sensory organs such as<br>
antennae, compound eyes, and cerci,<br>
which are capable of detecting slight<br>
movements, sounds, or chemicals.<br>
f)	 They undergo moulting (ecdysis)<br>
process<br>
during<br>
development<br>
by<br>
removing their hard covers, and<br>
become soft to facilitate growth.<br>
g)	 Their small and flattened body shapes<br>
enable them to hide into small cracks<br>
and crevices, where they live.<br>
h)	 They possess coloured cuticle, which<br>
provides camouﬂage against predators.<br>
i)	 Wings help them in dispersal and<br>
colonization of new habitats.<br>
<b>Economic importance of Arthropods</b><br>
<b>Arthropods play an important role in the</b><br>
world. However, not all arthropods are<br>
useful; some are harmful to other living<br>
organisms.<br>

189<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
<b>Advantages of Arthropods</b><br>
a)	 Insects such as bees and butterﬂies<br>
are useful in agriculture; as they act<br>
as pollinators; many plants depend on<br>
insects for pollination.<br>
b)	 They are used in industries for<br>
production<br>
of<br>
honey<br>
and<br>
wax<br>
materials; for example honey bees.<br>
c)	 Insects are used in biological control<br>
of pests; in certain cases, insects have<br>
been very effective predators to reduce<br>
the number of pests. For example, lady<br>
beetles are used to kill aphids (the pest<br>
insects that transmit viruses to plants).<br>
d)	 Many arthropods are used as food;<br>
they are a good source of protein for<br>
humans and other animals. Example<br>
include shrimps and lobsters, crabs,<br>
edible<br>
grasshoppers,<br>
locusts<br>
and<br>
grasshoppers, and many larvae of<br>
beetles and moths.<br>
e)	 Insects are used in research and<br>
biological studies.<br>
f)	 Insects such a as butterflies are valued<br>
for their beauty.<br>
g)	 Many arthropods are used as indicators<br>
of the quality of environment.<br>
<b>Disadvantages of Arthropods</b><br>
a)	 Some insects are vectors of animal<br>
and plant diseases. For example,<br>
mosquitoes carry Plasmodium, which<br>
cause malaria, and tsetse ﬂies carry<br>
<b>Trypanosoma, which cause sleeping</b><br>
sickness to human beings. Whiteflies<br>
carry Tomato yellow leaf curl virus<br>
which cause chlorosis in plants.<br>
b)	 Some arthropods such as scorpions<br>
and centipedes bite human beings,<br>
release their poisons causing severe<br>
pains.<br>
c)	 Some arthropods destroy crop plants;<br>
examples include locusts and the<br>
caterpillars of butterflies and moths.<br>
d)	 Some arthropods are parasitic to<br>
mammals. Good examples of such<br>
arthropods are ticks, jiggers, and<br>
bedbugs, which grip themselves on<br>
human skin and suck blood.<br>
3.7.5  Phylum Chordata<br>
<b>This is the third largest phylum in the</b><br>
kingdom Animalia. The phylum comprises<br>
of all animals that at some point during<br>
their lives, possess a hollow nerve cord<br>
and notochord.<br>
<b>General characteristics of phylum</b><br>
<b>Chordata</b><br>
a)	 They have a notochord at some stages<br>
of their development. Notochord is<br>
a stiff, ﬂexible rod located dorsal to<br>
the gut and ventral to the nerve cord.<br>
<b>In most chordates the notochord</b><br>
is replaced by a vertebral column<br>
(backbone) during adulthood.<br>
b)	 They have visceral clefts which are<br>
slits perforating the body wall in the<br>
pharynx. They are useful in ﬁshes<br>
and tadpole larva of amphibians for<br>
gaseous exchange as they use gills. In<br>
adult amphibians, reptiles, aves and<br>
mammals, the visceral clefts never<br>
develop gills.<br>
c)	 They have endoskeletons made up of<br>
bone and cartilage.<br>
d)	 They have post-anal tail.<br>
e)	 They have a dorsal hollow nerve cord<br>
found above the notochord and below<br>
the epidermis. In higher chordates,<br>
the anterior part forms a brain and the<br>
posterior part forms a spinal cord.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   189</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
