<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
190<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
f)	 They have ventrally positioned heart,<br>
which is a pumping organ as it pumps<br>
blood. Moreover, they have closed<br>
circulatory system.<br>
g)	 They are triploblastic, coelomate<br>
animals with bilaterally symmetrical<br>
body.<br>
h)	 They have two pairs of pentadactyl<br>
limbs or fins formed from more than<br>
one body segment. In higher chordates<br>
they are attached to the rest of the<br>
skeleton by pelvic and pectoral girdles.<br>
i)	 Some members (cartilagenous fish,<br>
bony fish, amphibians and reptiles)<br>
are poikilothermic organisms, in that<br>
their body temperature depends on the<br>
environmental temperature changes<br>
while others (birds and mammals)<br>
are homeothermic organisms, as their<br>
body temperature remains constant<br>
regardless of the changes in external<br>
environmental temperature.<br>
<b>Classes of phylum Chordata</b><br>
<b>There are six classes of chordates namely;</b><br>
<b>Chondrichthyes (cartilagenous ﬁsh such</b><br>
as shark and rays), Osteichthyes (bony<br>
ﬁsh such as tilapia and tuna), Amphibia<br>
(amphibians such as frog and toad),<br>
<b>Reptilia (reptiles such as lizard, crocodile,</b><br>
and snake), Aves (birds such as hen,<br>
parrot, eagle, and pigeon) and Mammalia<br>
(mammals such as human, mouse, bat,<br>
rabbit and monkey).<br>
<b>Class Chondrichthyes</b><br>
<b>All members of this class have paired fins,</b><br>
paired nares, jaws, and a two chambered<br>
heart. Additionally, they have scales<br>
and endoskeletons made of cartilage.<br>
<b>Members</b><br>
of<br>
class<br>
<b>Chondrichthyes</b><br>
include dogfish, sharks, skates, and rays.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of class</b><br>
<b>Chondrichthyes</b><br>
<b>Members of the class Chondrichthyes</b><br>
have the following unique features,<br>
which distinguish them from other fishes.<br>
a)	 They possess placoid scales (tooth-<br>
like) covering their skin.<br>
b)	 They have cartilaginous endoskeleton.<br>
c)	 They have five pairs of visceral clefts<br>
as separate gill slits for gaseous<br>
exchange,with no opercula  or gill<br>
covers.<br>
d)	 They have ventral mouths.<br>
e)	 They have heterocercal tail ﬁns<br>
(asymmetric in shape), which prevent<br>
them from sinking as they lack swim<br>
bladder for buoyancy.<br>
f)	 They have paired ﬂeshy pectoral and<br>
pelvic ﬁns for swimming.<br>
g)	 They undergo internal fertilisation.<br>
<b>Structure of a shark</b><br>
<b>The body is more or less completely</b><br>
covered by placoid scales and has a<br>
lateral line that runs along the side of its<br>
body for detecting water currents and<br>
wave intensity. Its pectoral fin is anterior<br>
to the pelvic fins; usually the latter has<br>
claspers (pterygopodes) which are used<br>
as copulatory organs in males, and the<br>
caudal fin is heterocercal in shape. The<br>
mouth is located ventrally with teeth,<br>
which are constantly replaced. It has small<br>
eyes, which have lower lids used to cover<br>
the eyes during feeding (Figure 3.60).<br>
<b>The gill slits are naked (no operculum)</b><br>
and has a spiracle which is a modified<br>
gill slit on top of the head that provides<br>
oxygenated blood directly to the eye and<br>
brain through a separate blood vessel.<br>
<b>The intestine is short but with absorptive</b><br>
features increased by spiral valves.<br>

<b>Class Osteichthyes</b><br>
<b>The class includes all bony ﬁshes, such</b><br>
as tuna, tilapia, rainbow fish, herring and<br>
catfish. They are all aquatic organisms<br>
found in marine and fresh water bodies.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of class</b><br>
<b>Osteichthyes</b><br>
<b>The</b><br>
following<br>
features<br>
differentiate<br>
members of the class Osteichthyes from<br>
other fishes.<br>
a)	 They have thin, round, and cycloid<br>
scales (made up of bones) on their<br>
skins, which are impermeable to water,<br>
hence reducing water loss in marine<br>
environment, and restrict entry of water<br>
in fresh water ﬁsh.<br>
b)	 They are bony fish, as they have bony<br>
skeletons.<br>
c)	 They have paired pectoral and pelvic<br>
ﬁns, supported by bony rays for<br>
swimming.<br>
d)	 They have homocercal (symmetrical)<br>
tail ﬁns.<br>
e)	 They have four pairs of visceral clefts<br>
as separate gill openings covered by<br>
opercula.<br>
f)	 They produce eggs and undergo<br>
external fertilisation.<br>
g)	 They have swim bladder, which is<br>
in the abdominal cavity next to the<br>
backbone. This helps them to rise and<br>
sink (buoyancy) in water at particular<br>
depth without using much energy.<br>
h)	 They have a terminal mouth.<br>
<b>Structure of Tilapia</b><br>
<b>The body is laterally ﬂattened and</b><br>
tapered at both ends. Has a streamlined<br>
shape to overcome water resistance<br>
during swimming. Its body surface is<br>
covered by cycloid scales, which point<br>
backwards in order to reduce resistance<br>
during swimming. The fish has a lateral<br>
line that runs along the side of its body.<br>
<b>The lateral line is a series of sensory</b><br>
organs called neuromasts that helps<br>
the ﬁsh to sense vibrations and water<br>
pressure for navigating and locating prey<br>
(Figure 3.61).<br>

<b>Class Amphibia</b><br>
<b>This class includes amphibians such</b><br>
as frogs, toads, caecilians as well as<br>
salamanders. Amphibians are tetrapod<br>
with the exception of caecilians, which<br>
are limbless. All amphibians lay eggs<br>
with the exception of some caecilians and<br>
some toads. For example members of the<br>
caecelian genera Scolecomorphus and<br>
Schistometopum give birth to live young.<br>
<b>Also the toad genus Nectophrynoides;</b><br>
the members of this genus also give<br>
birth to live young.  In the latter genus<br>
of Nectophrvnoides there is the Kihansi<br>
<b>Spray Toad (Nectophrynoides asperginis);</b><br>
a small toad endemic to Tanzania with this<br>
unique characteristic of giving birth to<br>
live young toadlets.<br>
<b>The word amphibia refers to “double life”,</b><br>
or life in water and on land. Amphibians<br>
are<br>
cold-blooded<br>
(ectothermic)<br>
vertebrates whose body temperature is not<br>
regulated by internal mechanisms. They<br>
inhabit a wide range of habitats, ranging<br>
from terrestrial to aquatic environments.<br>
<b>Most amphibians spend a part of their</b><br>
life in aquatic environment and another<br>
part in terrestrial environment. Due to<br>
their life cycle and physiology, many<br>
amphibians are “tied” to water bodies in<br>
order to survive and reproduce. Normally,<br>
amphibians undergo metamorphosis from<br>
a juvenile as an aquatic larval form into a<br>
terrestrial adult.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of class Amphibia</b><br>
<b>Amphibians have the following distinctive</b><br>
features:<br>
a)	 They dwell both in water and on land, as<br>
they depend on water for reproduction,<br>
hence the name amphibian, which<br>
means double life.<br>
b)	 Amphibians’ offspring begin their life<br>
cycle under water and breath by means<br>
of gills. As they grow to adulthood<br>
they move to terrestrial and breathe by<br>
means of either skin or lungs.<br>
c)	 They have soft moist skin without<br>
scale, used for gaseous exchange to<br>
supplement lungs and buccal cavity.<br>
d)	 Amphibian eggs have a jelly structure,<br>
which is prone to dehydration when<br>
exposed to air.<br>
e)	 Amphibian’s fertilization takes place<br>
outside the female body (with the<br>
exception of some caecilians and some<br>
toads including the Kihansi spray toad<br>
which give birth to live young).<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   192</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
193<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
f)	 They have two pairs of pentadactyl<br>
limbs for locomotion. The forelimbs<br>
have less musculature, while the<br>
hind limbs are webbed and long with<br>
powerful muscles for jumping.<br>
g)	 They have long and protruding eyes<br>
for viewing preys widely and for<br>
avoiding enemies.<br>
h)	 They have sticky tongue, which helps<br>
them in capturing prey.<br>
i)	 They undergo metamorphosis for<br>
development from larval to adult stage<br>
in their life cycle.<br>
<b>Structure of a frog</b><br>
<b>The body of the adult frog is divisible</b><br>
into the head and trunk. The neck and tail<br>
parts are absent. The head is blunt, and<br>
the mouth is terminal, with two ﬂexibly<br>
movable jaws. The head bears external<br>
nares, a pair of nostrils that lead into nasal<br>
passages. The eyes are large, bulged and<br>
covered by a nictitating membrane that<br>
protects them while in water. Behind the<br>
eye, there is a circular patch, a tympanum<br>
or eardrum, which receives sound signals.<br>
<b>The trunk widens in the middle, but</b><br>
tapers towards the end; four legs help in<br>
swimming and jumping. The hind limbs<br>
end in ﬁve digits and are larger and more<br>
muscular than the fore limbs that end in<br>
four digits. In both, fore and hind limbs,<br>
the digits are webbed for swimming<br>
purpose (Figure 3.62a). Frogs exhibit<br>
sexual dimorphism in that male frogs<br>
have sound producing vocal sacs and<br>
copulatory pads on the ﬁrst digit of the<br>
fore limbs, whereas in female frogs these<br>
are absent. Moreover, the abdomen of the<br>
male frog is much slender than that of the<br>
female frog.<br>
<b>Structure of Kihansi spray toad</b><br>
<b>The Kihansi spray toad is a tiny toad of</b><br>
just about two centimetres in length,<br>
endemic to a two hectare area of Kihansi<br>
<b>Gorge, of the Udzungwa Mountains, in</b><br>
the southern central of Tanzania. The<br>
toad is a highly specialized toad species,<br>
adapted to giving birth to a fully formed<br>
toadlet. Currently it is one of the very<br>
few amphibian species known to give<br>
birth to fully formed toadlets. The overall<br>
background colour of Kihansi spray toad<br>
is golden yellowish, with yellow and<br>
brown speckles on the dorsal surface, or<br>
dark lateral bands with adjacent lighter<br>
striping (Figure 3.62b). Ventrally, the skin<br>
is translucent, whitish near the throat and<br>
posterior, with the liver, fat bodies, and<br>
intestines visible through the ventral skin.<br>
<b>Moreover, these toads have flaps over the</b><br>
nostrils that may be a special adaptation<br>
for living in the spray zone of waterfalls.<br>
<b>On the feet, toes are partially webbed,</b><br>
with no external tympana.<br>
<b>Body systems of chordates</b><br>
<b>Chordates have a number of body systems</b><br>
which enable them to perform various<br>
physiological processes. Digestive and<br>
urinogenital systems of a frog or toad and<br>
rat or mouse will be emphasized in this text.<br>

<b>Digestive system of a frog</b><br>
<b>The alimentary canal of a frog consists</b><br>
of the mouth, buccal cavity, pharynx,<br>
oesophagus, duodenum, ileum, and the<br>
rectum, which open into the cloaca at the<br>
anus. The mouth is wide for ingestion of<br>
large pieces of food material. They have<br>
flattened buccal cavity that emerges with<br>
the pharynx and it contains small, conical<br>
and sharp pointed teeth which are similar<br>
(homodont) used for cutting and crushing<br>
the food particles.  Close to the angles of<br>
the jaws, there are two small openings, one<br>
on each side, known as eustachian tubes.<br>
<b>These tubes are used for balancing pressure</b><br>
in the inner ear while the frog is swimming.<br>
<b>Ventrally in the midline  there is a narrow</b><br>
longitudinal slit (glottis) which leads into<br>
the larynx.<br>
<b>They have a short oesophagus that bears</b><br>
longitudinal folds, which close to prevent<br>
entry of air into the stomach and allow<br>
dilation during swallowing of food. Buccal<br>
cavity, pharynx and oesophagus have cilia,<br>
which constantly drive the mucus backward<br>
into the stomach to support swallowing<br>
and ensure that small food particles are not<br>
retained in the anterior parts. The stomach<br>
is thick walled, folded and elongated to<br>
increase the surface area for secretion of<br>
gastric juice used for digestion.<br>
<b>The duodenum lies parallel to the stomach</b><br>
and receives the secretions of bile and<br>
pancreas via hepato-pancreatic duct. The<br>
internal surface of the duodenum has many<br>
folds, which increase the surface area for<br>
secretion and absorption. The duodenum<br>
connects to the ileum, which has several<br>
coils, and longitudinal internal folds,<br>
where most of the digested food substances<br>
are absorbed here. The alimentary canal<br>
terminates in a rectum, which is short, and<br>
wide. Rectum is the place where feaces<br>
accumulate, and later pass periodically<br>
through the anal sphincter into the cloaca<br>
and eventually egested out of the body.<br>
<b>The liver consists of two large lobes (the</b><br>
left lobe and the median lobe). The median<br>
lobe is smaller than the left lobe. The gall<br>
bladder (lies between the liver lobes)<br>
and the bile duct run into the duodenum<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   194</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
195<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
through the pancreas. The pancreas lies<br>
between the stomach and the duodenum.<br>
<b>The secretions are discharged into the bile</b><br>
duct, which become hepato-pancreatic duct<br>
and later discharge into the duodenum. In<br>
the mesentery near the duodenum lies a<br>
spherical red structure called spleen, which<br>
has no digestive role (Figure 3.63).<br>

<b>Urinogenital system of a male frog</b><br>
<b>A male frog is slender, with nuptial pads</b><br>
on the first finger which is one of the<br>
morphological features that distinguish<br>
it from female frog, which is fat, with an<br>
expanded abdomen due to the presence<br>
of eggs in the ovisacs. A male frog has<br>
two kidneys, which are dark red, oval,<br>
and somehow flattened, located near<br>
to the testes, ventrally and below the<br>
ureter. The ureter passes along the outer<br>
border of each kidney, and opens into the<br>
cloaca. The bladder is a thin sac arising<br>
as a ventral outgrowth, the cloaca, with<br>
its outlet closed by a sphincter. Since<br>
the ureter is not opening to the bladder,<br>
it is not a urinary bladder, but contains<br>
fluids possibly secreted by the blood<br>
vessels from its wall, which acts as a<br>
reserve of water during dry seasons.<br>
<b>Cloaca is an outlet that serves as the</b><br>
only opening for digestive, reproductive<br>
and urinary tracts. Also, a frog has two<br>
testes, each suspended by a double fold<br>
of mesorchium. They lie ventrally below<br>
the front part of the kidneys, and from<br>
them there are several fine vasa efferentia<br>
that cross the mesorchium and enter the<br>
anterior kidney tubules which convey<br>
sperms to the ureter. In both left and right<br>
ureter, there is a pouch-like gland situated<br>
on each side of the male urinary bladder<br>
(vesicula seminalis) which store sperms<br>
until the breeding period (Figure 3.64).<br>

<b>The ovaries have flattened and numerous</b><br>
half black and half white eggs which<br>
can be seen through epithelium. They<br>
are connected to a long thin tube called<br>
oviduct. Each oviduct is dilated in its<br>
posterior region to form an ovisac, which<br>
store eggs until the laying period. The<br>
kidneys are connected to a tube called<br>
ureter that is used solely as a urinary duct,<br>
while in male frogs it passes both, sperms<br>
and urine. The ovisac and ureter open to<br>
the cloaca (Figure 3.65).<br>
<b>Dissection of a frog or toad</b><br>
<b>Dissecting a frog or toad is a common and</b><br>
important experience in the structural and<br>
anatomical studies of a typical vertebrate.<br>
<b>The inside of a frog represents the general</b><br>
form for a vertebrate; as the organs present<br>
in a frog and the way they are laid out  are<br>
similar enough to that of other animals.<br>
<b>General procedure for dissection of a</b><br>
frog or toad<br>
a)	 Put on a pair of gloves.<br>
b)	 Wet a piece of cotton wool with<br>
chloroform and put it inside an airtight<br>
container such as desiccator.<br>
c)	 Put a live frog or toad inside the<br>
container, containing wet cotton wool,<br>
and leave it for about four to five<br>
minutes to anaesthetize it.<br>
d)	 Take the frog or toad from the airtight<br>
container using forceps, place it on the<br>
dissecting tray or board and leave it for<br>
about 30 minutes to allow evaporation<br>
of chloroform.<br>
e)	 Lay the frog or toad on its dorsal<br>
side (back), leaving the ventral side<br>
(abdomen) facing upwards (Figure<br>
3.66).<br>
<b>Figure 3.66 Positioning the frog or toad for</b><br>
dissection<br>
f)	 Pin the frog down on the tray through<br>
the fore and hind limbs; the pin on<br>
each hand and foot should be at an<br>
angle to the tension put on it.<br>
g)	 Use forceps to lift the skin of the<br>
abdomen and use scissors to make a<br>
slit in the mid ventral line, and then<br>
insert one blade of the scissors into the<br>
slit (Figure 3.67).<br>
<b>Figure 3.67 Opening the body cavity of an</b><br>
anesthetized frog or toad<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   197</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
198<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Cut the skin forward to the level of</b><br>
the lower jaw; cut transversely at the<br>
level of each arm as far as the elbows.<br>
<b>Similarly cut the skin back to the level</b><br>
of the pelvic girdle and cut towards<br>
the side of each hind limb down to the<br>
knee.<br>
h)	 Hold the skin with forceps and loosen<br>
the skin from the underlying muscles<br>
using the surgical blade, turn the skin<br>
flap back and pin it.<br>
i)	 Ligature the ventral abdominal vein at<br>
two sides. Use scissors to make two<br>
small slits and insert a loop of thread<br>
through the slits by using forceps and<br>
grip between their points; pull the<br>
thread through the slits and cut the<br>
loop, and then tie the threads apart<br>
in order to prevent bleeding and cut<br>
between the ligatured points (Figure<br>
3.68).<br>
<b>A hole</b><br>
ligature<br>
<b>Sting</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.68 Ligaturing of the a frog</b><br>
j)	 Lift the abdominal vein by using<br>
forceps and cut alongside it to the<br>
breast bone (xiphisternum). Then,<br>
hold the loose piece of the abdominal<br>
wall up and cut across the centre of<br>
the xiphisternum. Grip the cut piece,<br>
loosen it as far as the ligatured part and<br>
cut it close to that part; be careful not<br>
to cut the vein.<br>
k)	 Cut through the pectoral girdle on<br>
either side of the mid-line and remove<br>
the central piece of the girdle to expose<br>
the heart, and then cut the body wall<br>
transversely below the arms.<br>
l)	 Turn the tray in such a way that the legs<br>
are facing away from you, cut the body<br>
wall on either side of the mid-line to<br>
the pelvic area, remove the portion of<br>
the body wall and cut transversely to<br>
each leg. Pin aside the body wall and<br>
turn the tray to its original position.<br>
<b>At this point the body cavity will be</b><br>
already opened up.<br>
m)	Remove any debris and leave the<br>
dissection clean.<br>
n)	 Cover the dissection completely with<br>
water to avoid the drying of the organs<br>
and enhance clear observation.<br>
o)	 With the help of a hand lens, observe<br>
the visceral (general) view of the<br>
dissected frog and note the position<br>
and shape of the organs (Figure 3.69).<br>

abdominal vein<br>

<b>Stomach</b><br>
Figure 3.69 General view (visceral) of the dissected frog or toad<br>
p)	 In displaying the digestive system, pin<br>
out the stomach to either side of the<br>
animal. Pin out the lungs, and turn the<br>
liver lobes forward and hold them in<br>
place with pins and grip the ileum to<br>
cut the mesentery and loosen the coils,<br>
but do not cut the mesentery of the<br>
duodenum and rectum. Rearrange the<br>
duodenum in position to expose the<br>
pancreas and pin out the ileum to your<br>
right hand side to make all features<br>
visible.<br>
q)	 In displaying the urinogenital system,<br>
the cloaca should be opened by<br>
removing the ventral portion of the<br>
pelvic girdles. This should be done<br>
by inserting one blade of the scissors<br>
through the girdle, followed by cutting<br>
through the girdle as near to the mid<br>
line as possible, and lastly cutting on<br>
each side and removing the central<br>
portion of the girdle. Care should<br>
be taken to avoid cutting the blood<br>
vessels.<br>
r)	 In case of a female frog; pin the right<br>
ovary on top of the left ovary, loosen<br>
and pin out the right oviduct and pin<br>
the bladder aside.<br>
s)	 For the nervous system; remove<br>
the pins from the skin of the head<br>
and remove the floor of the mouth<br>
by cutting through angle of the jaw<br>
to expose the first spinal nerve (the<br>
hypoglossal nerve) which curves<br>
the pharynx and proceeds forward<br>
ventrally around the floor of the mouth<br>
to supply the tongue.<br>
t)	 Remove the flesh from both fore limbs<br>
around the shoulders and remove the<br>
pins from the lungs and the stomach.<br>
<b>Cut through the oesophagus and</b><br>
remove the lungs, heart, stomach and<br>
other parts of the alimentary canal.<br>
u)	 Remove the reproductive and excretory<br>
parts, when removing the kidney. Take<br>
care not to cut the aorta.<br>
v)	 Remove any remaining membranes<br>
surrounding the abdominal lymph sacs<br>
in order to expose the second spinal<br>
nerve called the brachial nerve (which<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   199</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>

receives branches from the first and<br>
third nerve to form the brachial plexus)<br>
and other 3rd to 10th spinal nerves.<br>
w)	Trace the sympathetic cords on<br>
either side of the aorta, notice the<br>
sympathetic ganglia and identify the<br>
rami communicanes between the<br>
spinal nerves and the sympathetic<br>
ganglia.<br>
x)	 Cut the flesh of the pelvic girdles<br>
through both thighs and trace the sciatic<br>
plexus (formed as an interconnection<br>
of the seventh, eighth, ninth and tenth)<br>
and the sciatic nerve (which passes<br>
down the leg close to the femur, and<br>
composed of the mainly joined eighth<br>
and ninth nerves).<br>
<b>Safety precautions</b><br>
1.	 Care should be taken when working<br>
with live specimens such as frogs<br>
or<br>
toads,<br>
also<br>
chemicals<br>
like<br>
chloroform, as well as apparatus and<br>
equipment including the dissection<br>
kit tools.<br>
2.	 The working area, equipment and<br>
instruments used must be thoroughly<br>
cleaned after use using appropriate<br>
methods. Good hygiene practices<br>
should be observed at all times;<br>
keep hands away from the mouth,<br>
nose, eyes and face during and<br>
after dissection; and wash hands<br>
thoroughly using an antiseptic or<br>
soap immediately after conducting a<br>
dissection practical session.<br>
3.	 All other laboratory safety rules and<br>
regulations should be adhered to<br>
under the supervision of a teacher<br>
or laboratory technician whenever<br>
working in the laboratory.<br>
<b>Activity 3.15 Dissection of a frog/toad</b><br>
to display the visceral or  general view<br>
(in situ) and digestive system.<br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>Fresh male or female frog or toad,</b><br>
dissecting kit, dissecting tray or board,<br>
a piece of thread, chloroform, water, a<br>
pair of gloves, and cotton wool.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Dissect the frog or toad in a usual<br>
way, open up the body cavity and<br>
pin aside the body wall to display the<br>
visceral (general) view of the animal<br>
in an undisturbed condition.<br>
b)	 Deflect the alimentary canal to your<br>
right hand side; which is the left hand<br>
side of the animal and pin it to fully<br>
and display the digestive system.<br>
<b>Questions</b><br>
1.	 Draw a large, neat well-labelled<br>
diagrams of the following:<br>
a)	 Visceral/general view.<br>
b)	 Digestive system.<br>
c)	 Compare your diagrams with<br>
that of Figures 3.63 and Figure<br>
3.69.<br>
2.	 Explain the significance of ligaturing<br>
and flooding the specimen with<br>
water.<br>
3.	 State the role(s) of each part of the<br>
digestive system.<br>
4.	 Classify the frog to class level.<br>

<b>Activity 3.16 Dissection of a frog to</b><br>
display the urinogenital system<br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>Fresh male or female frog or toad,</b><br>

cotton wool.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Dissect the frog or toad in a usual<br>
way to open up the body cavity.<br>
b)	 Deflect the alimentary canal to your<br>
right hand side and pin it to fully,<br>
display the urinogenital system.<br>

specimen.<br>
2.	 Draw a large well-labelled diagram<br>

diagram with that of Figure 3.64 and<br>
Figure 3.65.<br>
3.	 Outline the role (s) of each of the<br>
labelled parts.<br>
4.	 How does the urethra of a male<br>

<b>Class Reptilia</b><br>
<b>The class comprises a group of animals</b><br>
including turtles, crocodiles, alligators,<br>
chameleons, tortoises, snakes, and lizards.<br>
<b>Most of them have four limbs while others</b><br>
do not have limbs. Reptiles are found in<br>
diverse habitats, such as deserts, mountains,<br>
rocks, treetops and in water. They are mostly<br>
terrestrial with few aquatic members such<br>
as turtles and terrapins. They are cold-<br>
blooded (ectothermic) vertebrates. Their<br>
body temperature fluctuates according to<br>
the environmental temperature. Reptiles<br>
regulate their body temperature by<br>
behaviour, either by basking in the sun<br>
to warm themselves or by hiding under<br>
cover to cool their body. Most of them<br>
are tetrapods, with claws on their toes.<br>
<b>Reptiles such as snakes and some lizards</b><br>
are legless, although they are descendants<br>
of four-limbed ancestors.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of class Reptilia</b><br>
<b>Reptiles possess the following unique</b><br>
features, which differentiate them from<br>
other chordates:<br>
a)	 They have dry scaly skin with horny<br>
scales.<br>
b)	 They have two pairs of pentadactyl<br>
limbs, except some members, such as<br>
snakes, which have no limbs.<br>
c)	 They undergo internal fertilization and<br>
their fertilized yolky eggs are laid on<br>
land or retained until hatching.<br>
d)	 They lay amniotic eggs that have a<br>
leathery shell to prevent rapid water<br>
loss.<br>
e)	 Their eyes are located at the front of<br>
the head to facilitate binocular vision.<br>
<b>Some reptiles can move each eye</b><br>
independently, and this helps them to<br>
find food and escape from predators.<br>
<b>Structure of a lizard</b><br>
<b>Lizards have dry scaly skin, and most of</b><br>
them have clawed feet and external ear<br>
openings. Most lizards are small, with<br>
four legs and a long tail that, in many<br>
species, is fragile and easily broken but<br>
regenerate later (Figure 3.70). The legs<br>
of some lizards are greatly shortened or<br>
vestigial, making animals such as the<br>
glass lizard or a slow worm have a snake-<br>
like appearance. They are distinguished<br>
from true snakes by their movable eyelids.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   201</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>

<b>Class Aves</b><br>
<b>The class Aves includes all birds.  It is an</b><br>
extremely distinctive and successful class.<br>
<b>Aves are bipedal feathered and warm-</b><br>
blooded (homoeothermous) animals, as<br>
they are able to maintain a constant body<br>
temperature. They have unique “one-way”<br>
breathing system. They have light, yet<br>
strong hollow bones, forming a skeleton<br>
in which many bones are fused or lost and<br>
have powerful flight muscles. Birds have<br>
evolved specific adaptations to enable<br>
them fly.  They have fused hollow bones<br>
making birds have light weight, have a<br>
large keel for attaching flight muscles and<br>
have large chest muscles used for flight.<br>
<b>Birds also have their fore limbs modified</b><br>
for flight and have feathers which are used<br>
for flying.  They have also developed long<br>
flight feathers on the wings and tail to help<br>
birds attain balance and steer.  Birds have<br>
developed air sacs connected to the lungs<br>
enabling them to extract more oxygen to<br>
release more energy to power flight.  Also<br>
they have four chambered heart that enable<br>
them get more oxygen and avoid mixing<br>
oxygenated and deoxygenated blood.  Birds<br>
have well developed brain enabling them<br>
to have quick reaction during flight.  They<br>
have large eye to body ratio; and large<br>
eyes give birds good and keen eyesight<br>
important in flight. Birds can migrate during<br>
harsh conditions, since they have wings<br>
that enable them to move fast. Birds are<br>
widely spread all over the world; some are<br>
found in very cold snowy environments and<br>
others in dry and hot environments.<br>
<b>Structure of a pigeon</b><br>
<b>The body is spindle shaped and the size</b><br>
varies from 20-25 cm. The body is divided<br>
into the head, neck, trunk, and tail. Most<br>
parts of the body are covered by feathers.<br>
<b>The head is small and rounded (Figure</b><br>
3.71). It is anteriorly pointed into a short<br>
beak. On the lateral side of the head, there is<br>
a pair of prominent eyes. The ear comprises<br>
of small apertures on the posterior side of<br>
the eyes. Each aperture remains covered by<br>
a special group of feathers called auricular<br>
feathers. Each aperture leads to a canal<br>
called external auditory meatus, which is<br>
closed below by the tympanic membrane.<br>
<b>The trunk is the greatest and widest part of</b><br>
the body. It is boat-shaped and bears a pair<br>
of wings and a pair of legs. The entire foot is<br>
covered with horny epidermal scales. At the<br>
hind end of the trunk is the cloaca aperture.<br>

10/10/2019   14:06<br>

<b>Distinctive features of the class Aves</b><br>
<b>Aves are different from other chordates</b><br>
due to possession of the following<br>
features:<br>
a)	 Their<br>
bodies<br>
are<br>
covered<br>
by<br>
overlapping feathers.<br>
b)	 They lost teeth; instead, they have<br>
modiﬁed mouthparts into different<br>
types of beaks (bills). Beaks are<br>
adapted for many different feeding<br>
habits, such as seed crushing, fruit<br>
scooping, flesh tearing, nectar sipping,<br>
and wood chiseling.<br>
c)	 They have cylindrical, long necks<br>
connecting heads and trunks.<br>
d)	 They have two pairs of pentadactyl<br>
limbs. The front limbs are modiﬁed<br>
into a pair of wings, which bear quill<br>
feathers for ﬂight, while the hind limbs<br>
are covered by scales and are adapted<br>
for perching, walking or swimming.<br>
e)	 They undergo internal fertilisation and<br>
produce eggs with calcareous shells.<br>
f)	 Their sternum forms a sharp ventral<br>
keel, providing muscles attachment<br>
for ﬂight.<br>
g)	 They have oil gland above the cloaca,<br>
which preens the feather so that they<br>
become waterproof. Ostrich and parrot<br>
lack oil gland.<br>
h)	 Their alimentary canal has additional<br>
chambers called crop and gizzard. The<br>
crop stores and softens the food, while<br>
the gizzard helps in crushing and<br>
churning the food.<br>
i)	 Birds are capable of flying except for a<br>
few species such as ostrich, penguins,<br>
and kiwi.<br>
<b>Class Mammalia</b><br>
<b>The class Mammalia consists of all animals</b><br>
with mammary glands. It is an extremely<br>
diverse and very advanced group in the<br>
kingdom Animalia. Members of the class<br>
<b>Mammalia include: human, mouse, rabbit,</b><br>
cow, lion, bat, whale, and donkey.<br>
<b>Distinctive features of class mammalia</b><br>
<b>Mammals are different from other chordates</b><br>
because:<br>
a)	 They have fur or hair that cover their<br>
skin. The skin is glandular, with two<br>
types of glands, namely, sebaceous<br>
and sweat glands.<br>
b)	 Females (mothers) have mammary<br>
glands, which produce milk for their<br>
newborn.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   203</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
204<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
c)	 They have external ears called pinna<br>
in addition to middle and inner ears,<br>
which are used for collection of sound<br>
waves and leading them to ear canal.<br>
<b>The middle ear has three small soft</b><br>
bones called ear ossicles namely;<br>
malleus, incus, and stapes.<br>
d)	 They have heterotrophic mode of<br>
nutrition with different types of teeth<br>
(heterodont dentition) for different<br>
functions, depending on the mode of<br>
feeding.<br>
e)	 They have highly developed brains.<br>
f)	 They have muscular diaphragm, which<br>
separates the thorax from abdominal<br>
cavity.<br>
g)	 They are viviparous (give birth to<br>
young ones). The developing foetus is<br>
held in the uterus, and gets nourishment<br>
through placenta, except in primitive<br>
animals like kangaroo, which bear<br>
immature young ones and duck-billed<br>
platypus, and echidna which lay eggs.<br>
<b>Structure of a mouse</b><br>
<b>Structurally, an adult mouse has an average</b><br>
length of 7.5-10 cm. Its body is divided<br>
into head, neck, and trunk. The head bears<br>
external ear ﬂaps called pinnae, eyes,<br>
nostrils and a mouth. The latter has long,<br>
hair extensions called vibrissae or tactile<br>
whiskers (Figure 3.72). The neck is short<br>
and wide, connecting the head to the trunk.<br>
<b>The trunk bears four walking legs, two</b><br>
short hind legs and two long front legs,<br>
each with ﬁve digits. The trunk also bears<br>
a long tail which is either hairless or has<br>
sparse covering of hair. The whole body<br>
of the mammal is covered by fur (pelage).<br>
<b>Eye</b><br>
<b>Sensory</b><br>
whisker<br>
<b>Short legs clawed feet</b><br>
<b>Hairless ear</b><br>
<b>Tail with fur</b><br>
<b>Hind limb</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.72  Structure of a mouse</b><br>
<b>Digestive system of a mouse</b><br>
<b>The alimentary canal of a mouse starts</b><br>
from the mouth to the anus. The mouth<br>
consists of sixteen teeth; twelve molar and<br>
four incisors (two on the upper jaw and the<br>
other two on the lower jaw). The lower<br>
incisors teeth are more developed, pointed,<br>
sharp and longer than the rest, and are used<br>
for cutting the food into small chewable<br>
pieces. Down from the mouth, there are<br>
oesophagus, stomach, pancreas, and small<br>
intestine. The small intestine has three<br>
segments (duodenum, jejunum and ileum),<br>
followed by the large intestine, with four<br>
segments namely: caecum, colon, rectum<br>
and anus. The stomach is a hollow organ<br>
(pouch-shaped) lying in the ventral part of<br>
the abdomen, and is partly covered by liver<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   204</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
205<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
<b>The liver has four lobes; one on the left, two</b><br>
on the right, and one in the centre. The liver<br>
has two functions in digestion: secretion<br>
of bile and receiving the absorbed food<br>
from the small intestine through hepatic<br>
portal vein. It lacks gall-bladder, instead<br>
the cystic ducts from the liver lobes join<br>
to form the bile duct which conveys bile to<br>
the duodenum. The hepatic portal vein runs<br>
from the liver to the intestines; covered by<br>
a chain of lymph nodes and form branches<br>
in the mesentery (plural mesenteries) and<br>
other parts of the intestine.<br>
<b>The mesenteries are the continuous set of</b><br>
support tissues, which attach the intestines<br>
to the posterior wall of the abdomen. They<br>
support efficient digestion and maximum<br>
absorption of digested food by helping in<br>
storage of fat and allowing lymphatics, blood<br>
vessels, and nerves to supply the intestines.<br>
<b>The pancreas secretes digestive enzymes,</b><br>
which pass through the numerous small<br>
pancreatic ducts that enter the duodenum for<br>
digestion. The main functions of the small<br>
intestine are digestion and absorption of<br>
the products of digestion. Duodenum is the<br>
first part of the small intestine, connected<br>
to the stomach by a pyloric sphincter;<br>
which receives and digests the released<br>
food from the stomach using bile and<br>
other digestive juices. Jejunum and ileum<br>
are similar in both structure and function.<br>
<b>They are both involved in digestion of</b><br>
food as well as absorption of nutrients. The<br>
overall functions of the large intestine is to<br>
complete absorption of  water mainly from<br>
undigested food particles, manufacture of<br>
certain vitamins, formation of feaces and<br>
expelling faeces from the body through<br>
the anus. The main role of caecum is the<br>
absorption of water and salts remained<br>
after completion of digestion in the small<br>
intestine, as well as mixing and lubricating<br>
lobes. It is concerned with the digestion of food and temporary storage before further<br>
digestion in the small intestine (Figure 3.73).<br>

its contents using the mucus secreted from<br>
its internal thick wall of mucous membrane.<br>
<b>Rectum connects the colon to the anus,</b><br>
which aids in temporary storage of feaces<br>
before its release (when the sphincter<br>
contracts), and aids their release through<br>
the anus (when the sphincter relaxes).<br>
<b>Urinogenital system of a male mouse</b><br>
<b>The urinary and reproductive systems are</b><br>
integrated in some ways and are usually<br>
studied together as urinogenital system.<br>
<b>However, the excretory system eliminates</b><br>
waste product and the reproductive system<br>
produces sperms in male and eggs in female<br>
and provide conducive environment for<br>
fertilization and their developing foetus<br>
or embryo.<br>
<b>The kidneys are bean shaped structures</b><br>
found on the back of the abdominal cavity<br>
on either side of the spine and are embedded<br>
in the fats. On top of each kidney are small<br>
glands called adrenal glands (Figure 3.74).<br>
<b>In addition, a small and delicate tube called</b><br>
ureter is attached to each kidney, which<br>
leads the urine to the urinary bladder. The<br>
urinary bladder is connected to the urethra,<br>
which carries urine from the bladder to the<br>
urethral orifice then to the outside through<br>
the penis.<br>
<b>Ureter</b><br>
<b>Spermatic cord</b><br>

Males have two openings on the pelvic area; genital opening and the anus. The genital<br>
opening passes both urine and sperms via a penis hanging out from or between the two<br>

10/10/2019   14:06<br>

<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
scrotal sacs. The scrotal sacs protect the<br>
testes (singular testis). The testes (which<br>
secrete sperms) are connected to a coiled<br>
tube called epididymis which collects<br>
the produced sperms and store them. The<br>
epididymis is very long twice the length of<br>
the testis. It has caput, corpus, and caudal<br>
regions and it is connected to the tubular<br>
vas deferens which transports sperms from<br>
the epididymis to the urethra. The urethra<br>
not only carries the urine, but also sperms<br>
through the penis to the outside of the body.<br>
<b>On the left and right of the urinary bladder</b><br>
there are folded glands called the seminal<br>
vesicles (vesicula seminalis) bearing a<br>
coagulating gland, and below the urinary<br>
bladder, which store urine. There are other<br>
glands known as prostate glands at each<br>
side of the urethra and Cowper’s glands,<br>
which are small ovoid structures, found at<br>
the root of the penis. There are two preputial<br>
glands, each wrapped on one side of the<br>
ventral wall.<br>
<b>Urinogenital system of a female mouse</b><br>
<b>The female pelvic area has three openings;</b><br>
urethral opening, genital (vaginal) opening<br>
and the anus. The kidney and ureter have<br>
the same composition as in males, except<br>
that the urethra, which passes both urine<br>
and sperms in males, in females the urethra<br>
passes only urine conveyed from the bladder<br>
and passes to the outside environment<br>
through urethral opening. The vagina<br>
terminates to the long duplex tubes one<br>
on each side called uterus, which later<br>
accommodates multiple developing foetus.<br>
<b>At the tip of each uterus is an ovary (eggs</b><br>
producing gland) which is enclosed within<br>
a thin-walled ovarian sac surrounded by<br>
fats. Each ovary is connected to the uterus<br>
via a single small undulating coils called<br>
fallopian tube (Figure 3.75).<br>

<b>Dissection of mouse or rat</b><br>
<b>A mouse or rat is a typical vertebrate</b><br>
animal whose many aspects of structural<br>
organisation are similar to those of<br>
other mammals, including human being.<br>
<b>Moreover, mice or rats are also important</b><br>
research tool for modeling human disease<br>
progression and development in the<br>
laboratory. They have also helped to speed<br>
up the progress of research and enabled<br>
the development of important new drugs.<br>
<b>Therefore, by examining the physiology,</b><br>
anatomy and metabolism of a mouse or rat,<br>
scientists can gain a valuable insight into<br>
how human system functions. Despite the<br>
differences in their body size and appearance,<br>
<b>Mice/rats share a distinct genetic similarity</b><br>
to humans; and their ability to reproduce<br>
and mature quickly make them efficient<br>
and economical candidate mammals for<br>
scientific studies.<br>
<b>The purpose of conducting rat or mouse</b><br>
dissection is to explore and study the<br>
internal organs and systems of the basic<br>
mammalian anatomy.<br>
<b>General procedure of dissection of a</b><br>
mouse or rat<br>
a)	 Put on a pair of gloves.<br>
b)	 Place a live male or female mouse<br>
or rat in a container with a lid and<br>
anaesthetize it using chloroform soaked<br>
in a small roll of cotton wool for five<br>
minutes.<br>
c)	 Lay the mouse or rat on the dissecting<br>
tray or dish, with the ventral side<br>
(abdomen) facing upwards (Figure<br>
3.76). Pin it down on the tray through<br>
the fore and hind limbs, make sure that<br>
the pins face outwards.<br>
<b>Point to</b><br>
start a cut<br>
<b>Pinned limb</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.76 Positioning the mouse or rat</b><br>
for dissection<br>
d)	 Lift the skin in the mid-ventral line<br>
using forceps, and cut to make a small<br>
slit (Figure 3.77). Cut forward to the<br>
level of the lower lip and backwards<br>
around the penis and between the<br>
scrotal for a male specimen while for<br>
the female cut the skin backward as<br>
far as the anus, passing either side of<br>
urinary and genital apertures.<br>
<b>Penis</b><br>
<b>Scrotal sac</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.77 Opening the body cavity of an</b><br>
anesthetized mouse or rat<br>
e)	 Use fingers to pull the skin aside,<br>
loosen it from the body wall, stretch<br>
it and pin it back.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   208</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
209<br>
<b>Comparative studies of natural groups of organisms</b><br>
f)	 Lift the abdominal wall using forceps,<br>
make an incision and cut up to the<br>
xiphoid cartilage and down to the left<br>
and right ribs. Stretch the body wall<br>
and pin it aside (Figure 3.78). Examine<br>
the contents of the abdominal cavity<br>
in undisturbed condition (in situ or<br>
visceral/general view) and draw a well<br>
labelled diagram.<br>

<b>Liver</b><br>
<b>Small</b><br>
intestine<br>
<b>Large</b><br>
intestine<br>
<b>Stomach</b><br>
<b>Spleen</b><br>

<b>Pancreas</b><br>
<b>Caecum</b><br>
<b>Anus</b><br>
<b>Rectum</b><br>
<b>Figure 3.78 General view of the dissected mouse</b><br>
or rat<br>
g)	 To display the digestive system, adjust<br>
the positions of the alimentary canal<br>
by moving the bulk of the intestine to<br>
the left hand side of the specimen to<br>
expose the duodenum and the colon.<br>
h)	 Grip the duodenum and colon, pull<br>
them apart to expose hepatic portal<br>
vein and turn the bulk of the intestines<br>
over to untwist them, rearrange the<br>
digestive structures and draw a well<br>
labelled diagram.<br>
i)	 When displaying urinogenital system,<br>
the alimentary canal should be removed<br>
first followed by the removal of fats<br>
from the kidneys using blunt forceps<br>
and clear the ureter.<br>
j)	 In male mouse, open the scrotal sac<br>
by cutting its ventral wall to expose<br>
the testes, caudal and caput epididymis<br>
as well as the vas deferens. Lay the<br>
bladder, seminal vesicles, coagulating<br>
glands and prostate glands on one side.<br>
k)	 In female mouse/rat grip the clitoris,<br>
pull it gently so that the urethra is<br>
held away from the pelvis, and cut<br>
the ventral part of the girdle. Lift the<br>
oviducts and remove the mesovarium<br>
to expose ureters on both sides, remove<br>
the fat bodies from the kidneys and<br>
ureter, but leave them around the<br>
ovaries (the ovaries are enclosed<br>
within the thin walled ovarian sac and<br>
connected to a very small and much<br>
coiled fallopian tube) and observe the<br>
very long tube called fallopian tube<br>
near to the uterus.<br>
<b>Safety precautions</b><br>
1.	 Consider issues such as allergies or<br>
sneezing from loose rat or mouse furs.<br>
2.	 Rat or mouse may bite a person<br>
and cause pains, so be careful when<br>
dealing with live a rat or mouse.<br>
3.	 Good hygiene practices should be<br>
observed all the time; keep hands<br>
away from the mouth, nose, and eyes<br>
and face during and after dissection<br>
and wash hands with antiseptic<br>
soap immediately after a dissection<br>
practical session.<br>

for about five minutes.<br>
b)	Dissect a mouse in the usual way, to<br>
fully display the digestive system and<br>
urinogenital system.<br>

<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Four</b><br>
<b>4.1  Nervous coordination in mammals</b><br>
<b>As in other animals, nervous coordination</b><br>
in mammals is accomplished by the<br>
nervous system. The mammalian nervous<br>
system consists of a central nervous<br>
system and peripheral nervous system.<br>
<b>The central nervous system is made up</b><br>
of the brain and spinal cord, whereas the<br>
peripheral nervous system is made up of<br>
the nerve ﬁbres. The nerve ﬁbres branch<br>
from the central nervous system and extend<br>
to all parts of the body. Coordination is<br>
accomplished through a set of signals<br>
channeled into a series of nerve cells.<br>
<b>The nerves that transmit signals from the</b><br>
body to the central nervous system are<br>
called afferent nerves, and they include<br>
sensory and relay neurones, while those<br>
nerves which transmit signals from the<br>
brain are called motor or efferent nerves.<br>
<b>Nervous coordination enables a mammal</b><br>
to respond rapidly to external and internal<br>
stimuli.<br>
<b>Nervous tissue</b><br>
<b>The nervous tissue is a specialized tissue</b><br>
made up of nerve cells (neurones). A<br>
neurone is the basic unit structure of<br>
the nervous tissue which consists of the<br>
cell body, dendrites and axon. Nervous<br>
tissues make up the Central Nervous<br>
<b>System (CNS) and Peripheral Nervous</b><br>
<b>System (PNS). The central nervous</b><br>
system consists of the brain and spinal<br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
In organisms the body parts work together for various roles, resulting into a coordinated<br>
body. Animals have two systems of coordination namely nervous coordination and<br>
endocrine coordination, while plants have hormonal coordination. Nervous coordination<br>
is accomplished by the nervous system composed of nerve ﬁbres, brain and spinal<br>
cord, whereas hormonal coordination is accomplished by endocrine system which is<br>
composed of the endocrine glands. In plants, responses are in the form of slow modiﬁed<br>
growth or movements called turgor movement. In this chapter you will learn about<br>
nervous coordination in mammals, hormonal coordination in mammals, coordination<br>
in plants, and phytohormones (plant hormone).<br>
<b>Coordination and</b><br>
irritability<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   212</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
213<br>
<b>Coordination and Irritability</b><br>
cord, while the peripheral nervous system<br>
comprises of the cranial and spinal<br>
nerves, which are packed together with<br>
their motor and sensory endings. Nervous<br>
tissue is the main tissue component of the<br>
nervous system and it consists of closely<br>
packed nerve cells or neurones with very<br>
small intercellular spaces. Nervous tissue<br>
contains two types of cells, namely;<br>
neurones and neuroglia. Neurones are<br>
specialized nerve cells which generate<br>
and conduct nerve impulses. Neuroglia<br>
are non-neuronal cells which assist in<br>
propagation of the nerve impulses and<br>
provision of nutrients to the neurones.<br>
<b>Neuroglia also serve as supporting cells</b><br>
that provide electrical insulation and<br>
remove debris. Cells of the neuroglia<br>
produce myelin sheath that increases the<br>
speed of impulses along the axon of the<br>
neuronal ﬁbres and offers protection for<br>
the axon.<br>
<b>Types of neuroglia</b><br>
<b>There are six types of neuroglia, four</b><br>
of which are found in the CNS and two<br>
in the PNS. The neuroglia found in the<br>
<b>CNS include astrocytes, microglial cells,</b><br>
ependymal cells and oligodendrocytes,<br>
while those found in PNS include satellite<br>
cells and Schwann cells (Figure 4.1 a to f).<br>
<b>The collection of glia cells residing within</b><br>
the walls of intestinal tract, beginning in<br>
the oesophagus and extending down to<br>
the anus are known as enteric glia. The six<br>
types of neuroglia are explained below:<br>
a)	 Microglial cell<br>
<b>These are the smallest neuroglial cells</b><br>
(Figure 4.1a). They are macrophage cells<br>
that make up the primary immune system<br>
for the CNS. These phagocytic cells help<br>
to remove bacteria and waste (cleaning<br>
neuronal debris).<br>

214<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
b)	 Astrocytes<br>
<b>These are star-shaped macroglial cells with</b><br>
many processes (Figure 4.1b). They are<br>
the most abundant glial cells in the CNS.<br>
<b>Their roles are to provide metabolic and</b><br>
structural support to the neurones.<br>
c)	 Oligodendrocytes<br>
<b>These are cells with very few processes</b><br>
(Figure 4.1c). They are found in the CNS.<br>
<b>They form myelin sheath on the axons of a</b><br>
neurone which have lipid-based insulation<br>
for increasing the speed at which the action<br>
potential can travel down the axons.<br>
d)	 Ependymal cells<br>
<b>These are ciliated cells which line up the</b><br>
central cavities of the brain and spinal cord<br>
where they form a fairly permeable barrier<br>
between cerebrospinal ﬂuid that ﬁlls these<br>
cavities (Figure 4.1d).<br>
e)	 Schwann cells<br>
<b>These are equivalent to oligodendrocytes</b><br>
(Figure 4.1e). They surround nerve ﬁbres<br>
in the PNS. They help to maintain axons<br>
and form myelin sheaths in the PNS.<br>
f)	 Satellite cells<br>
<b>They line the surface of neuronal cell</b><br>
bodies in ganglia within the PNS (Figure<br>
4.1f). They are analogous to astrocytes.<br>
<b>Adaptive features of nervous tissues</b><br>
<b>The nervous tissues have the following</b><br>
adaptive features:<br>
a)	 They have nerve cells (neurones) that<br>
receive information from sensory parts<br>
and send or transmit it to the CNS for<br>
interpretation and then to the effector<br>
for a response. An effector is any part<br>
of the body that produces response.<br>
<b>Examples of effectors include a muscle</b><br>
and a gland.<br>
b)	 The cells of the nervous tissues are<br>
tightly packed for effective working of<br>
the tissue.<br>
c)	 The cells of the nervous tissues have<br>
large number (high concentration) of<br>
mitochondria which help to generate<br>
energy. This is important because<br>
nervous tissues require large amount<br>
of energy for efﬁcient functioning.<br>
d)	 They have neuroglia (glial cells) that<br>
provide protection and support to the<br>
tissues.<br>
e)	 The cells in the PNS are capable of<br>
regenerating themselves. This is due<br>
to the presence of neurolemma (also<br>
known as neurilemma).<br>
f)	 The cells in the nervous tissues<br>
produce neurotransmitter chemicals<br>
which act as conveyors that carry<br>
impulses from one neurone to another<br>
across the synaptic gap.<br>
g)	 Nerve cells or neurones have nodes<br>
of Ranvier and fatty myelin sheath<br>
which facilitate rapid transmission of<br>
impulses.<br>
<b>The Central Nervous System (CNS)</b><br>
<b>The CNS, which consists of the brain and</b><br>
the spinal cord has the grey matter and<br>
white matter. The grey matter is comprised<br>
of cell bodies, dendrites, unmyelinated<br>
axons, and very few myelinated axons. In<br>
contrast, the white matter is comprised of<br>
myelinated axons. The main function of<br>
the central nervous system is to integrate<br>
information from various sources. The<br>
collection, both from internal and external<br>
environment is done by receptors. They<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   214</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
215<br>
<b>Coordination and Irritability</b><br>
usually form the sensory system along<br>
with neurones which transmit the collected<br>
information from different parts. The<br>
collected information is processed and<br>
integrated in the central nervous system,<br>
and ﬁnally the information is transmitted<br>
to effectors (muscles and glands) which<br>
produce appropriate responses.<br>
<b>Functions of the nervous system</b><br>
<b>The nervous system has the following</b><br>
functions:<br>
a)	 It<br>
receives<br>
stimuli<br>
from<br>
the<br>
environment using receptor cells or<br>
sensory input.<br>
b)	 It converts the stimuli into electrical<br>
impulses by the process called<br>
transduction.<br>
c)	 It transmits nerve impulses. The<br>
impulse is transmitted from the<br>
sensory receptor to the CNS and then<br>
to the effector, which is capable of<br>
producing an appropriate response.<br>
d)	 It stores information so that behaviour<br>
can be modified according to the past<br>
stimuli.<br>
<b>Neurones</b><br>
<b>Nerve cells or neurones are conducting</b><br>
cells of the nervous system found between<br>
the receptors and effectors. These spread<br>
throughout the body of an organism and<br>
form a communication network. Neurones<br>
are the basic structural and functional<br>
units of the nervous system. They are<br>
responsible for transmission of impulses<br>
from one part of the body to another.<br>
<b>The main portion of the neurone is the</b><br>
cell body, which contains a nucleus. The<br>
cytoplasm of the cell bodies contains<br>
granules called Nissl’s bodies. Extending<br>
from the cell body are one or more short<br>
extensions called dendrites. These receive<br>
signals from the sensory receptors and<br>
transmit electrical signals or impulses to<br>
the cell body. The neurone also contains<br>
long extension called axon. In some cells,<br>
axons are covered by a fatty layer of<br>
material known as myelin sheath. Within<br>
the cell body there are ﬁne neuroﬁbrils<br>
that extend from the dendrites to the axon.<br>
<b>An axon is a long, stem-like part of the</b><br>
cell that sends action potential signals to<br>
the next cell. Outside the myelin sheath<br>
is a cellular layer called neurilemma,<br>
containings sheath of Schwan cells which<br>
are essential for rapid propagation of<br>
nerve impulse as well as nourishment and<br>
insulation of the axon. The myelin sheath<br>
together with neurilemma constitute the<br>
medullary sheath (Figure 4.2), inside the<br>
axon there is a space containing charged<br>
ions called axoplasm.<br>

<b>Neurones are supported, protected, and</b><br>
nourished by non-neuronal cells of the<br>
nervous system, which are known as<br>
glial cells. Together with extracellular<br>
tissue, glial cells make up the neuroglia.<br>
<b>Some neuroglias are phagocytic cells</b><br>
that remove bacteria and debris from the<br>
neurones while others provides metabolic<br>
and structural support.<br>
<b>Types of neurones</b><br>
<b>Based on their function and structure there</b><br>
are three types of neurones; which are<br>
sensory neurones, intermediate neurones<br>
(interneurones), and motor neurones.<br>
<b>Sensory neurones (Afferent neurones)</b><br>
<b>Sensory and intermediate neurones are</b><br>
also known as afferent neurones. These<br>
are neurones with long dendrites and short<br>
axon. The cell body and dendrites of the<br>
sensory neurones mostly lie outside the<br>
brain and spinal cord. Sensory neurones<br>
transmit nerve impulses from sensory<br>
receptors to the CNS (Figure 4.3) for the<br>
interpretation.<br>

<b>Interneurones (Intermediate neurones)</b><br>
<b>These are also known as connector</b><br>
neurones or relay neurones (Figure 4.4).<br>
<b>They are much smaller nerve cells with</b><br>
many<br>
interconnections.<br>
<b>Interneurones</b><br>
have short dendrites and short or long<br>
axons. They lie entirely within the CNS<br>
(brain and spinal cord). They transmit<br>
nerve impulses within the CNS; that is,<br>
they relay information between sensory<br>
and motor neurones. The sensory neurones<br>
and intermediate neurones both carry<br>
impulses towards the CNS.<br>

<b>Motor neurones (Efferent neurones)</b><br>
<b>Motor neurone have short dendrites and</b><br>
long axons; their dendrites and cell bodies<br>
are located in the CNS; and the axon is<br>
outside the CNS. Motor neurones transmit<br>
nerve impulses from the CNS to the<br>
effector organs such as muscles or glands,<br>
which eventually respond to the stimulus<br>
(Figure 4.5). Axon always transmits<br>
impulses away from the cell body while<br>
dendron carries impulses towards the cell<br>
body.<br>

<b>Classiﬁcation of neurones based on the</b><br>
number of dendrites<br>
<b>Neurones can also be classiﬁed on the</b><br>
basis of the number of their dendrites<br>
arising from the soma (cell body).  In<br>
this classiﬁcation, there are three main<br>
types of neurones. These include unipolar,<br>
bipolar and multipolar neurones. Unipolar<br>
neurones have a single short dendrite<br>
terminating onto bush-like tufts or<br>
dendrites. These are found in the granular<br>
layer of the cerebellum. Bipolar neurones<br>
are sensory neurones that have two<br>
processes coming from the cell body; one<br>
dendron and one axon. Bipolar sensory<br>
neurones are found in the retina of the eye,<br>
ganglia of the vestibulocochlear nerve<br>
and the olfactory epithelium. Multipolar<br>
neurones have three or more processes<br>
coming from the cell body. They possess<br>
one axon and two or more dendrites.<br>
<b>Multipolar neurones form the major part</b><br>
of the CNS. They include interneurones<br>
and motor neurones. (Figure 4.6).<br>

<b>Exercise 4.1</b><br>
1.	 Describe the structure of the<br>
nervous tissue.<br>
2.	 Explain the adaptive features of<br>
the nervous tissue.<br>
3.	 With the help of diagrams, describe<br>
the types of neurones.<br>
<b>Nerve impulses</b><br>
<b>A nerve impulse is an electrical signal that</b><br>
travels along the axon. It is generated when<br>
the nerve cell is activated. Movement of<br>
ions in and out of the neurone causes a<br>
sudden change in the voltage across the<br>
wall of the axon. This triggers a wave of<br>
electrical activity that passes from the cell<br>
body along the length of the axon to the<br>
synapse.<br>
<b>Resting potential</b><br>
This occurs when the neurone is at rest.<br>
<b>In an inactive neurone, the axoplasm is</b><br>
negatively charged with respect to the<br>
outside of the cell. The difference in<br>
electrical charge is maintained by active<br>
transport of sodium ions out of axoplasm.<br>
<b>A cell in this state is said to have a resting</b><br>
potential and it is polarised. At this state,<br>
the potential difference existing across the<br>
cell surface membrane inside the cell with<br>
respect to the outside part is negative,<br>
which is about -70 mV. At this time, the<br>
axon does not conduct any impulse. The<br>
cytoplasm inside the axon has a high<br>
concentration of K+ and low concentration<br>
of Na+. This is contrary to the outside<br>
part which has a low concentration of<br>
<b>K+ and high concentration of Na+. The</b><br>
resting potential is maintained by active<br>
transport and passive diffusion of ions.<br>
<b>It is active transport of ions against the</b><br>
electrochemical gradient of sodium/<br>
potassium (Na+/K+) pump. These are<br>
carrier substances located in the cell<br>
surface membrane. They are driven by<br>
energy supplied by ATP (Figure 4.7). The<br>
rate of diffusion is characterised by the<br>
permeability of the axon membrane to the<br>
ion. The K+ has membrane permeability<br>
of about 20 times greater than that of Na+.<br>

10/10/2019   14:06<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>

<b>Action potential</b><br>
<b>An action potential is a rapid, temporary</b><br>
change in membrane potential. It may<br>
qualify as the most important type of<br>
electrical signal in cells. In a neurone,<br>
action potential is generated by special<br>
types of voltage-gated ion channels<br>
embedded in cell’s plasma membrane. A<br>
neurone displaying the nerve impulse is<br>
said to have an action potential and the<br>
cell is depolarised. Stimulation of axon by<br>
electrical impulse may result into a change<br>
in potential difference of about +40 mV<br>
across the axon membrane, from more<br>
negative inside to more positive inside.<br>
<b>Action potential is generated by a sudden</b><br>
opening of sodium channels. This occurs<br>
in response to stimulus which brings<br>
about a slight depolarisation or loss of<br>
charge of the axon membrane. Opening<br>
of Na+ gates increases permeability<br>
of the axon membrane to sodium ions<br>
which enter the axon by diffusion. This<br>
increases the number of positive ions<br>
inside the axon, which consequently<br>
becomes more depolarized. Since sodium<br>
gates are sensitive to depolarization,<br>
the greater the depolarization, the more<br>
sodium gates are open, allowing more<br>
sodium ions to enter into the cell, hence<br>
greater depolarization and this is called a<br>
positive feedback loop. Positive feedback<br>
loop causes acceleration in the entry of<br>
sodium to the potential difference peaks<br>
at about +40 mV. This peak corresponds<br>
to the maximum concentration of sodium<br>
inside the axon. The total depolarization<br>
associated with the action potential has<br>
therefore been from -70 mV to +40 mV.<br>
<b>The action potential has the following</b><br>
three distinct phases:<br>
a)	 Depolarisation<br>
<b>This is the decrease in voltage across</b><br>
the membrane. It occurs when there is a<br>
stimulus that leads to the opening of the<br>
sodium channel to increase inﬂow of<br>
sodium ions. The process makes the inside<br>
of the cell less negative.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   219</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:06<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
220<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
b)<br>
<b>Rapid repolarisation</b><br>
<b>This is the process that changes the</b><br>
membrane potential back to negative<br>
inside and positive outside. A fraction<br>
of a second after the sodium gates open,<br>
depolarisation of the axon membrane<br>
causes the potassium gates to open too,<br>
and potassium diffuses out of the cell.<br>
<b>Since potassium is positively charged, the</b><br>
inside of the cell becomes less positive and<br>
starts the process of repolarisation, then, it<br>
returns to its original resting potential.<br>
c)<br>
<b>Hyperpolarisation</b><br>
<b>This occurs after the impulse has been</b><br>
transmitted; the action potential falls down<br>
and the sodium gates close immediately.<br>
<b>But potassium gates delay to close, causing</b><br>
more K+ ions to exit the axon and this makes<br>
the membrane slightly more negative than<br>
the resting potential. Hyperpolarisation of<br>
the axon overshoots into more negative<br>
potential than the original potential.<br>
<b>All the three phases of the action potential</b><br>
occur within few milliseconds (ms) for the<br>
action potential to begin in a giant axon,<br>
the membrane potential must shift from<br>
its resting potential of -70 mV to about<br>
+40 mV. If the membrane depolarises<br>
less than that, an action potential does not<br>
occur. However, if this threshold potential<br>
is reached, sodium ion channels in the<br>
axon membrane open. Ions, thus rush into<br>
the axon following their electrochemical<br>
gradients. The inside of the membrane<br>
becomes less negative and then positive<br>
with respect to the outside. When the<br>
membrane potential reaches about +40 mV,<br>
a rapid change occurs and the repolarisation<br>
phase begins (Figure 4.8). The change<br>
is caused by the closure of sodium ion<br>
channels and the opening of potassium ion<br>
channels.<br>

<b>Coordination and Irritability</b><br>
In summary, an action potential occurs due to opening or closing of speciﬁc channels<br>
in the plasma membrane in response to changes in voltage. An action potential always<br>
has the same three-phase form; even though the size of the resting potential, threshold<br>
potential, and peak depolarization may vary among the species or even among different<br>
types of neurones in one species.<br>
<b>Conduction of nerve impulse along the axon</b><br>
The mechanism for impulse transmission along the axon involves the following steps:<br>
a)<br>
<b>Polarization of the neurone’s membrane</b><br>
Sodium is in high concentration on the outside, whereas potassium is in high concentration<br>
on the inside. Cell membranes surround neurones, like any other cell in the body which<br>
has a membrane. When a neurone is not stimulated, just sitting with no impulse to<br>
carry or transmit, its membrane is said to be polarised. Being polarized means that the<br>
electrical charge on the outside of the membrane is positive while the electrical charge<br>
on the inside of the membrane is negative (Figure 4.9).<br>
<b>Inside of axon</b><br>
<b>Outside of axon</b><br>
<b>Plasma membrane of axon</b><br>
<b>Figure 4.9  Polarised axon membrane</b><br>
b)	 The resting potential gives the neurone a break<br>
When the neurone is polarised, it is said to be at its resting potential. It remains in this<br>
state until when the stimulus comes along (that is, when it is stimulated). When action<br>
potential is initiated, a region of the membrane depolarises. As the result the adjacent<br>
region becomes depolarised as well (Figure 4.10).<br>
<b>Na+</b><br>
<b>Passive depolarization spreads</b><br>
Figure 4.10 Propagation of nerve impulse across a membrane (depolarization)<br>
c)	 Action potential<br>
Sodium ions move inside the membrane when a stimulus reaches a resting neurone. The<br>
gated ion channels on the resting neurone’s membrane open suddenly to allow the Na+<br>
that was on the outside of the membrane to rush into the cell. While this happens, the<br>
neurone changes from being polarised to being depolarised. After more positive ions<br>
enter inside the membrane, the inside becomes positive and polarisation is removed and<br>
the threshold is reached (Figure 4.11).<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   221</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
222<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Na+</b><br>
Figure 4.11 Propagation of nerve impulse across a membrane (action potential)<br>
d)	 Repolarisation<br>
Localised electrical circuits are established, causing further inﬂux of sodium ions and<br>
so progression of the impulse. Behind the impulse, potassium ions begin to leave the<br>
axon along the concentration gradient, hence repolarisation beginning to occur due to<br>
the outward flow of K+ ions. The depolarisation speeds forward, triggering an action<br>
potential (Figure 4.12).<br>
Figure 4.12 Propagation of nerve impulse across a membrane (localization)<br>
During repolarisation, potassium ions move outside, while sodium ions stay inside the<br>
membrane. After repolarisation, the inside of the cell becomes ﬂooded with Na+; the gated<br>
ion channels on the inside of the membrane open to allow K+ to move to the outside of<br>
the membrane. With K+ moving to the outside, the membrane’s repolarisation restores<br>
electrical balance, although it is the opposite of the initial polarised membrane that had<br>
Na+ gates close. Otherwise, the membrane could not repolarize (Figure 4.13). Then Na+<br>
ions are actively forced out of the axoplasm in the process called sodium pump. However,<br>
since K+ ions are also involved in this process, the process is best called cation pump.<br>
Figure 4.13 Propagation of nerve impulse across a membrane (repolarization)<br>
<b>Characteristics of nerve impulses</b><br>
<b>Nerve impulses have the following characteristic features:</b><br>
a)	 Transmission speed<br>
Impulses are always transmitted at a very high speed. Depending on the nature of a nerve<br>
cell, the speed of transmission varies from 0.5 to 100 metres per millisecond. The speed of<br>
impulse transmission is determined by body temperature, axon diameter, and presence of<br>
myelin sheaths.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   222</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
223<br>
<b>Coordination and Irritability</b><br>
b)	   Unidirectionality<br>
<b>Impulses always ﬂow in only one direction,</b><br>
that is, from cell body to terminal dendrites<br>
in the neurone, or from pre-synaptic to post<br>
– synaptic neurone across a synapse.<br>
c)	 Refractory period<br>
<b>During this period, a part of an axon is unable</b><br>
to conduct a new impulse immediately after<br>
propagation of an action potential. The<br>
resting potential is restored by outward<br>
movement of K+ ions and prevention of<br>
inward movement of Na+ ions.<br>
<b>Furthermore, the refractory period is</b><br>
divided into two phases namely; absolute<br>
refractory period and relative refractory<br>
period. Absolute refractory period lasts<br>
for 1 ms. In this period, a part of an axon<br>
is unable to propagate an action potential<br>
regardless of the strength of the stimulus.<br>
<b>The relative refractory period lasts for 5</b><br>
ms. During this period, the impulse can<br>
be propagated if its strength exceeds a<br>
threshold value. The significance of the<br>
refractory period is to ensure unidirectional<br>
flow of impulse and to separate one action<br>
potential to the next.<br>
d)	 All-or-nothing law<br>
<b>According to this law, for the action</b><br>
potential to be propagated, the stimulus<br>
applied should exceed a threshold value.<br>
<b>The threshold value is the minimum</b><br>
energy level, and when reached, the action<br>
potential will be generated. However,<br>
the size of the action potential will not<br>
decrease as it is transmitted along the<br>
i)    Body temperature<br>
<b>The speed of impulse transmission depends</b><br>
on the body temperature of the organism,<br>
such that, the speed of impulse transmission<br>
in homoeothermic organsms is greater than<br>
in    poikilothermic organisms.<br>
ii)   Axon diameter<br>
<b>The greater the axon diameter the higher</b><br>
the transmission speed. This is because<br>
increased axon diameter minimizes the<br>
resistance of the axoplasm.<br>
iii)   Presence of myelin sheaths<br>
<b>Axons with myelin sheaths conduct</b><br>
impulses at higher speed than those<br>
without sheaths. The myelin sheath<br>
allows impulses to leap from one node<br>
of ranvier to another, thereby increasing<br>
transmission speed (Figure 4.14). This is<br>
the saltatory conduction.<br>
<b>Nerve impulse propagation</b><br>
<b>Axon</b><br>
<b>Myelin sheath cells</b><br>
<b>Nodes of ranvier</b><br>
<b>K+Na+</b><br>
<b>K+Na+</b><br>
Figure 4.14 Leap of impulse from one node of Ranvier to another in a myelinated axon<br>

neurone, but it will always remain the<br>
same. In other words, the action potential<br>
is both generated and kept the same or<br>
not generated if the threshold value is not<br>
exceeded.<br>
e)	 Propagation (Conduction)<br>
<b>A nerve impulse is conducted as a wave</b><br>
of depolarisation that moves along the<br>
surface of the nerve cell. This means<br>
that progressive depolarisation of the<br>
axon membrane leads into impulse<br>
transmission.<br>
<b>Structure of a chemical synapse</b><br>
<b>The point where the axon of one neurone</b><br>
joins the dendrite or cell body of another<br>
neurone is known as a synapse. The<br>
membrane of the ﬁrst neurone connecting<br>
to the synapse is called a presynaptic<br>
membrane while the other membrane of<br>
the next neurone is called a postsynaptic<br>
membrane. These membranes between<br>
the two neurones are separated by a gap of<br>
about 20 nm called the synaptic cleft. At<br>
the end of the presynaptic neurone, there<br>
is a bulge called synaptic knob.<br>
<b>The cytoplasm of the synaptic knob</b><br>
contains numerous mitochondria and<br>
small synaptic vesicles. Transmission<br>
across a synapse is carried out by chemical<br>
substances<br>
called<br>
neurotransmitters;<br>
which are stored in synaptic vesicles. The<br>
presynaptic membrane is modiﬁed for the<br>
attachment of synaptic vesicles and the<br>
release of transmitter substance into the<br>
synaptic cleft. The postsynaptic membrane<br>
contains large protein molecules, which<br>
act as receptor sites for the transmitter<br>
substances and numerous channels and<br>
pores for the movement of ions into the<br>
postsynaptic neurone (Figure 4.15).<br>
<b>The neurotransmitter substance is either</b><br>
produced by the cell body of the neurone<br>
or synaptic knob. These substances<br>
are synthesised by enzymes stored in<br>
the cell body. The neurotransmitters<br>
allow<br>
the<br>
transmission<br>
of<br>
signals<br>
from one neurone to the next across<br>
synapses. There are many types of<br>
neurotransmitters, and these include<br>
acetylcholine, norepinephrine, serotonin,<br>
dopamine, and glutamate. The two<br>
common neurotransmitters in vertebrates<br>
are acetylcholine (Ach; an ammonium<br>
compound)  and  norepinephrine (also<br>
called noradrenaline). Neurones using<br>
acetylcholine<br>
as<br>
a<br>
neurotransmitter<br>
are described as cholinergic neurones,<br>
while<br>
those<br>
using<br>
norepinephrine<br>
(noradrenaline) are called adrenergic<br>
neurones. Norepinephrine is released in<br>
the sympathetic nervous system by some<br>
nerves, while acetylcholine is released by<br>
all nerves except some nerves in the brain.<br>

<b>Synaptic transmission of nerve</b><br>
impulses<br>
<b>The nerve impulse passes down the</b><br>
dendrite, through the cell body, and<br>
down the axon. At the end of the axon,<br>
the impulse encounters a ﬂuid-ﬁlled<br>
space separating the end of the axon<br>
from the dendrite of the next neurone or<br>
from a muscle cell. This space is called<br>
the synapse. The synapse located at the<br>
junction of a neurone and muscle ﬁbre<br>
is called a neuromuscular junction. Such<br>
synapses can be classiﬁed based on their<br>
means of transmission of impulses across<br>
their gaps. In this typology, two types are<br>
revealed, namely electrical and chemical<br>
synapses. An electrical synapse is the<br>
one in which the physiological continuity<br>
between pre and post synaptic neurones<br>
is provided by a special channel called a<br>
gap junction between the two neurones.<br>
<b>These channels are capable of passing</b><br>
an electrical current, causing voltage<br>
changes in the pre synaptic cell to induce<br>
voltage changes in the post synaptic cell.<br>
<b>This synapse can be effective when the</b><br>
neurones are very close together (2 nm).<br>
<b>The main advantage of electrical synapse</b><br>
is that it facilitates the rapid transfer<br>
of signals from one cell to the next. In<br>
chemical synapses, impulse transmission<br>
is facilitated by chemical substances<br>
called neurotransmitters.<br>
<b>Mechanism for synaptic transmission</b><br>
a)	 An action potential arrives at the end<br>
of the axon and induces changes in the<br>
cell membrane (Figure 4.16).<br>

calcium ions through the open channels (Figure 4.17).<br>

c)	 In response to the increase of calcium concentration inside the axon, synaptic vesicles<br>
fuse with the presynaptic membrane and release neurotransmitters into the gap between<br>
the cells which is called the synaptic cleft. The delivery of neurotransmitters into the cleft<br>
is an example of exocytosis (Figure 4.18).<br>

d)	 The vesicles then return to the cytoplasm and are reﬁlled with transmitter substance.<br>
The neurotransmitter diffuses across the synaptic cleft, a process which takes 0.5ms<br>
per synapse. Upon reaching the postsynaptic membrane, it binds with receptor<br>
molecules which recognise the molecular structure of the acetylcholine molecule<br>
(Figure 4.19).<br>
<b>Neurotransmitters</b><br>

10/10/2019   14:07<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
