e)	 The arrival of acetylcholine at the<br>
postsynaptic<br>
membrane<br>
changes<br>
the shape of the receptor site. This<br>
initiates ion channels to open up.<br>
<b>The excitatory synapse which opens</b><br>
ion channels on the postsynaptic<br>
membrane allowing sodium ions to<br>
enter and potassium ions to leave. This<br>
creates a new potential known as the<br>
excitatory postsynaptic potential in<br>
the post synaptic neurone. Once the<br>
neurotransmitter has depolarised the<br>
post synaptic neurone, it is hydrolysed<br>
to form acetyl and choline by the<br>
enzyme “acetylcholinesterase” which<br>
is found in the postsynaptic membrane.<br>
<b>This prevents the successive impulse</b><br>
merging at the synapse (Figure 4.20).<br>
<b>Hydrolytic enzymes such as</b><br>
acetylcholinesterase  break down<br>
transmitter molecules<br>
<b>Local depolarisation</b><br>
and propagation of an action potential<br>
Figure 4.20 Local depolarisation and propagation of an action potential in postsynaptic membrane<br>
<b>The resulting acetyl and choline diffuses</b><br>
across the synaptic cleft into the synaptic<br>
knob of the presynaptic neurone where<br>
they get stored in their vesicles for further<br>
use. The process is facilitated by energy<br>
produced by numerous mitochondria<br>
in the knob. The overall depolarising<br>
effect of several Excitatory Postsynaptic<br>
Potential (EPSPs) is called summation.<br>
<b>When two or more EPSPs simultaneously</b><br>
arising at different regions either on the<br>
same or different neurones, producing<br>
sufﬁcient depolarisation which can starts<br>
an action potential in the postsynaptic<br>
neurone, the phenomenon is known as<br>
spatial summation.<br>
<b>The excitatory postsynaptic potential</b><br>
builds up as more neurotransmitter<br>
substance<br>
arrives<br>
until<br>
sufﬁcient<br>
depolarisation occurs to exceed the<br>
threshold value and so generate an action<br>
potential in the postsynaptic neurone, this<br>
is called temporal summation. All events<br>
described are for excitatory synapse,<br>
some known inhibitory synapses respond<br>
to neurotransmitter by opening potassium<br>
ion channels and leaving the sodium ion<br>
channels closed. Potassium, therefore,<br>
moves out causing the membrane to be<br>
polarised; consequently, it prevents the<br>
threshold value to be exceeded. Thus no<br>
action potential will be created.<br>
<b>Functions of synapses</b><br>
a)	 They transmit information between<br>
neurones.<br>
<b>The</b><br>
synapses<br>
pass<br>
impulses in one direction only.<br>
<b>Such impulses are transmitted at the</b><br>
presynaptic membrane and received<br>
by the postsynaptic membrane, which<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   228</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
229<br>
<b>Coordination and Irritability</b><br>
ensures one direction of ﬂow along a<br>
given pathway.<br>
b)	 They<br>
amplify<br>
impulses<br>
by<br>
acetylcholine which is released at a<br>
neuromuscular junction which excite<br>
the post synaptic membrane and<br>
ampliﬁes any weak impulse arriving.<br>
<b>Repeated low level stimuli can be</b><br>
amplified as each impulse is arriving<br>
at the synapse, causing the release of<br>
more neurotransmitter, resulting in<br>
one larger impulse in the postsynaptic<br>
neurone. Therefore, this allows the<br>
body to respond to the stimuli more<br>
effectively.<br>
c)	 They act as junctions that transmit<br>
electric<br>
nerve<br>
impulses<br>
between<br>
neurones, or between neurone and<br>
effector cells. A synaptic connection<br>
between a neurone and muscle cell is<br>
known as neuromuscular junction.<br>
d)	 They ﬁlter out low level stimuli; as<br>
more neurotransmitter diffuses across<br>
the synaptic cleft, the excitatory<br>
postsynaptic potential increases. For<br>
the impulses to be generated in the<br>
postsynaptic neurone, it must reach<br>
the action potential of about +40<br>
mV. This implies that weak impulses<br>
in the presynaptic neurone do not<br>
cause enough neurotransmitter to be<br>
released for an action potential in the<br>
postsynaptic neurone to be generated.<br>
<b>As a result, synapses are able to filter</b><br>
out low level stimuli that the body<br>
does not need to respond, that is a way<br>
of conserving energy.<br>
e)	 They allow adaptation to intense<br>
stimulation and fatigue. The amount<br>
of transmitted substance which is<br>
released by synapse steadily falls off<br>
in a response to a constant stimulation<br>
until the supply of the transmitted<br>
substance is exhausted.<br>
f)	 They<br>
allow<br>
convergence,<br>
spatial<br>
summation and integration of the<br>
stimuli. In convergence of the stimuli,<br>
the<br>
postsynaptic<br>
nerves<br>
receive<br>
impulses from a large number of<br>
excitatory and inhibitory presynaptic<br>
neurones. In a spatial summation<br>
postsynaptic neurone is able to sum-<br>
up the stimuli from all presynaptic<br>
neurones, where the synapse act as a<br>
centre for the integration of stimuli<br>
from different sources; hence produces<br>
the coordinated response.<br>

<b>4.2  Sensory receptor</b><br>
<b>The receptor is a cell or a nerve ending or</b><br>
a group of nerve endings specialised for<br>
reception of stimuli and change speciﬁc<br>
stimuli into nerve impulse. The ability<br>
of receptors to convert stimuli or events<br>
which occur in the environment into a<br>
nerve impulse is known as transduction.<br>
<b>The structures which transform stimulus</b><br>
energy into electrical responses which<br>
is the nerve impulses in axons are called<br>
transducers. Receptors are therefore<br>
biological<br>
transducers<br>
that<br>
convert<br>
energy from both external and internal<br>
environments into electrical impulses.<br>
<b>They may be grouped together to form</b><br>
a sense organ, such as the eye or ear, or<br>
they may be scattered, as in those of the<br>
skin and viscera. The coordinated activity<br>
of an organism relies upon a continuous<br>
input of information from internal<br>
and external environments. When the<br>
information received leads to a change in<br>
activity or behaviour of the animals, it is<br>
called stimulus. The specialised region of<br>
the body which has the ability of detecting<br>
the stimulus is known as sensory receptor.<br>
<b>Types of sensory receptors</b><br>
<b>Receptors can be classiﬁed into different</b><br>
groups based on their structure, type, and<br>
the location of stimuli they detect.<br>
a)	 Classification of sensory receptors<br>
based on structure<br>
<b>Based on their structure there are two</b><br>
types of receptors, and these are: single<br>
sensory neurone receptors and complex<br>
receptors (sense organs).<br>
<b>Single sensory neurone receptor</b><br>
<b>These receptors are simple and mostly</b><br>
primitive. They consist of a single sensory<br>
neurone which is capable of detecting the<br>
stimulus and giving rise to a nerve impulse<br>
passing to the central nervous system.<br>
<b>Examples include: skin mechanoreceptors</b><br>
in the pacinian corpuscles.<br>
<b>Complex receptors</b><br>
<b>These sense cells consist of modiﬁed</b><br>
epithelial cells. They can detect stimuli.<br>
<b>Sensory cells, sensory neurones, and</b><br>
other associated or accessory structures<br>
are examples of complex receptors. The<br>
cones, rods, lens and iris (in the eye) are<br>
examples of sense cells.<br>
b)	 Classification of sensory receptors<br>
based on type of stimuli<br>
<b>Based on the type of stimulus they</b><br>
detect in the environment, there are<br>
several types of sensory receptors, which<br>
include: mechanoreceptor, photoreceptor,<br>
thermoreceptor, nociceptor, chemoreceptor,<br>
osmoreceptor and electroreceptors.<br>
<b>Mechanoreceptors</b><br>
<b>They detect mechanical stimuli which</b><br>
are caused by mechanical forces such as<br>
sound or vibration, touch, pressure, and<br>
gravity. Touch receptors are found all over<br>
the body. Other touch receptors include<br>
<b>Merkel’s discs and Meissner’s corpuscles</b><br>
which detect light and pacinian corpuscles<br>
which sense deep pressure and vibration.<br>
<b>Mechanoreceptors are responsible for</b><br>
detecting changes that are perceived<br>
such as sound or touch. They are also<br>
responsible for maintaining equilibrium<br>
balance and proper tone in muscles and<br>
joints (Figure 4.21).<br>

10/10/2019   14:07<br>

<b>Photoreceptors</b><br>
These are receptors which detect electromagnetic stimuli such as light. There are two<br>
types of photoreceptors namely; rods and cones. These are found in the retina of an eye<br>
for detecting dim and bright light respectively (Figure 4.22).<br>

<b>Thermoreceptors</b><br>
<b>Thermoreceptors are specialised nerve cells</b><br>
or receptors that can detect differences<br>
in temperature. They can detect hotness<br>
(heat) and coldness (cold). They are thus<br>
of two types, heat and cold receptors.<br>
<b>They are found throughout the skin to</b><br>
allow<br>
sensory<br>
reception<br>
throughout<br>
the body. The location and number of<br>
thermoreceptors determine the sensitivity<br>
of the skin to temperature changes.<br>
<b>Examples of thermoreceptors are bulbs of</b><br>
<b>Krauze which sense coldness and organ</b><br>
of Rufﬁn which detects heat. These cells<br>
are connected to heat gain and heat loss<br>
centres of the hypothalamus.<br>
<b>Nociceptors (pain receptors)</b><br>
<b>These are receptors that can detect pain</b><br>
and they are found in the skin, muscles,<br>
bones, blood vessels, and some organs<br>
(4.23).<br>

<b>Chemoreceptors</b><br>
<b>These are receptors which detect chemical</b><br>
stimuli such as smell, taste, and humidity.<br>
<b>They have the ability to respond to a diverse</b><br>
range of chemical substances in food,<br>
nasal passage, and blood. For example,<br>
olfactory receptors in the roof of the nasal<br>
cavity can be stimulated by odours. Nerve<br>
impulses from these receptors travel to the<br>
olfactory bulb (Figure 4.24). When odour<br>
molecules enter the nose, they stimulate<br>
the olfactory cilia (tiny hairs) attached to<br>
receptor cells, causing nerve impulses to<br>
pass to the olfactory bulb and then to the<br>
brain.<br>

Taste buds are located on the upper surface of the tongue. Each bud contains about<br>
25 sensory receptor cells with tiny taste hairs exposed to drink and food dissolved in<br>
saliva (Figure 4.25). Such buds sense the five basic tastes: bitter, sour, salty, sweet, and<br>
umami (a savoury, meaty taste). A combination of odours and the basic tastes produce<br>
subtler tastes.<br>

<b>Osmoreceptor</b><br>
<b>They detect the changes in osmotic</b><br>
pressure. The osmoreceptors are primarily<br>
found in the hypothalamus and kidney of<br>
most homoeothermic organisms. They<br>
contribute to regulate fluid balance in<br>
the body (osmoregulation) and modulate<br>
osmolarity in the kidney.<br>
<b>Electroreceptors</b><br>
<b>Electroreceptors detect natural electrical</b><br>
stimuli. They are almost found in aquatic<br>
or amphibious animals because salt water<br>
is a better conductor of electricity than air.<br>
<b>The ampullae of Lorenzini are an example</b><br>
of electroreceptors in sharks. Some<br>
terrestrial organisms such as arachnids,<br>
cockroaches and bees are known to have<br>
electroreceptors.<br>
c) Classification of sensory receptors<br>
based on the location of the stimuli they<br>
detect<br>
<b>Based on the location of stimulus they</b><br>
detect in the environment, three types of<br>
receptors; interoceptors, exteroceptors<br>
proprioceptors.<br>
<b>Interoceptors</b><br>
<b>These receptors detect stimulus which</b><br>
originates from the inside of the body;<br>
especially from internal organs and the<br>
gut. For example, stomach pain stimulus is<br>
detected by pain receptor (nociceptor) and<br>
blood pressure change stimulus is detected<br>
by pressure receptor (baroreceptor).<br>
<b>Exteroceptors</b><br>
<b>These are receptors which detect external</b><br>
stimuli such as light, temperature,<br>
olfactory and tactile. For example, the skin<br>
thermoreceptors detect the temperature<br>
changes of the external environment.<br>
<b>Ear, eye and nose also detect stimuli that</b><br>
originate from external environment.<br>
<b>Proprioceptors</b><br>
<b>These are internal sensory receptors that</b><br>
monitor the degree of stretch of muscles<br>
and tendons around the body. This<br>
information gives an individual a sense<br>
of balance and awareness of the position<br>
of various parts of the body in relation to<br>
each other (Figure 4.26).<br>
<b>Tendon</b><br>
<b>Sensory neurons</b><br>
<b>Motor</b><br>
neurons<br>

<b>Muscle</b><br>
<b>Figure 4.26 Structure of the proprioceptors</b><br>
<b>Mammalian eye</b><br>
<b>The eye is a sense organ which receives</b><br>
light of various wavelengths, reﬂected<br>
from objects at varying distances in the<br>
visual ﬁeld and converts it into electrical<br>
impulses (Figure 4.27). Optic nerves<br>
transmit these impulses to the brain<br>
where an image of remarkable precision<br>
is perceived. The principal functions of<br>
the eye include: controlling the amount of<br>
light entering it, focusing images from the<br>
external world by means of a lens system,<br>
and processing the captured image into a<br>
pattern that can be seen.<br>

<b>Eye accommodation</b><br>
<b>This is the reﬂex mechanism by which</b><br>
light rays from an object are brought<br>
to focus on the retina. It involves two<br>
processes namely; reﬂex adjustment of the<br>
pupil’s size and refraction of light rays.<br>
<b>Reﬂex adjustment of the pupil’s size</b><br>
<b>This involves the control of the amount of</b><br>
light entering the eye, either bright light<br>
or dim light. In bright light, the circular<br>
muscles of the iris diaphragm contract, the<br>
radial muscles relax, the pupil becomes<br>
smaller, and less light enters the eye. This<br>
process prevents damaging the retina and<br>
increases the depth of focus. In dim light,<br>
the circular muscles of the iris diaphragm<br>
relax, the radial muscles contract, the<br>
pupil become large, and more light enters<br>
the eye. This decreases the depth of focus<br>
of the eye (Figure 4.28).<br>
<b>Dim light</b><br>
<b>Circular muscle</b><br>
relax<br>
<b>Pupil</b><br>
<b>Pupil dilates</b><br>
<b>Pupil constricts</b><br>
<b>Radial muscle</b><br>
contract<br>
<b>Bright light</b><br>
<b>Radial muscle</b><br>
relax<br>
<b>Circular muscle</b><br>
contract<br>
(a)<br>
(b)<br>
Figure 4.28  Reﬂex adjustment of the pupil’s size in (a) dim and (b) bright light<br>
<b>Refraction of light rays</b><br>
<b>Refraction of light rays involves bending</b><br>
of light rays as they move through different<br>
media in the eye. At all distance range,<br>
light rays enter to the eye and refracted<br>
to come into exact focus on retina. Light<br>
rays from a distant object (more than 6m<br>
away), are nearly parallel to one another<br>
towards the eye while those from a closer<br>
object tend to spread out (diverging)<br>
as they make angle from the object. In<br>
all these two cases, light rays must be<br>
refracted or bent to focus on the retina.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   235</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
236<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Refraction is greater for light from near</b><br>
objects than for distant objects. The<br>
refraction is achieved at the air-cornea<br>
surface and at the lens when the light<br>
passes from one medium to another with a<br>
different refractive index. The function of<br>
the lens is to produce the ﬁnal refraction<br>
that brings light to a sharp focus on the<br>
retina. The lens is elastic and changes the<br>
shape by the contraction and relaxation<br>
of the ciliary muscle which encircles it.<br>
<b>This assists the lens in adjusting the light</b><br>
from both distant and near objects, which<br>
cannot be done by cornea. When the<br>
ciliary muscle contracts, the tension on<br>
the suspensory ligaments is reduced, and<br>
the lens fattens due to its elastic nature<br>
which increases the degree of refraction of<br>
light. When the ciliary muscles relax, the<br>
suspensory ligaments are stretched, pulling<br>
the lens outwards, making it thinner, and<br>
decreasing the degree of light refraction.<br>
<b>Changing the shape of lens in different</b><br>
manners causes the lens to focus light rays<br>
from near and distant objects on the retina.<br>
This process is called accommodation.<br>
<b>Light rays refraction in an eye at different</b><br>
distances is shown in Table 4.1.<br>

<b>Light from a distant object</b><br>

2.	 Cornea refracts (bends) light ray.<br>
3.	 Circular ciliary muscle relaxes.<br>
4.	 Suspensory ligament stretched.<br>
5.	 The lens is pulled out.<br>
6.	 Light focused on the retina.<br>

<b>The structure of the retina</b><br>
<b>The retina is comprised of three layers</b><br>
of cells. The outermost layer is the<br>
photoreceptor<br>
layer<br>
containing<br>
rods<br>
and cones, partially embedded in the<br>
pigmented epithelial cells of the choroid<br>
layer. The rods and cones convert light<br>
energy into the electrical energy of the<br>
nerve impulse. The middle layer is an<br>
intermediate part which contains bipolar<br>
neurones with synapse, connecting the<br>
photoreceptor layer and the inner layer.<br>
<b>Horizontal and amacrine cells are found in</b><br>
the middle layer. The innermost layer is an<br>
internal surface layer containing ganglion<br>
cells with dendrites in contact with<br>
bipolar neurones and axons of the optic<br>
nerve (Figure 4.29). It is important to note<br>
that cats and some nocturnal carnivorous<br>
mammals possess a reﬂective layer called<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
237<br>
<b>Coordination and Irritability</b><br>
tapetum which is found behind the retina.<br>
<b>This protein layer reﬂects light back into</b><br>
the eye and gives an opportunity for rod<br>
cells to absorb it. This character improves<br>
the vision of cats and nocturnals in dim<br>
light. The bright light shown by cats’ eyes<br>
at night is the result of the reﬂection from<br>
the tapetum.<br>

<b>Structural differences between rods</b><br>
and cones<br>
<b>Rods and cones are generally similar</b><br>
in structure, but they differ in size and<br>
shape, as well as in the arrangement of the<br>
membranous discs in their outer segments.<br>
<b>At the lowest levels of light, only rods are</b><br>
activated. Such rod mediated perception<br>
is called scotopic vision; the difﬁcult of<br>
making visual discriminations under very<br>
low light conditions where only the rod<br>
system is active. The problem is primarily<br>
the poor resolution of the rod system and,<br>
to a lesser degree, the lack of perception<br>
of colour in dim light since such cones are<br>
not involved to a signiﬁcant degree.<br>
<b>Although cones begin to contribute to</b><br>
visual perception at about the level of<br>
starlight, spatial discrimination is still<br>
very poor. As illumination increases,<br>
cones become increasingly dominant in<br>
determining what is seen. In addition, they<br>
are the major determinant of perception<br>
under relatively bright conditions, such<br>
as normal indoor lighting or sunlight. The<br>
contribution of rods to vision drops out<br>
nearly entirely in the so called photopic<br>
vision because their response to light<br>
saturates, that is, the membrane potential<br>
of individual rods no longer varies as<br>
a function of illumination because all<br>
of the membrane channels are closed.<br>
<b>Mesopic vision occurs in levels of light</b><br>
at which both rods and cones contribute<br>
to vision at twilight. Thus, from these<br>
considerations, it should be clear that<br>
most of what we think of as “seeing” is<br>
mediated by the cone system. Thus, the<br>
loss of cone function is devastating, as<br>
it occurs in elderly individuals suffering<br>
from macular degeneration. Individuals<br>
who have lost cone function are blind,<br>
whereas those who have lost rod function<br>
only experience difﬁculty seeing at low<br>
levels of illumination (night blindness).<br>
<b>Differences</b><br>
in<br>
the<br>
transduction<br>
mechanisms of the two receptor types<br>
also contribute to the ability of rods and<br>
cones to respond to different ranges of<br>
light intensity. For example, rods produce<br>
a reliable response to a single photon of<br>
light, whereas more than 100 photons<br>
are required to produce a comparable<br>
response in a cone. Another difference is<br>
that, the response of an individual cone<br>
does not saturate at high levels of steady<br>
illumination, as does the rod response.<br>
<b>Although both rods and cones adapt to</b><br>
operate over a range of luminance values,<br>
the adaptation mechanisms of the cones<br>
are more effective. This difference in<br>
adaptation is evident in the time course<br>
of the response of rods and cones to light<br>
ﬂashes. The response of a cone, even<br>
to a bright light ﬂash that produces the<br>
maximum change in photoreceptor current<br>
pick up is about 200 ms; which is more<br>
than four times faster than rod recovery.<br>
<b>The arrangement of the circuits that</b><br>
transmit rods and cones information to<br>
retinal ganglion cells also contributes to<br>
the different characteristics of scotopic<br>
and photopic vision. In most parts of the<br>
retina, rods and cones signals converge<br>
on the same ganglion cells; that is,<br>
individual ganglion cells respond to both<br>
rod and cone inputs, depending on the<br>
level of illumination. The early stages<br>
of the pathways that link rods and cones<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   238</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
239<br>
<b>Coordination and Irritability</b><br>
to ganglion cells, however, are largely<br>
independent. For example, the pathway<br>
from rods to ganglion cells involves a<br>
distinct class of bipolar cells called rod<br>
bipolar that, unlike cone bipolar cells,<br>
does not contact retinal ganglion cells.<br>
<b>Instead, rod bipolar cells synapse with the</b><br>
dendritic processes of a speciﬁc class of<br>
amacrine cells that makes gap junctions<br>
and chemical synapses with the terminals<br>
of cone bipolars. These processes, in turn,<br>
make synaptic contacts on the dendrites of<br>
ganglion cells in the inner surface layer.<br>
<b>Moreover, the rod and cone systems</b><br>
differ dramatically in their degree of<br>
convergence; a factor which contributes<br>
greatly to their distinct properties. Each rod<br>
bipolar cell is contracted by a number of<br>
rods, and many rod bipolar cells contract a<br>
given amacrine cell. In contrast, the cone<br>
system is much less convergent. Thus,<br>
each retinal ganglion cell that dominates<br>
central vision (called midget ganglion<br>
cells) receives input from only one cone<br>
bipolar cell, which in turn is contacted by<br>
a single cone. Convergence makes the rod<br>
system a better detector of light, because<br>
small signals from many rods are pooled<br>
to generate a large response in the bipolar<br>
cell. At the same time, convergence<br>
reduces the spatial resolution of the rod<br>
system; since the source of a signal in a<br>
rod bipolar cell or retinal ganglion cell<br>
could have come from anywhere within a<br>
relatively large area of the retinal surface.<br>
<b>The one-to-one relationship of cones to</b><br>
bipolar and ganglion cells is, of course,<br>
required to maximize acuity.<br>
<b>Mechanism of photoreception</b><br>
<b>Rods contain light sensitive pigment</b><br>
rhodopsin, which is attached to the<br>
outer surface of vesicles. Rhodopsin is a<br>
molecule formed by the combination of a<br>
protein called scotopsin with a small light-<br>
absorbing molecule called retinene which<br>
is a carotenoid molecule derived from<br>
vitamin A. When the rhodopsin molecule<br>
is exposed to bright light, it breaks down<br>
into retinene and scotopsin. This process<br>
is called bleaching.<br>
<b>Bleaching</b><br>
<b>Rhodopsin</b><br>
<b>Retinene + Scotopsin</b><br>
<b>Rhodopsin is reformed immediately when</b><br>
light stimulation decreases. Trans retinene<br>
is ﬁrst converted into cis retinene and then<br>
recombined with scotopsin. This process<br>
is called dark adaptation. Similary, the<br>
cone system has a very high spatial<br>
resolution but it is relatively insensitive to<br>
light. It is therefore specialised for acuity<br>
at the expense of sensitivity. This property<br>
of the cone system allows us to see colour.<br>
<b>Physiology of seeing</b><br>
<b>The eye works on the same principle as</b><br>
that of the camera. Light rays from the<br>
object pass from the external part of the<br>
eye to the retina through the conjunctiva,<br>
cornea, aqueous humour and pupil<br>
(Figure 4.30). The pupil is an opening<br>
(an aperture) which is controlled by the<br>
iris (like camera shutters) depending<br>
on the amount of light. The stronger the<br>
amount of light, the smaller the size of the<br>
aperture. The lens is positioned between<br>
the outer and inner chambers of the eye,<br>
and its major function is to focus images<br>
on the retina by changing its thickness<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   239</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
240<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
depending on the amount of light from<br>
distant or nearby objects. On the retina,<br>
there are cone and rod photoreceptors,<br>
which are connected to the brain via a<br>
bundle of ﬁbres called optic nerve. The<br>
information received is processed in the<br>
brain, and consequently, the object can<br>
be seen. Thus, the role of the retina is<br>
to translate light into nerve signals and<br>
allow us to see under various conditions<br>
ranging from starlight to sunlight. It also<br>
distinguishes the wavelengths for us to<br>
discriminate colors.<br>

<b>Light</b><br>
<b>Retina</b><br>
<b>Optic nerves</b><br>
<b>Lens</b><br>
<b>Object</b><br>
<b>Inverted image</b><br>
of object<br>
Figure 4.30: Structure of a mammalian eye showing physiology of seeing<br>
<b>The mammalian ear</b><br>
<b>The mammalian ear consists of three</b><br>
parts, namely the outer, middle and inner<br>
ear. The outer ear comprises of an external<br>
ﬂap of skin covered by elastic cartilage<br>
called pinna. The pinna collects sound<br>
waves and directs them into the ear canal<br>
(external auditory meatus). Across the end<br>
of ear canal is a tympanic membrane or<br>
ear drum which separates the outer ear<br>
from the middle ear. The opening of the<br>
auditory canal is lined with ﬁne hairs and<br>
glands which secrete earwax, which is<br>
located in the upper wall of the auditory<br>
canal. Ear wax guards the ear against<br>
entrance of foreign materials such as dust<br>
and microorganisms.<br>
<b>In</b><br>
the<br>
middle<br>
ear,<br>
the<br>
tympanic<br>
membrane begins and ends at a bony wall<br>
containing two small openings covered by<br>
membranes. The two openings are oval<br>
window (fenestra ovalis) and the round<br>
window (fenestra rotunda). There are<br>
three connected bones called ear ossicles,<br>
which are held in position by muscles.<br>
<b>These are malleus (hammer), incus (anvil),</b><br>
and stapes (stirrup). The middle ear is air<br>
ﬁlled part that depends on the equalization<br>
of pressure outside and inside the ear to<br>
prevent damaging the ear drum. There is<br>
a eustachian tube (auditory tube) which<br>
connects the middle ear to the pharynx.<br>
<b>Via this tube, air enters and leaves the</b><br>
middle ear during swallowing to equalise<br>
pressure (Figure 4.31).<br>
<b>Unlike the outer ear and middle ear which</b><br>
is ﬁlled with air, the inner ear comprises<br>
of a complex system of ﬂuidﬁlled tubes.<br>
<b>The cochlea, the coiled structure, is an</b><br>
organ for hearing while the semicircular<br>
canal and the vestibule are organs for<br>
body balance.<br>

<b>The structure of the membranous</b><br>
labyrinth of the mammalian inner ear<br>
<b>There are two labyrinths in the inner</b><br>
ear, namely the bony labyrinth and<br>
membranous labyrinth; one is inside<br>
the other. The membraneous labyrinth<br>
is comprised of the cochlea, vestibule<br>
and three semicircular canals. All these<br>
structures contain a ﬂuid called perilymph.<br>
<b>Membranous labyrinth lies within the</b><br>
bony labyrinth.<br>
<b>The membranous labyrinth is a continuous</b><br>
system of ducts ﬁlled with endolymph. It<br>
is comprised of the cochlear duct, three<br>
semicircular ducts, saccule and the utricle.<br>
<b>The cochlear duct is situated within the</b><br>
cochlea and is the organ of hearing. The<br>
semicircular ducts, saccule, and utricle<br>
are the organs for balance, are also known<br>
as the vestibular apparatus. The cochlear<br>
duct is located within the bony scaffolding<br>
the cochlea. It is held in place by the spiral<br>
lamina. The saccule and utricle are two<br>
membranous sacs located in the vestibule.<br>
<b>The utricle is larger than saccule, and it</b><br>
receives the three semicircular ducts.<br>
<b>The saccule is globular in shape and</b><br>
receives the cochlear duct. Endolymph<br>
drains from the saccule and utricle into<br>
the endolymphatic duct. The duct extends<br>
through the vestibular aqueduct to the<br>
posterior aspect of the petrous part of the<br>
temporal bone. Here, the duct expands to a<br>
sac where endolymph can be secreted and<br>
absorbed. Semicircular ducts are located<br>
within the semicircular canals, and share<br>
their orientation (Figure 4.32).<br>

<b>Mechanism of hearing</b><br>
<b>The mammalian ear functions as an organ</b><br>
for hearing and balance. The process<br>
of hearing starts when sound waves<br>
enter the outer ear and travel through<br>
the external auditory canal until they<br>
reach the tympanic membrane, causing<br>
the membrane and the attached chain of<br>
auditory ossicles to vibrate. The malleus<br>
then takes the pressure from the inner<br>
surface of the tympanic membrane and<br>
passes it by means of the incus to the stapes.<br>
<b>There are about 20 times multiplication of</b><br>
sound pressure as it moves from outside<br>
to inner ear.<br>
<b>The motion of the stapes against the oval</b><br>
window sets up waves in the ﬂuids of the<br>
cochlea, causing the basilar membrane to<br>
vibrate. This stimulates the sensory hair<br>
cells of the organ of corti, on the basilar<br>
membrane, to send nerve impulses to the<br>
brain. When they reach the auditory area<br>
of the cerebral cortex, they are interpreted<br>
as a sound. Many sensory hair cells with<br>
different thresholds at which they are<br>
stimulated exist. The louder the sound, the<br>
greater the number of sensory hair cells<br>
will be stimulated at any one point of the<br>
basilar membrane (Figure 4.33).<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   242</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
243<br>
<b>Coordination and Irritability</b><br>
<b>Maintaining body balance in mammals</b><br>
<b>The parts of the ear which are concerned</b><br>
with balance are the semicircular canals<br>
and vestibule. The semi circular canals are<br>
three curved tubes containing endolymph,<br>
which communicates with the middle<br>
chamber of the cochlea via the utriculus<br>
and sacculus. Each of the three canals is<br>
set in a plane at right angles to the other.<br>
<b>Any movement in any plane will cause</b><br>
movement of canals in the direction of the<br>
head. Each of the three canals possesses a<br>
swollen portion, the ampulla, within which<br>
there is a ﬂat gelatinous plate, the cupula.<br>
<b>The movement of endolymph displaces</b><br>
the cupula in the opposite direction to<br>
the head movement. The sensory hair<br>
found at the base of the cupula detects<br>
the displacement and sends impulse to<br>
the brain through the vestibular nerve.<br>
<b>The brain then initiates motor impulses to</b><br>
various muscles to correct the imbalance<br>
(Figure 4.34).<br>
<b>All information that aid balance and</b><br>
positioning of the body relative to<br>
gravity, as well as changes in the position<br>
due to acceleration and deceleration is<br>
provided by utriculus and sacculus. Such<br>
information is provided by granules<br>
known as otoliths which are embedded<br>
in jellylike materials. Various movements<br>
of the head cause this otolith to displace<br>
sensory hair cells on the regions of the<br>
walls of utriculus and sacculus which<br>
respond to vertical and lateral movements<br>
respectively. Then the sensory hair cells<br>
send appropriate sensory impulse to the<br>
brain.<br>

<b>4.3 Hormonal coordination in</b><br>
mammals<br>
<b>Hormonal coordination in mammals is a</b><br>
function of the endocrine system, which is<br>
composed of a series of glands known as<br>
endocrine glands. They are called endocrine<br>
glands because they are ductless. The<br>
system is called “endocrine” to distinguish<br>
it from “exocrine” glands that use ducts to<br>
convey their chemical agents to the target<br>
cells or substances e.g. glands producing<br>
digestive enzymes. These glands secrete<br>
speciﬁc chemical ﬂuids called hormones<br>
directly into the blood stream. Due to this<br>
reason, the endocrine gland is surrounded<br>
by numerous blood vessels. A hormone is a<br>
chemical messenger produced by a ductless<br>
gland, transported by blood and shows its<br>
effects in a region away from the site of<br>
production. Table 4.2 gives a list of some<br>
hormones, endocrine glands and where they<br>
are produced (Figure 4.35), and the activity<br>
that they regulate.<br>

<b>Properties of hormones</b><br>
<b>Hormones  are all produced by specific</b><br>
cells of endocrine system, they are<br>
transported through  the blood, they<br>
show their effect to the site away from<br>
its source, hormones are speciﬁc for a<br>
particular target, they are soluble organic<br>
molecules and are effective even at low<br>
concentration.<br>
<b>Feedback mechanisms for hormonal</b><br>
coordination<br>
<b>The release of hormones by glands is</b><br>
controlled by several factors, including<br>
the presence of speciﬁc metabolites in<br>
the blood. For example, excess glucose<br>
in the blood triggers the pancreas to<br>
release insulin to lower the level of<br>
blood glucose. Another factor is the<br>
presence of another hormone in the<br>
blood. Majority of the hormones released<br>
from the anterior pituitary gland are<br>
stimulating hormones, which direct other<br>
glands to secrete their hormones. For<br>
instance, the growth hormone releasing<br>
factor causes the release of the thyroxin<br>
hormone. Stimulation by neurones from<br>
the autonomic nervous system can also<br>
cause secretion of hormones. Under the<br>
condition of stress, fear or danger, the<br>
body secretes hormones to combat the<br>
situation.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   245</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
246<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>The timing of hormone release and the</b><br>
amount of hormone to be released are<br>
regulated by a feedback mechanism.<br>
<b>Feedback mechanisms are self regulatory</b><br>
mechanisms in which  when there is a<br>
disturbance or deviation in a system,<br>
series of events occur to either remove the<br>
disturbance (negative feedback) or make<br>
the system to deviate further (positive<br>
feedback). Usually, it is a negative<br>
feedback mechanism which regulates<br>
the release of hormones. In rare cases<br>
positive feedback mechanisms may occur.<br>
<b>An example of the negative feedback</b><br>
mechanism is the release of the thyroxine<br>
hormone (Figure 4.36).<br>
<b>Hypothalamus</b><br>
<b>Anterior pituitary</b><br>
<b>Thyroid</b><br>
<b>Thyroxine</b><br>
<b>Thyroxine</b><br>
<b>Inhibition</b><br>
<b>Stimulation</b><br>
<b>Thyroxine</b><br>
<b>Small effect</b><br>
<b>Main effect</b><br>
<b>TRH</b><br>
<b>TSH</b><br>
<b>Increased BMR</b><br>
<b>Key:</b><br>
<b>Blood/tissue</b><br>
<b>Figure 4. 36 Factors regulating thyroxine</b><br>
secretion and homeostatic control of the<br>
metabolic rate<br>
<b>Thyroxine</b><br>
helps<br>
in<br>
regulating<br>
the<br>
metabolic rate, growth and development<br>
of an organism. The control of the release<br>
of thyroxine is determined by the level<br>
of thyroxine with four iodine atoms<br>
(T4) circulating in the blood. If there is<br>
high concentration of T4 in the blood, it<br>
inhibits further production by inhibiting<br>
the production of Thyrotropin Releasing<br>
<b>Hormone (TRH) by the hypothalamus and</b><br>
thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) by<br>
anterior pituitary gland. Therefore, in this<br>
case, the product of a series of reactions<br>
controls its own production by turning<br>
off the pathway, when it reaches a certain<br>
level.<br>
<b>Negative feedback is also observed in</b><br>
production and regulation of insulin in the<br>
body. A rise of sugar level in the blood is<br>
detected by β cells of islets of Langerhans<br>
of   the pancreas, which release the insulin<br>
hormone. The hormone will then convert<br>
glucose into glycogen which is stored in<br>
the liver and muscles. This causes the<br>
level of sugar in the blood to decrease. The<br>
lower level of glucose then causes the b<br>
cells to reduce production of insulin. This,<br>
inturn, triggers the release of glucagon<br>
from α-cells of islets of Langerhans of<br>
the pancreas. This hormone converts<br>
glycogen into glucose.<br>
<b>The interaction between hormonal and</b><br>
nervous systems<br>
<b>Coordination process is achieved when</b><br>
nervous and endocrine systems act<br>
together. Although the nervous system<br>
and the endocrine system are two different<br>
systems, both release chemical substances<br>
as a means of communication between<br>
cells. The principal role of both systems<br>
is to coordinate and control various<br>
physiological activities in organisms. The<br>
major centres for linking the two systems<br>
are the pituitary gland (control centre for<br>
endocrine glands) and the hypothalamus<br>
(the control centre for the nervous system).<br>
<b>The hypothalamus collects information</b><br>
from the brain and blood vessels passing<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   246</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
247<br>
<b>Coordination and Irritability</b><br>
through it to the pituitary gland. The<br>
pituitary gland directly or indirectly<br>
controls the secretions of other endocrine<br>
glands.<br>
<b>The pituitary gland, which is located</b><br>
at the base of the brain, is directly<br>
connected to the brain region called<br>
hypothalamus. This physical link between<br>
the hypothalamus and pituitary is the basis<br>
for the connection or link between the<br>
central nervous system and the endocrine<br>
system. The pituitary has two distinct<br>
segments, namely the anterior pituitary<br>
and the posterior pituitary. The anterior<br>
pituitary gland is connected by blood<br>
vessels called the portal system, which<br>
has one capillary bed in the hypothalamus<br>
and another in the anterior pituitary. Also<br>
it has nerve terminals that release two<br>
groups of chemical substances known as<br>
releasing and inhibiting factors into the<br>
blood capillaries at the hypothalamus<br>
end of portal system. These chemical<br>
substances pass to the pituitary end and<br>
cause the release of six trophic hormones<br>
(hormones that stimulate other endocrine<br>
hormones to release hormones) which are<br>
stored in the anterior pituitary gland.<br>
<b>In the year 1930, a biologist documented</b><br>
the consequences of removing the entire<br>
pituitary from laboratory rats. As a<br>
result of pituitary removal, the animals<br>
stopped growing, failed to maintain<br>
normal body temperature, and suffered<br>
atrophy (shrinkage) of their genitals,<br>
thyroid glands, and adrenal cortex. Not<br>
surprisingly, their life span shortened<br>
dramatically.<br>
<b>These</b><br>
experiments<br>
suggested that, in addition to secreting<br>
growth hormones, the pituitary secretes<br>
hormones that regulate the production<br>
of a wide variety of other hormones. All<br>
pituitary hormones stimulate the release<br>
of target gland hormones. As their levels<br>
increase, they inhibit the secretion of<br>
hypothalamus and pituitary hormones.<br>
<b>When their levels in the blood fall below a</b><br>
certain level, hypothalamus and pituitary<br>
inhibition stop and start secreting their<br>
chemicals again. This is also referred to as<br>
a negative feedback mechanism.<br>
<b>The posterior pituitary gland is an</b><br>
extension of the brain. It stores and releases<br>
antidiuretic hormone (ADH) or vasopressin<br>
and oxytocin hormone which are produced<br>
by neurosecretory cell bodies, lying in the<br>
hypothalamus. They pass down the nerve<br>
ﬁbres. Nerve impulses are relayed to the<br>
cell bodies of these neurosecretory cells<br>
from other regions of the brain. They<br>
are transmitted down the axons, where<br>
hormones are stored in vesicles (Figure<br>
4.37). The whole process involves both<br>
nervous and endocrine systems. This is<br>
referred to as a neuroendocrine response,<br>
resulting into a pattern of behaviour<br>
known as a neuroendocrine reﬂex.<br>
<b>ADH is released in response to a fall in the</b><br>
water content of blood plasma and leads<br>
to an increase in the permeability to water<br>
of distal convoluted tubule in the nephron<br>
of the kidney. On the other hand, oxytocin<br>
causes the contraction of the uterus during<br>
birth and ejection of milk from nipples.<br>
<b>Oxytocin and contraction of the uterus</b><br>
is a positive feedback mechanism. The<br>
more the concentrations of oxytocin, the<br>
stronger the contraction of the uterus<br>
becomes.<br>

<b>Neurosecretory cells</b><br>
Figure 4.37 Relationship between the hypothalamus and pituitary gland<br>

<b>4.4  Coordination in plants</b><br>
<b>Plants do not have a nervous system; they</b><br>
rely on chemical coordination. Therefore,<br>
most of their responses are slower<br>
compared to animals. Unlike animals,<br>
plants do not move from one place to<br>
another; although the movement is shown<br>
by the characteristics of plants’ sensitivity<br>
and response to external stimuli which<br>
results into growth movements and also<br>
movement of organs. There are three types<br>
of growth movements in plants, namely<br>
tactic movement, nastic movement, and<br>
tropic movement.<br>
a) Tactic movement<br>
<b>Tactic movement; also known as taxis</b><br>
(plural taxes) is the type of movement<br>
which involves the entire cell or organism<br>
moving from one place to another in<br>
response to external stimuli such as light,<br>
water, and chemicals.  Tactic movement<br>
occurs in plant reproductive cells and in<br>
some organisms such as Euglena, and<br>
<b>Chlamydomonas. Taxes may be grouped</b><br>
as positive taxis and negative taxis.<br>
<b>A positive taxis is when an organism</b><br>
or cell moves towards the source of<br>
stimulation, while negative taxis is when<br>
an organism or cell moves away from<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   248</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
249<br>
<b>Coordination and Irritability</b><br>
the source of stimulation. For example,<br>
<b>Chlamydomonas always moves towards</b><br>
light of low intensity and away from light<br>
of high intensity. This type of response<br>
is known as phototactic response or<br>
phototaxis. In this example, the movement<br>
towards light of low intensity is a positive<br>
phototaxis, whereas, that away from light<br>
of high intensity is a negative phototaxis.<br>
<b>Types of tactic movements (taxes)</b><br>
<b>Based on the nature or kind of a stimulus</b><br>
involved,<br>
different<br>
types<br>
of<br>
tactic<br>
movements (taxes) can be identiﬁed.<br>
<b>Examples of taxes include phototaxis</b><br>
(response to light) which can be seen<br>
in Euglena and blue green algae and<br>
chemotaxis  (response to chemicals) as seen<br>
in lower plants such as Funaria where by<br>
male gamete move towards  the chemical<br>
substance released by egg cells. Others<br>
include  geotaxis (gravity), aerotaxis<br>
(oxygen), magneotaxis (magnetic field)<br>
and rheotaxis (water current).<br>
b) Nastic movement<br>
<b>Nastic movement is a non-directional</b><br>
movement of a part of stationary plant in<br>
response to an external stimulus. Based on<br>
the nature of stimuli, nastic movements<br>
can be divided into the following groups:<br>
<b>Nyctinasty</b><br>
<b>This is a nastic movement which</b><br>
occurs in response to diurnal changes<br>
of temperature (thermonasty) or light<br>
intensity (photonasty). An example of<br>
nyctinasty is the opening and closing<br>
of petals of some ﬂowers that occur in<br>
response to alternation in the duration of<br>
day and night. Another example is sleep<br>
movements of leaves of some leguminous<br>
plants in response to the onset of darkness.<br>
<b>Thermonasty</b><br>
<b>This is a movement of plant parts in</b><br>
response to temperature. For example,<br>
some flowers close when the temperature<br>
drops, or some leaves fold when the<br>
temperature is too high.<br>
<b>Chemonasty</b><br>
<b>This is the movement of plant parts</b><br>
in response to chemicals stimuli, for<br>
example, sundew plants close glandular<br>
hairs due to chemical produced by insect<br>
when land on it, this prevents escape of<br>
the insect.<br>
<b>Photonasty</b><br>
<b>This is the movement of plant parts in</b><br>
response to light. For instance, some<br>
flowers or leaves fold following the sun<br>
shine, or the opening and closing of<br>
flowers depending on whether it is day or<br>
night (Figure 4.38).<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   249</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>

(a)<br>
(b)<br>
Figure 4.38 Nastic movement in flower (a) during the day the flower opens and (b) during the night the<br>
flower closes<br>
<b>Seismonasty</b><br>
<b>This is the movement of plant parts</b><br>
in response to touch. Seismonastic<br>
movement is also known as thigmonastic<br>
movement. An example of seismonastic<br>
movement is folding of leaves of Mimosa<br>
pudica in response to touch (Figure 4.39).<br>
<b>Generally, nastic movement can result</b><br>
from changes in turgor or growth rate<br>
between different sides of the plant part.<br>
<b>For example, changes in the water contents</b><br>
of the tissues at the base of the leaf of the<br>
<b>Mimosa plant results in the shrinkage</b><br>
and folding of its leaﬂets. Such response<br>
may be due to different stimuli, such as<br>
light, touch, heat, or electric shock. After a<br>
suitable recovery period, the leaﬂets open<br>
again. Normally, they remain expanded<br>
during the day while closed at night.<br>
Figure 4.39 Nastic movement in the leaﬂets of the Mimosa plant<br>

251<br>
<b>Coordination and Irritability</b><br>
c) Tropic movement<br>
<b>This is the movement of part of a plant</b><br>
associated with growth of plant tissue<br>
caused by a differential concentration of<br>
plant hormones, usually auxins, under a<br>
specific stimulus. The stimuli involved<br>
include light, gravity, water/humidity, and<br>
pressure or touch, among others. Some<br>
common examples of tropic movement in<br>
plants include phototropism, geotropism,<br>
and hydrotropism.<br>
<b>Phototropism. Plant growth movement</b><br>
associated with light. Shoots tend to<br>
grow towards light (positive phototropic<br>
movement), and if the light  is coming<br>
from a single direction the plant will bend<br>
towards that source of light (Figure 4.40).<br>
<b>The roots show the opposite tendency</b><br>
(negative phototropism).<br>
<b>Figure 4.40 Shoot grow towards light</b><br>
<b>Geotropism. This is caused by the earth’s</b><br>
gravitational pull. Shoots show negative<br>
geotropic movement, that is, they grow<br>
away from the force of gravity, while roots<br>
show positive geotropism. If a potted plant<br>
is kept in a horizontal position, the shoot<br>
will bend and start growing in an upright<br>
direction while the roots change their<br>
direction of growth downwards (Figure<br>
4.41).<br>

<b>Negative</b><br>
geotropic<br>

takes<br>
place in response to moisture. Roots are<br>
generally positively hydrotropic (Figure<br>

<b>4.5  Plant hormones (Phytohormones)</b><br>
<b>Plant hormones or plant growth hormones</b><br>
are chemical substances that regulate<br>
plant growth. They are also known as<br>
phytohormones or plant growth regulators.<br>
<b>Plant hormones are signal molecules</b><br>
produced within the plants and usually<br>
occur in extremely low concentrations.<br>
<b>They regulate cellular processes in</b><br>
targeted cells within the plant.<br>
<b>Characteristics of plant hormones</b><br>
a)	 Plant hormones are chemicals that are<br>
required in small amounts to promote<br>
and inﬂuence growth, development<br>
and differentiation of plant cells and<br>
tissues. They are active and effective<br>
even at very low concentrations.<br>
b)	 They are produced in certain parts<br>
of the plant and transported to other<br>
parts of the plant where they elicit<br>
speciﬁc biochemical, physiological or<br>
morphological responses.<br>
c)	 They are transported within the plants<br>
by four different types of movements<br>
namely;<br>
localised<br>
movement,<br>
cytoplasmic streaming, slow diffusion<br>
of ions, and through vascular tissues<br>
(xylem and phloem).<br>
d)	 Each plant hormone evokes many<br>
different responses.<br>
e)	 The effects of different plant hormones<br>
overlap and may be stimulatory or<br>
inhibitory.<br>
f)	 Each plant hormone performs speciﬁc<br>
functions in the plant body.<br>
g)	 The biosynthesis of plant hormones<br>
within plant tissues is always diffuse,<br>
not localized.<br>
h)	 The production of plant hormones<br>
occurs very often at sites of active<br>
growth within meristems, before cells<br>
have been fully differentiated. After the<br>
production, they are sometimes moved<br>
to other parts of the plant, where they<br>
cause an immediate effect; or they can<br>
be stored in cells to be released later.<br>
<b>Types of plant hormones</b><br>
<b>There are ﬁve main types or groups of plant</b><br>
hormones. These are auxins, gibberellins,<br>
cytokinins, ethene (ethylene), and abscisic<br>
acid (ABA). These hormones can perform<br>
their functions either independently or<br>
synergistically (working together to effect<br>
a certain function). For example, auxins<br>
are known as growth promoters, since<br>
they inﬂuence plant growth and assist in<br>
producing a phototropic response which<br>
results into growth. Sometimes, auxins<br>
and gibberellins act together to bring about<br>
cell elongation. This is called synergism.<br>
<b>Alternatively, two plant hormones may</b><br>
work opposing one another, like auxins<br>
which induces apical dominance while<br>
cytokinins prevent it. This is known as<br>
antagonism.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   252</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
253<br>
<b>Coordination and Irritability</b><br>
<b>Auxins</b><br>
<b>Auxins are chemical compounds produced</b><br>
in the root and shoot apices of the plants.<br>
<b>One of their common forms is Indoleacetic</b><br>
<b>Acid (IAA). Auxins have the following role</b><br>
in plants:<br>
a)	 They inﬂuence cell enlargement, bud<br>
formation, and root initiation.<br>
b)	 They facilitate production of phototropic<br>
response.<br>
c)	 Auxins<br>
together<br>
with<br>
cytokinins<br>
control the growth of stems, roots, and<br>
fruits.<br>
d)	 They affect cell elongation by altering<br>
cell wall plasticity.<br>
e)	 They<br>
stimulate<br>
cambium<br>
of<br>
meristematic cells to develop stems,<br>
leaves and flower buds, while in stems,<br>
they differentiate secondary xylem.<br>
f)	 They inhibit the growth of lateral buds,<br>
hence promote apical dominance.<br>
g)	 They promote lateral and adventitious<br>
root development and growth.<br>
h)	 They<br>
regulate<br>
speciﬁc<br>
protein<br>
synthesis in seeds as they develop<br>
within the ﬂower after pollination.<br>
i)	 They stimulate development of fruits<br>
without fertilisation (parthenocarpy).<br>
j)	 They inhibit abscission in leaves and<br>
fruits.<br>
<b>Application of synthetic auxins in crop</b><br>
production<br>
<b>Auxins can be synthesised for agricultural</b><br>
use. It is a proven fact that synthetic auxins<br>
are more useful and cheaper than naturally<br>
produced auxins. Synthetic auxins play<br>
the following roles in agriculture:<br>
a)	 Promote fruit setting<br>
<b>The synthetic auxins such as Indolebutyric</b><br>
acid (IBA) and Naphthalene acetic acid<br>
(NAA) are used to promote fruit setting.<br>
<b>Fruit setting involves a series of changes</b><br>
taking place after fertilisation in the ovary,<br>
resulting into development of a fruit.<br>
<b>Auxins can be used for setting some fruits</b><br>
such as tomatoes and pepper, resulting into<br>
production of fruits without fertilisation.<br>
<b>NAA can also be used to control pre-</b><br>
harvest fruit drop and fruit thinning in<br>
various crops such as apples.<br>
b)	 Promote rooting in cuttings<br>
<b>Auxins (IBA and NAA) are very effective</b><br>
in stimulating root development from stem<br>
cuttings. This is commonly applied in crops<br>
that are propagated through stem cutting,<br>
such as cassava. This form of asexual<br>
reproduction ensures that no change can<br>
occur in the genetic make up of the plants.<br>
c)	 Weed killers<br>
<b>Phenoxyacetic acid is used as a selective</b><br>
weed killer as it kills broad leaf plant<br>
species, especially dicotyledons; hence it is<br>
used in removing dicotyledonous weeds in<br>
cereal (monocotyledonus) crops.  Benzoic<br>
acids are also used as a powerful weed<br>
killer, thus they are used against deep-<br>
rooted weeds.<br>
d)	 Prevent sprouting in potatoes<br>
<b>Phenoxyacetic acid is used to prevent</b><br>
sprouting in potatoes. It is also used to<br>
prevent premature falling of fruits and<br>
leaves in crop plants.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   253</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>

254<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Gibberellins</b><br>
<b>Gibberellins such as Gibberellic Acid</b><br>
(GA) include a wide range of chemicals<br>
that are produced naturally within plants.<br>
<b>Like auxins which can be produced in</b><br>
industry, gibberellins are also commercially<br>
produced from fungal culture. They<br>
perform the following role in plants:<br>
a)	 They stimulate stem elongation and<br>
pollen tube growth.<br>
b)	 Inﬂuence cell division.<br>
c)	 They<br>
promote<br>
ﬂowering,<br>
seed<br>
germination and differentiation after<br>
germination.<br>
d)	 They are important in seed germination,<br>
as they effect enzyme production that<br>
mobilises food production needed for<br>
growing new cells. This is done by<br>
modulating chromosomal transcription<br>
in the growing seedling.<br>
e)	 Promote bolting in rosette plants,<br>
hence increasing inter nodal lengths.<br>
f)	 Promote cell division and elongation<br>
in the presence of auxins.<br>
g)	 Reverse the inhibition of shoot growth<br>
and dormancy induced by Abscisic<br>
acid.<br>
h)	 Break seed dormancy.<br>
<b>Application of synthetic gibberellins in</b><br>
crop production<br>
<b>The synthetic gibberellins have the</b><br>
following role in plants:<br>
a)	 They promote fruit setting.<br>
b)	 They enhance malting in brewing<br>
industries.<br>
c)	 They reverse genetic dwarﬁsm.<br>
d)	 They break seed dormancy.<br>
e)	 They increase plants’ resistance to<br>
pests and diseases.<br>
<b>Cytokinins</b><br>
<b>Cytokinins (CKs) are a group of chemicals</b><br>
that inﬂuence cell division and shoot<br>
formation in plants. In the past, when the<br>
cytokinins were ﬁrst isolated from yeast<br>
cells they were called ‘kinins’. They are<br>
found mostly in regions of rapid cell<br>
division, particularly in seeds and fruits,<br>
where embryos develop. They perform the<br>
following role in plants:<br>
a)	 They promote cell division in the<br>
presence of auxins.<br>
b)	 They induce delayed senescence of<br>
leaves and fruits.<br>
c)	 They are responsible for mediating<br>
transportation of auxins throughout<br>
the plant and affecting internodal<br>
length and growth.<br>
d)	 They promote lateral bud growth.<br>
e)	 They counter the apical dominance<br>
induced by auxins.<br>
f)	 They break seed and bud dormancy at<br>
appropriate temperatures.<br>
<b>Application of synthetic cytokinins in</b><br>
crop production<br>
<b>Synthetic cytokinins have the following</b><br>
application in crop production:<br>
a)	 They are used to prolong the shelf life<br>
of fresh crops such as cabbage and<br>
lettuce.<br>
b)	 They break bud and seed dormancy.<br>
c)	 They keep ﬂowers fresh.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   254</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
255<br>
<b>Coordination and Irritability</b><br>
<b>Ethene (Ethylene)</b><br>
<b>Unlike other plant hormones, ethene is in</b><br>
gaseous state. It is formed from the amino<br>
acid methionine and released from ripening<br>
fruits, nodes of stems, ageing leaves, and<br>
ﬂowers. It is produced as a metabolic<br>
byproduct of most plant organs. It plays<br>
the following role in plants:<br>
a)	 It stimulates ripening of fruits.<br>
b)	 It is involved in axillary bud inhibition.<br>
<b>This</b><br>
occurs<br>
when<br>
auxins<br>
are<br>
transported from the apical meristem<br>
of the stem downward, stimulating<br>
the production of ethylene, which<br>
suppresses axillary bud development.<br>
c)	 It suppresses stem and root elongation,<br>
especially during physiological stress<br>
as in drought.<br>
d)	 It breaks bud dormancy.<br>
<b>Application of synthetic ethylene in</b><br>
crop production<br>
<b>Synthetic ethylene has the following</b><br>
application in agriculture:<br>
a)	 It promotes fruit ripening within a<br>
relatively short period of time.<br>
b)	 It is applied to citrus fruits to attain<br>
attracting<br>
colours<br>
before<br>
being<br>
presented for sale.<br>
c)	 It induces ﬂowering in pineapples.<br>
d)	 It stimulates the latex ﬂow in rubber<br>
trees.<br>
<b>Abscisic Acid (ABA)</b><br>
<b>ABA is also known as stress hormone;</b><br>
because its production is stimulated by<br>
environmental stress or other adverse<br>
conditions, such as drought and water<br>
logging. In contrast to other growth<br>
substances such as auxins, gibberellins,<br>
and cytokinins, which are plant growth<br>
promoters, ABA is a growth inhibitor;<br>
hence it acts antagonistically to the<br>
growth promoters. It is produced by<br>
any tissue containing chloroplasts. It is<br>
concentrated in leaves, fruits, and seeds.<br>
<b>The term abscisic acid originates from the</b><br>
belief that it was a direct cause of leaf fall<br>
(abscission), although this is now known to<br>
be not true. Abscisic acid has the following<br>
role in plants:<br>
a)	 It promotes dormancy and inhibits<br>
growth.<br>
b)	 It causes abscission (fall of leaves and<br>
fruits). When fruit ripens, the level<br>
of auxins which inhibits abscission<br>
falls, while that of abscisic acid which<br>
promotes abscission increases.<br>
c)	 It promotes closing of stomata under<br>
water stress conditions.<br>
<b>Application of synthetic abscisic acid</b><br>
in crop production<br>
<b>Abscisic acid can be sprayed on tree crops</b><br>
to regulate fruit fall at the end of the season.<br>
<b>This helps to ensure that fruits fall at the</b><br>
time; hence avoiding the need for repeating<br>
picking of fruits over a long time span.<br>

10/10/2019   14:07<br>

<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Five</b><br>
<b>Nutrition</b><br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
Living organisms require energy in order to accomplish various metabolic activities.<br>
The main source of energy for organisms in all ecosystems is the sun. Organisms<br>
obtain their food in different ways. Autotrophs use light energy and simple inorganic<br>
substances such as water and carbon dioxide from their environment to synthesize<br>
their own food. Heterotrophs, on the other hand, obtain already made complex organic<br>
molecules such as carbohydrates, proteins and fats, break them down, assimilate and<br>
use them to maintain their life functions. In this chapter, you will learn about food<br>
manufacturing in plants and digestion in mammals.<br>
<b>5.1 Concept of nutrition</b><br>
<b>There are different modes of nutrition</b><br>
by which living organisms obtain food<br>
in order to obtain energy and nutrients<br>
that are required for different metabolic<br>
activities and survival. Nutrition entails<br>
a process by which living organisms take<br>
in food and use it for metabolic activities<br>
such as growth, and body repair. Based on<br>
their modes of nutrition, living organisms<br>
are categorised into two main groups<br>
namely; autotrophs and heterotrophs. All<br>
organisms that can make their own food<br>
from simple inorganic substances such<br>
as water and carbon dioxide are called<br>
autotrophs. Autotrophs such as plants,<br>
green algae, and some bacteria, have<br>
the green pigment (chlorophyll) used<br>
to synthesize food. This is the process<br>
known as photosynthesis. A second group<br>
of autotrophs use a process known as<br>
chemosynthesis. Sulphur bacteria, for<br>
example, use inorganic sulphur as the<br>
source of energy for synthesizing  their<br>
food, and Methanobacter sp. (bacteria<br>
living in deep oxygen-depleted oceans)<br>
use methane as a source of energy. Other<br>
organisms that cannot make their own<br>
food but depend on other organisms for<br>
food and energy are called heterotrophs.<br>
<b>These include animals, fungi and some</b><br>
bacteria.<br>
<b>5.2 Autotrophic nutrition</b><br>
<b>In this type of nutrition, organisms acquire</b><br>
energy from sunlight and utilize it to<br>
257<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   257</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
258<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
synthesize their food using carbon dioxide<br>
(CO2) obtained from the atmosphere. The<br>
organisms that are capable of utilizing<br>
sunlight or chemical energy to make<br>
their own food from simple inorganic<br>
materials are referred to as autotrophs,<br>
which<br>
literally<br>
mean<br>
self-feeders.<br>
<b>Autotrophs such as plants, cyanobacteria,</b><br>
algae, purple sulphur bacteria, purple<br>
non-sulphur bacteria, and green sulphur<br>
bacteria use sunlight energy and chemial<br>
energy to manufacture their food. These<br>
are collectively called photoautotrophs,<br>
which is a combination of three Greek<br>
words:<br>
photos,<br>
auto,<br>
and<br>
trophos<br>
meaning ‘light,’ ‘self,’ and ‘nourishment<br>
or feeders’ respectively. Therefore the<br>
term photoautotroph literally refers to an<br>
organism capable of manufacturing its own<br>
food using light energy. Photoautotrophs<br>
such<br>
as<br>
plants,<br>
cyanobacteria<br>
and<br>
algae contain green pigments such as<br>
chlorophyll 'a', 'b' and 'carotenoid'. These<br>
pigments are important in absorbing light<br>
energy which is vital in the synthesis<br>
of food in plants by the process known<br>
as<br>
photosynthesis.<br>
<b>Bacteria</b><br>
contain<br>
bacteriochlorophyll which performs the<br>
same function as chlorophyll. Generally,<br>
the light absorbed by the chlorophyll in<br>
green plants is converted into chemical<br>
energy during photosynthesis.<br>
<b>Besides light, inorganic chemicals are</b><br>
the alternative sources of energy for the<br>
synthesis of food. Energy from such<br>
chemicals is normally acquired by these<br>
organisms through oxidation reactions. For<br>
example, Iron bacteria called Ferrobacillus<br>
obtain their energy by oxidising ferrous<br>
to ferric ion, sulphur bacteria called<br>
<b>Thiobacillus thiooxidans (also known as</b><br>
<b>Acidithiobacillus thiooxidans) found in</b><br>
hot springs containing hydrogen sulphide<br>
obtain energy by oxidizing inorganic<br>
sulphur. Organisms which obtain their<br>
energy from chemicals through oxidation<br>
reaction are called chemoautotrophs. The<br>
energy obtained by both photoautotrophs<br>
and chemoautotrophs is used to build<br>
food in form of complex molecules such<br>
as sugar, starch, protein, and lipids from<br>
carbondioxide.<br>
<b>Food manufacturing in plants</b><br>
(Photosynthesis)<br>
<b>Photosynthesis refers to a process whereby</b><br>
organisms containing chlorophyll and<br>
carotenoid pigments manufacture their<br>
own food in the form of carbohydrates<br>
from<br>
simple<br>
inorganic<br>
substances<br>
(carbon dioxide and water) using sunlight<br>
energy.<br>
<b>Examples</b><br>
of<br>
photosynthetic<br>
organisms are plants and some bacteria<br>
(bacteriochlorophyll).<br>
<b>However,</b><br>
photosynthesis<br>
in<br>
plants<br>
differs from that of bacteria in terms of<br>
the products released. Unlike bacteria,<br>
plants release oxygen as a by product.<br>
<b>In addition, photosynthesis in plants</b><br>
involves pigments such as chlorophyll,<br>
and<br>
carotenoids<br>
while<br>
bacterial<br>
photosynthesis uses bacteriochlorophylls<br>
in most cases. Bacteria therefore lack<br>
deﬁnite chloroplasts as opposed to plants<br>
which contain deﬁnite chloroplasts.<br>
<b>In green plants, photosynthesis can be</b><br>
summarised by the following equation.<br>
sunlight<br>
chlorophyll<br>
<b>6CO2+ 6H2O</b><br>
<b>C6H12O6+ 6O2</b><br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   258</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
259<br>
<b>Nutrition</b><br>
<b>Thus, the raw materials for photosynthesis</b><br>
are carbon dioxide and water. Plants<br>
obtain<br>
carbon<br>
dioxide<br>
from<br>
the<br>
atmosphere, which diffuses into the<br>
leaf through stomata. Water is normally<br>
absorbed from the soil into the plant<br>
body by the roots and transported via<br>
xylem tissue to the leaves which are the<br>
photosynthetic organs of plants. The light<br>
energy is trapped by chloroplasts which<br>
contain chlorophyll, the photosynthetic<br>
pigments of the leaves or stems in some<br>
plant species. One of the functions of<br>
light energy is photolysis of water, which<br>
refers to splitting of a water molecule in<br>
presence of light to produce hydrogen,<br>
oxygen, and electrons. These products<br>
of photolysis of water are required in the<br>
ﬁrst stages of photosynthesis. The oxygen<br>
released during photosynthesis comes<br>
from water molecules. Two molecules of<br>
water produce one molecule of oxygen.<br>
<b>Light is also important in producing</b><br>
biochemical energy in plants. This energy<br>
is required in ﬁxing carbondioxide<br>
into phosphoglycerate which is rapidly<br>
converted into sugars and other forms<br>
of organic molecules, such as starch and<br>
protein for storage and cellular uses.<br>
<b>Ultimately,</b><br>
carbohydrates<br>
and<br>
other<br>
forms of organic molecules synthesized<br>
by autotrophs are the major sources of<br>
energy for all heterotrophs. Therefore,<br>
they are regarded as energy harvesters in<br>
all ecosystems.<br>
<b>The traditional equation of photosynthesis</b><br>
highlighted above does not illustrate the<br>
real situation of photosynthesis because,<br>
among other reasons, it gives the end<br>
product of photosynthesis as a hexose<br>
sugar. However, the end product is a triose<br>
sugar called 3-phosphoglyceraldehyde<br>
(3-PGAL). The equation shows that the<br>
source of oxygen is carbon dioxide. This<br>
is not correct because experiments using<br>
the two oxygen isotopes, O18 and O16,<br>
show that the oxygen given off during<br>
photosynthesis comes from water, and not<br>
from carbon dioxide. Thus, more water<br>
enters the reaction so that some of it is<br>
evolved as a byproduct as revealed in a more<br>
accurate equation below. In this equation,<br>
<b>CH2O is an empirical formula for the</b><br>
carbohydrate formed by photosynthesis.<br>
sunlight<br>
chlorophyll<br>
<b>CH2O + O2 + H2O</b><br>
<b>2H2O + CO2</b><br>
<b>The site for photosynthesis</b><br>
<b>Photosynthesis takes place in the green</b><br>
parts of the plant leaves’ mesophyll<br>
and bundle sheath cells which have<br>
chloroplasts. A chloroplast is a double<br>
membranous organelle found in the<br>
cytoplasm. Its matrix is called stroma<br>
which contains DNA, free ribosomes,<br>
membrane ribosomes, starch grains, lipid<br>
droplets and photosynthetic enzymes. The<br>
stroma also contains stacks of ﬂattened<br>
sacs or membranes called thylakoids<br>
which contain photosynthetic pigments<br>
called chlorophyll and other accessory<br>
pigments. Thylakoids are stacked together<br>
to form column-like structures called<br>
grana (singular granum).<br>
<b>Structurally,</b><br>
chlorophyll<br>
is<br>
a<br>
long<br>
hydrocarbon chain with a hydrophobic<br>
(water hating) tail a complex ring of<br>
hydrophilic (water loving) head in the<br>
interior. Based on the structure, different<br>
types of chlorophylls can be discerned.<br>
(Figure 5.1)<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   259</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>

<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>There are several types of chlorophyll</b><br>
pigments, including chlorophyll 'a', 'b',<br>
'c', and 'd'. Some organisms contain more<br>
than one type of chlorophyll pigment.<br>
<b>However, chlorophyll a and b are more</b><br>
abundant in most plants while the majority<br>
of protoctists and cyanobacteria contain<br>
chlorophyll c and d. Chlorophyll absorbs<br>
red, blue and violet light but reﬂects green<br>
light. This makes all plants to appear<br>
green in color. Principally, the function of<br>
chlorophyll is to convert light energy into<br>
chemical energy. Other photosynthetic<br>
pigments<br>
are<br>
beta<br>
carotenes<br>
and<br>
phycobilins (found in organisms such<br>
as red algae and cyanobacteria), the red<br>
phycobilin is called phycoerythrobilin<br>
and the blue phycobilin is known as<br>
phycocyanin. They act as light energy<br>
transfer to the chlorophyll to be utilized in<br>
the photosynthesis process. Photosynthetic<br>
bacteria contain photosynthetic pigments<br>
called bacteriochlorophyll which are not<br>
contained in the chloroplast. This is because<br>
bacteria are single celled organisms,<br>
and the entire cell is an equivalent of<br>
a chloroplast. Bacteriochlorophyll are<br>
distributed in the membranes of the<br>
cytoplasm.<br>
<b>Mechanism of photosynthesis</b><br>
<b>The initial stage of photosynthesis can be</b><br>
viewed in two major phases or reactions<br>
and these are light reaction (light<br>
dependent reaction) which takes place in<br>
the thylakoid (grana) of the chloroplasts,<br>
and dark reaction (light independent<br>
reaction) which occurs in the stroma of<br>
the chloroplasts (for the structure of the<br>
chloroplast, refer to Chapter One, Figure<br>
1.12). The light reaction is vital for the<br>
subsequent stage of photosynthesis (dark<br>
reaction), because it is during this stage<br>
when energy, electrons, and hydrogen are<br>
produced.<br>
<b>Light reaction (Light dependent</b><br>
reaction)<br>
<b>Light reaction stage of photosynthesis</b><br>
occurs in the thylakoids of the chloroplasts.<br>
<b>Normally, chloroplasts are aligned in</b><br>
such a way that their thylakoids are held<br>
at right angle to the light source in order<br>
to maximize light absorption. Within<br>
the thylakoids, chlorophyll pigments are<br>
arranged in groups called photosystems,<br>
which are specialised form of chlorophyll<br>
that commonly exists in two forms<br>
namely; Photosystem I and Photosystem<br>
<b>II. Chlorophyll photosystems are named in</b><br>
chronological order of their discovery, not<br>
in a way they are involved in light reaction.<br>
<b>CH2CH3</b><br>
<b>CH3</b><br>
<b>H3C</b><br>
<b>H2C=CH</b><br>
<b>CH3</b><br>
<b>N</b><br>
<b>N</b><br>
<b>N</b><br>
<b>N</b><br>
<b>Mg</b><br>
<b>CH3</b><br>
<b>CH2CH2CO2CH2CH=C(CH2CH2CH2CH2CH)3CH3</b><br>
<b>O</b><br>
<b>H</b><br>
<b>H</b><br>
<b>CO2CH3</b><br>
<b>H</b><br>
<b>CH3</b><br>
<b>CH3</b><br>
<b>Figure 5.1 The Chemical structure of chlorophyll</b><br>
