<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
261<br>
<b>Nutrition</b><br>
<b>Thus, photosystem I was discovered</b><br>
before photosysytem II. Photosystem I has<br>
a specialised form of chlorophyll called<br>
pigment 700 abbreviated as (P700) while<br>
<b>Photosystem II has specialised form of</b><br>
chlorophyll pigment, 680 abbreviated as<br>
(P680). The numbers 700 and 680 stand for<br>
peak of wavelength of light which excite<br>
electrons in these pigments. This implies<br>
that the respective absorption peak for<br>
<b>P700 and P680 is at the wavelength of 700</b><br>
nm and 680 nm. Structurally, photosystem<br>
is a complex of chlorophyll, accessory<br>
pigments, proteins, and other molecules.<br>
<b>They are important in the absorption and</b><br>
transfer of light energy through a series of<br>
reduction oxidation (redox) reactions.<br>
<b>Light reaction uses both photosystems I</b><br>
and II, which are embedded in the thylakoid<br>
membrane. Essentially, light reaction<br>
starts in photosystem II. Light energy<br>
(or photons) is absorbed by photosystem<br>
<b>II. The absorbed energy is transferred to</b><br>
the chlorophyll centre causing electrons<br>
in the chlorophyll centre to be energised.<br>
<b>The energised electrons are excited within</b><br>
the chlorophyll molecule; consequently,<br>
they move from ground to a higher<br>
energy state. This process is known as<br>
photo activation. The excited electrons<br>
are progressively passed on from one<br>
chlorophyll molecule to the subsequent<br>
one via a series of electron carriers to<br>
a nearby electron acceptor molecule<br>
found in the electron transfer chain. The<br>
movement of electrons can be in one<br>
direction because they do not move back<br>
to their original position in photosystem<br>
<b>II but progress to photosystem I, the</b><br>
process which is described as non- cyclic<br>
photophosphorylation. On the other hand,<br>
the electrons can move back to their original<br>
position in photosystem I, in the process<br>
called cyclic photophosphorylation.<br>
<b>Non-cyclic photophosphorylation</b><br>
<b>The</b><br>
mechanism<br>
of<br>
non-cyclic<br>
photophosphorylation involves both<br>
photosystems I and II in a non-cyclic<br>
movement of electrons to produce ATP.<br>
<b>When the chlorophyll in Photosystem II</b><br>
absorbs light, releases an excited pair of<br>
electrons which are transferred to a series<br>
of electron acceptor and carrier systems<br>
including cytochromes. As the electrons<br>
pass through different carrier systems, they<br>
release energy, but these electrons are not<br>
returned to their original position in the<br>
photosystem II rather they are handed over<br>
to the photosystem I. The energy released in<br>
a series of electron flow system is used by<br>
an enzyme called ATP synthase located in<br>
the thylakoid membrane to synthesize ATP<br>
by binding inorganic phosphate to ADP.<br>
<b>This process is called phosphorylation,</b><br>
which literally means phosphate addition.<br>
<b>However, the energy used in phosphorylation</b><br>
emanates from the sunlight. Hence, this<br>
<b>ATP synthesis is precisely described as</b><br>
photophosphorylation. It should be noted<br>
that as the two excited electrons leave<br>
the photosystem II in the ﬁrst stage after<br>
being activated by the light of wavelength<br>
680 nm, they are replaced immediately<br>
by electrons released from the splitting<br>
of water molecules within the thylakoid.<br>
<b>Water molecule splits into hydrogen ion</b><br>
or proton (H+), oxygen and two electrons<br>
(H2O →2H+ + ½O2 + 2e-). This process is<br>
called photolysis, because it only occurs in<br>
the presence of light. Thus, oxygen does<br>
not come from carbondioxide, but from the<br>
split of water molecules.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   261</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
262<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>The chlorophyll of photosystem I is also</b><br>
activated and absorb light energy of the<br>
wavelength 700 nm. The absorbed energy<br>
excites the electrons, causing them to<br>
move from Photosystem I and transfer<br>
through a series of electron acceptor and<br>
carrier molecules, including Ferredoxin<br>
and ferredoxin-NADP reductase. As<br>
these electrons move through the carrier<br>
systems, they lose their energy but they<br>
are not returned to their original position,<br>
instead they are handed to NADP+, where<br>
together with hydrogen proton (H+)<br>
from photolysis of water, reduce NADP+<br>
to NADPH2, also written as NADPH+<br>
or NADPH + H+, as indicated by the<br>
following equation:<br>
<b>NADP+ + 2H + 2e−</b><br>
<b>NADPH + H+</b><br>
<b>The light energy is now stored in the</b><br>
<b>NADPH molecule which accumulates in</b><br>
the stroma to be used in the dark reaction<br>
(Figure 5.2).<br>
<b>NADP</b><br>
is<br>
therefore<br>
important<br>
in<br>
maintaining the ﬂow of electrons in the<br>
thylakoid membrane as it is the ﬁnal<br>
electron acceptor.Therefore, the normal<br>
ﬂow of electrons can be inhibited, if this<br>
electron acceptor is limited.<br>
<b>Thus, the synthesis of ATP in this way  is</b><br>
described<br>
as<br>
a<br>
non-cyclic<br>
photophosphorylation, because the electrons<br>
from photosystem II move in one direction.<br>
<b>They do not move back to their original</b><br>
molecule, instead they move through<br>
different electron carriers, and they are<br>
taken up by photosystem I to fill the gap<br>
of the lost electrons used to reduce NADP.<br>
<b>The formed ATP and NADPH are used</b><br>
in the Calvin cycle or dark reaction to<br>
produce carbohydrates while the oxygen<br>
gas from photolysis of water is released<br>
into the air as a byproduct.<br>

<b>Cyclic photophosphorylation</b><br>
<b>Cyclic photophosphorylation involves</b><br>
production of ATP which uses photosystem<br>
<b>I but not photosystem II. This begins</b><br>
with photo activation of photosystem I<br>
through which the electrons are excited<br>
and pass on to a chain of electron carriers.<br>
<b>These electrons travel back along a chain</b><br>
of carriers to their original position in<br>
photosystem I (Figure 5.3). As electrons<br>
travel back, they release energy which<br>
is utilised to bind inorganic phosphate<br>
to ADP forming ATP with the aid of<br>
<b>ATP synthase. In this cycle, only ATP is</b><br>
produced, but NADPH and oxygen are<br>
not produced.<br>
<b>The amount of ATP required in Calvin</b><br>
cycle is much higher than that produced in<br>
the non-cyclic photophosphorylation. This<br>
makes the cyclic photophosphorylation<br>
important to balance the ATP deﬁcit<br>
without increasing NADPH.<br>

<b>Non-cyclic photophosphorylation</b><br>
<b>Excited electrons always return to the original</b><br>
position or molecule.<br>
<b>Excited electrons do not return to the</b><br>
original position or molecule.<br>
It involves only one photosystem (P700).<br>
<b>It involves two photo systems (P700</b><br>
and P680).<br>
Photolysis of water does not occur.<br>
Photolysis of water occurs.<br>
<b>Reduced compounds are not formed; the only</b><br>
product is ATP.<br>
<b>Reduced compounds especially NADPH</b><br>
are formed together with ATP.<br>
<b>The ﬁnal destination of electrons is photo</b><br>
system I.<br>
<b>The ﬁnal destination of electrons is</b><br>
<b>NADP.</b><br>
The ﬁrst source of electrons is photosystem I.<br>
The ﬁrst source of electrons is water.<br>
Oxygen is not produced.<br>
Oxygen is produced.<br>

<b>Activity 5.1 Experiment to prove</b><br>
the importance of light during<br>
photosynthesis<br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>A healthy potted plant, petri dish or</b><br>
white tile, a beaker containing water,<br>
forceps, hot water bath, a piece of wire<br>
gauze, tripod stand, burner, match box,<br>
alcohol (90% ethanol), strip of black<br>
paper (carbon paper), Iodine solution,<br>
and clips (Figure 5.4).<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Take a potted plant and keep it in a<br>
dark place for 2-3 days so that the<br>
leaves get destarched.<br>
b)	 Cover part of one of its leaves with a<br>
strip of black paper. Make sure that<br>
you cover both the lower and the<br>
upper sides of the leaf to prevent the<br>
entry of light.<br>
c)	 Place the plant in sunlight for 3-4<br>
hours.<br>
d)	 Detach the selected covered leaf and<br>
remove the black paper covering it.<br>
e)	 Place the detached leaf in the beaker<br>
containing water. Boil it for about 10<br>
minutes.<br>
f)	 Take out the leaf and boil it in boiling<br>
90% ethanol in the hot water bath for<br>
10 minutes.<br>
g)	 Take out the leaf and wash it under<br>
running water to remove traces of<br>
ethanol and softening the tissues.<br>
h)	 Spread the leaf on a petri dish or<br>
white tile and put a few drops of<br>
<b>Iodine solution on it. Observe the</b><br>
changes in colour.<br>

<b>Exercise 5.1</b><br>
1.	 Explain<br>
how<br>
plants<br>
obtain<br>
light energy and convert it to<br>
biochemical energy.<br>
2.	 Brieﬂy explain the importance of<br>
cyclic photophosphorylation in<br>
photosynthesis.<br>
3.	 Differentiate cyclic from non-<br>
cyclic photophosphorylation.<br>
<b>Dark reaction (Light independent</b><br>
reactions)<br>
<b>Dark reaction does not require light</b><br>
energy, and it takes place in the stroma of<br>
the chloroplasts. The overall purpose of<br>
dark reaction is to convert carbon dioxide<br>
from the atmosphere into carbohydrates<br>
or sugars which are used to power<br>
primary activities in plants and build their<br>
structures. The process of converting<br>
carbon<br>
dioxide<br>
into<br>
carbohydrates<br>
requires energy produced by ATP and the<br>
reducing power obtained from NADPH<br>
both produced from the light reaction.<br>
<b>The fact that these chains of reactions</b><br>
are described as dark reactions does not<br>
imply that the reactions cannot take place<br>
in the presence of light. It simply means<br>
that light energy is not a vital factor in<br>
this phase of photosynthesis. The vital<br>
factor here is atmospheric carbondioxide,<br>
and the products of light reaction are ATP<br>
and NADPH. Dark reaction is therefore<br>
a synthetic stage of photosynthesis.<br>
<b>However, such reactions, as cited earlier</b><br>
are energetically dependent on light<br>
reactions. The dark reactions involve<br>
a chain of enzyme controlled reactions<br>
described by Melvin Calvin, Andrew<br>
<b>Benson and James Bassham in 1946-</b><br>
1953. These reactions involve a full<br>
cycle of reactions also called the Calvin<br>
cycle or Calvin-Benson cycle; named<br>
after Calvin and Benson. The Calvin<br>
cycle (Figure 5.5) has three major phases<br>
which are carbondioxide (CO2) ﬁxation,<br>
reduction of glycerate phosphate (GP),<br>
also called phosphoglyceric acid (PGA),<br>
and regeneration of ribulose biphosphate<br>
(RuBP).<br>
<b>Carbon dioxide ﬁxation</b><br>
<b>Carbondioxide (CO2) used in dark</b><br>
reaction comes from the atmosphere. It<br>
diffuses into the plant tissue via stomata<br>
pores located in the leaves or stems in<br>
some plants such as cactus. The absorbed<br>
<b>CO2 is attached to a 5-carbon compound</b><br>
called RuBP to form unstable 6-carbon<br>
compound by carboxylation reaction. The<br>
6-carbon molecule quickly splits into two<br>
3-carbon energy rich molecules called<br>
glycerate phosphate which, in principle<br>
are carboxylic acid, 3-phosphoglyceric<br>
acid (3-PGA or PGA).<br>
<b>RuBP is therefore called the carbon</b><br>
dioxide acceptor due to its ability to<br>
accept or combine with carbondioxide.<br>
<b>The reaction is catalysed by Ribulose</b><br>
1,5-biphosphate carboxylase (Ribulose<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   265</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
266<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
biphosphate carboxylase) enzyme, which<br>
is abundant in the stroma of the chloroplast,<br>
and it is usually abbreviated as RuBP<br>
carboxylase, or RuBisCo, or Rubisco, or<br>
<b>RuBPCase, or RuBPco. The PGA are the</b><br>
ﬁrst stable product of photosynthesis and<br>
their formation marks the ﬁrst major step<br>
in the ﬁxing of carbon dioxide in green<br>
plants and photoautotrophic bacteria into<br>
energy rich molecules.<br>
<b>RuBP Carboxylase</b><br>
<b>(5C)</b><br>
<b>(3C)</b><br>
<b>RuBP + CO2 + H2O</b><br>
<b>2PGA</b><br>
<b>Carboxylation is followed by a chain of</b><br>
reactions which involve reduction of PGA<br>
<b>Reduction of phosphoglyceric acid</b><br>
<b>(PGA)</b><br>
<b>The reduction reaction begins when energy</b><br>
supplied by ATP and the reducing power of<br>
<b>NADPH are used to remove oxygen from</b><br>
the PGA. This reduction reaction produces<br>
a<br>
3-phosphoglyceraldehyde<br>
<b>(3-PGAL</b><br>
or PGAL) also called glyceraldehyde<br>
phosphate (GP), which is a triose sugar<br>
(3-Carbon sugar) containing phosphate<br>
group.<br>
<b>PGA</b><br>
<b>PGAL</b><br>
<b>Phosphoglyceraldehyde</b><br>
(3 carbon sugar)<br>
<b>Phosphoglyceric acid</b><br>
(3 carbon acid)<br>
<b>ADP + Pi</b><br>
<b>NADP + + H2O</b><br>
<b>NADPH+H+</b><br>
<b>ATP</b><br>
<b>The difference between the PGA and the</b><br>
<b>PGAL is that, the former is a 3-carbon</b><br>
acid (-COOH) while the latter is 3-carbon<br>
aldehyde (-CHO), therefore, PGAL is the<br>
first carbohydrate in photosynthesis which<br>
is a sugar with more chemical energy than<br>
<b>PGA. It should be noted that the ATP and</b><br>
<b>NADPH utilised in this phase of Calvin</b><br>
cycle are obtained from the light dependent<br>
reactions. The ADP and NADP+ return to<br>
the thylakoids to be converted back to ATP<br>
and NADPH, respectively, by the light<br>
reactions. One of the PGAL molecules is<br>
set aside to be used as a building block<br>
of glucose and other molecules such as<br>
sucrose, starch, cellulose, amino acids,<br>
fatty acids and glycerol. These products<br>
are used for various biological roles<br>
including respiration, building plant cell<br>
wall, building the body, and component of<br>
cell membranes. The majority of PGAL<br>
molecules will be forwarded to the third<br>
phase of the Calvin cycle to regenerate<br>
<b>RuBP consumed in the first reaction of</b><br>
carbon dioxide fixation.<br>
<b>Regeneration of RuBP</b><br>
<b>RuBP is an important molecule utilised</b><br>
in the synthesis of sugars, therefore, it is<br>
necessary for plants to produce enough of<br>
it for synthesis of more sugars and other<br>
vital molecules. RuBP is regenerated<br>
from PGAL, which combines with<br>
<b>Ribulose phosphate (RuP) molecules</b><br>
as summarized in the equation below.<br>
<b>This process involves a complex series</b><br>
of rearrangement of carbon atoms<br>
between sugar phosphates to generate<br>
5-carbon sugar from 3 carbon-sugar. The<br>
formed 5-carbon sugar (RuP) is then<br>
phosphorylated by the remaining ATP<br>
from light reaction to generate ribulose<br>
bisphosphate (RuBP). The produced<br>
<b>RuBP can combine with the additional</b><br>
carbon dioxide molecules and continue to<br>
the Calvin cycle reaction.<br>

one carbon from the incoming carbon<br>

that is, six molecules of carbon are<br>
consumed to synthesize twelve molecules<br>
of PGAL, where by only two molecules<br>
of PGAL are used to make carbohydrate<br>
(glucose) while ten molecules are used<br>
to regenerate 6 molecules of RuBP in the<br>
regeneration phase (Figure 5.5).<br>

<b>Exercises 5.2</b><br>
1.	 Explain how light and dark reactions<br>
of photosynthesis are interdependent.<br>
2.	 Describe the steps involved in the<br>
conversion of phosphoglyceric acid<br>
into sugar.<br>
3.	 What is the fate of PGAL formed in<br>
photosynthesis?<br>
4.	 Naming Calvin cycle as a dark<br>
reaction is sometimes misleading.<br>
Substantiate.<br>
<b>C3 and C4 plants</b><br>
<b>Different plants use different pathways</b><br>
to fix carbon during the process of<br>
photosynthesis. Fixing carbon is the way<br>
plants remove carbon from atmospheric<br>
carbon dioxide and turn it into organic<br>
molecules like carbohydrates. Basing on<br>
the first product of photosynthesis just<br>
after carboxylation, plants are grouped<br>
into C3 and C4 plants.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   267</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
268<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>C3 plants</b><br>
<b>In some plants, the ﬁrst product of</b><br>
photosynthesis<br>
immediately<br>
after<br>
carboxylation is a 3-phosphogylceric<br>
acid (3-PGA). These plants ﬁx carbon<br>
dioxide, following the Calvin cycle as<br>
explained in the preceding section. Plants<br>
which yield a 3-PGA, as the ﬁrst product<br>
after carboxylation, are described as C3<br>
plants. Fixation of carbon dioxide occurs<br>
in the chloroplasts of the bundle sheath<br>
cells. C3 plants account for approximately<br>
85% of all plants including Bryophytes,<br>
ferns, most grasses, and trees. They also<br>
include crops such as cotton, tobacco,<br>
spinach, soybean, and cereal grains (rice,<br>
wheat). This implies that most food that<br>
we eat comes from C3 plants. C3 plants<br>
perform well in temperate conditions, but<br>
they suffer photorespiration which is also<br>
called oxidative photosynthetic carbon<br>
cycle or C2 photosynthesis in hot and dry<br>
conditions.<br>
<b>Photorespiration</b><br>
<b>This is a condition which occurs when</b><br>
carbon dioxide concentration in the<br>
chloroplast drops below 50 ppm while the<br>
level of oxygen is relatively higher. Under<br>
this condition, oxygen and carbon dioxide<br>
compete for the same active site on RuBP<br>
carboxylase enzyme. This in turn triggers<br>
the enzyme to utilize oxygen to oxidise<br>
<b>RuBP to form one molecule of 3-PGA</b><br>
(three carbon) and 2-phosphoglycolate<br>
(two carbon compound) instead of<br>
acting on carbon dioxide to produce two<br>
molecules of PGA (three carbon).<br>
<b>O2 + RuBP</b><br>
<b>Phosphoglycolate + PGA</b><br>
<b>RuBP carboxylase</b><br>
<b>5C</b><br>
<b>2C</b><br>
<b>3C</b><br>
<b>Since the reaction is an oxygenation</b><br>
reaction, the enzyme RuBP carboxylase is<br>
also known as RuBP oxygenase. There is<br>
no energy-rich compound produced, since<br>
2-phosphoglycolate<br>
produced<br>
cannot<br>
enter the Calvin cycle; rather it enters in<br>
the conversion pathways and use NADPH<br>
and ATP to generate PGA, hence decrease<br>
in yield. Decreased concentration of<br>
carbon dioxide occurs during dry or hot<br>
conditions because the stomata pores<br>
through which carbon dioxide diffuses<br>
into the plant normally close to minimise<br>
water loss through transpiration under<br>
such conditions. The global increase in<br>
temperatures and drought conditions in<br>
some places emanating from climatic<br>
changes is likely to impact C3 plants more<br>
than C4 plants, which are more efﬁcient<br>
photosynthetically. Photorespiration involves<br>
a complex network of enzyme reactions<br>
which exchange the cellular metabolites<br>
between<br>
three<br>
organelles,<br>
namely<br>
chloroplasts,<br>
leaf<br>
peroxisomes<br>
and<br>
mitochondria (Figure 5.6). The formed<br>
2-phosphoglycolate is dephosphorylated<br>
by removing of phosphate group (Pi)<br>
to form glycolate which leaves the<br>
chloroplast to the peroxisome. In the<br>
peroxisomes, Glycolate is oxygenated<br>
to give glyoxylate and in this process<br>
hydrogen peroxide gas is evolved. The<br>
formed glyoxylate is converted into<br>
glycine, then this product enters the<br>
mitochondrion in which it is converted<br>
into serine through the series of reactions.<br>
<b>The produced serine is transported to</b><br>
the peroxisome and it is converted into<br>
hydroxypyruvate<br>
then<br>
to<br>
<b>Glycerate</b><br>
(Glyceric acid). The glycerate formed is<br>
transported to the chloroplasts, and with<br>
the help of ATP it is converted into 3-PGA<br>
by adding Pi in its carbon number 3.<br>

10/10/2019   14:07<br>

<b>Nutrition</b><br>
<b>Figure 5.6 Photorespiration pathway</b><br>
<b>C4 plants</b><br>
<b>Photosynthesis in most tropical and</b><br>
subtropical plant species, such as maize<br>
and sugar cane yields a 4-carbon compound<br>
called oxaloacetate (oxaloacetic acid)<br>
after carboxylation, and they are thus<br>
referred to as C4 plants. C4 plants have 2<br>
major biochemical pathways (Figure 5.7).<br>
<b>The ﬁrst pathway involves transporting</b><br>
carbon dioxide from the mesophyll to<br>
the chloroplast of the bundle sheath, a<br>
pathway which was described in 1966<br>
by Hatch and Slack. This pathway is<br>
therefore called Hatch-Slack pathway in<br>
honor of these two Australian scientists.<br>
<b>The second pathway in C4 plants is the</b><br>
normal C3 pathway (Calvin cycle) which<br>
commences once the carbon dioxide is<br>
released in the bundle sheath cells. C4<br>
pathway is characterised by four major<br>
events: fixation of carbon dioxide in<br>
the mesophyll cell, shunting of malate,<br>
regeneration of carbon dioxide acceptor,<br>
and re-ﬁxation of carbon dioxide in the<br>
bundle sheaths.<br>
a)	 Fixation of carbon dioxide in the<br>
mesophyll cell<br>
<b>Carbon dioxide ﬁxation occurs in the</b><br>
mesophyll   cells   and  involves<br>
carbondioxide<br>
acceptor<br>
known<br>
as<br>
phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP). The carbon<br>
dioxide is attached to PEP to form a<br>
4-carbon molecule called oxaloacetate.<br>
<b>This carboxylation reaction is catalysed by</b><br>
phosphoenolpyruvate carboxylase (PEP<br>
carboxylase) enzyme. In this reaction,<br>
therefore, hydrogen and carbon dioxide<br>
are added to PEP to form oxaloacetate.<br>
<b>The PEP carboxylase has relatively higher</b><br>
carbon dioxide afﬁnity compared to RuBP<br>
carboxylase which is used in carboxylation<br>
in C3 plants.<br>
<b>PEP carboxylase</b><br>
<b>PEP + CO2</b><br>
<b>Oxaloacetate</b><br>
<b>The oxaloacetate formed is further</b><br>
converted<br>
into<br>
another<br>
4-carbon<br>
compound called malate<br>
<b>Oxaloacetate</b><br>
(4c)<br>
<b>NADPH + H+</b><br>
<b>NADP+</b><br>
<b>Malate</b><br>
(4c)<br>
b)	 Shunting of malate<br>
<b>The shunting commences as malate gets</b><br>
streamed to chloroplasts of the bundle<br>
sheath cells through small pores called<br>
plasmodesmata, found in the cell wall.<br>
<b>In the chloroplasts, malate dissociates,</b><br>
yielding hydrogen, carbon dioxide and<br>
a 3-carbon acid called pyruvate (pyruvic<br>
acid).<br>

10/10/2019   14:07<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
270<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Malate</b><br>
<b>(4C)</b><br>
<b>NADPH + H+</b><br>
<b>NADP+</b><br>
<b>CO2</b><br>
<b>Pyruvate</b><br>
<b>(3C)</b><br>
<b>This ultimately leads to high accumulation</b><br>
of carbon dioxide in the bundle sheaths. It<br>
should be noted that shunting of malate is<br>
necessary to ensure movement of carbon<br>
dioxide and hydrogen from the mesophyll<br>
to the bundle sheath cells. The hydrogen<br>
liberated reduces NADP into NADPH<br>
which will be used in carbon dioxide re-<br>
ﬁxation. The stages (a) and (b) accomplish<br>
the Hatch-Slack pathway.<br>
<b>Epidermal cells (Non</b><br>
photosynthetic)<br>
<b>Chloroplasts of</b><br>
mesophyll cells<br>
<b>Chloroplasts of Bundles</b><br>

c)	 Regeneration of carbon dioxide<br>
acceptor<br>
<b>Pyruvate generated in the shunting of</b><br>
malate as explained in (b) is converted<br>
back into PEP by the addition of organic<br>
phosphate supplied by ATP.<br>
<b>Pyruvate</b><br>
<b>(3C)</b><br>
<b>ATP</b><br>
<b>ADP</b><br>
<b>Phosphoenol</b><br>
pyruvate (PEP)<br>
<b>(3C)</b><br>
d)	 Re-ﬁxation of carbon dioxide in<br>
bundle sheaths<br>
<b>The released carbon dioxide from the</b><br>
dissociation of malate (refer to the<br>
shunting of malate above) enters the C3<br>
pathway (Calvin cycle). At this stage, the<br>
carbon dioxide is accepted by RuBP to<br>
produce 3-PGA, a reaction is catalysed by<br>
<b>RuBP carboxylase enzyme. The NADPH</b><br>
will reduce 3-PGA to sugar (3-PGAL) in<br>
the same way as C3 plants.<br>
<b>Generally, the C4 pathway consumes more</b><br>
energy (ATP), and it is more efﬁcient in<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   270</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
271<br>
<b>Nutrition</b><br>
yielding carbon dioxide compared to the<br>
<b>C3 pathway. However, ATP consumption</b><br>
is not a problem, considering that much<br>
of it is produced during light reactions.<br>
<b>The fact that the C4 pathway produces</b><br>
high yields under high concentration of<br>
carbon dioxide makes photosynthesis in<br>
<b>C4 plants more efﬁcient in carboxylation</b><br>
compared to C3 plants which under certain<br>
conditions such as hot, dry conditions<br>
undergo<br>
photorespiration.<br>
<b>The</b><br>
<b>PEP</b><br>
carboxylase in the mesophyll of C4 plants<br>
has a high afﬁnity to carbon dioxide, and<br>
it does not get competitively inhibited<br>
by oxygen to cause photorespiration.<br>
<b>Although carbon dioxide is a limiting</b><br>
factor in photosynthesis, C4 plants can<br>
photosynthesize, even if the stomata are<br>
closed due to the presence of high levels<br>
of carbon dioxide in the bundle sheath<br>
cells, just about 20 to 120 times higher<br>
than normal.<br>
<b>Differences between C3 and C4 plants</b><br>
<b>The major differences between these</b><br>
two types of plants are evident in the<br>
photosynthesis process. Generally, the<br>
basic metabolism of the C3 and C4 plants<br>
is similar, but the latter is more complex.<br>
<b>The C4 plants are therefore evolutionarily</b><br>
more advanced than C3 plants (Table 5.2).<br>
<b>Table 5.2 Differences between C3 and C4 plants</b><br>
<b>C3 plants</b><br>
<b>C4 plants</b><br>
<b>The ﬁrst product in carbon dioxide ﬁxation is</b><br>
a 3-carbon compound called phosphoglyceric<br>
acid.<br>
<b>The ﬁrst product in carbon dioxide</b><br>
ﬁxation is a 4-carbon compound called<br>
oxaloacetic acid.<br>
<b>Photosynthesis is limited by low levels</b><br>
of atmospheric carbon dioxide, hence<br>
photorespiration may occur in a limited<br>
supply of carbon dioxide.<br>
<b>Photosynthesis is not limited by low levels</b><br>
of atmospheric carbon dioxide, hence<br>
photorespiration does not occur.<br>
<b>Carboxylation reaction is accomplished by</b><br>
the enzyme RuBP carboxylase to ﬁx carbon<br>
dioxide.<br>
<b>Carboxylation reaction is accomplished by</b><br>
the enzymes RuBP carboxylase and PEP<br>
carboxylase to ﬁx carbon dioxide.<br>
<b>The carbondioxide acceptor is a 5-carbon</b><br>
compound called RuBP.<br>
<b>The carbondioxide acceptors are the</b><br>
3-carbon compound (PEP) and the 5-carbon<br>
compound (RuBP).<br>
<b>Carbondioxide ﬁxation occurs in bundle</b><br>
sheath cells only.<br>
<b>Carbondioxide ﬁxation occurs in both,</b><br>
mesophyll cells and bundle sheath cells.<br>
<b>They are less efﬁcient in ﬁxing carbon diox­</b><br>
ide.<br>
<b>They are more efﬁcient in ﬁxing carbon</b><br>
dioxide.<br>
<b>They have only one type of chloroplast in</b><br>
their leaf bundle sheath cell.<br>
<b>They have two types of cells, each with its</b><br>
own type of chloroplast. (Kranz aatomy).<br>
<b>They do not tolerate hot and dry</b><br>
conditions. They are adapted to cool and<br>
wet environment.<br>
They tolerate both hot and dry conditions.<br>
<b>Example of C3 plants: soybeans spinach,</b><br>
rice and grasses.<br>
<b>Example of C4 plants: millet, sorghum and</b><br>
sugar cane.<br>

1.	 Describe how C4 plants are adapted<br>
to synthesize sugars in the tropical<br>

<b>Factors affecting the rate of</b><br>
photosynthesis<br>
<b>For photosynthesis to take place efﬁciently,</b><br>
certain conditions must be met, and raw<br>
materials must be present. The process<br>
of photosynthesis is influenced by both<br>
internal and external factors.<br>
a)	 Internal factors<br>
<b>The internal factors which are also called</b><br>
plant factors are chlorophyll concentration,<br>
enzymes, inhibitors, and leaf structure and<br>
position.<br>
<b>Chlorophyll concentration</b><br>
<b>Chlorophyll is a green photosynthetic</b><br>
pigment located in the chloroplast,<br>
especially in the grana. Chlorophyll is<br>
responsible for trapping light energy<br>
during light reaction. When chlorophyll<br>
concentration in the leaves is very low,<br>
the rate of light reaction will be reduced,<br>
because only a little amount of light will<br>
be trapped for the reaction, resulting in<br>
reduction in the rate of photosynthesis.<br>
<b>Low concentrations of chlorophyll in plant</b><br>
leaves can be caused by several factors,<br>
such as mineral deﬁciency, ageing, lack of<br>
light and diseases such as fungal diseases<br>
or infections.<br>
<b>Inhibitors</b><br>
<b>An inhibitor is a substance or factor which</b><br>
may slow down the rate of reaction.<br>
<b>For example, many herbicides such as</b><br>
<b>Dichlorophenyl Dimethyl Urea (DCMU)</b><br>
interfere with the electron ﬂow in the<br>
chloroplast; thus, inhibit the light reaction,<br>
and hence no photophosphorylation. In<br>
addition, a relatively higher concentration<br>
of oxygen (above 21%) in the atmosphere<br>
tends to inhibit the rate of photosynthesis,<br>
because<br>
it<br>
lowers<br>
the<br>
amount<br>
of<br>
carbondioxide available to the plant.<br>
<b>Leaf structure and position</b><br>
<b>Leaf is a photosynthetic organ of a plant. It</b><br>
contains chloroplasts, which are organelles<br>
for food synthesis. Leaves have special<br>
adaptation to enhance carbon dioxide and<br>
light absorption for photosynthesis. For<br>
example, they have a broad surface area<br>
exposed to light illumination and thin<br>
leaves with large surface area absorb more<br>
light than small and thick leaves. Carbon<br>
dioxide penetrates more quickly in thin<br>
leaves than in thick leaves. Additionally,<br>
leaves with thin and transparent epidermis<br>
allow more light to reach the chloroplasts.<br>
<b>Furthermore, leaves, particularly of most</b><br>
tropical plant tree species, are positioned<br>
at a certain angle which is normally more<br>
or less vertical. This reduces excessive<br>
light interception and temperature on the<br>
leaf. Reducing this natural angle of leaf<br>
may be detrimental to the chloroplast<br>
because it increases light interception and<br>
excessive temperature. Thus, maintaining<br>
high leaf angle orientation in tropical<br>
trees is necessary in reducing excessive<br>
light interception and leaf temperature to<br>
protect chlorophyll from photo damage<br>
due to excessive light. This in turn<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   272</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
273<br>
<b>Nutrition</b><br>
enhances photosynthetic activity and<br>
helps to sustain high plant productivity.<br>
<b>Enzymes</b><br>
<b>Photosynthesis is an enzyme controlled</b><br>
process. For example, the enzyme PEP<br>
carboxylase is crucial in the ﬁxation of<br>
carbon dioxide in the mesophyll cells.<br>
<b>Similarly, RuBP carboxylase is an</b><br>
important enzyme necessary for carbon<br>
dioxide ﬁxation in bundle sheath cells<br>
during dark reaction. Another enzyme<br>
is ATP synthase found in the thylakoid<br>
membrane. This enzyme is vital for ATP<br>
synthesis during photophosphorylation<br>
process. These enzymes perform their role<br>
actively under optimal condition below<br>
which they become inactive or above<br>
which they are denatured. If the enzymes<br>
are inactive, the rate of photosynthesis<br>
proceeds very slowly. In contrast, when<br>
the enzymes are very active, the rate of<br>
photosynthesis becomes very high.<br>
<b>Activity 5.2 Investigating the role of</b><br>
chlorophyll in photosynthesis<br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>Variegated plant, Iodine solution, bea­</b><br>
ker, ethanol, water, and white tile.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Take a potted plant with variegated<br>
leaves which has been exposed to<br>
sunlight for three to four hours.<br>
b)	Take one leaf and draw its diagram<br>
indicating the pattern of the colour<br>
of the patches.<br>
c)	 Place the leaf in the beaker containing<br>
hot water and boil it for about 10<br>
minutes.<br>
d)	 Take out the leaf and place it in<br>
boiling ethanol in a water bath for<br>
about 10 minutes.<br>
e)	 Remove the leaf from ethanol and<br>
wash it with running water.<br>
f)	 Place the leaf on a white tile and add<br>
a few drops of Iodine solution.<br>
g)	 Record any observable changes.<br>
h)	 Draw a diagram of the leaf showing<br>
the colour pattern after being stained<br>
with iodine solution.<br>
i)	 Compare the colour pattern of the<br>
patches with that of the original leaf<br>
in b).<br>
<b>Safety precaution</b><br>
<b>Avoid direct ﬂame near ethanol because</b><br>
it is highly inﬂammable.<br>
<b>Questions</b><br>
1.	 Why was the variegated leaf used in<br>
the above experiment?<br>
2.	 Explain your observations.<br>
3.	 What was the reason  for boiling the<br>
leaf in ethanol?<br>
4.	 From the observations made in the<br>
experiment, what is the role of chlo­<br>
rophyll in the leaf?<br>
b)	 External factors<br>
<b>The external factors which are also</b><br>
termed as environmental factors include;<br>
atmospheric carbon dioxide concentration,<br>
water availability, wind, temperature,<br>
mineral and nutrients supply as well as<br>
light.<br>

<b>Atmospheric carbon dioxide</b><br>
concentration<br>
<b>The average concentration of carbon</b><br>
dioxiode in the atmosphere is about<br>
0.03%. As the concentration of carbon<br>
dioxide increases from the average<br>
value, it also increases the rate of<br>
photosynthesis.<br>
<b>Carbon</b><br>
dioxide<br>
is<br>
required in light independent reactions,<br>
but the concentration above 0.1% can<br>
damage plant leaves. On the other hand,<br>
low levels of carbon dioxide tend to limit<br>
the rate of photosynthesis. It should be<br>
noted that, C3 plants are affected by low<br>
concentration of carbon dioxide in the<br>
atmosphere, while C4 plants are efﬁcient<br>
in utilising carbon dioxide even when the<br>
concentration in the atmosphere is low.<br>
<b>Temperature</b><br>
<b>Within the optimum range of temperature,</b><br>
the rate of photosynthesis tends to double<br>
for every 10 oC rise of temperature. For<br>
example, the optimum temperature for<br>
plants that survive in temperate climates<br>
is 25 oC. The temperature above 35 oC<br>
usually causes denaturation of enzymes<br>
catalyzing photosynthesis in both, dark<br>
and light reactions, leading to slowing<br>
down, or stopping of photosynthesis.<br>
<b>In low temperature such as below 10 oC,</b><br>
enzymes<br>
catalysing<br>
photosynthesis<br>
become inactive, hence lower its rate.<br>
<b>Nutrients supply</b><br>
<b>Minerals</b><br>
such<br>
as<br>
magnesium<br>
and<br>
nitrogen are components of chlorophyll.<br>
<b>Deﬁciency of these minerals reduces the</b><br>
rate of photosynthesis because the plant<br>
lacks enough chlorophyll molecules for<br>
trapping enough light energy.<br>
<b>Water availability</b><br>
<b>Water is one of the essential raw materials</b><br>
for photosynthesis. It also plays other roles<br>
in the plant body such as translocation of<br>
minerals and gases as well as products of<br>
photosynthesis. Shortage of water leads to<br>
wilting of plant leaves which in turn causes<br>
closing of the stomata. This results into a<br>
reduced diffusion rate of carbon dioxide<br>
into the chloroplast, and hence a decreased<br>
rate of photosynthesis. Additionally, water<br>
shortage causes cells to become ﬂaccid;<br>
thus they cannot function well and this<br>
affects the translocation of synthesised<br>
products.<br>
<b>Light</b><br>
<b>The effect of light on the rate of</b><br>
photosynthesis can be described on the<br>
basis of its quality (light wavelength) and<br>
quantity (light intensity).<br>
Light quality (wavelength of colours).<br>
<b>Photosynthesis usually occurs when green</b><br>
plants absorb light within a limit of visible<br>
light spectrum. The most effective range<br>
is within a red-orange band (600- 700 nm)<br>
and a blue-violet band (400-500 nm). The<br>
central band is also effective, but less than<br>
others because the chlorophyll molecules<br>
show very little absorption of light in<br>
this region. Maximum light absorption<br>
corresponds to the maximum rate of<br>
photosynthesis.<br>
<b>Light intensity. The rate of photosynthesis</b><br>
varies proportionally to light intensity.<br>
<b>However, at a certain point called light</b><br>
saturation point, the rate of photosynthesis<br>
reaches its maximum and therefore<br>
attains constancy. Any further increase<br>
in light intensity brings no effect on the<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   274</b><br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>

275<br>
<b>Nutrition</b><br>
rate of photosynthesis, if other factors<br>
are limiting. However, unless the plant<br>
is adapted to high light intensity, the<br>
chlorophyll might undergo bleaching.<br>
<b>Leaf adaptation to very high light intensity</b><br>
includes presence of thick cuticle and<br>
hairs. Light intensity varies with location<br>
and seasons of the year. In the tropical and<br>
subtropical areas, light intensity is higher<br>
than in the temperate areas. Light intensity<br>
also decreases during rainy seasons due to<br>
cloud cover.<br>
<b>During photosynthesis, the plant consumes</b><br>
carbon dioxide via respiration as it<br>
eliminates oxygen, which is important for<br>
respiration. A point is reached when light<br>
intensity causes the rate of photosynthesis<br>
to balance with that of respiration. This<br>
means that the rate of carbon dioxide<br>
production from respiration is equals<br>
to that of carbon dioxide consumption<br>
in photosynthesis. This is called a<br>
compensation point of a plant, at which<br>
net gain of atmospheric air is zero (Figure<br>
5.8).<br>

<b>Wind velocity</b><br>
<b>Wind moving at high speed reduces the</b><br>
rate of photosynthesis because strong wind<br>
usually facilitates transpiration and hence<br>
affects the availability of water. Therefore,<br>
speedy wind reduces the amount of<br>
water available as the raw material for<br>
photosynthesis, and consequently reduces<br>
the rate of photosynthesis.<br>

<b>5.3  Heterotrophic nutrition</b><br>
<b>Heterotrophic nutrition is the mode of</b><br>
nutrition in which an organism feeds by<br>
taking in organic substances made by other<br>
organisms. Organisms that obtain organic<br>
food substances from other organisms<br>
are termed as heterotrophs. Examples:<br>
animals, fungi, some protoctists and most<br>
monerans.<br>
<b>Food  materials  synthesized by autotrophs</b><br>
are in the form of complex molecules,<br>
which contain chemical energy locked<br>
in their bonds. Heterotrophs ingest large<br>
molecules and they require a digestive<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   275</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
276<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
system to break down large food<br>
molecules into simpler forms which can<br>
be absorbed for utilization in their bodies.<br>
<b>Furthermore, they require energy to carry</b><br>
out life processes like cellular metabolism.<br>
<b>Some bacteria form a special group of</b><br>
heterotrophs called photoheterotrophs.<br>
<b>These bacteria can utilize organic raw</b><br>
materials to synthesize their food in<br>
the presence of light energy. Animals,<br>
including human beings utilize six<br>
different types of nutrients for proper<br>
body functioning. These nutrients can<br>
be categorized into two major groups:<br>
organic and inorganic nutrients. Organic<br>
nutrients include carbohydrate which<br>
supply energy for body functions, fats or<br>
lipids which constitute the major part of<br>
cell membrane and stored form of body<br>
energy, proteins for growth and repair<br>
of body tissues, and vitamins for body<br>
defence. Inorganic nutrients include water<br>
which is a vital ﬂuid required for chemical<br>
reactions to take place and transport of<br>
materials and minerals which are essential<br>
for proper body functioning.<br>
<b>Types of heterotrophic nutrition</b><br>
<b>Heterotrophs</b><br>
obtain<br>
their<br>
food<br>
by<br>
various ways. There are three forms<br>
of<br>
heterotrophic<br>
nutrition,<br>
namely<br>
saprophytic, symbiotic, and holozoic<br>
nutrition.<br>
a)	 Saprotrophic nutrition<br>
<b>This type of nutrition is also referred to as</b><br>
saprophytic nutrition. The term saprotroph<br>
comes from two Greek words; sapros<br>
and trophos where sapros means ‘rotten’<br>
and trophos means ‘feeder.’ This type<br>
of nutrition involves organisms feeding<br>
on soluble organic compounds obtained<br>
from dead or decaying bodies of other<br>
organisms, mainly plants and animals.<br>
<b>Digestion is accomplished by producing</b><br>
extracellular digestive enzymes which<br>
reduce the tissues of the dead or decaying<br>
organisms into solution form which can<br>
be readily taken up. Most bacteria and<br>
fungi, such as Mucor, Rhizopus, and yeast<br>
are saprotrophs.<br>
b)	 Symbiotic nutrition (Symbiosis)<br>
<b>The term symbiosis literally means ‘living</b><br>
together.’ Symbiosis is therefore a natural<br>
association between two or more different<br>
species. There are three common types<br>
of symbiotic relationships which are<br>
mutualism, commensalism and parasitism.<br>
<b>Mutualism.  This refers to the association</b><br>
between two living organisms of different<br>
species in which both benefit. Therefore,<br>
the association or relationship is beneﬁcial<br>
to both partners. The cellulose digesting<br>
bacteria living in the ruminant herbivores<br>
is an example of organisms which show<br>
a mutualistic relationship. These bacteria<br>
can only survive in anaerobic conditions<br>
found in a ruminant’s alimentary canal,<br>
and they feed on cellulose contained<br>
in the host’s diet. On the other hand,<br>
ruminants cannot digest cellulose directly<br>
but, as the cellulose digesting bacteria<br>
feed on the cellulose, they convert it into<br>
simple compounds which can be digested,<br>
absorbed and assimilated by the ruminants.<br>
<b>Mutualistic form of symbiotic nutrition is</b><br>
also evident between leguminous plants<br>
and nitrogen ﬁxing bacteria, where by<br>
the bacteria accommodated within the<br>
root nodules of these plants fix nitrogen<br>
from the air by converting it into nitrogen<br>
useful compounds such as nitrates. These<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   276</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
277<br>
<b>Nutrition</b><br>
nutrients are required by plants for their<br>
growth and development, while on<br>
the other hand, the bacteria benefit by<br>
getting shelter and synthesised nutrients<br>
from the plants. Algae and fungi form a<br>
mutualistic association called lichen. In<br>
this relationship algae synthesise food<br>
and supply some to the fungi while<br>
the fungi cover the algae and protect it<br>
from desiccation. Also, fungi and roots<br>
of vascular plants form a mutualistic<br>
association called mycorrhizae. The role of<br>
fungi in this relationship is to increase the<br>
surface area for the plant roots absorption<br>
of nutrients. The plant supplies some of<br>
the manufactured food to the fungi which<br>
normally cannot photosynthesise its food.<br>
<b>Commensalism. Commensalism is a close</b><br>
association between two living organisms<br>
of different species in which one organism<br>
benefits and the other organism neither<br>
benefits nor is harmed. For example, cattle<br>
egrets follow herds of cattle or buffalo and<br>
feed on insects disturbed by the animals.<br>
<b>The cattle and buffalo are not harmed by</b><br>
the feeding activities of the cattle egret.<br>
<b>Therefore, in this association one member</b><br>
(the cattle egret) benefits while the other<br>
(cattle or buffalo) neither benefits nor is<br>
harmed.<br>
Parasitism.<br>
<b>Parasitism</b><br>
is<br>
a<br>
close<br>
association between two organisms of<br>
different species, whereby one organism<br>
called a parasite beneﬁts by obtaining its<br>
nutrients and shelter from another living<br>
organism called a host. Normally, the<br>
parasite may ultimately cause harm to<br>
the host but, in some cases, it can exist<br>
without killing the host. Some parasites<br>
such as tape worms and liver ﬂuke live in<br>
the internal organs or tissues of the host<br>
body. These types of parasites are called<br>
endoparasites. On the other hand, some<br>
parasites such as ticks and bed bugs attach<br>
themselves on the surface of the body<br>
of their living host and suck ﬂuids from<br>
them; and these are called ectoparasites.<br>
<b>Both endoparasites and ectoparasites are</b><br>
highly adapted to their mode of nutrition.<br>
<b>For example, most parasites have hooks</b><br>
and haustoria for attachment and sucking<br>
nutrients from the host.<br>
c)	 Holozoic nutrition<br>
<b>Holozoic</b><br>
nutrition<br>
is<br>
a<br>
form<br>
of<br>
heterotrophic nutrition exhibited mainly<br>
by free living animals which have a<br>
specialized digestive tract, also called<br>
alimentary canal. The nutrition of this<br>
form involves several steps such as<br>
taking in solid or liquid food, followed<br>
by mechanical and chemical digestion in<br>
the alimentary canal. The digested food<br>
materials are reduced into simple forms,<br>
which can be absorbed and assimilated by<br>
body cells. Holozoic organisms include<br>
animals such as herbivores which feed on<br>
plants, carnivores which feed on living<br>
animals; omnivores which feed on both<br>
plants and ﬂesh, and decomposers feeding<br>
on remains of dead animals and plants,<br>
or on dung and faeces. The undigested<br>
food is eliminated from the body while<br>
the digested soluble food molecules get<br>
absorbed and later assimilated to generate<br>
energy and some are incorporated into the<br>
body cells.<br>
<b>Holozoic nutrition in mammals</b><br>
<b>The food taken in by mammals in most</b><br>
cases is in solid form; therefore, it must be<br>
converted into simpler form, for it to be<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   277</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
278<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
absorbed and assimilated in their bodies.<br>
<b>The holozoic nutrition involves ingestion,</b><br>
digestion, absorption, assimilation, and<br>
egestion.<br>
a)	 Ingestion<br>
<b>This process involves taking in solid or</b><br>
liquid food into the gut or alimentary canal<br>
or gastrointestinal tract of an organism.<br>
<b>It is aided by structures such as teeth,</b><br>
tentacles and claws through which food<br>
substance is taken into the mouth through<br>
eating or drinking.<br>
b)	 Digestion<br>
<b>Digestion is the process of breaking</b><br>
down large biochemical compounds<br>
contained in food into smaller and soluble<br>
molecules that can easily be absorbed<br>
into the body. It involves mechanical and<br>
chemical digestion. Mechanical digestion<br>
involves the physical break down of the<br>
food into smaller pieces; and this begins<br>
in the mouth where the food is chewed<br>
and rolled by the tongue, while lubricated<br>
by the saliva before it is swallowed. The<br>
tongue, teeth and saliva are thus important<br>
in preparing the food from large particles<br>
into bolus which can easily be swallowed<br>
for subsequent digestion stages. The<br>
mastication of food from larger particles<br>
to small ones is necessary for reducing<br>
the food into ﬁne particles which makes it<br>
easy to be swallowed as well as harnessing<br>
nutrients from the food during digestion.<br>
<b>Additionally,</b><br>
mechanical<br>
digestion<br>
involves mechanical contraction of the<br>
gut, which pounds up and mixes the semi-<br>
solid foods.<br>
<b>Similarly, digestion process is done</b><br>
chemically by the action of digestive<br>
enzymes on smaller pieces to yield<br>
simpler molecules with suitable size<br>
for absorption by the epithelia cells.<br>
<b>Chemical digestion is a complex process</b><br>
that involves a diversity of digestive<br>
enzymes secreted by the alimentary canal,<br>
which convert various food substances<br>
into simpler and absorbable form. For<br>
example, polysaccharides are broken<br>
down into monosaccharides; proteins are<br>
broken down into amino acids; and fats or<br>
lipids are broken down into fatty acids and<br>
glycerol.<br>
c)	 Absorption<br>
<b>This is the uptake of the digested soluble</b><br>
products and other substances across the<br>
linings of the gut into the blood stream and<br>
lymphatic system. The soluble substances<br>
include vitamins and minerals. The small<br>
intestine is the site where digested foods<br>
are absorbed through diffusion or active<br>
transport. The inner wall of the small<br>
intestine; also called the mucosa is lined<br>
with microscopic ﬁnger-like projections<br>
called villi. The epithelial cells of the<br>
small intestine have microvilli. Both villi<br>
and microvilli have a role of increasing<br>
surface area for nutrients absorption.<br>
<b>The villi are equipped with a network of</b><br>
blood capillaries and lymphatic vessels.<br>
<b>Normally, the absorbed nutrients enter the</b><br>
blood vessels and are supplied to different<br>
body organs.<br>
d)	 Assimilation<br>
<b>This is the process of incorporating and</b><br>
using the absorbed food molecules into<br>
the body. The digested materials, which<br>
are absorbed into the blood, are carried<br>
by the blood stream to the body cells and<br>
tissues for use in life processes such as<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   278</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
279<br>
<b>Nutrition</b><br>
respiration, growth, repairing body tissues<br>
and maintaining good health. In addition,<br>
excess of such food materials may be<br>
stored for future use in the liver, muscles<br>
and adipose tissues.<br>
e)	 Egestion<br>
<b>Egestion is the process of eliminating</b><br>
undigested food materials from the gut<br>
through the anus. This is the ﬁnal stage<br>
in holozoic nutrition in mammals during<br>
which the undigested food materials or<br>
faeces are removed from the alimentary<br>
canal.<br>
<b>The process of digestion</b><br>
<b>Digestion in mammals such as human</b><br>
beings   occurs throughout the alimentary<br>
canal. The alimentary canal in humans<br>
comprises<br>
of<br>
mouth,<br>
oesophagus,<br>
stomach, small intestine, large intestine<br>
(colon),<br>
caecum,<br>
rectum<br>
and<br>
anus<br>
(Figure 5.9). The alimentary canal is also<br>
associated with the accessory organs such<br>
as the liver, salivary glands, and pancreas,<br>
which assist in digestion. The wall of the<br>
alimentary canal has almost the same<br>
basic structure throughout its length.  The<br>
wall is lined by the simple epithelium or<br>
glandular epithelium tissue and all parts of<br>
the gut contain circular and longitudinal<br>
muscles.<br>

<b>Epithelial tissue</b><br>
<b>Epithelial tissue is a living tissue of</b><br>
various parts of the body. It consists of a<br>
single cell layer which usually occurs on<br>
the surface and covers the organs, cavities,<br>
and tubes. For instance, it lines the<br>
heart, blood vessels, lymphatic vessels,<br>
and the intestine within the organism.<br>
<b>There are two major types of epithelial</b><br>
tissue; one is the simple epithelial tissue<br>
which has a single layer of tissue and is<br>
further subdivided into ﬁve sub types<br>
namely; squamous, cuboidal, columnar<br>
pseudostratiﬁed, ciliated, and glandular<br>
epithelial tissues. The second group of<br>
epithelial tissue comprises of epithelia<br>
tissues which have more than one cell<br>
thick. This group includes transitional and<br>
stratiﬁed epithelial tissues.<br>
<b>Columnar epithelium</b><br>
<b>This tissue consists of thin columns of</b><br>
elongated cells arranged at right angle<br>
to the basement membrane. The cells<br>
possess nuclei at their bases, and they are<br>
mostly associated with regions such as the<br>
walls of the intestines and stomach, where<br>
secretion and absorption are the major<br>
functions. Columnar epithelium tissues<br>
are adapted to withstand wear and tear.<br>
<b>Epithelial tissue cells are interspaced by</b><br>
the goblet cells that are responsible for<br>
secreting mucus which helps to protect<br>
the stomach from digestive enzymes and<br>
acidic content of the gastric glands. Some<br>
epithelial tissues are ciliated while others<br>
have microvilli that increase the surface<br>
area for absorption. Part of the surface<br>
that is occupied by the microvilli is called<br>
brush boarder (Figure 5.10).<br>
<b>Cilia (microvilli)</b><br>
<b>Mucus in goblet cell</b><br>
<b>Nucleus</b><br>
<b>Absorptive cell</b><br>
<b>Basement membrane</b><br>
<b>Connective tissue</b><br>
<b>Figure 5.10 Simple columnar epithelium</b><br>
<b>Glandular epithelium</b><br>
<b>This epithelium tissue contains secretory</b><br>
cells that are closely packed (Figure 5.11).<br>
<b>Secretory cells secrete materials such as</b><br>
mucus into the cavity or a space lined by it.<br>
<b>For example, in the stomach and the small</b><br>
intestine the mucus protects and lubricates<br>
the lumen of the intestine and stomach. In<br>
some parts, glandular epithelium is folded<br>
in various ways to form glands such as<br>
gastric glands whose major function is<br>
to secrete enzymes and ﬂuids. Glandular<br>
epithelium can be individual cells, such<br>
as goblet cells or aggregates of glandular<br>
cells, such as multicellular glands like<br>
exocrine and endocrine glands. Examples<br>
of glandular epithelial tissues found in the<br>
digestive system include simple tubular<br>
glands such as crypts of Lieberkuhn found<br>
in the intestines, simple branched tubular<br>
glands such as gastric glands found in<br>
the stomach walls (gastric mucosa), and<br>
compound saccular and compound tubular<br>
glands of the salivary glands.<br>

<b>Cuboidal epithelium</b><br>
<b>This tissue is made up of cells which are</b><br>
cube-like in shape with a central nucleus.<br>
<b>They usually form a lining of the salivary</b><br>
glands where they perform secretory<br>
functions (Figure 5.12). In some cases<br>
the cell surface of this tissue is ciliated or<br>
ﬂagellated.<br>

<b>Digestion in the mouth</b><br>
<b>Mechanical and chemical digestion of food</b><br>
starts in the mouth. Mechanical digestion<br>
is achieved by teeth through mastication<br>
(chewing). During mastication, the food<br>
is mixed with saliva, a watery mixture of<br>
mucus and amylase secreted by the salivary<br>
glands in response to thought, smell, taste<br>
or sight of food. Saliva is a neutral or very<br>
weak alkali with the pH ranging between<br>
<b>6.5 and 7.5. Saliva has the following</b><br>
functions with regard to digestion: it<br>
lubricates food so that it can move through<br>
the oesophagus easily; it catalyses the<br>
hydrolysis of starch into maltose using<br>
the enzyme called salivary α-amylase; It<br>
maintains pH of the mouth between 6.5<br>
and 7.5. This level is optimum for the<br>
action of salivary amylase to function<br>
which is accomplished by its constituent<br>
mineral salts (example NaHCO3).<br>
<b>Chemically, the digestion of food in the</b><br>
mouth involves converting starch into<br>
maltose by salivary α-amylase. The<br>
tongue which is located at the back of the<br>
buccal cavity rolls the food into a ball-like<br>
structure called bolus and forces it against<br>
the soft palate during swallowing, thereby<br>
closing the nasal cavity. The opening in<br>
the larynx (voice box) called glottis, is<br>
also closed by a flap like structure called<br>
epiglottis. Then, the bolus enters the<br>
oesophagus.<br>
<b>The oesophagus</b><br>
<b>In the oesophagus the bolus is moved by</b><br>
a series of wave-like movement caused<br>
by involuntary contraction and relaxation<br>
of its circular and longitudinal smooth<br>
muscles. This process is described as<br>
peristalsis. The contraction of circular<br>
muscles (inner muscles) causes the<br>
oesophagus to become narrow and long.<br>
<b>The contraction of longitudinal muscles</b><br>
(outer muscles) causes the oesophagus<br>
to become wide and short. These<br>
contractions and relaxations of circular<br>
and longitudinal muscles push the bolus<br>
down the alimentary canal. Additionally,<br>
the peristalsis waves of contractions assist<br>
in mechanical digestion (Figure 5.13).<br>
<b>Oesophagial sphincter</b><br>
contracted<br>
<b>Contracted muscles</b><br>
<b>Sphincter relaxed</b><br>
<b>Stomach</b><br>

<b>The stomach</b><br>
<b>The stomach is a highly elastic muscular</b><br>
organ. It has two valve-like rings of<br>
smooth muscles called sphincters that can<br>
open and close. One sphincter is called<br>
cardiac sphincter. It is located between the<br>
oesophagus and the stomach. The second<br>
sphincter is called pyloric sphincter. It is<br>
positioned between the small intestine and<br>
the stomach. The stomach wall consists<br>
of a layer of mucous membrane called<br>
gastric mucosa, it is highly folded and<br>
is equipped with small pits (gastric pits)<br>
leading to gastric glands (Figure 5.14), in<br>
which gastric juice is secreted.<br>
<b>Gastric pits</b><br>
<b>Gastric gland</b><br>
<b>Gastric gland</b><br>
<b>Chief cells</b><br>
<b>Gastric glands</b><br>
<b>Parietal cells</b><br>
<b>Mucous</b><br>
cell neck<br>
<b>Surface</b><br>
epithelium<br>
<b>Pepsinogen</b><br>
<b>Pepsin</b><br>
<b>HCl</b><br>
<b>Figure 5.14 Structure of the gastric mucosa</b><br>
<b>Gastric</b><br>
juice<br>
has<br>
the<br>
following<br>
components:<br>
<b>Water. This is a solvent involved in</b><br>
hydrolysis by which food substances are<br>
broken down.<br>
<b>Hydrochloric acid (HCl). This is an acid</b><br>
produced by parietal cells of the gastric<br>
mucosa. Hydrochloric acid activates<br>
prorennin and pepsinogen into rennin and<br>
pepsin respectively, also kills any bacteria<br>
that might have entered the stomach<br>
through food.<br>
<b>Mucus. This protects the stomach from its</b><br>
own digestive enzymes and lubricates the<br>
wall for easy passage of food to the small<br>
intestine.<br>
<b>Pepsinogen. It is produced by the chief</b><br>
cells of the stomach wall. It is a precursor<br>
or inactive form of pepsin. Pepsinogen<br>
is activated by hydrochloric acid in the<br>
stomach to form pepsin; an enzyme<br>
responsible for the breaking down of<br>
polypeptides into peptides.<br>
<b>Prorennin: This is a precursor of rennin,</b><br>
the enzyme that catalyses the conversion<br>
of soluble milk protein into an insoluble<br>
milk protein. In other words, rennin is<br>
an important enzyme in coagulation or<br>
curdling of milk in the stomach. The<br>
coagulated milk is semi solid and it can<br>
be retained in the stomach for a relatively<br>
long time for proper digestion. This is very<br>
important in lactating young mammals.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   282</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
283<br>
<b>Nutrition</b><br>
<b>In the stomach, mechanical digestion also</b><br>
occurs due to continuous contractions of<br>
the stomach wall which enhance breaking<br>
down of food mechanically. The combined<br>
action of mechanical and chemical<br>
digestion produces a creamy paste, an<br>
acid chyme. The formation of this chyme<br>
stimulates the receptors on the stomach<br>
wall, which in turn stimulates the pyloric<br>
sphincter muscles to relax and allow food<br>
to pass into the duodenum. Digestion of<br>
food in the stomach may take about 4 to<br>
6 hours.<br>
<b>The intestines</b><br>
<b>The intestines are contained within the</b><br>
abdominal part of the body and they<br>
constitute the longest and the massive part<br>
of the alimentary canal. The word intestine<br>
comes from a Latin word meaning “gut or<br>
internal”. The intestines are of two types<br>
namely; the small intestine which forms a<br>
vital part in the digestion and absorption<br>
of foods. The second type is the large<br>
intestine which among other functions<br>
is mainly important in re-absorption of<br>
water from the food residues before they<br>
are egested.<br>
<b>The small intestine</b><br>
<b>This part of the intestine has smaller</b><br>
diameter compared to that of the large<br>
intestine and for this reason it is called a<br>
small intestine. The diameter of the small<br>
intestine of human being is approximately<br>
<b>2.5 cm while the large intestine has a</b><br>
diameter of about 7.6 cm, which is about<br>
three times wider than the small intestine.<br>
<b>The small intestine is the longest of all</b><br>
parts of the alimentary canal. It has the<br>
length of about 9 metres in a living person.<br>
<b>The small intestine muscles are tight in</b><br>
the living body which makes it slightly<br>
shorter, unlike in a dead person where it<br>
becomes somewhat longer (about twice as<br>
much longer as its normal size in a living<br>
person). This is because death makes the<br>
small intestine to lose its muscle tone<br>
and stretches as it become loose. Unlike<br>
the large intestine, the small intestine has<br>
numerous folds and projections called<br>
villi on its lining which are important in<br>
increasing the surface area for digestion<br>
and absorption of food to take place.<br>
<b>The surface area of the small intestine</b><br>
of a human is about 200 m2 which is<br>
surprisingly closer to 100 times the surface<br>
area of our body skin. Schematically, the<br>
small intestine is normally surrounded by<br>
the large intestine in three sides.<br>
<b>The small intestine is not straight but rather</b><br>
a coiled tube which literally has three<br>
major distinct regions namely duodenum,<br>
jejunum, and ileum. The sectioning of<br>
the small intestine is based on its internal<br>
structure and function in digestion and<br>
absorption of foods.<br>
<b>Duodenum. This is the initial part of the</b><br>
small intestine immediately bordering<br>
with the pyloric sphincter of the stomach<br>
on the fore or proximal side and with the<br>
jejunum on the distal end. This part of the<br>
intestine is curved and it assumes a C-<br>
shape. The mid region of duodenum is an<br>
important part in which secretions from<br>
the pancreas and gall bladder together<br>
with intestinal wall secretions meet. The<br>
acidic chyme that enters the duodenum<br>
is subjected to chemical digestion by the<br>
secretions coming from the pancreas,<br>
intestinal wall, and the liver.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   283</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
284<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>The liver produces a secretion called</b><br>
bile which is released into duodenum via<br>
the bile duct from the gall bladder. The<br>
bile contains bile salts such as sodium<br>
taurocholate and glycocholate which are<br>
responsible for emulsiﬁcation of fats.<br>
<b>Sodium bicarbonate has no digestive role,</b><br>
but rather it neutralises the acidic chyme<br>
from the stomach. The bile and pancreatic<br>
juice which ﬂow into the duodenum are<br>
regulated by hepatopancreatic sphincter.<br>
<b>The pancreas is linked to the duodenum</b><br>
by a pancreatic duct. This exocrine gland<br>
produces various secretions which are<br>
collectively termed as pancreatic juice<br>
which contains the following components<br>
and their function in brackets: amylase<br>
(converts<br>
starch<br>
into<br>
maltose),<br>
trypsinogen (inactive form of trypsin<br>
which is converted into active form<br>
called trypsin by enterokinase). Others<br>
are trypsin (converts protein into smaller<br>
peptides), lipase (converts fats into fatty<br>
acids and glycerols), peptidase (converts<br>
peptides into amino acids), nucleases<br>
(convert nucleic acids into nucleotides),<br>
chymotrypsin (converts protein into small<br>
polypeptides), and sodium hydrogen<br>
carbonate (neutralizes the acid from the<br>
stomach).<br>
<b>Jejunum. This is the second region</b><br>
of the intestine and middle portion of<br>
small intestine measuring approximately<br>
between 2 and 3 meters long. This<br>
region stretches from the duodenum and<br>
connects it with the ileum. However<br>
there is no clear demarcation between<br>
jejunum and the ileum. It is responsible<br>
for nutrients absorption from the digested<br>
food to the blood stream with the aid of<br>
finger like structures known as villi. The<br>
absorbed materials are in form of mineral<br>
electrolytes, proteins, carbohydrates, and<br>
fats. These materials are utilized by the<br>
body.<br>
<b>Ileum. This is the final section of the</b><br>
small intestine in most of high vertebrates,<br>
including mammals, reptiles and birds. It<br>
is the longest region of the small intestine<br>
stretching from the distal portion of<br>
the jejunum through proximal portion<br>
of caecum of the large intestine at the<br>
ileocecal sphincter which is also known as<br>
a valve. In human being, it range between 2<br>
and 4 metres long and pH ranging between<br>
7 and 8 (neutral to slightly alkaline). In<br>
comparison to the other two parts of the<br>
small intestine ileum, is characteristically<br>
thicker; more vascularised and has more<br>
mucosal folds. Parasympathetic and<br>
sympathetic nerve ﬁbres provide extrinsic<br>
innervations to the small intestine. The<br>
wall of ileum secretes intestinal juices<br>
(saccus entericus) which contain mucus,<br>
sodium hydrogen carbonate (NaHCO3)<br>
and digestive enzymes. Mucus and<br>
sodium hydrogen carbonate are secreted<br>
by Brunner’s glands, which are found in<br>
the interstitial wall. The role of mucus<br>
is to lubricate the interstitial wall and<br>
prevent<br>
corrosion,<br>
whereas<br>
sodium<br>
hydrogen carbonate helps to neutralise<br>
acidic chyme.<br>
<b>The digestive enzymes produced by</b><br>
the epithelial cells of interstitial wall<br>
include carbohydrase and proteases.<br>
<b>Carbohydrase enzymes such as amylase</b><br>
converts remained starch into maltose,<br>
lactase converts lactose to galactose and<br>
glucose, and sucrase converts sucrose into<br>
fructose and glucose. Protease (erepsin)<br>
enzymes convert peptide into amino<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   284</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
285<br>
<b>Nutrition</b><br>
acids. Additionally the ileum performs<br>
absorptive role as it absorbs vitamin B12,<br>
bile salts and any other nutrients which<br>
are not absorbed in the jejunum.<br>
<b>The ileum as a site for absorption</b><br>
<b>Ileum is a site for digestion and absorption</b><br>
of digested food in the alimentary canal.<br>
<b>The absorption is done through diffusion or</b><br>
active transport aided by various adaptive<br>
features including presence of circular<br>
folds, villi, and microvilli. These features<br>
increase the surface area for absorption of<br>
nutrients. Villi are ﬁnger-like projections<br>
on the intestinal wall which are equipped<br>
with smaller folds called microvilli on<br>
their surface. The surface occupied by<br>
microvilli is called a brush boarder. Folds<br>
are deep ridge like structures found on the<br>
mucosa and submucosa wall. These folds<br>
are important in disrupting the straight<br>
movement of food into meander-like<br>
movement which ultimately delays food<br>
movement in the ileum, thus availing<br>
more time for digestion and absorption to<br>
take place.<br>
<b>The</b><br>
villi<br>
are<br>
numerous<br>
hair-like<br>
projections found on the surface of the<br>
folds. The function of the villi is to<br>
increase the surface area of the intestinal<br>
epithelium for an effective absorption.<br>
<b>Normally in 1 mm2 there can be as many</b><br>
as 40 villi. Mucosal epithelium composed<br>
of absorptive cells which covers the<br>
villi. The villi are equipped with a large<br>
number of blood vessels that carry sugars,<br>
minerals, vitamins, and amino acids to the<br>
liver for processing. Each villus is supplied<br>
with venules, arterioles, capillaries and<br>
lymphatic capillaries. Moreover, there<br>
are lacteal vessels in the villi which<br>
are important for the transportation of<br>
fat soluble substances (fatty acids and<br>
glycerol) into lymph vessels.<br>
<b>The mucosa between the folds contains</b><br>
deep tissues lined by cells that lead to a<br>
tubular intestinal gland called crypt of<br>
<b>Lieberkühn. This gland secretes slightly</b><br>
alkaline juice which is triggered by<br>
irritation on the mucosa caused by acidic<br>
chyme.<br>
<b>Microvilli are cylindrical extensions of</b><br>
the epithelial mucosa cells. They are<br>
much smaller than the villi, measuring<br>
about 1µm. Due to their small size, a<br>
mass of microvilli appears as ﬁne bristle<br>
brush called brush borders. The surface<br>
of microvilli contains enzymes which<br>
accomplish digestion of proteins and<br>
carbohydrates. The brush borders increase<br>
the surface area of the membrane plasma<br>
to increase absorption. Microvilli increase<br>
the surface area for absorption (Figure<br>
5.15). The surface of the villus is lined with<br>
the epithelial cells having large number of<br>
mitochondria to provide energy for active<br>
transport of nutrients, such as amino acids<br>
and glucose which are taken against their<br>
concentration gradient.<br>
<b>These nutrient-rich blood from the</b><br>
small intestine are carried to the liver<br>
via the hepatic portal vein. Presence of<br>
longitudinal and circular muscles in the<br>
villus enhances contraction and relaxation<br>
which brings villus into contact with the<br>
food.<br>
