<b>Figure 5.15 Transverse section of a small intestine</b><br>
<b>The large intestine</b><br>
<b>This is the last part of the alimentary canal</b><br>
that extends from a blind pouch called<br>
caecum through anus. It surrounds the<br>
small intestine in three sides. This type<br>
of the intestine is involved in the ﬁnal<br>
stage of water absorption, synthesis of<br>
certain vitamins, formation and expulsion<br>
of faeces to outside the body. The region<br>
between the ileum and caecum has a<br>
sphincter known as ileocaecal sphincter<br>
and it is responsible for the movement of<br>
undigested food materials from the ileum<br>
to the large intestine.<br>
<b>The large intestine differs in many ways</b><br>
from the small intestine as explained<br>
earlier. Besides its diameter, which<br>
is relatively larger than that of small<br>
intestine; it has a few enzymes secreting<br>
cells in its walls. The villi which are<br>
numerous in the small intestine are missing<br>
in the large intestine. The wall of the large<br>
intestine is thus simple in structure and<br>
has simple columnar epithelium. The<br>
intestinal mucus secreting glands are<br>
relatively more in number than they are in<br>
the small intestine. The mucus in the large<br>
intestine is important in lubricating the<br>
food residues moving out as faeces while<br>
also protecting the intestine from effects<br>
of acids and gases produced by enteric<br>
bacteria. The large intestine therefore,<br>
had four major regions, namely: caecum,<br>
colon, rectum and anus.<br>
<b>Caecum. This is a sac-like structure</b><br>
of about 6 cm suspended interior to<br>
the ileocecal valve. Undigested food<br>
materials from the ileum are ﬁrst received<br>
by this region of the large intestine where<br>
absorption of water and salts is continued.<br>
<b>In this region a winding tube called</b><br>
appendix is attached and its function is<br>
not clearly known hence it is considered<br>
as a vestigial organ. However, appendix is<br>
reported to have immunological function<br>
because it contains a group of white blood<br>
cells. Recent studies have also shown<br>
appendix to have bacteria reservoir which<br>
are important in repopulating the enteric<br>
bacteria in the early stages of individuals<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   286</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
287<br>
<b>Nutrition</b><br>
suffering diarrheal illness. As the faeces<br>
pass along the large intestine, some water<br>
is absorbed into the blood stream.<br>
<b>Colon. This region of the intestine borders</b><br>
with caecum and it is made up of four sub<br>
regions namely the ascending, transverse,<br>
descending and sigmoid regions. Food<br>
residues from caecum enter the ascending<br>
region of the colon ﬁrst which is on the<br>
right side of abdomen and travel up<br>
through the ﬁrst bend of the colon to the<br>
transverse region of the colon. The residue<br>
continues through the second bend to the<br>
descending colon which is on the left side<br>
of the posterior abdominal wall and enters<br>
the sigmoid colon.<br>
<b>Generally, colon is very important in the</b><br>
water balance of the body. Every day,<br>
about seven litres of water from drinks<br>
and watery secretions produced internally,<br>
enter the gut. If most of these were not<br>
reabsorbed, it could lead to dehydration of<br>
the body. Thus, re-absorption of water in<br>
the large intestine is necessary to prevent<br>
water loss.<br>
<b>Rectum. After food residues have passed</b><br>
through the sigmoid colon, the remaining<br>
intestinal content is stored as faeces in<br>
the rectum which measures about 20<br>
cm long. Rectum is located interior to<br>
the pelvis and it produces some mucus<br>
materialwhich are added to the faeces in<br>
order to lubricate it for easy passage to the<br>
outside by the process called defecation.<br>
<b>The desire for defecation is caused by the</b><br>
presence of a large quantity of faeces in<br>
the rectum. The walls of the rectum have<br>
curved contours and lateral bends which<br>
create internal transverse folds called<br>
rectal valves. The function of these valves<br>
is to separate faeces from gas in order to<br>
prevent simultaneous passage of faeces<br>
and gas.<br>
<b>Anus. This region is also called anal canal</b><br>
and constitutes the ﬁnal part of the large<br>
intestine. The length of the anal canal is<br>
between 3.8 to 5 cm and it opens to the<br>
exterior of the body at the anus. It has<br>
two types of muscles; the internal anal<br>
sphincter and external anal sphincter. The<br>
former ﬁrst type of muscle is made up<br>
of smooth muscles, and its contractions<br>
are involuntary, while the later is made<br>
up of skeletal muscles and they are<br>
under voluntary control. Under normal<br>
conditions, these two types of muscles<br>
make the sphincter remain closed except<br>
when defaecating.<br>
<b>Nervous and hormonal control of the</b><br>
gastric juice secretion<br>
<b>Digestion is vital for the functions of all</b><br>
systems of a human body. It is under the<br>
nervous and hormonal control systems.<br>
<b>Until 19th century, biologists regarded</b><br>
the digestive system as being entirely<br>
under nervous control. However, in 1902<br>
a British physiologist Sir William M.<br>
<b>Baylyss discovered secretin hormone</b><br>
as vital in the control of gastrointestinal<br>
functions.  Three types of hormones<br>
involved in digestion are secretin, gastrin<br>
and<br>
cholecystokinin.<br>
<b>Secretion</b><br>
and<br>
release of these hormones is stimulated by<br>
presence of certain food molecules in the<br>
stomach and small intestine.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   287</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
288<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Digestion process is controlled by</b><br>
two types of nervous system namely;<br>
the somatic nervous system and the<br>
autonomic nervous system. The somatic<br>
nervous system enables the body to adapt<br>
to stimuli such as touch and smell from<br>
the external environment and is the system<br>
that controls the gastrointestinal tract. This<br>
somatic nervous system is further divided<br>
into sympathetic and parasympathetic<br>
nervous<br>
systems.<br>
<b>Stimulation</b><br>
of<br>
sympathetic system brings about shunting<br>
of blood from gastrointestinal tract. This<br>
reduces digestive activity and causes dry<br>
mouth and cessation of salivation.<br>
<b>The parasympathetic nervous system, on</b><br>
the contrary, is responsible for the secretion<br>
of saliva, production of digestive enzymes,<br>
activation of digestion process and<br>
increased peristalsis. The parasympathetic<br>
system dilates blood vessels to increase<br>
blood ﬂow to the gastrointestinal tract<br>
after the consumption of food, due to<br>
great or high metabolic demand placed<br>
on the body by the gut. The control of<br>
gastric juice secretion occurs in three<br>
phases, namely, nervous, gastric, and<br>
intestinal phases. The mechanisms of the<br>
nervous and hormonal control of digestion<br>
phasewise is as detailed here under.<br>
<b>The nervous or cephalic phase. This is</b><br>
the initial stage which is initiated by sight,<br>
thought, taste, or smell of food, which<br>
later triggers a reﬂex in which nerve<br>
impulses relayed from the brain cause<br>
gastric glands to release their secretions.<br>
<b>Generally, the nervous signals that trigger</b><br>
this phase emanate from cerebral cortex<br>
appetite centres and are transmitted to<br>
the stomach through the vagus nerve.<br>
<b>This in turn causes the secretions of</b><br>
histamine and increases hydrochloric acid<br>
and gastrin secretion in the stomach. In<br>
comparison to other phases, the cephalic<br>
phase contributes about 20% of the gastric<br>
secretions while eating.<br>
<b>Gastric phase. This is the second phase</b><br>
and it is so called because it triggers<br>
gastric activity. It occurs once the food is<br>
chewed and has arrived in the stomach.<br>
<b>The partially digested food substances</b><br>
such as proteins in the stomach stimulate<br>
the endocrine cells in the stomach walls to<br>
secrete gastrin from gastric glands which<br>
increase the secretion of the gastric juice.<br>
<b>Normally, the ingested food stimulates</b><br>
gastric activity by stretching the stomach<br>
and raising pH of its contents. Stretching<br>
activates the short and long reﬂexes that<br>
lead into the production of acetylcholine<br>
secreted by the parasympathetic nerve<br>
fibres. The acetylcholine and histamine<br>
from the gastric glands together with<br>
gastrin stimulate parietal cells to secrete<br>
hydrochloric<br>
acid.<br>
<b>The</b><br>
chief<br>
cells<br>
secrete pepsinogen in response to gastrin<br>
secretion. Digestion of protein into<br>
peptides and amino acids, stimulate gastrin<br>
cells directly to secrete more gastrin<br>
to accelerate further protein digestion.<br>
<b>Normally, peptides buffer the stomach</b><br>
acidity; therefore, as they leave the<br>
stomach acidity increases and as pH gets<br>
below 2, a negative feedback is triggered<br>
to inhibit the parietal and gastrin cells.<br>
<b>This process winds up the gastric phase</b><br>
as the need for pepsin and HCl declines.<br>
<b>Furthermore, when fat-containing food</b><br>
enters the stomach, the hormone called<br>
enterogastrone<br>
or<br>
gastric<br>
inhibitory<br>
peptide is released from the wall of the<br>
stomach. This hormone decreases the ﬂow<br>

10/10/2019   14:07<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>

289<br>
<b>Nutrition</b><br>
of gastric juice and reduces movement<br>
of the muscles of the stomach (churning<br>
motions) or gastric peristalsis.<br>
<b>Intestinal phase. This phase occurs</b><br>
when chyme arrives in the ﬁrst region<br>
of the small intestine called duodenum<br>
and triggers gastric activity and nervous<br>
reﬂexes. Food material in the duodenum<br>
stimulates both the alkaline and enzyme-<br>
rich components of pancreatic juice. The<br>
alkaline component of pancreatic juice is<br>
secreted in response to the presence of acid<br>
in the duodenum. The acidiﬁed chyme in<br>
the duodenum triggers the secretion of<br>
secretin and cholecystokinin (CCK) or<br>
pancreozymin (PZ) from the duodenal<br>
walls. Secretin causes the production of<br>
bile and mineral salts from gall bladder and<br>
pancreas respectively. CCK stimulates the<br>
secretion of enzymes from pancreas and<br>
contraction of gall bladder to release bile.<br>
<b>Moreover, secretin and CCK suppress</b><br>
gastric secretion and motility in which a<br>
decline in gastrin secretion and contraction<br>
of pyloric sphincter will limit admission<br>
of more chyme into the duodenum. This<br>
gives the duodenum ample time to work<br>
on the chyme it has received before<br>
receiving more. The enteroendocrine cells<br>
also secrete gastric-inhibitory peptide,<br>
also called gastrointestinal inhibitory<br>
peptide (GIP) which inhibits the secretion<br>
of gastric acid in the stomach. Also GIP<br>
stimulates insulin secretion in preparation<br>
for processing nutrients that are about to<br>
be absorbed by the small intestine. Trypsin<br>
in the duodenum inhibits the release of<br>
enzymes via the inhibition of CCK.This<br>
is a feedback control mechanism which<br>
limits the quantity of enzymes in the<br>
small intestine and may have a protective<br>
function.<br>

<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Six</b><br>
<b>Gaseous exchange and</b><br>
respiration<br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
All living organisms exchange gases between their respiratory surfaces and the<br>
surrounding environment. Respiration is an energy yielding process that liberates<br>
energy, which is used by living organisms to perform various body activities. It is a<br>
vital process to all living organisms as it provides energy for all metabolic processes.<br>
In this chapter, you will learn about the mechanisms of gaseous exchange in mammals<br>
and plants and the process of respiration.<br>
<b>6.1    Gaseous exchange in mammals</b><br>
<b>Gaseous exchange entails a process of</b><br>
exchanging respiratory gases between the<br>
cell of an organism and its environment.<br>
<b>Oxygen</b><br>
is<br>
required<br>
for<br>
aerobic<br>
respiration. All organisms that undergo<br>
aerobic respiration obtain oxygen from<br>
their environment and carbon dioxide, a<br>
waste product of respiration is returned<br>
to the environment. The area for gaseous<br>
exchange within the organism’s body is<br>
called respiratory surface. The process<br>
of gaseous exchange takes place by<br>
diffusion. In mammals and other animals,<br>
the principal organ for gaseous exchange<br>
is the lung(s).<br>
<b>The internal structure of the</b><br>
mammalian lung<br>
<b>A pair of lungs is situated in the thoracic</b><br>
cavity together with the heart. The cavity<br>
has a protective bony cage called the rib<br>
cage. The rib cage has ribs and intercostal<br>
muscles that allow its movements during<br>
exhalation and inhalation of air. It is<br>
surrounded by a double pleural membrane<br>
between which pleural fluid is found.<br>
<b>The fluid aids as a lubricant, preventing</b><br>
abrasion of the lungs during breathing.<br>
<b>It is also associated with the muscular</b><br>
sheath known as the diaphragm. The<br>
diaphragm separates the thorax from the<br>
abdomen. During inhalation the volume<br>
of the thoracic cavity increases, thus<br>
lowering pressure. This is caused by the<br>
downward movement of the diaphragm<br>
and the outward movement of the ribs.<br>
<b>During exhalation, the volume of the</b><br>
thoracic cavity decreases, thus raising the<br>
pressure. This is caused by the upward<br>
291<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   291</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
292<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
movement of the diaphragm and inward<br>
movement of the ribs.<br>
<b>The lungs consist of the trachea, bronchi,</b><br>
bronchioles, alveolar ducts, alveolar sacs<br>
and alveoli (Figure 6.1). The alveoli are<br>
the main functional units of the lungs.<br>
<b>Trachea is the windpipe that connects the</b><br>
larynx and the bronchi. It receives air from<br>
the nostrils through the nasal cavity. In the<br>
latter, the air is warmed and cleared from<br>
dust and germs that may have entered the<br>
cavity with it. The nasal cavity possesses<br>
hairs which help to trap dusts and other<br>
tiny particles. The trachea has cartilages<br>
in its walls that keep it ﬁrm and intact. It<br>
is associated with ciliated epithelium and<br>
goblet cells. The cilia beating moves the<br>
trapped dusts and bacteria back to the cavity<br>
where they get swallowed. The goblet<br>
cells are essential for mucus production<br>
that traps dusts and bacteria altogether.<br>
<b>It also moistens the air that passes down</b><br>
to the alveoli. The trachea branches at<br>
its lower end into two bronchi. Like the<br>
trachea, each bronchus has cartilage and<br>
ciliated epithelium with goblet cells which<br>
play a role of trapping dust and bacteria.<br>
<b>Each bronchus subdivides into many</b><br>
smaller tubes known as bronchioles. They<br>
have cartilage, cilia, and the goblet cells<br>
that work similarly as in the trachea and<br>
bronchi; they trap foreign substances. The<br>
bronchioles branch into very ﬁne tubes,<br>
alveolar ducts, ending into alveolar sacs<br>
and each alveolar sac opens into a group of<br>
alveoli. The alveolar ducts, alveolar sacs,<br>
and alveoli do not possess cartilage, cilia<br>
and goblet cells. The alveolus possesses<br>
features that allow it to function in a<br>
special way compared to other structural<br>
parts of the lungs.<br>

<b>Clavicle (collar  bone)</b><br>

<b>Gaseous exchange and respiration</b><br>
<b>Alveolus</b><br>
<b>The alveolus is a tiny air-ﬁlled sac of</b><br>
the mammalian lung. Gaseous exchange<br>
takes place in the alveolus and actually,<br>
it is a functional unit of the lungs. The<br>
estimated total amount of the alveoli in the<br>
average human is about 700 million that<br>
has a total surface area of over 80 square<br>
metres which facilitate the rate of gaseous<br>
exchange.  The alveoli are thin, moist and<br>
are well supplied with a dense capillary<br>
networks, hence they are highly adapted<br>
for efﬁcient gaseous exchange. In the<br>
process of gaseous exchange, oxygen gas<br>
from the alveoli diffuse quickly across the<br>
alveolar wall through the air-blood barrier<br>
into the blood capillaries and carried to<br>
the heart through pulmonary vein. On<br>
the other hand, the carbon dioxide from<br>
the body tissues enters the heart and<br>
carried from the heart to the lungs by the<br>
pulmonary artery. It diffuses through the<br>
blood capillary to the alveoli and is then<br>
exhaled (Figure 6.2).<br>
<b>Trachea with rings</b><br>
of cartilage<br>
<b>Aorta</b><br>
<b>Right pulmonary</b><br>
arteries<br>
<b>Superior</b><br>
venacava<br>
<b>Right</b><br>
pulmonary<br>
vein<br>
<b>Right lung</b><br>
<b>Left lung</b><br>
<b>Bronchioles</b><br>
<b>Alveoli</b><br>
<b>Alveoli</b><br>
<b>Blood capillary</b><br>
networks<br>
<b>Carbondioxide</b><br>
leave blood<br>
capillaries and<br>
enter to the<br>
alveolus<br>
<b>Oxygen leave the</b><br>
alveolus and enter to<br>
the  blood capillaries<br>
<b>Pulmonary</b><br>
vein<br>
<b>Pulmonary</b><br>
artery<br>
Figure 6.2 Structure of the mammalian lungs showing the association between alveoli and blood<br>
vessels during gaseous exchange<br>
<b>Structurally,</b><br>
the<br>
alveoli<br>
possess<br>
connective tissues with elastic collagen<br>
ﬁbres that allow them to expand and<br>
recoil during breathing. Alveoli have<br>
special epithelia cells in their walls that<br>
secrete a detergent-like chemical known<br>
as surfactant into the alveolar space. The<br>
surfactant lowers the surface tension of<br>
the ﬂuid inside the alveoli, hence reducing<br>
the energy required to breathe in and<br>
inﬂate the lungs. It also kills bacteria<br>
trapped in the incoming air through the<br>
nostrils. In addition, it increases the rate of<br>
gaseous exchange in mammals. They also<br>
have macrophages which are protective<br>
cells that remove debris and microbes by<br>
phagocytosis (Figure 6.3).<br>

<b>Figure 6.3 Structure of the alveolus</b><br>
<b>Surface tension of the lungs</b><br>
<b>The surface tension provided by surfactant</b><br>
stabilizes the alveoli during inflation<br>
and dilation resulting from inhalation<br>
and exhalation of gases. The surfactant<br>
is a phospholipid and a protein chemical<br>
produced in early development stage<br>
of foetus. This chemical helps to lower<br>
the surface tension and promote lungs’<br>
expansion during inspiration (inhalation)<br>
and preventing alveolar collapse. It is<br>
also believed to protect the lungs from<br>
infections.<br>
<b>The proteins and lipids that make up the</b><br>
surfactant have both hydrophobic and<br>
hydrophilic region that are important in<br>
lowering the surface tension by adsorbing<br>
to the air–water surface interface, with<br>
hydrophobic head groups in water and<br>
hydrophobic tail facing towards the air.<br>
<b>Activity 6.1 Observation of the</b><br>
structure of the mammalian lungs<br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>Mouse or rat, dissection kit, dissecting</b><br>
tray, chloroform, and cotton wool.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Use your dissection knowledge to<br>
open up the body cavity of a mouse<br>
in the usual way to display the<br>
lungs.<br>
b)	 Use a hand lens to observe the<br>
thoracic and abdominal cavities as<br>
well as the diaphragm.<br>
<b>Questions</b><br>
1.	 Draw a well labelled diagram of<br>
your dissection.<br>
2.	 Explain the functions of each part of<br>
the mammalian lungs.<br>
<b>Safety precautions</b><br>
1.	 Laboratory rules and regulations<br>
should be adhered to.<br>
2.	 Rat or mouse may bite and also<br>
their fur may cause allergy to a<br>
human being.<br>

<b>Gaseous exchange and respiration</b><br>
<b>Factors governing gaseous exchange at</b><br>
respiratory surface<br>
<b>Gaseous</b><br>
exchange<br>
occurs<br>
on<br>
the<br>
respiratory surface; a boundary between<br>
the external environment and the interior<br>
of the body of an organism. Mammalian<br>
respiratory surface consists of many air<br>
sacs called alveoli which are found inside<br>
a pair of lungs. An efﬁcient gaseous<br>
exchange occurs when the respiratory<br>
surface has large surface area and can<br>
quickly distribute and collect gases<br>
throughout the body. Moreover, an<br>
efﬁcient gaseous exchange can occur<br>
when the respiratory surface has ability<br>
to speed up or slow down the exchange<br>
rate to meet the body demand. In general,<br>
for an efﬁcient gaseous exchange in<br>
mammals, the respiratory surface must<br>
have the following features:<br>
a)	 Large surface area of the membrane<br>
<b>There is a direct relationship between</b><br>
the surface area and the rate of gaseous<br>
exchange. Generally, the larger the surface<br>
area of the membrane, the higher the rate<br>
of gaseous exchange. This is because<br>
when the surface area is large, more blood<br>
and air can circulate, hence increasing the<br>
rate of gaseous exchange.<br>
b)	 Concentration gradient<br>
<b>Concentration gradient is created when</b><br>
the two sides separated by a membrane<br>
have different concentrations of gases.<br>
<b>This difference in concentration is</b><br>
actually what facilitates the process of<br>
gaseous exchange because gases can<br>
move from areas of high concentration to<br>
those of low concentration. Therefore, for<br>
gaseous exchange to occur there must be<br>
a concentration gradient.<br>
c)	 Availability of high supply of blood<br>
capillaries<br>
<b>The mammalian respiratory surfaces</b><br>
are well supplied with blood by blood<br>
capillaries. This enables high uptake of<br>
oxygen in the alveoli, which increases the<br>
rate of gaseous exchange. In the red blood<br>
cells, there is haemoglobin that helps to<br>
transport oxygen by combining with it<br>
to form oxyhaemoglobin which helps in<br>
transportation of oxygen to different parts<br>
of the body where it is required.<br>
d)	 Thickness of the membrane<br>
<b>The rate of gaseous exchange is affected</b><br>
by the thickness of the membrane across<br>
which the gases have to diffuse. A thick<br>
membrane reduces the rate at which gases<br>
diffuse from areas of high concentration<br>
to those of low concentration. This is due<br>
to long distance of travel as the result<br>
of membrane thickness. Generally, the<br>
thickness of the membrane is inversely<br>
proportional to the rate of diffusion.<br>
e)	 Diffusion distance<br>
<b>The distance across which air, blood, or</b><br>
plasma ﬂuid has to diffuse also determines<br>
the rate of gaseous exchange. In single-<br>
celled organisms, gaseous exchange<br>
tends to be faster because the gases<br>
have to diffuse through only one cell<br>
surface membrane while in multi-cellular<br>
organisms, gaseous exchange requires a<br>
complex transportation and respiratory<br>
system as the gases are transported<br>
through a longer distance.<br>
f)	 Moist surface<br>
<b>Since respiratory gases are transported</b><br>
in solution form, they must dissolve in<br>
liquid before they are carried away from<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   295</b><br>

the respiratory surfaces. Thus, an efﬁcient<br>
respiratory surface must be moist for rapid<br>
exchange and transportation of respiratory<br>
gases.<br>
g)	 Permeability<br>
<b>Since the membrane form the boundary</b><br>
between organism and its extracellular<br>
environment, its permeability affects the<br>
rate of gaseous exchange. Therefore, a<br>
respiratory surface must be permeable to<br>
allow gases to pass through.<br>
<b>Oxygen and carbon dioxide transport</b><br>
in vertebrates<br>
<b>Oxygen and carbon dioxide are the</b><br>
respiratory gases that have to be transported<br>
from one part of the body to another.<br>
<b>Oxygen is transported from the respiratory</b><br>
surfaces to the respiring body tissues and<br>
carbon dioxide has to be transported from<br>
the respiring body tissues to the respiratory<br>
surfaces. Vertebrates such as mammals,<br>
birds, reptiles, amphibians and ﬁsh, have<br>
a variety of respiratory surfaces, ranging<br>
from gills in ﬁsh and tadpoles to lungs in<br>
adult amphibians, reptiles and mammals.<br>
<b>Oxygen transport in vertebrates</b><br>
<b>In vertebrates oxygen is transported in</b><br>
two ways: as dissolved oxygen in blood<br>
plasma and by means of red blood cells.<br>
<b>The oxygen transported in solution form in</b><br>
the blood plasma accounts for only about<br>
two percent (2%), and the remaining ninty<br>
eight percent (98%) is transported by the<br>
red blood cells. The red blood cells have<br>
a red pigment called haemoglobin which<br>
is responsible for transport of oxygen and<br>
carbon dioxide in the blood. Structurally,<br>
the haemoglobin molecule consists of<br>
four Iron-containing parts and four protein<br>
chains. Each haemoglobin molecule binds<br>
to four oxygen molecules, forming the<br>
oxyhaemoglobin molecule. This is carried<br>
to individual cells in the body tissue where<br>
it is released. The binding or combination<br>
of oxygen and haemoglobin is a reversible<br>
process as shown in the equation below:<br>
<b>According to the above equation, at high</b><br>
oxygen concentration, oxyhaemoglobin<br>
is formed, whereas at low oxygen<br>
concentration, oxyhaemoglobin dissociates<br>
into haemoglobin and oxygen. This<br>
dissociation<br>
releases<br>
oxygen<br>
from<br>
haemoglobin. The balance can be shown<br>
by an oxygen dissociation curve of<br>
oxyhaemoglobin (Figure 6.4). In the<br>
oxygen dissociation curve, the greater<br>
the concentration or partial pressure of<br>
oxygen, the more saturated with oxygen<br>
the haemoglobin becomes. Usually the<br>
graph is S-shaped or sigmoid.<br>
<b>The curve shows the following:</b><br>
a)	 At relatively low oxygen concentration,<br>
there is uncombined haemoglobin in the<br>
blood and little or no oxyhaemoglobin<br>
exists. This is most likely to occur<br>
in the body tissues, where oxygen<br>
concentration is likely be low.<br>
b)	 At relatively high oxygen concentration,<br>
haemoglobin combines with oxygen<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   296</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
297<br>
<b>Gaseous exchange and respiration</b><br>
in the blood to form oxyhaemoglobin.<br>
<b>Therefore, most haemoglobin molecules</b><br>
are saturated with oxygen. This situation<br>
is likely to occur in the lungs, where<br>
oxygen concentration is high.<br>
<b>The effect of carbon dioxide in the</b><br>
transport of oxygen in blood<br>
<b>Increased concentration of carbon dioxide</b><br>
can affect oxygen transport in the blood.<br>
<b>This is because haemoglobin can combine</b><br>
with carbon dioxide (although to a lesser<br>
extent) to form carbaminohaemoglobin.<br>
<b>The presence of carbon dioxide lowers</b><br>
the afﬁnity of haemoglobin to oxygen<br>
and causes the release of oxygen from<br>
haemoglobin. Therefore, an increase in<br>
carbon dioxide in the tissues causes faster<br>
release of oxygen from haemoglobin and<br>
this is known as the Bohr Effect which is<br>
the result of shifting of dissociation curves<br>
to the right in areas with increased partial<br>
pressure of carbon dioxide (Figure 6.5).<br>
<b>The effect of carbon dioxide concentration</b><br>
on oxygen transport by haemoglobin can<br>
be revealed by comparing the oxygen<br>
dissociation curves when less carbon<br>
dioxide is present and when more carbon<br>
dioxide is present in the blood.<br>
<b>The Bohr Effect</b><br>
<b>The Bohr Effect is an increase in carbon</b><br>
dioxide partial pressure of the blood or<br>
decrease in blood pH resulting into a<br>
lower afﬁnity of haemoglobin to oxygen.<br>
<b>It is manifested by right-ward shifting</b><br>
of oxygen dissociation curve, resulting<br>
from enhanced unloading of oxygen by<br>
haemoglobin. The relationship between<br>
carbon dioxide partial pressure and blood<br>
pH is mediated by carbonic anhydrase<br>
which catalyses conversion of gaseous<br>
carbon dioxide to carbonic acid, that in<br>
turn releases a free hydrogen ion, hence<br>
reducing the local pH of blood.<br>

<b>Transport of carbon dioxide</b><br>
<b>Carbon dioxide diffuses out of the tissues</b><br>
into the blood for transportation. The body<br>
does not allow accumulation of carbon<br>
dioxide because it forms an acid with<br>
water that could lead to fatal changes of<br>
blood pH. Carbon dioxide is transported<br>
both, in plasma and in red blood cells by<br>
three different ways.<br>
a)	 It can be transported either as physical<br>
solution (as dissolved carbon dioxide)<br>
or as carbonic acid (H2CO3). However<br>
it is only about ﬁve percent (5%) of<br>
carbon dioxide that is transported in<br>
solution form and a small amount as<br>
carbonic acid.<br>
b)	 It can be transported in combination<br>
with<br>
proteins:<br>
carbon<br>
dioxide<br>
combines reversibly with haemoglobin<br>
to form a compound known as<br>
carbaminohaemoglobin. It does not bind<br>
to iron as oxygen does but to amine group<br>
(NH2) at the end of each polypeptide chain<br>
of haemoglobin and plasma proteins.<br>
<b>About 10 – 20% of carbon dioxide is</b><br>
transported in this way.<br>
c)	 It can be transported as hydrogen<br>
bicarbonate ions (HCO3<br>
-). Most of<br>
the carbon dioxide (about 85%) in<br>
the body is transported as hydrogen<br>
carbonate. Carbon dioxide enters red<br>
blood cells in the tissue’s capillaries<br>
where it combines with water to form<br>
carbonic acid (H2CO3). This reaction<br>
is catalysed by the enzyme carbonic<br>
anhydrase which is found in red<br>
blood cells. Then, the carbonic acid<br>
dissociates to form bicarbonate ions<br>
<b>(HCO3</b><br>
-) and hydrogen ions (H+).<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   298</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>

<b>Gaseous exchange and respiration</b><br>
<b>Most of the hydrogen carbonate ions</b><br>
formed in the red blood cells diffuse<br>
from the cytoplasm to the plasma and<br>
combine with sodium to form sodium<br>
hydrogen<br>
carbonate.<br>
<b>The</b><br>
negatively<br>
charged hydrogen carbonate ions are lost<br>
from the blood cells, leaving them with<br>
a more positive charge. This is balanced<br>
by diffusion of chloride ions (Cl-) in the<br>
opposite direction, maintaining the balance<br>
of negative and positive ions in either<br>
side of the plasma and red blood cells.<br>
<b>This is called   the chloride shift which</b><br>
is also known as the Hamburger shift or<br>
<b>Hamburger phenomenon. It is named after</b><br>
the founder, Hartog Jakob Hamburger.<br>
<b>The chloride shift reaction occurs in the</b><br>
respiring cell. When the red blood cells<br>
reach the lungs the reverse process or<br>
reaction occurs and carbon dioxide is<br>
released (Figure 6.6).<br>
<b>The dissociation of carbonic acid increases</b><br>
the acidity of the blood. Hydrogen ions<br>
(H+) then react with oxyhaemoglobin<br>
to release bound oxygen and reduce the<br>
acidity of the blood. This buffering action<br>
allows large quantities of carbonic acid<br>
to be carried in the blood without major<br>
changes in blood pH.<br>
<b>This reversible reaction accounts for the</b><br>
<b>Bohr Effect. Carbon dioxide is a waste</b><br>
product of respiration. Its concentration is<br>
high in the respiring cells; it is here where<br>
the haemoglobin releases oxygen.<br>

<b>Haemoglobin is strongly attracted to</b><br>
carbon dioxide molecules. Carbon dioxide<br>
is removed to reduce its concentration in<br>
the cell and is transported to the lungs<br>
where its concentration is low. This process<br>
is continuous since oxygen concentration<br>
in the lungs, is always higher than that of<br>
carbon dioxide, while in the respiring cells<br>
carbon dioxide concentration is always<br>
higher than that of oxygen.<br>
<b>Adaptation of organisms to oxygen</b><br>
uptake<br>
<b>The percentage of oxygen in the air</b><br>
does not change signiﬁcantly at different<br>
altitudes, but the atmospheric pressure<br>
falls with increasing altitudes. The<br>
oxygen tension (partial pressure of<br>
oxygen molecules dissolved in blood<br>
plasma) varies with altitudes (Table 6.1).<br>
<b>Organisms living in a particular altitude</b><br>
have to develop adaptive features that<br>
can enable them to sustain their lives in<br>
their particular environment. Among the<br>
organisms that live in the environments<br>
that need them to develop some adaptive<br>
features aimed at facilitating oxygen<br>
uptake include mountain dwellers, divers,<br>
and mammalian foetus.<br>

<b>Adaptations to oxygen uptake for</b><br>
mountain climbers and dwellers<br>
<b>In mountain climbers and high altitude</b><br>
dwelling mammals, the rate of metabolism<br>
is high in order to ensure constant<br>
generation of enough energy to cope with<br>
climbing activities, hence the supply of<br>
oxygen should be assured. However, the<br>
partial pressure of oxygen in high altitude<br>
is low which makes the climbers difficult<br>
to pick up oxygen. For these reasons,<br>
mountain climbers and mammals living<br>
in high altitudes develop the following<br>
adaptive features:<br>
a)	 Their bone marrow produce more red<br>
blood cells in order to raise the oxygen-<br>
carrying capacity of the blood.<br>
b)	 They possess a form of haemoglobin<br>
with higher afﬁnity to oxygen.<br>
<b>This allows loading of oxygen by</b><br>
haemoglobin even at low oxygen<br>
tension.<br>
c)	 They secrete more alkaline urine<br>
that<br>
normalises<br>
blood<br>
pH. The<br>
chemoreceptors<br>
become<br>
sensitive<br>
to<br>
carbon<br>
dioxide<br>
concentration<br>
and normal ventilation rates are<br>
maintained.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   300</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>

301<br>
<b>Gaseous exchange and respiration</b><br>
d)	 Their tissues are tolerant to high levels<br>
of lactic acid due to oxygen deficiency.<br>
e)	 They<br>
have<br>
larger<br>
lungs,<br>
hence<br>
increased lung volumes and total lung<br>
capacity, therefore large amount of<br>
haemoglobin loaded with oxygen.<br>
f)	 They have deep and slow breathing rate<br>
that improves ventilation efficiency<br>
for oxygen, since blood oxygenation<br>
increases, and it reduces systemic and<br>
pulmonary blood pressures.<br>
<b>Adaptations to oxygen uptake for</b><br>
divers<br>
<b>Divers in the deep water do not depend</b><br>
on lungs as a source of oxygen; instead,<br>
they rely on enhanced oxygen stored in<br>
their blood and muscles. Collapse of the<br>
lungs forces air away from the alveoli,<br>
where gaseous exchange between the<br>
lungs and blood occurs. This blunting of<br>
gaseous exchange is important in the deep<br>
diver because it prevents the absorption of<br>
nitrogen into the blood and the subsequent<br>
development of high blood nitrogen<br>
levels. High blood nitrogen pressure can<br>
exert a narcotic effect (so-called nitrogen<br>
narcosis) on the diver. It may also lead to<br>
nitrogen bubble formation during ascent,<br>
a phenomenon known as decompression<br>
sickness or “the bends”. Collapse of the<br>
lungs in deep divers helps to avoid these<br>
two problems. Thus, diving mammals,<br>
besides the collapse of their lungs, have<br>
the following adaptive mechanisms to<br>
oxygen uptake:<br>
a)	 They use oxygen more efﬁciently, that<br>
is, they ﬁll their lungs and exchange<br>
90% of their air in each breath. Thus,<br>
before a dive is taken, they take a deep<br>
breath to accumulate oxygen in their<br>
lungs.<br>
b)	They have a high blood volume with<br>
plenty of haemoglobin and myoglobin.<br>
This allows long oxygen retention time.<br>
c)	 They have a high tolerance to lactic<br>
acid and carbon dioxide, that is, their<br>
muscles can work anaerobically while<br>
holding their breath.<br>
d)	They<br>
can<br>
tolerate<br>
tremendous<br>
atmospheric pressure at great depths.<br>
<b>Their lungs and ribs are collapsible;</b><br>
air spaces are minimised; and nitrogen<br>
absorption is limited.<br>
e)	 Diving mammals slow their heart rate,<br>
stop their breathing, and shunt blood<br>
flow from their extremities to the brain,<br>
heart, and muscles when starting a dive.<br>
f)	 Seals can hold their breath for about<br>
two hours. They rely on internal oxygen<br>
stores when they are down there.<br>
g)	Myoglobin of the seals and dolphins is<br>
more concentrated than that of humans,<br>
almost ten times, this gives them a<br>
chance of storing oxygen for a long time<br>
when under water.<br>
<b>Adaptations of the mammalian foetus</b><br>
to oxygen uptake<br>
<b>The foetus lives and develops inside</b><br>
the maternal womb (uterus). It obtains<br>
nutrients, exchange gases and waste<br>
products with the maternal blood via the<br>
placenta. For the foetus to obtain oxygen<br>
from maternal blood, the oxygen has to<br>
diffuse from maternal blood to foetus<br>
blood. There are some adaptations to this,<br>
which are;<br>
a)	 Foetal haemoglobin has higher afﬁnity<br>
to oxygen; hence it can readily combine<br>
with oxygen to form oxyhaemoglobin<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   301</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
302<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
(Figure.6.7). This allows the foetus<br>
to extract oxygen from the maternal<br>
blood supply. In adult hemoglobin,<br>
the protein subunits are identical<br>
beta-chain subunits, while in foetal<br>
hemoglobin, the two subunits are<br>
identical gamma subunits. This change<br>
in structure of foetal hemoglobin also<br>
leads to a change in function when<br>
compared to adult hemoglobin. It has<br>
been shown that the structural changes<br>
in foetal hemoglobin caused by the<br>
substitution of gamma subunits for<br>
beta-chains, allows foetal hemoglobin<br>
to have a higher affinity to oxygen.<br>
b)	 There is increased intake of oxygen<br>
by maternal blood due to increased<br>
haemoglobin so as to ensure a large<br>
supply of oxygen to the foetus blood.<br>
c)	 The uterine wall is highly vascularised<br>
(provided with blood vessels) to<br>
ensure continuous supply of oxygen<br>
to the foetus, as a result, the foetus<br>
loads its haemoglobin with oxygen<br>
efficiently.<br>

the wombs of their mothers despite the<br>
fact that they are not in direct contact<br>
with the atmospheric air.<br>
2.	Describe the ways by which oxygen<br>
and carbon dioxide are transported in<br>
vertebrates.<br>

the<br>

dissociation curve and comment on its<br>
shape.<br>
4.	What is chloride shift? Explain how it<br>
occurs.<br>

<b>Gaseous exchange and respiration</b><br>
<b>6.2 Gaseous exchange in plants</b><br>
<b>Plants obtain oxygen and carbondioxide</b><br>
mainly through their leaves. They also<br>
use lenticels (raised pores found on stems<br>
of  woody plants) for exchanging minute<br>
amounts of gases. They require oxygen<br>
for respiration and carbon dioxide for<br>
photosynthesis. These gases diffuse into<br>
the intercellular spaces of the leaf through<br>
stomatal pores which are normally found<br>
on the underside of the leaf and the gases<br>
diffuse into the cells that require them<br>
(Figure 6.8). The lenticel is a porous tissue<br>
consisting of cells with large intercellular<br>
spaces in the periderm of secondarily<br>
thickened organs; the bark of woody stems,<br>
and roots of dicotyledonous ﬂowering<br>
plants. Like the stomata, it functions as<br>
a pore that provides a pathway for the<br>
direct exchange of gases between internal<br>
tissues and the atmosphere.<br>
<b>Stomatal opening and closing</b><br>
<b>The opening and closing of stomata</b><br>
depends on changes in the turgor pressure<br>
of the guard cells. When water ﬂows<br>
into the guard cells by osmosis, their<br>
turgor increases and they expand. Due<br>
to the relatively inelastic inner wall, the<br>
guard cells bend and draw away from<br>
each other, allowing the opening of the<br>
pore, hence the air passes into the leaf.<br>
<b>If the guard cells lose water, the opposite</b><br>
happens and the pore closes. The guard<br>
cells lower their water potential to draw<br>
in water from the surrounding epidermal<br>
cells by actively accumulating potassium<br>
ions. This process requires energy in the<br>
form of ATP. The energy is supplied by<br>
the mitochondria that are found in the<br>
guard cells.<br>
<b>CO2</b><br>
<b>Palisade</b><br>
mesophyll<br>
<b>Stoma</b><br>
<b>O2</b><br>
<b>Spongy</b><br>
mesophyll<br>
<b>Guard cell with</b><br>
chloroplasts<br>
<b>Lower epidermis</b><br>
<b>Air space</b><br>
<b>Upper epidermis</b><br>
<b>Waxy cuticle</b><br>
<b>Vascular bundle</b><br>
containing xylem and<br>
phloem<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
304<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>The process of respiration in plants</b><br>
occurs throughout the day and night,<br>
providing energy to the plants. The light<br>
dependent phase of photosynthesis can<br>
only occur during the day time and stops<br>
at night or during darkness. One product<br>
of respiration is carbon dioxide gas.<br>
<b>This can be used by the plant directly</b><br>
for photosynthesis. However, during the<br>
day, the rate of photosynthesis can be 10<br>
or even 20 times faster than respiration<br>
(depending on light intensity). Therefore,<br>
the stomata must stay open for longer<br>
periods to allow adequate diffusion of<br>
carbon dioxide from the atmosphere into<br>
the plant cells.<br>
<b>Activity 6.2 Observation of stomata</b><br>
on a plant leaf<br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
<b>A potted Tradescantia plant, water, for­</b><br>
ceps, needle, surgical or razor blade,<br>
watch glasses, light microscope, slide,<br>
slide cover, blotting paper, safranin, and<br>
glycerin.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Remove a healthy leaf from the<br>
potted plant.<br>
b)	 Use a surgical or razor blade and<br>
forceps to remove a thin outer layer<br>
from the lower surface of the leaf.<br>
(Alternatively, you can do this by<br>
folding the leaf, and gently pulling<br>
the thin layer apart using forceps).<br>
c)	 Place the leaf layer in a watch glass<br>
containing water.<br>
d)	 Add a few drops of safranin stain<br>
in a watch glass containing a thin<br>
layer of the leaf, then leave it for 2-3<br>
minutes.<br>
e)	 Using forceps and a needle, take<br>
out the layer of the leaf and place<br>
it on a slide.<br>
f)	 Add a drop of glycerin over the leaf<br>
layer to prevent the leaf layer from<br>
getting dry, then place a slide cover<br>
over it.<br>
g)	 Use a blotting paper to remove the<br>
excess stain and glycerin.<br>
h)	 Observe the leaf layer on a slide<br>
under the microscope.<br>
<b>Questions</b><br>
1.	 Draw and label what you have<br>
observed.<br>
2.	 What conclusion can you make from<br>
your observation?<br>
<b>Safety precaution</b><br>
<b>Be careful when working with sharp</b><br>
objects such as needle, surgical or razor<br>
blade.<br>
<b>Exercise 6.2</b><br>
1.	 Explain the factors that govern<br>
efﬁcient gaseous exchange at the<br>
respiratory surfaces.<br>
2.	 Describe the adaptations of mountain<br>
climbers or dwellers to oxygen<br>
uptake.<br>
3.	 Explain the mechanisms of gaseous<br>
exchange in plants.<br>
4.	 Discuss why it is not advisable<br>
to sleep in a room with air-tight<br>
windows while the burning charcoal<br>
or potted plants are inside.<br>
5.	 Explain how partial pressures of CO2<br>
and O2 may inﬂuence the process<br>
of gaseous exchange in plants and<br>
animals.<br>

<b>Gaseous exchange and respiration</b><br>
<b>6.3  Respiration</b><br>
<b>Respiration is a process by which food</b><br>
substances<br>
are<br>
oxidised<br>
to<br>
release<br>
energy in the form of ATP, which is<br>
needed for metabolic activities in the<br>
body. Respiration takes place within the<br>
mitochondria of a cell. Since respiration<br>
takes place in a living cell, it is commonly<br>
referred to as cellular respiration. There are<br>
two types of respiration, namely aerobic<br>
respiration and anaerobic respiration.<br>
<b>Aerobic respiration occurs in the presence</b><br>
of oxygen, while anaerobic respiration<br>
occurs in the absence of oxygen (due<br>
to that, it is sometimes referred to as<br>
fermentation).<br>
<b>Aerobic</b><br>
respiration<br>
involves three main stages or processes,<br>
namely glycolysis, Kreb’s cycle, and<br>
oxidative phosphorylation. Respiration is<br>
an important process as it yields chemical<br>
energy in the form of ATP, which enables<br>
organisms to perform life activities<br>
such as movement, growth, excretion<br>
and reproduction. Respiration process<br>
involves oxidation of organic compounds<br>
known as respiratory substrates. These are<br>
carbohydrates, fats, or proteins.<br>
<b>Respiratory substrates and their</b><br>
energy value<br>
<b>Different respiratory substrates release</b><br>
different amounts of energy (Table<br>
6.2). The difference in energy values<br>
of respiratory substrates is due to the<br>
amount of hydrogen atoms present in each<br>
substrate. The more hydrogen atom in the<br>
molecule of a respiratory substrate, the<br>
more energy (ATP) is generated during<br>
respiration.<br>
<b>Mitochondria</b><br>
synthesize<br>
water using the hydrogen atoms removed<br>
from organic molecules, such as glucose,<br>
and the oxygen atoms they take in as<br>
they respire. So, the more the hydrogen<br>
bonds are broken, the larger the amount<br>
of energy released. Lipids’ energy density<br>
is more than twice that of carbohydrates<br>
because of their long fatty acid tails with<br>
large number of hydrogen atoms.<br>
<b>Table 6.2 Respiratory substrates and their energy</b><br>
values<br>
<b>S/N</b><br>
<b>Respiratory</b><br>
substrate<br>
<b>Energy value</b><br>
(KJ/g)<br>
1<br>
<b>Carbohydrates</b><br>
15.8<br>
2<br>
<b>Lipids</b><br>
39.4<br>
3<br>
<b>Protein</b><br>
17.0<br>
a)	 Carbohydrates<br>
<b>These</b><br>
are<br>
the<br>
main<br>
respiratory<br>
substrates used by most respiring cells.<br>
<b>Carbohydrates include polysaccharides,</b><br>
disaccharides,<br>
and<br>
monosaccharides.<br>
<b>Polysaccharides such glycogen, cellulose,</b><br>
and starch must be hydrolysed into simple<br>
monosaccharides such as glucose; in<br>
contrast, disaccharides have to undergo<br>
hydrolysis into three monosaccharides,<br>
namely<br>
glucose,<br>
galactose,<br>
and<br>
fructose, depending on the type of the<br>
disaccharide that has been hydrolysed.<br>
<b>Monosaccharides are usually in the form</b><br>
of hexose sugars. After their production,<br>
they are then utilized in the respiratory<br>
pathways to release energy.<br>
b)	 Lipids (fats and oils)<br>
<b>Lipids can be used as a respiratory</b><br>
substrate when the carbohydrates are<br>
exhausted.<br>
<b>When</b><br>
hydrolysed,<br>
each<br>
molecule releases three fatty acids and one<br>
glycerol molecule. Fatty acid are energy-<br>
rich compounds that enter the respiratory<br>
pathways in order to release energy.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   305</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
306<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
c)	 Protein<br>
<b>Generally, protein is not used as an energy</b><br>
source unless the body has no other option.<br>
<b>When all carbohydrate and lipid reserves</b><br>
are fully utilised, proteins come into use.<br>
<b>Proteins are not used in the ﬁrst place</b><br>
because of the variety of vital roles they<br>
play in the body. Proteins are hydrolysed<br>
into amino acids and then deaminated.<br>
<b>Deamination involves the removal of the</b><br>
amino group. The remaining acid may<br>
enter the Kreb’s cycle directly or may<br>
be converted into fatty acids before they<br>
enter the Kreb’s cycle. The amount of<br>
<b>ATP produced from protein metabolism is</b><br>
slightly less than glucose metabolism for<br>
equivalent weights.<br>
<b>Respiratory reactions</b><br>
<b>There are two fundamental types of</b><br>
reactions in cellular respiration, and these<br>
are oxidation or decarboxylation.<br>
<b>Oxidation. During respiration, oxidation</b><br>
can take place in three ways; through<br>
addition of oxygen, removal of hydrogen<br>
(dehydrogenation) removal of electrons.<br>
Decarboxylation.<br>
<b>Decarboxylation</b><br>
is<br>
the removal of carbon from a compound<br>
to form carbon dioxide. For a glucose<br>
molecule which contains carbon, hydrogen<br>
and oxygen, it is the hydrogen which is<br>
required for respiration. Thus, carbon has<br>
to be removed by decarboxylation.<br>
<b>Glycolysis</b><br>
<b>Cellular respiration occurs in all living</b><br>
organisms. Glycolysis is the ﬁrst stage<br>
of cellular respiration whereby glucose<br>
is oxidised into pyruvate. This process<br>
takes place in the cytoplasm of the cell<br>
and it does not require the presence<br>
of oxygen. Physiologically, glycolysis<br>
produces energy at a high rate, but for a<br>
short duration. In anaerobic condition,<br>
pyruvate can be reduced to lactate<br>
by lactate dehydrogenase. Anaerobic<br>
glycolysis is also an effective means of<br>
energy production during short intense<br>
exercise.<br>
<b>Under</b><br>
aerobic<br>
condition<br>
pyruvate is converted to Acetyl CoA and<br>
continues to the citric acid cycle (Kreb’s<br>
cycle) in the mitochondria. Pyruvate can<br>
also be converted to ethanol and CO2<br>
(fermentation) in anaerobic conditions<br>
and allows cells to make small amounts<br>
of ATP.<br>
<b>Some cells such as yeast are unable</b><br>
to carry out aerobic respiration and<br>
will automatically move into a type of<br>
anaerobic respiration called alcoholic<br>
fermentation. This occurs with the help of<br>
the enzyme pyruvate decarboxylase which<br>
removes a carbon dioxide molecule from<br>
the pyruvate to yield an acetaldehyde.<br>
<b>The acetaldehyde is then reduced by the</b><br>
enzyme alcohol dehydrogenase, which<br>
transfers the hydrogen from NADH to the<br>
acetaldehyde to yield NAD and ethanol.<br>
<b>Glycolysis process</b><br>
<b>Normally, glycolysis is a determined</b><br>
sequence<br>
of<br>
ten<br>
enzyme-catalyzed<br>
reactions and each step is facilitated by<br>
a different enzyme. The intermediates<br>
provide entry points to glycolysis, although,<br>
usually starts with glucose or glycogen<br>
to produce glucose-6-phosphate. The<br>
starting points for other monosaccharides<br>
such as galactose and fructose can be<br>
converted into one of these intermediates.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   306</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
307<br>
<b>Gaseous exchange and respiration</b><br>
<b>All the reaction steps for the glycolysis</b><br>
take place in the cytoplasm. Glycolysis<br>
yields an overall of two molecules of<br>
<b>ATP which are free energy-containing</b><br>
molecules, two molecules of pyruvic acid<br>
and two “high energy” electron carrying<br>
molecules of Nicotinamide Adenine<br>
<b>Dinucleotide (NADH). Glucose can also</b><br>
be synthesized from non-carbohydrate<br>
precursors by reactions referred to as<br>
gluconeogenesis. The pentose phosphate<br>
pathway enables cells to convert glucose-<br>
6-phosphate, a derivative of glucose,<br>
to ribose-5-phosphate and other types<br>
of monosaccharides. The NADH is an<br>
important cellular reducing agent, which is<br>
also produced by this pathway. Generally,<br>
glycolysis involves three main stages,<br>
namely phosphorylation of glucose, lysis<br>
and oxidation by dehydrogenation.<br>
a)	 Phosphorylation of glucose<br>
<b>Phosphorylation refers to the addition of</b><br>
the phosphate group (Pi) in an organic<br>
molecule. In this stage of phosphorylation,<br>
two phosphate groups attach to the<br>
glucose in order to make it more reactive.<br>
<b>In the first step of glycolysis, a phosphate</b><br>
group from ATP is transferred to glucose<br>
producing glucose-6-phosphate, a more<br>
reactive form of glucose. The enzyme<br>
hexokinase,<br>
with<br>
broad<br>
specificity<br>
catalyzes the phosphorylation of six-<br>
carbon sugars by addition of a phosphate<br>
group to glucose in the cell’s cytoplasm.<br>
<b>The enzyme hexokinase splits ATP into</b><br>
ADP and the Pi is added on to the glucose.<br>
<b>Phosphorylation prevents transport of</b><br>
glucose out of the cell and increases the<br>
reactivity of the oxygen in the resulting<br>
phosphate ester. The negative charge of the<br>
phosphate prevents passage of the sugar<br>
phosphate through the plasma membrane,<br>
trapping glucose inside the cell.<br>

b)	 Lysis<br>
<b>In this stage the phosphorylated 6-carbon</b><br>
sugar is broken down with the help<br>
of enzymes into two 3-carbon sugar<br>
phosphate. The enzyme aldolase splits<br>
fructose 1, 6-biphosphate into two sugars<br>
that are isomers of each other. These two<br>
sugars are dihydroxyacetone phosphate<br>
and<br>
glyceraldehyde<br>
phosphate<br>
also<br>
known as glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate<br>
or 3-phosphoglyceraldehyde (3-PGAL).<br>
<b>Only the glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate</b><br>
can<br>
proceed<br>
immediately<br>
through<br>
glycolysis. Dihydroxyacetone phosphate<br>
must be isomerised to glyceraldehyde-3-<br>
phosphate by the enzyme phosphotriose<br>
isomerase to continue with the next steps<br>
of the glycolytic pathway.<br>

c) Oxidation by dehydrogenation<br>
<b>In this step, an enzyme removes one</b><br>
hydrogen atom and two electrons from each<br>
three-carbon molecules. Both hydrogen<br>
atoms are modiﬁed to hydrogen ions,<br>
positively charged particles. The hydrogen<br>
ion and two electrons from each three-<br>
carbon molecule are transferred as a unit<br>
to a large molecule called Nicotinamide<br>
<b>Adenine Dinucleotide (NAD) to form</b><br>
two molecules of reduced NADH. The<br>
hydrogen ions and electrons stored in<br>
each molecule of NADH are used to make<br>
<b>ATP in later stages of cellular respiration</b><br>
during oxidative phosphorylation of the<br>
electron transport chain.<br>
<b>In the ﬁnal steps of glycolysis, two</b><br>
hydrogen atoms are removed from each<br>
three-carbon compound. These hydrogen<br>
atoms bond to free-ﬂoating oxygen<br>
atoms in the cytoplasm to form water.<br>
<b>This step prepares the two three-carbon</b><br>
compounds for action by the next enzyme<br>
in the pathway. This enzyme removes the<br>
phosphate group from each of the three-<br>
carbon compounds. Each phosphate<br>
group then bonds to a single molecule<br>
of adenosine diphosphate (ADP). ADP<br>
is composed of three carbon-based rings<br>
and a tail of two phosphate groups. The<br>
addition of the third phosphate group to<br>
the tail forms ATP. In this step, two new<br>
<b>ATP molecules are produced; this is the</b><br>
substrate-level phosphorylation. When<br>
cells require energy, another enzyme<br>
breaks off the third phosphate group,<br>
releasing energy that powers the cell. The<br>
removal of the third phosphate from ATP<br>
converts ATP back to ADP, which is used<br>
again in cellular respiration to make more<br>
<b>ATP.</b><br>
<b>When the two 3 carbon compounds</b><br>
are<br>
separated<br>
from<br>
the<br>
phosphate<br>
groups, the three-carbon compounds are<br>
converted to two molecules of pyruvate,<br>
each composed of three carbons, three<br>
oxygen, and three hydrogen atoms.<br>
<b>When glycolysis is complete, important</b><br>
products are produced; two molecules<br>
of NADH, two molecules of pyruvate<br>
(pyruvic acid) and two molecules of ATP.<br>
<b>NADH and pyruvate are used in the next</b><br>
steps of cellular respiration and the ATP<br>
molecules are used for reactions in the cell<br>
that require energy.<br>

<b>The process can be detailed as follows:</b><br>
<b>Glyceraldehyde</b><br>
-3-<br>
phosphate<br>
or<br>
3-phospho-glyceraldehyde (3-PGAL) is<br>
converted to 1, 3-biphosphoglycerate or 1,<br>
3-diphosphoglyceric acid (1,3-diPGA) by<br>
glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase<br>
enzyme where NAD is reduced to<br>
<b>NADH2. Then 1,3-diPGA is converted to</b><br>
3-phophoglycerate or 3-phosphoglyceric<br>
acid (3-PGA) by phosphoglycerate kinase<br>
enzyme, this process involve the loss of<br>
phosphate group (Pi) from 1,3-diPGA to<br>
form (3-PGA), and the Pi is transferred to<br>
a molecule of ADP to form the first ATP<br>
molecules. Actually, two molecules of<br>
<b>ATP are formed because there were two</b><br>
triose phosphate produced in the lysis of<br>
<b>Fructose-1,6-biphosphate. But the two</b><br>
<b>ATP formed are used to pay back the</b><br>
initial ATP used during the first process<br>
of glycolysis (phosphorylation of glucose<br>
stage).<br>
<b>The formed 3-PGA is converted to</b><br>
2-phosphoglycerate or 2-phosphoglyceric<br>
acid (2-PGA) by phosphoglyceromutase<br>
(phosphoglycerate<br>
mutase)<br>
enzyme.<br>
<b>This enzyme rearranges the phosphate</b><br>
group from the third carbon of 3-PGA<br>
to the second carbon, hence forming<br>
2-PGA. The 2-phosphoglycerate is then<br>
converted<br>
to<br>
phosphoenol<br>
pyruvate<br>
(PEP) under the influence of enolase<br>
enzyme, in this reaction water molecule is<br>
removed from 2-phosphoglycerate. Then<br>
the phosphoenol pyruvate is converted<br>
into pyruvate (pyruvic acid) by pyruvate<br>
kinase enzyme, where by the phosphate<br>
group (Pi) from PEP is transferred to<br>
<b>ADP molecule and combine to form ATP</b><br>
molecule. Again two molecules of ATP<br>
and two pyruvate are formed; as explained<br>
earlier, because the triose phosphate has<br>
to enter twice in the glycolysis process<br>
that is; when 3-PGAL is used, the<br>
dihydroxyacetone phosphate is converted<br>
to 3-PGAL and enters the cycle. The<br>
formation of pyruvate ends the glycolysis<br>
process. (Figure 6.9).<br>

5.	 The initial stages of glycolysis<br>
involve the use of ATP. Explain.<br>
<b>The fate of pyruvic acid</b><br>
<b>The pyruvate produced during glycolysis</b><br>
has two possible fates, depending on the<br>
availability of oxygen in the cell. In the<br>
presence of oxygen, the pyruvate will<br>
enter Kreb’s cycle in which they will be<br>
completely oxidised into carbon dioxide<br>
and water. Alternatively, in the absence<br>
of oxygen (in anaerobic condition) the<br>
pyruvate will undergo fermentation.<br>

<b>The fate of pyruvic acid under aerobic</b><br>
respiration<br>
<b>In aerobic respiration, the pyruvate from</b><br>
glycolysis is completely oxidised to<br>
carbon dioxide and water using oxygen.<br>
<b>In the ﬁrst stage, pyruvic acid is broken</b><br>
down to carbon dioxide and hydrogen.<br>
<b>This occurs in the matrix of mitochondria</b><br>
and involves the Kreb’s cycle. In the<br>
second stage, hydrogen is oxidised by<br>
oxygen to water in a series of reactions<br>
that constitute the respiratory chain or<br>
electron transport system. This occurs on<br>
the cristae of the mitochondria.<br>
<b>The transition between glycolysis and</b><br>
<b>Kreb’s cycle</b><br>
<b>There is a transitional stage between</b><br>
glycolysis and Kreb’s cycle or tricarboxylic<br>
acid (TCA) cycle. During this stage, each<br>
pyruvic acid molecules enters the matrix<br>
of the mitochondrion where it undergoes<br>
two types of reactions:<br>
a)	 Decarboxylation, by losing a carbon<br>
atom as carbon dioxide: The products<br>
of this oxidative decarboxylation<br>
(acetyl) are carried by coenzyme A<br>
(CoA) resulting into formation of<br>
acetyl Coenzyme A (acetyl CoA).<br>
b)	 Oxidation by dehydrogenation, in the<br>
presence of dehydrogenase enzyme<br>
and NAD.<br>
<b>The Kreb’s cycle</b><br>
<b>Kreb’s cycle is also known as citric acid</b><br>
cycle. When oxygen is available the<br>
pyruvate produced during glycolysis<br>
enters Kreb’s cycle, named after Sir Hans<br>
<b>Adolf Krebs (1900-1981), who worked</b><br>
out the details of the cycle in 1930. Kreb’s<br>
cycle takes place in the matrix of the<br>
mitochondrion.<br>
<b>Steps involved in the Kreb’s cycle</b><br>
<b>The Kreb’s cycle consists of a series of</b><br>
enzyme-catalysed reactions. It involves<br>
the following steps:<br>
<b>The ﬁrst step is the reaction of acetyl-</b><br>
<b>CoA with oxaloacetate to form citrate,</b><br>
in which the acetyl CoA (2C) is joined<br>
to oxaloacetate (4C) to form citrate (6C).<br>
<b>This process requires the input of water,</b><br>
and it is catalysed by citrate synthetase<br>
enzyme.<br>
<b>Acetyl - CoA + Oxaloacetate + H2O</b><br>
<b>Citrate + Coa-SH</b><br>
<b>Citrate synthetase</b><br>
The second step is the formation of isocitrate. In the presence of aconitase enzyme,<br>
citrate is converted into isocitrate. This process is accomplished by dehydration and<br>
rehydration to yield an isomer called isocitrate.<br>

313<br>
<b>Gaseous exchange and respiration</b><br>
The third step of reaction is oxidation of Isocitrate. In this reaction the Isocitrate is<br>
oxidatively decarboxylated to form α- ketoglutarate. The enzymes involved are<br>
isocitrate dehydrogenase and oxalosuccinate decarboxylase together with NAD, and<br>
the products are NADH2 and CO2.<br>
The fourth step is the oxidation of α- Ketoglutarate to form Succinyl – CoA. In the<br>
presence of α- ketoglutarate dehydrogenase enzyme, α- ketoglutarate is oxidatively<br>
decarboxylated to form succinyl CoA (4C). During this reaction NAD+ is reduced to<br>
NADH2. The products are NADH2 and CO2.<br>
The ﬁfth step is the conversion of Succinyl CoA into succinate. This reaction is<br>
catalysed by an enzyme succinyl CoA synthetase. It involves the removal or loss of<br>
CoA from succinyl – CoA. The CoA is replaced by phosphate group which is then<br>
removed and attached to guanosine diphosphate (GDP), thereby forming guanosine<br>
triphosphate (GTP). GTP is an energy yielding molecule and is used to generate ATP<br>
when it donates a phosphate group to ADP.<br>
<b>There are two forms of the enzyme, called</b><br>
isoenzymes, for this step, depending upon<br>
the type of animal tissue in which they are<br>
found. One form is found in tissues that<br>
use large amounts of ATP, such as heart<br>
and skeletal muscle. The second form of<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   313</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
314<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
the enzyme is found in tissues that have a high number of anabolic pathways, such<br>
as liver. This form produces guanosine triphosphate (GTP). GTP is energetically<br>
equivalent to ATP; however, its use is more restricted. In particular, protein synthesis<br>
primarily uses GTP.<br>
The sixth step is the oxidation of succinate to form fumarate. The conversion of<br>
succinate into fumarate involves removal of hydrogen and is catalysed by succinate<br>
dehydrogenase. In this reaction FAD is reduced to FADH2.<br>
<b>It is important to note that glycolysis yields</b><br>
two pyruvate molecules, each of which<br>
enters the Kreb’s cycle separately. Thus,<br>
for a molecule of glucose, there must be<br>
two Kreb’s cycles, and, to avoid repetition,<br>
each component of the cycle must be<br>
multiplied by two. Thus, the products of<br>
the Kreb’s cycle are 6NADH2, 2FADH2,<br>
4CO2, 2ATP, and oxaloacetate molecules.<br>
<b>The transitory step, that is from pyruvate</b><br>
to acetate, yields 2NADH2 and 2CO2<br>
molecules (Figure 6.10 (a) and (b)).<br>
<b>Succinate</b><br>
dehydrogenase<br>
The seventh step is the hydration of fumarate to malate. This reversible reaction is<br>
catalysed by fumarase, which is also known as fumarate hydratase.<br>
<b>Fumarase</b><br>
Eighth step is the oxidation of malate to form oxaloacetate. This reaction is catalysed<br>
by malate dehydrogenase and results in the production of oxaloacetate which is the<br>
starting compound of the Kreb’s cycle. In this process, NAD is reduced into NADH2.<br>
<b>Malate</b><br>
dehydrogenase<br>

<b>Importance of the Kreb’s cycle</b><br>
<b>Kreb’s cycle is an economical way of</b><br>
turning food components in the cell into<br>
usable energy. Only the acetyl groups are<br>
destroyed in the cycle. The seven enzymes<br>
that carry out the various reactions and the<br>
intermediate compounds on which these<br>
enzymes act can be used again and again.<br>
<b>Moreover, many of the intermediate</b><br>
compounds produced in the Kreb’s cycle<br>
are of value as starting materials for the<br>
synthesis of amino acids, carbohydrates,<br>
and other cellular products. Pyruvates are<br>
broken down to carbon dioxide. Thus, the<br>
<b>Kreb’s cycle degrades macromolecules</b><br>
into simpler molecules. The Kreb’s cycle<br>
releases ATP and NADH and FADH. The<br>
<b>ATP is directly utilised by the cellular</b><br>
activities while the NADH and FADH are<br>
metabolites for oxidative phosphorylation<br>
of the electron transport system in which<br>
ATP are produced.<br>
<b>The fate of pyruvic acid under</b><br>
anaerobic condition<br>
<b>In the absence of oxygen; the principle</b><br>
product of glycolysis (pyruvate) enters<br>
a fermentation process. The organisms<br>
that do not use oxygen as the ﬁnal<br>
proton and electron acceptor are known<br>
as anaerobes.  In anaerobes, other less-<br>
oxidising substances such as sulphate<br>
ion (SO4<br>
2-), nitrate ion (NO3-), sulphur,<br>
and fumarate are used. These terminal<br>
electron acceptors have smaller reduction<br>
potentials than oxygen. This means<br>
that less energy is released per oxidised<br>
molecule. Generally, anaerobic respiration<br>
is less efﬁcient in energy production than<br>
aerobic respiration. It is mainly used by<br>
prokaryotes that live in environment<br>
devoid of oxygen. Many anaerobic<br>
organisms are obligate anaerobes as they<br>
can only respire anaerobically and they<br>
die in the presence of oxygen. Muscles<br>
do also respire anaerobically whenever<br>
there is deﬁciency or lack of oxygen at<br>
a particular tissue point. This creates<br>
the basis for categorising anaerobic<br>
respiration into alcoholic fermentation<br>
and lactic acid fermentation.<br>
<b>Alcoholic fermentation</b><br>
<b>Alcoholic fermentation is also called</b><br>
ethanol fermentation which occurs in<br>
cells, such as plant and yeast cells (Figure<br>
6.11). This biological process converts<br>
two pyruvates into two acetaldehydes<br>
producing two carbondioxide molecules<br>
as a waste product. The two acetaldehydes<br>
are then converted to two ethanol<br>
molecules by using hydrogen ions from<br>
<b>NADH; converting NADH back into</b><br>
<b>NAD+. Ethanol fermentation has many</b><br>
uses. These include producing alcoholic<br>
beverages, ethanol fuel, as well as raising<br>
reagent in baking bread.<br>
2 Pyruvic Acid<br>
<b>2 NADH</b><br>
<b>2 CO2</b><br>
<b>2 NAD+</b><br>
2 Ethanol<br>
<b>Figure 6.11 Alcoholic fermentation pathway</b><br>
<b>Lactic acid fermentation</b><br>
<b>This is also referred to as lactate</b><br>
fermentation, which is a biological<br>
process by which pyruvate is converted<br>
into the metabolite lactate. In lactic acid<br>
fermentation, the pyruvic acid from<br>
glycolysis is reduced to lactic acid by<br>
NADH, which is oxidised to NAD+.<br>
<b>Lactic acid fermentation allows glycolysis</b><br>
to continue by ensuring that NADH is<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   316</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
317<br>
<b>Gaseous exchange and respiration</b><br>
returned to its oxidised state (NAD+). This<br>
anaerobic fermentation reaction occurs in<br>
some bacteria and animal cells such as in<br>
muscle cells (Figure 6.12).<br>
<b>Pyruvate</b><br>
<b>Lactate dehydrogenase</b><br>
<b>NADH</b><br>
<b>NAD+</b><br>
<b>Lactic acid</b><br>
<b>Figure 6.12 Lactic acid fermentation pathway</b><br>
<b>If oxygen is present in the cell, many</b><br>
organisms will bypass fermentation and<br>
undergo aerobic respiration. However,<br>
facultative anaerobes will undergo both<br>
fermentation and aerobic respiration<br>
in the presence of oxygen. Sometimes,<br>
even when oxygen is present and<br>
aerobic metabolism is taking place in<br>
the mitochondria, if pyruvate is building<br>
up faster than it can be metabolised, the<br>
fermentation process will occur. The<br>
enzyme responsible for catalysing the<br>
interconversion of pyruvate to lactate is<br>
known as lactate dehydrogenase.<br>

<b>How is it related to glycolysis?</b><br>
2.	 Illustrate the steps involved in the<br>
Kreb’s cycle.<br>
3.	 What<br>
is<br>
the<br>
importance<br>
of<br>
regeneration of oxaloacetate in the<br>
ﬁnal step of Kreb’s cycle?<br>
4.	 Carry out library search to find<br>
out if the bacteria and yeast form<br>
alcohols in the absence of oxygen<br>
or they possess a metabolic pathway<br>
that does not involve oxygen. Write<br>
down your findings.<br>
5.	 Explain the fate of pyruvic acid<br>
during respiration.<br>
<b>Events of electron transport chain in</b><br>
the formation of ATP<br>
<b>The electron transport chain is the ﬁnal</b><br>
and most important step of cellular<br>
respiration. The electron transport chain<br>
(electron transport system or cytochrome<br>
system) is a process by which the energy<br>
carrier molecules (NADH2 and FADH2)<br>
produced during glycolysis, Kreb’s cycle<br>
and other catabolic processes are oxidized<br>
to release energy in the form of ATP.<br>
<b>The electron transport chain is a series</b><br>
of electron transporters embedded in the<br>
inner mitochondrial membrane that shuttles<br>
electrons from NADH2 and FADH2. In<br>
the process, protons are pumped from the<br>
mitochondrial matrix to the intermembrane<br>
space, and oxygen is reduced to form water.<br>
<b>The hydrogen atoms carried by reduced</b><br>
<b>NAD and FAD are transferred to a chain</b><br>
of other carriers at progressively lower<br>
energy levels. As the hydrogen passes<br>
from one carrier to the next, the energy<br>
released is used to combine ADP and<br>
inorganic phosphate (Pi) to form ATP. A<br>
series of carriers is termed the respiratory<br>
chain. The latter has four electron carriers,<br>
namely NAD, FAD, coenzyme Q and<br>
cytochromes. The hydrogen atoms carried<br>
by NAD are shunted into the chain at<br>
carrier one, NAD (a step ahead FAD), and<br>
produce a total of 3 ATP molecules as they<br>
pass through the carriers (Figure 6.13).<br>
<b>Meanwhile, the hydrogen atoms carried</b><br>
by FAD are introduced in the chain at<br>
carrier two, FAD. Therefore, a pair of<br>
hydrogen atoms carried by FAD makes<br>
a total of 2 ATP molecules as it passes<br>
through the carriers. Initially, hydrogen<br>
atoms pass along the chain. However, after<br>
the FAD stage, they split into protons (H+)<br>
and electrons. The former takes another<br>
route out of the chain as the electrons<br>
pass through the cytochrome system.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   317</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
318<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Accordingly, the pathway can be called</b><br>
the electron transport system. Oxygen is<br>
the ﬁnal electron acceptor in the electron<br>
transport system. Finally, protons and<br>
electrons recombine to form hydrogen<br>
atoms which create a link with oxygen<br>
to form water, this reaction is catalysed<br>
by the enzyme cytochrome oxidase. The<br>
formation of ATP through the oxidation<br>
of hydrogen atoms is called oxidative<br>
phosphorylation (Figure 6.13).<br>

<b>Generally, in the electron transport chain</b><br>
each reduced NAD molecule results in<br>
production of 3 ATP and the release of<br>
hydrogen which combines with oxygen<br>
to form water. Therefore, the 10 reduced<br>
<b>NAD molecules  (2 from glycolysis, 2</b><br>
from conversion of pyruvate to Acetyl<br>
<b>CoA and 6 from Kreb’s cycle) results in the</b><br>
production of 30 ATP, 10 water molecules<br>
and uses 10 oxygen atoms (5 molecules<br>
of oxygen). Moreover, each reduced FAD<br>
molecule (from Kreb’s cycle) results in<br>
the production of 2 ATP, hence 2 FAD<br>
molecules produce 4 ATP. Overall, the<br>
electron transport chain produces a total<br>
of 34 ATP molecules from one molecule<br>
of glucose (Figure 6.14).<br>

<b>Gaseous exchange and respiration</b><br>
<b>ATP yield in aerobic and anaerobic</b><br>
respiration of glucose<br>
a)	In aerobic respiration<br>
<b>A total of 38 ATP molecules are produced</b><br>
for every one molecule of glucose oxidized<br>
completely (Table 6.3). The balanced<br>
equation for aerobic respiration is:<br>
<b>C6H12O6 + 6O2 ⎯→</b><br>
<b>⎯ 6CO2 + 6H2O + 38ATP</b><br>
<b>ΔG = −2880 kJ per mol of C6H12O6</b><br>
<b>Thus, the energy released by complete</b><br>
oxidation of one molecule of glucose is<br>
2880 kJ, the negative ΔG shows that the<br>
reaction can occur spontaneously.<br>
<b>The amount of energy contained in one</b><br>
mole of ATP is 30.6 kJ. The amount of<br>
energy contained in 38 moles of ATP is<br>
38 x 30.6 = 1162.8 kJ. The efﬁciency of<br>
energy transfer in aerobic respiration is<br>
1162.8/2880 x 100% = 40.4 %.<br>

b)	In anaerobic respiration<br>
<b>During alcoholic fermentation 2 molecules</b><br>
of ATP are produced for every molecule<br>
of glucose used (Table 6.4). Conversion<br>
of glucose to ethanol produces 210 kJ per<br>
mole, thus the energy contained in the 2<br>
molecules of ATP is 2 x 30.6 = 61.2 kJ.<br>
<b>The efﬁciency of energy transfer during</b><br>
alcoholic fermentation is 61.2/210 x 100%<br>
= 29.1%<br>
<b>Alternatively, in the lactate fermentation,</b><br>
2 ATP molecules are produced for every<br>
molecule of glucose used.<br>
<b>Glucose</b><br>
2Lactate + 2ATP<br>
<b>The amount of energy contained in 2 ATP</b><br>
molecules is 2 x 30.6 = 61.2 kJ.<br>
<b>The total energy released during the</b><br>
conversion of glucose to lactate is 150 kJ<br>
per mole. Thus, the efﬁciency of energy<br>
transfer during lactate fermentation is<br>
61.2/150 x 100% = 40.8%.<br>
<b>The total energy released when glucose</b><br>
molecule is respired aerobically is 38 ATP,<br>
but the total energy released in anaerobic<br>
respiration of glucose is only 2ATP.<br>
<b>Therefore, aerobic respiration is more</b><br>
efficient than anaerobic respiration.<br>

<b>Respiratory pathway using lipids and</b><br>
protein<br>
<b>Lipids and proteins are respiratory</b><br>
substrates in addition to carbohydrates,<br>
which are used by most cells. The<br>
respiratory pathways for both aerobic<br>
and anaerobic process involve the stages<br>
of respiration such as glycolysis, the<br>
Kreb’s cycle, and electron transport chain.<br>
<b>Aerobes use them all, whereas anaerobes</b><br>
use only glycolysis. Lipids and proteins<br>
are not directly accommodated in these<br>
pathways, they are converted in a form to<br>
suit the types of the metabolites required<br>
by such pathways (Figure 6.15).<br>
<b>Lipids are polymers of fatty acids and</b><br>
glycerol. They have to be hydrolysed<br>
into glycerol and fatty acids. Glycerol<br>
can be converted into glucose and follow<br>
the patterns in glycolysis to the electron<br>
transport chain. The fatty acids join the<br>
<b>Kreb’s cycle as fatty acid fragments, each</b><br>
with 2 carbons that are joined with Acetyl<br>
<b>CoA. The fatty acid will make several</b><br>
<b>Kreb’s cycles depending on the number of</b><br>
fragments formed. The products of Kreb’s<br>
cycles will add up in generating a huge<br>
account of ATP from lipids.<br>
Proteins are polymers of amino acids.<br>
<b>Amino acids have amino and carboxylic</b><br>
groups. They also have a varied number of<br>
carbon atoms. Being polymers, proteins<br>
should be hydrolysed into amino acids<br>
which are deaminated to remove the<br>
amino groups. The remaining portions<br>
enter the respiratory pathway depending<br>
on the number of carbons each has. The<br>
portions with 3 carbons are converted<br>
into pyruvate; those with 4 carbons form<br>
oxaloacetate while those with 5 carbons are<br>
converted into α-ketoglutarate. Example<br>
aspartate and glutamate enter directly<br>
into the Kreb’s cycle at oxalaoacetate and<br>
α-ketoglutarate respectively.<br>
<b>Respiratory quotient (RQ)</b><br>
<b>Respiratory quotient (RQ) is a measure</b><br>
of the ratio of carbon dioxide evolved by<br>
an organism to that of oxygen consumed<br>
over a given period of time.<br>
<b>RQ = Volume of CO2 evolved</b><br>
<b>Volume of O2 consumed</b><br>
<b>For example, the equation for a complete</b><br>
aerobic oxidation of hexose sugar is<br>
represented below:<br>
<b>C6H12O6 + 6O2 ⎯→</b><br>
<b>⎯6CO2 + 6H2O</b><br>
<b>RQ = 6CO2</b><br>
<b>6O2</b><br>
= 1<br>
<b>However, it is difﬁcult to have this</b><br>
theoretically calculated value, because a<br>
substrate is rarely completely oxidised.<br>

<b>Signiﬁcance of RQ</b><br>
a)	 It helps to indicate  the type of substrate<br>
oxidised; for example, the RQ value<br>
of 1.0 implies complete oxidation of<br>
glucose; RQ of 0.7 means oxidation<br>
of fats (fatty acids); and, for proteins,<br>
the RQ value varies, but it is around<br>
0.9. The RQ values of less than one<br>
means that oxidation of a mixture of<br>
substrates.<br>
b)	 It helps to indicate the type of<br>
metabolism taking place. For example,<br>
if RQ values are less than one, the<br>
following are possible;<br>
i)	 Aerobic oxidation, as the volume<br>
of carbon dioxide evolved is less<br>
than that of oxygen taken in.<br>
ii)	 The carbondioxide produced is<br>
used by another process in the<br>
same<br>
organism,<br>
for<br>
instance<br>
photosynthesis<br>
in<br>
plants<br>
or<br>
formation of calcareous shells in<br>
some animals.<br>
<b>On the other hand, high RQ values imply</b><br>
kinds of anaerobic respiration. Therefore,<br>
the volume of carbondioxide evolved is<br>
greater than that of oxygen consumed<br>
(Table 6.5).<br>
<b>Examples</b><br>
a)	 The equation for respiration of the fat<br>
tripalmitin is as follows:<br>
