<b>⎯102CO2 + 98H2O</b><br>
<b>What is the RQ for tripalmitin?</b><br>
<b>Solution:</b><br>
<b>RQ = Volume of CO2 evolved</b><br>
<b>Volume of O2 consumed</b><br>
<b>RQ = 102O2</b><br>
<b>145O2</b><br>
= 0.7<br>
<b>So, the RQ value for tripalmitin is 0.7</b><br>
b)	 What is the RQ when glucose is<br>
respired anaerobically to ethanol and<br>
carbondioxide?<br>
<b>Solution:</b><br>
<b>RQ = Volume of CO2 evolved</b><br>
<b>Volume of O2 consumed</b><br>
<b>Since the process is anaerobic, the volume</b><br>
of oxygen evolved is zero.<br>
<b>Thus:</b><br>
<b>RQ = 2CO2</b><br>
<b>0O2</b><br>
= ∞(Infinity)<br>

<b>Anaerobic respiration</b><br>
<b>The basal metabolic rate (BMR)</b><br>
<b>The basal metabolic rate of an organism</b><br>
is the minimum rate of energy conversion<br>
required just to stay alive during rest or<br>
sleep. It is actually the amount of energy<br>
needed to maintain body functioning while<br>
at rest, to keep the heart beating, blood<br>
ﬂowing, food digested and body breathe.<br>
<b>It also referred to as resting metabolic rate</b><br>
(RMR). In humans, BMR is measured<br>
after an individual has undergone a<br>
standardised rest period of between 12 to<br>
18 hours of physical and mental relaxation<br>
without taking a meal during that period.<br>
<b>Factors which cause variation of the</b><br>
<b>BMR of an individual</b><br>
<b>The following factors inﬂuence the</b><br>
variation of basal metabolic rates among<br>
individuals:<br>
<b>Body size</b><br>
<b>Small organisms have larger surface area</b><br>
to volume ratio, hence larger BMR than<br>
large organisms.<br>
<b>Body composition</b><br>
<b>Fat tissue has a lower metabolic activity</b><br>
than muscle tissue. As lean muscle mass<br>
increases, the metabolic rate increases.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   322</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:07<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
323<br>
<b>Gaseous exchange and respiration</b><br>
<b>Sex</b><br>
<b>The basal metabolic rate (BMR) of females</b><br>
is lower than that of males. In average the<br>
<b>BMR of females is 5 to 10 percent lower</b><br>
than that of males. The difference is that;<br>
generally, women possess more body fat<br>
and less muscle mass than men of similar<br>
size.<br>
<b>Age</b><br>
The BMR decreases with age (aging).<br>
<b>A decrease in lean muscle mass during</b><br>
adulthood results in a slow, steady decline<br>
in BMR.<br>
<b>Climate and body temperature</b><br>
<b>The BMR of people in tropical climates is</b><br>
generally up to 20 percent higher than their<br>
counterparts in more temperate climates<br>
because it takes energy to keep the body<br>
cool. Exercise performed in hot weather<br>
also imposes an additional metabolic<br>
load. Body fat content and effectiveness<br>
of clothing determine the magnitude of<br>
energy metabolism in cold environments;<br>
it takes energy to keep the body warm if<br>
you work or exercise in very cold weather.<br>
<b>Hormonal levels</b><br>
<b>Thyroxine (T4) is the key hormone</b><br>
released by the thyroid glands which<br>
has a signiﬁcant effect upon metabolic<br>
rate.<br>
<b>Hypothyroidism</b><br>
is<br>
relatively<br>
common, especially in women near<br>
or after menopause. Everyone with a<br>
weight problem should have their thyroid<br>
function checked by their doctor and<br>
treated appropriately if it turns out to be<br>
low.<br>
<b>Healthy status</b><br>
<b>Fever, illness, or injury may increase</b><br>
resting metabolic rate. Therefore, a sick<br>
person has higher rate of metabolism than<br>
a healthy person.<br>

<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
<b>Chapter</b><br>
<b>Seven</b><br>
<b>Regulation</b><br>
(Homeostasis)<br>
<b>Introduction</b><br>
The internal environment of multi-cellular organisms is made up of  tissue ﬂuids whose<br>
conditions, such as pH, temperature, pressure, glucose concentration and salt contents<br>
are always kept at a relatively constant level regardless of the fluctuations of the external<br>
environments. The ability to maintain a constant internal body environment enables an<br>
organism to survive in a variety of habitats. Keeping a stable internal environment of<br>
an organism requires constant adjustments as conditions change inside and outside the<br>
cells. Since the internal and external environments of a cell are constantly changing,<br>
adjustments must be made continuously to stay at or near the normal level. This<br>
regulatory mechanism is called homeostasis. In this chapter, you will learn about the<br>
concept of body regulation, temperature regulation, excretion and osmoregulation.<br>
You will also learn about the processes through which water content in the body is<br>
controlled.<br>
<b>7.1 The concept of regulation</b><br>
<b>The importance of regulation (homeostasis)</b><br>
in animals was ﬁrst pointed out by the French<br>
physiologist Claude Bernard in 1857. In one<br>
of his researches, he used dogs to study the<br>
importance of constant internal environment<br>
in mammals. He described variations in<br>
glucose concentration in the blood. His<br>
study revealed that the concentration<br>
of glucose in the blood of mammals<br>
remained relatively constant regardless of<br>
variations in diet. For example, dogs that<br>
were well fed with food rich in meat or<br>
sugar had similar glucose concentration<br>
in the blood as starving dogs. From these<br>
results, he concluded that mammals must<br>
have a control mechanism that keeps their<br>
internal environment constant, despite the<br>
changes in external environment. This<br>
tendency enables them to exploit a wide<br>
variety of habitats. For example, in human<br>
beings, the internal mechanism maintains<br>
constant body temperature of about 37 0C<br>
despite the wide range of variation in the<br>
environmental temperatures. This constancy<br>
enables human beings to be active in<br>
different environments, while other animals<br>
such as amphibians and reptiles have non-<br>
constant body temperature and they cannot<br>
be active in a wide range of environmental<br>
temperatures.<br>

<b>Homeostatic control mechanism</b><br>
<b>The homeostatic control mechanism</b><br>
involves a regulator which compares<br>
the actual output with a set point. Then,<br>
it produces some sort of error signal,<br>
which sends information to the corrective<br>
mechanism or effector regarding the<br>
difference between the set point and the<br>
actual output. The error signal is usually in<br>
the form of nerve impulses or hormones in<br>
the body. The corrective mechanism may<br>
include one or more effectors that set the<br>
controlled system and restore the output<br>
to its set point. In some physiological<br>
processes, such as temperature regulation,<br>
separate but coordinated mechanisms<br>
control deviations in different directions<br>
from the set point such as the rise or fall<br>
in body temperature, and lead to a greater<br>
degree of control.<br>
<b>The corrective mechanism explained</b><br>
above is said to be the key component of<br>
homeostatic control mechanism. It varies<br>
the output so that it can be brought back<br>
to the set point. Homeostasis is a dynamic<br>
process which works by making continual<br>
adjustments to compensate for ﬂuctuations<br>
of output. Thus, it is more accurate to<br>
describe the homeostatic system as being<br>
in a steady state or in a dynamic equilibrium<br>
rather than being constant. In addition,<br>
homeostatic controls can be either extrinsic<br>
or intrinsic. Extrinsic control is one which<br>
originates from outside of the body organ<br>
or tissue while the intrinsic control is the<br>
one which originates from within the body,<br>
organ or tissue.<br>
<b>Components of homeostatic mechanism</b><br>
<b>The homeostatic control mechanism</b><br>
consists of different components, including,<br>
a set point, stimulus, variables, receptors,<br>
effectors, inputs, outputs, control centre and<br>
compensatory response. These components<br>
are integrated to bring about homeostatic<br>
control (Figure 7.1). A set point is the<br>
desired or optimal physiological state for<br>
the output. It is also known as the norm or<br>
reference point. This is usually determined<br>
genetically in the physiological process.<br>
<b>In the homeostatic control mechanism</b><br>
variables are factors that are being regulated.<br>
<b>Such factors include body temperature,</b><br>
pH, blood pressure, and plasma potassium<br>
concentration. Anything that produces<br>
change on a variable is called a stimulus.<br>
<b>Therefore, a stimulus can trigger changes in</b><br>
various body parameters such as temperature,<br>
pH, blood pressure, and plasma potassium<br>
concentration, resulting into deviation from<br>
a set point. The detectors or receptors tend<br>
to detect a change in variables.<br>
<b>Different types of sensory receptors exist,</b><br>
including thermoreceptors which detect<br>
change in body temperature. Input<br>
communicates the information from the<br>
receptor to the control centre. An example of<br>
inputs in the homeostatic control mechanism<br>
is afferent nervous system. Effectors in the<br>
homeostatic control mechanism are organs<br>
or glands that carry out the response from<br>
the control centre. Examples of effectors<br>
include: the sweat glands, blood vessels,<br>
and muscles. The control centre of the<br>
homeostatic control is the brain, particularly<br>
the hypothalamus. The control centre<br>
analyses the information from the receptor<br>
and determines the appropriate response to<br>
the change or stimulus.<br>
<b>The output sends the response instructions</b><br>
from the control centre to the effector.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   326</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
327<br>
<b>Regulation (Homeostasis)</b><br>
<b>For example, if the temperature needs to</b><br>
be lowered, the hypothalamus will send<br>
the information to the effector for the<br>
response. Compensatory response is the<br>
action of the effector which will counteract<br>
the stimulus and bring the variable back to<br>
its normal range.<br>
<b>Feedback mechanism for homeostatic</b><br>
control<br>
<b>Any change from the set point activates</b><br>
the control system to initiate a sequence<br>
of events so as to either restore conditions<br>
towards their normal state, or to make the<br>
system deviate further. Feedback requires<br>
the action of the system to be related to<br>
a reference point or set-point (optimum<br>
level) of the variable being controlled.<br>
<b>Two forms of feedback, namely negative</b><br>
and positive feedback are recognised.<br>
<b>Negative feedback</b><br>
<b>A negative feedback occurs in a situation</b><br>
where the disturbance in a system sets<br>
in motion a sequence of events which<br>
tends to restore the system to its original<br>
state. A negative feedback in homeostatic<br>
control mechanism keeps a variable,<br>
such as the blood glucose level close to a<br>
particular value or set point (Figure 7.2).<br>
<b>A change in the state of an internal factor</b><br>
in the body causes effectors to restore the<br>
internal environment to its original state.<br>
<b>For instance, an increase in the level of</b><br>
glucose in the body triggers a sequence<br>
of events that tend to remove excess<br>
glucose from the blood by converting it<br>
into glycogen. In contrast, a decrease in<br>
the level of glucose in the body causes<br>
the liver to break the stored glycogen to<br>
glucose in order to supply more glucose<br>
to the cells of the body. This corrective<br>
measure allows blood glucose level to<br>
remain constant. This type of system in<br>
which change in the level of an internal<br>
factor causes a corrective mechanism is<br>
referred to as a self-adjusting system. The<br>
science of a self-regulating control system<br>
which operates via feedback mechanisms<br>
in organisms is known as cybernetics.<br>

<b>Output - Response</b><br>

<b>Positive feedback</b><br>
<b>Positive feedback is the self-regulatory</b><br>
mechanism which operates when the<br>
system is deviated from a set point which<br>
initiates a sequence of events that tends to<br>
deviate further the system. The positive<br>
feedback mechanism makes the system to<br>
be unstable; that is why it is not common in<br>
living organisms. An example of positive<br>
feedback occurs during labour, when the<br>
hormone oxytocin stimulates muscular<br>
contraction of the uterus; which in turn<br>
stimulates the release of more oxytocin.<br>
<b>Positive feedback mechanism may also</b><br>
occur in the nerves where a small stimulus<br>
can bring about a large response to the<br>
effectors (Figure 7.3).<br>
<b>Further excess</b><br>
<b>Positive feedback</b><br>
<b>Further deficiency</b><br>
<b>Deficiency</b><br>
<b>Excess</b><br>
<b>Set point</b><br>
<b>Positive feedback</b><br>
<b>Figure 7.3 A positive feedback mechanism</b><br>
<b>Another example of positive feedback</b><br>
mechanism is the release of volatile<br>
plant hormone (ethylene) by the ripening<br>
fruits that accelerates the ripening of<br>
unripe fruits in its vicinity. The fruit<br>
ripening process involves changing the<br>
fruit texture, softening, and colour. As<br>
more fruits get ripe they produce more<br>
hormones that further continue to ripen<br>
more the surrounding fruits.<br>

<b>Regulation (Homeostasis)</b><br>
Activity 7.1 Investigating the effect of positive feedback mechanism in ripening fruit<br>
<b>Materials</b><br>
One bunch of ten unripe green bananas, six containers, and dry banana leaves.<br>
<b>Procedure</b><br>
a)	 Label the six containers from A to F.<br>
b)	 Isolate five bananas from the bunch and put one banana in each of the five<br>
containers labelled B to F.<br>
c)	 Put the remaining five undetached bananas in the container labelled A.<br>
d)	 Cover all the containers with dry banana leaves and arrange them in series; starting<br>
with the one with five bananas. The distance from one container to another should<br>
be not less than one meter (Figure 7.4).<br>
e)	  Note the number of days it will take for all bananas in all containers to get ripe.<br>

<b>Questions</b><br>
1.	 Compare the time taken for the bunch and individual bananas to ripen.<br>
2.	 What might be the cause of the differences in ripening time?<br>
3.	 Explain the significance of the phenomenon in our everyday life.<br>

<b>7.2  Temperature regulation</b><br>
<b>Most organisms can survive in a narrow</b><br>
range of temperature from 10 °C to 30 °C.<br>
<b>In order to survive, most animals have</b><br>
to regulate their body temperature. The<br>
tendency of animals to adjust their body<br>
temperature to suit the living   conditions of<br>
varying temperatures in the environment<br>
is termed as temperature regulation<br>
(thermoregulation).<br>
<b>Animals</b><br>
with<br>
a<br>
varying body temperature according to the<br>
changes in the environmental temperature<br>
are called poikilotherms (poikilos means<br>
‘various’ and thermo means ‘heat’). These<br>
animals obtain most of their body heat<br>
from the sources outside their bodies;<br>
hence they are sometimes referred to as<br>
ectotherms. Examples of poikilothermic<br>
organisms include reptiles, amphibians<br>
and most fish. With exception of birds and<br>
mammals, most animals are ectotherms.<br>
<b>Animals that maintain a stable body</b><br>
temperature regardless of ﬂuctuations<br>
in the environmental temperature are<br>
called homoiotherms or homoeotherms<br>
or homeotherms. Their heat is generated<br>
internally by metabolic activities; hence,<br>
they are also known as endotherms; and<br>
they include mammals and birds. The<br>
body temperature of endotherms ranges<br>
between 37 °C and 44 °C. The higher<br>
the body temperature, the greater the<br>
metabolic rate in the animal’s body. For<br>
example, birds have higher metabolic rate<br>
to cater for the energy needed for ﬂight.<br>
<b>Their body temperature ranges between</b><br>
<b>38.5 °C and 44 °C. Homoeotherms can</b><br>
regulate their body temperature through<br>
negative feedback control. They control<br>
their body temperature independently<br>
of the environment; they use internal<br>
physiological control mechanisms as well<br>
as behavioural mechanisms.<br>
<b>Adaptation of endotherms to cold and</b><br>
hot climatic conditions<br>
<b>In order to survive in a variety of habitual</b><br>
environment,<br>
species<br>
of<br>
mammals<br>
have developed various physiological<br>
adaptive features or mechanisms. This<br>
diversity of living environment include<br>
the hot arid environment of the desert,<br>
the colder and glacial environment of<br>
the Arctic and Antarctic poles, the salty<br>
environment of the oceans, low oxygen<br>
environment of mountain peaks and the<br>
dark environments of the deep sea and<br>
oceans. Animals in the cold environment<br>
such as arctic fox have insulating layers<br>
with a thick coat of fur or thick layer of<br>
fat that help to reduce the rate of heat<br>
transfer thereby retaining body heat and<br>
keeping the animal’s body temperature<br>
at constant level. Another strategy of<br>
cold weather used by some animals is a<br>
temporary decrease of metabolic rate and<br>
body temperature. This helps to decrease<br>
the temperature difference between the<br>
animal body and the environment thereby<br>
minimizing heat loss.<br>
<b>Other adaptations for survival of animals</b><br>
such as bears in the extreme cold climate<br>
include: hibernation during the cold<br>
period. The body temperature falls to a<br>
few degrees and an animal falls into a deep<br>
sleep. This tendency enables the animal to<br>
conserve energy.<br>
<b>In the hot and dry environment, endotherms</b><br>
develop cooling mechanism by means<br>
of evaporation of water across their<br>
respiratory surfaces or across the skin<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   330</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
331<br>
<b>Regulation (Homeostasis)</b><br>
through sweat glands. Animals with fur<br>
such as cattle, dogs, and birds have limited<br>
ability to sweat; therefore, they rely on<br>
panting to increase evaporation of water<br>
across the moist surface of their tongue<br>
and mouths.<br>
<b>Another adaptation to the hot environment</b><br>
is the possession of long loops of Henle of<br>
the kidney. This enables animals to produce<br>
super-concentrated urine that helps to<br>
conserve water. This tendency is common<br>
for the desert dwellers such as kangaroo<br>
rats, and camels. Most small animals with<br>
high surface area to volume ratio usually<br>
face major difﬁculties in overcoming high<br>
temperatures. Therefore, they remain in<br>
burrows during the day when it is hot and<br>
come out at night when it is cool. This<br>
nocturnal tendency enables small animals<br>
like rodents to survive in extremely hot<br>
climates. Some mammals that live in<br>
deserts have special adaptations in their<br>
kidneys and sweat glands that allow them<br>
to survive when only very small amounts<br>
of water are available to them.<br>
<b>Regulation of body temperature in</b><br>
endotherms<br>
<b>Endotherms have developed a variety of</b><br>
adaptive mechanisms for maintaining<br>
a constant or stable body temperature.<br>
<b>The</b><br>
adaptation<br>
for<br>
balancing<br>
heat<br>
gain and heat loss may be anatomical,<br>
behavioural,<br>
or<br>
physiological.<br>
<b>The</b><br>
process of controlling body temperature<br>
in endotherms falls under two homeostatic<br>
systems, namely regulation of skin<br>
temperature and regulation of the body<br>
core temperature, which includes vital<br>
organs of the body and the brain. The<br>
regulation of skin temperature involves<br>
four major components: the set point,<br>
detector, comparator or regulator, and<br>
corrective mechanism. First, the set point<br>
is the favourable skin temperature or<br>
the temperature at which an individual<br>
feels comfortable and relaxed. Second,<br>
the detectors are the thermo-receptors<br>
in the skin containing heat and cold<br>
receptors, which are responsible for<br>
detecting increase and decrease in the<br>
skin temperatures respectively. Third,<br>
the comparator is the cortex of the brain<br>
which is responsible for controlling<br>
conscious thoughts and feelings. Thus,<br>
if the affected individual feels too cold<br>
or too hot can decide to take appropriate<br>
action, either to move to a cooler or<br>
warmer place, remove or add more<br>
clothes or take other appropriate voluntary<br>
actions that may help to bring the skin<br>
temperature back to favourable state. The<br>
error signals are sent to voluntary skeletal<br>
muscles through nerve impulses. Fourth,<br>
corrective<br>
mechanisms<br>
are<br>
initiated<br>
through behavioural responses.<br>
<b>In regulating the body’s core temperature,</b><br>
actions are involuntary, controlled by<br>
internal physiological responses. The<br>
set point of the homeostatic control<br>
mechanism is the mean body temperature<br>
(36.7 °C or 37 °C) which is genetically<br>
determined. In this system, the sensor and<br>
control centre for body temperature are<br>
located in a small part of the brain called<br>
the hypothalamus which is sensitive to<br>
temperature ﬂuctuations. It possesses two<br>
thermoreceptors, namely the heat loss and<br>
heat gain centres. The heat loss centre<br>
is located in the anterior hypothalamus<br>
and is activated by increase in blood<br>
temperature; where as heat gain centre<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   331</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
332<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
is located in the posterior hypothalamus<br>
and is activated by decrease in blood<br>
temperature.<br>
<b>On the other hand, if the body core</b><br>
temperature rises to above 40 °C, the<br>
homeostatic control mechanism breaks<br>
down and positive feedback takes place.<br>
<b>This causes a person to go into a state called</b><br>
hyperthermia. The patient experiences a<br>
very weak pulse rate, becomes excessively<br>
irrational, and sluggish. If the situation is<br>
not controlled, the patient may go into a<br>
coma and death may occur.<br>
<b>Maintenance of a constant body</b><br>
temperature in warm environments<br>
<b>When the environment is overheated,</b><br>
the animals use the following adaptive<br>
mechanisms to overcome the effects of<br>
overheating.<br>
a)<br>
<b>Vasodilation</b><br>
<b>This is an increase in the diameter of</b><br>
superﬁcial blood vessels near the body<br>
surface caused by nerve signals, resulting<br>
into the relaxation of the vessel’s walls.<br>
<b>The blood in the capillaries in the skin</b><br>
may take three alternative routes; through<br>
capillaries close to the skin surface, in<br>
the dermis, and beneath the layer of<br>
subcutaneous fat. In warm climates,<br>
superﬁcial arterioles dilate in order to<br>
allow blood flow close to the skin surface.<br>
<b>Heat from the blood is rapidly conducted</b><br>
through the epidermis to the skin surface<br>
from where it is radiated away from the<br>
body. Rise in blood pressure within the<br>
capillaries cause them to dilate, that<br>
facilitate heat loss due to radiation,<br>
convection and conduction resulting into<br>
an increase of blood flow near to the skin<br>
surface. In cold environments, the blood<br>
flow in the body escapes the skin through<br>
the shunt vessels, resulting into reduction<br>
in heat loss. Just a small amount of blood<br>
passes into the skin to keep the tissue<br>
alive.<br>
b)<br>
<b>Sweating</b><br>
<b>Human beings have the ability to control</b><br>
body<br>
temperature<br>
through<br>
sweating<br>
because their skins are not covered by<br>
fur or feathers. They have sweat glands<br>
over the whole body that enable them<br>
to be more efﬁcient at cooling through<br>
sweating. The human being can produce<br>
about 1000 ml of sweat per hour. Animals<br>
with fur have limited sweat glands which<br>
are conﬁned to areas that do not have fur,<br>
for example pads of the feet in dogs and<br>
cats. Animals with feathers such as birds<br>
lack sweat glands. Their skins are covered<br>
by feathers which prevent evaporation<br>
through the skin (evaporation occurs from<br>
the surface of their lungs and air sacs).<br>
c)<br>
<b>Panting and licking</b><br>
<b>In animals with few or no sweat glands</b><br>
such as dogs and birds, cooling by<br>
evaporation takes place through the mouth<br>
and the nose. Dogs hang out their tongues;<br>
this may result in an increase of breathing<br>
rate and excessive removal of carbon<br>
dioxide from the blood thereby reducing<br>
heat from the body. Some animals lick<br>
their bodies to deposit saliva onto their<br>
body surfaces, which provide similar<br>
means of evaporative cooling. Licking<br>
is common to some animals that do not<br>
sweat, instead they make use of saliva to<br>
cool their bodies. For example; rabbits<br>
lick their front legs and chests, cats lick<br>
inside of their front paws and spread the<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   332</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
333<br>
<b>Regulation (Homeostasis)</b><br>
saliva across their ears and face, rats lick<br>
their testicle, and kangaroos lick their fore<br>
arms and wrists.<br>
d)<br>
<b>Use of body extremities</b><br>
<b>When compared to related species from</b><br>
cold climates, animals in warm climates<br>
usually have large extremities such as<br>
ears and large bushy tail. They are well<br>
supplied with blood vessels and covered<br>
by relatively short hairs, making them<br>
good radiators of heat.<br>
e)	 Large surface area to volume ratio<br>
<b>Animals with a large surface area to</b><br>
volume ratio (relatively small animals)<br>
lose energy (temperature) faster than those<br>
with smaller surface to volume ratio. To<br>
compensate for this, small animals such<br>
as mice feed more frequently compared<br>
to large animals such as lions. The former<br>
animals also tend to utilize an energy -<br>
rich diet such as nuts rich in lipids.<br>
f)<br>
<b>Behavioural mechanisms</b><br>
<b>Many desert animals regulate their</b><br>
body temperature by using different<br>
behavioural mechanisms. For example,<br>
some animals such as mice and hedgehogs<br>
avoid heat by sheltering under the rocks<br>
or by burrowing in the soil during drought<br>
season. Others, like bat avoid hottest<br>
periods by being nocturnal, a tendency of<br>
being active during the night and inactive<br>
during the day. This minimises the rate<br>
of body metabolic activities during the<br>
day and increases at night. Some animals<br>
like rodent, ground squirrels and bears<br>
hibernate<br>
during<br>
cold<br>
temperatures.<br>
<b>Hibernation is the behavioural state where</b><br>
by an animal becomes inactive, both<br>
during the day and at night. This tendency<br>
usually takes place during winter season,<br>
which sometimes is referred to as winter<br>
dormancy.<br>
<b>Winter dormancy is associated with</b><br>
reduced body metabolic rate, low body<br>
temperatures, slow breathing and low<br>
heartbeat rate. The animal appears to be<br>
in a deep sleep for several days, weeks<br>
or months depending on the species.<br>
<b>This helps to conserve energy, especially</b><br>
during winter when food is scarce. Before<br>
entering hibernation, most animals eat<br>
a large amount of food and store energy<br>
in fat deposits to survive the winter.<br>
<b>Hibernation can also take place during</b><br>
the hottest period or summer. This state is<br>
called aestivation or summer dormancy.<br>
<b>Some animal species that aestivate</b><br>
include reptiles. The arousal of an animal<br>
from hibernation state usually occurs<br>
spontaneously. It is driven by some internal<br>
mechanisms. External stimuli often fail<br>
to wake the hibernating or aestivating<br>
animal, making it subject to predation if it<br>
is discovered by its enemies.<br>
g)<br>
<b>Insulation</b><br>
<b>This is provided by a layer of fur or fat that</b><br>
protects or blubs the body against changes<br>
in the environmental temperature. The<br>
thick coat of fur is called insulator while<br>
the thick layer of fat is called blubber.<br>
<b>The insulator or blubber layer helps</b><br>
retain body heat and keeps the animal’s<br>
body temperature constant. Animals<br>
in the warm climates usually have fur<br>
with light colours to enhance sun rays’<br>
reﬂection and thereby minimising heat<br>
gain and accelerating heat loss from the<br>
body. During this process, the hair erector<br>
muscles in the skin are relaxed and the fur<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   333</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
334<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
lies close to the skin surface. This causes<br>
the thickness of insulating warm hair to<br>
be reduced, and therefore, the body heat is<br>
readily dispersed.<br>
<b>Maintenance of a constant body</b><br>
temperature in cold climates<br>
<b>Endotherms living in cold environment</b><br>
and those in hot climates experiencing cold<br>
weather have the following adaptations<br>
which enable them to maintain constant<br>
body temperatures:<br>
a)	 Vasoconstriction<br>
<b>When the animal is subjected to cold</b><br>
conditions, the superﬁcial arterioles are<br>
constricted. This reduces the quantity of<br>
blood reaching the skin surface. Much<br>
blood passes beneath the insulating layer<br>
of subcutaneous fat; therefore, little heat<br>
is lost to the outside.<br>
b)	Shivering<br>
<b>In cold conditions, the skeletal muscles</b><br>
of the body may undergo rhythmic<br>
involuntary contractions which increase<br>
the amount of heat produced in the body.<br>
c)	 Insulation<br>
<b>It is achieved by an external covering of</b><br>
fur or feathers and or an internal layer<br>
of subcutaneous fat. Their thickness is<br>
related to the intensity of coldness to that<br>
environment. It is an effective means of<br>
reducing heat loss from the body.<br>
d)	Increased metabolic rate<br>
<b>During cold conditions, the liver increases</b><br>
its metabolic rate. There is also increased<br>
activity of the adrenal, thyroid, and<br>
pituitary glands resulting into secretion of<br>
hormones that help to increase the body<br>
metabolic rate; hence, additional heat<br>
is produced in the body. The increased<br>
metabolic rate of the body requires<br>
increased food consumption; that is why<br>
animals feed on large amount of food in<br>
cold climates.<br>
<b>Temperature regulation by ectotherms</b><br>
<b>Ectotherms</b><br>
regulate<br>
their<br>
body<br>
temperature  mainly by behavioural means,<br>
depending on external heat sources; since<br>
these organisms do not have temperature<br>
control center like endotherms. The<br>
exchange is controlled by three factors<br>
which are; radiation, conduction and<br>
flow. Their body temperature rises and<br>
falls along with the temperature of the<br>
surrounding environment. Although they<br>
generate metabolic heat like endotherms,<br>
they cannot increase heat production to<br>
maintain an internal body temperature.<br>
<b>Most of the adjustment mechanisms are</b><br>
by behavioural means such as: huddling,<br>
hibernation,<br>
burrowing,<br>
aestivation,<br>
clustering,<br>
migration,<br>
and<br>
exposing<br>
themselves to the sun in hot environment.<br>
a)	 Huddling<br>
<b>In cold regions, animals are usually active</b><br>
during the day. Huddling of individuals<br>
is also another common way of reducing<br>
heat loss. Some animals are able to crowd<br>
together in a tightly packed group to keep<br>
them warm and reduce much heat loss<br>
when an individual animal is exposed<br>
to cold open air.Therefore, this is also a<br>
means of conserving heat.<br>
b)	 Hibernation<br>
<b>Some animals in cold climates undergo</b><br>
a period of long sleep. During this time,<br>
the metabolic rate is reduced 20-100<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   334</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
335<br>
<b>Regulation (Homeostasis)</b><br>
times below normal which consequently<br>
reduces food and oxygen utilisation.<br>
c)	 Sun basking<br>
<b>Ectothermic animals may use radiant heat</b><br>
provided by the environment to warm<br>
their bodies. Solar radiation is the most<br>
common way, as many ectotherms use<br>
the sun’s rays to warm up their bodies.<br>
<b>Reptiles and some amphibians bask in the</b><br>
sun with their bodies spread out to increase<br>
the surface area for heat absorption. When<br>
it is too hot, they hide in the shade or near<br>
water bodies; allowing their bodies to<br>
cool.<br>
d)	 Clustering<br>
<b>Some animals exhibit group behavioral</b><br>
mechanisms to regulate their body<br>
temperatures. A good example is how<br>
honey bees cuddle together in large<br>
groups to retain and generate heat. A<br>
similar example is how some gregarious<br>
caterpillars bask in the sun in large groups<br>
to gather heat.<br>
e)	 Burrowing<br>
<b>Some ectotherms burrow themselves and</b><br>
hide deep in the ground. This helps them<br>
to survive in cold environments.<br>
f)	 Aestivation<br>
<b>Some ectothermic animals like earthworms,</b><br>
snails, frogs, crocodiles, lizards, and tortoise<br>
maintain their body temperature by<br>
reducing body metabolic activities and<br>
protecting themselves from very high<br>
temperature. During summer time, some<br>
animals usually tend to rest in shady or<br>
cool places. Normally, they take a sleep<br>
during the hot hours of daytime as a means<br>
of avoiding environmental stress.<br>
<b>Some ectotherms have developed some</b><br>
chemical processes to survive the cold.<br>
<b>These animals, such as chorus frogs,</b><br>
and gray tree frogs endure the cold by<br>
undergoing chemical changes to prevent<br>
their tissues from freezing. Other animals<br>
such as wood frog can tolerate and regulate<br>
a frozen state by changing the chemical<br>
composition of their blood to a sugary state<br>
that helps them avoid freezing.<br>

1.	 Explain<br>
the<br>
mechanisms<br>
of<br>
temperature regulation in endothermic<br>
organism.<br>
2.	 Show the role of the hypothalamus in<br>
temperature regulation.<br>
3.	 Describe the adaptations of mammal<br>
to cold and hot climatic conditions.<br>
<b>7.3  Excretion</b><br>
<b>The maintenance of homeostasis in</b><br>
animals<br>
involves<br>
osmoregulation<br>
or<br>
balancing the levels of water and salt in<br>
the body. It also involves the removal of<br>
metabolic wastes from the body through<br>
excretion; the process by which metabolic<br>
waste is eliminated from an organism’s<br>
body. In vertebrates, this is primarily<br>
carried out by the lungs, kidneys and skin.<br>
<b>Signiﬁcance of excretion</b><br>
<b>It is important that living organisms must</b><br>
get rid of excretory wastes from their<br>
bodies, because the removal of wastes<br>
prevents unbalanced body’s chemical<br>
equilibria. Moreover, it is a means for<br>
removal of toxic wastes which, if allowed<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   335</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
336<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
to accumulate, inhibit action of many<br>
enzymes involved in metabolic pathways.<br>
<b>Consequently, this will lead to failure in</b><br>
many physiological processes. Excretion<br>
also helps to regulate water content of the<br>
body ﬂuids. Excretory nitrogenous wastes<br>
are removed from the bodies of living<br>
organisms in a form which is determined<br>
by the availability of water. The pH of<br>
blood is regulated by excretion. For<br>
example, organisms tend to excrete ions<br>
such as hydrogen ions (H+) and hydrogen<br>
carbonate ions (HCO3<br>
-) which have major<br>
inﬂuence on pH.<br>
<b>Major excretory products in animals</b><br>
<b>The major excretory products in animals</b><br>
are nitrogenous compounds such as urea,<br>
ammonia, and uric acid from breakdown<br>
of proteins and nucleic acids, carbon<br>
dioxide from cellular respiration and bile<br>
pigments from breaking down of worn out<br>
red blood cells in the liver.<br>
<b>Nitrogenous waste products</b><br>
<b>Breakdown</b><br>
of<br>
nitrogen<br>
containing<br>
molecules such as amino acids results in<br>
excess nitrogen that must be removed from<br>
the body. When amino acids are broken<br>
down by the body to generate energy or<br>
converted into fats or carbohydrates, the<br>
amino (NH2) group must be removed<br>
because they are not needed, and they<br>
may be toxic. This excess nitrogen may<br>
be excreted in the form of ammonia, urea,<br>
or uric acid.<br>
<b>Ammonia. Ammonia is quite toxic</b><br>
and highly soluble; hence it can be a<br>
nitrogenous excretory product if sufﬁcient<br>
water is available to wash it from the body.<br>
<b>It is excreted by most ﬁsh and other aquatic</b><br>
animals whose gills or body surface are in<br>
direct contact with water. These animals<br>
are called ammonotelic.<br>
Urea.<br>
<b>Animals</b><br>
like<br>
sharks,<br>
adult<br>
amphibians,<br>
and<br>
mammals<br>
usually<br>
excrete urea as their nitrogenous waste.<br>
<b>Urea is a much less toxic, and less soluble</b><br>
than ammonia. It can be excreted in a<br>
moderately concentrated solution. This<br>
elimination strategy allows body water<br>
to be conserved, an important advantage<br>
for terrestrial animals with limited access<br>
to water. The animals in this group are<br>
termed as ureotelic.<br>
<b>Uric acid. Uric acid is not toxic and it is</b><br>
insoluble in water. Poor solubility is an<br>
advantage if water conservation is needed.<br>
<b>Uric acid can be concentrated even more</b><br>
readily than urea can. Uric acid is usually<br>
excreted by insects, reptiles, and birds,<br>
these are collectively termed as uricotelic<br>
animals.<br>
<b>Kidney position, structure and</b><br>
functions in human body<br>
<b>Kidneys are paired bean-shaped organs</b><br>
found on each side of the back of the lower<br>
portion of the abdominal cavity (Figure<br>
7.5). The larger left kidney is located a bit<br>
higher than the right kidney. Unlike other<br>
organs found in the abdomen, kidneys are<br>
located behind the lining (peritoneum)<br>
of the abdominal cavity. Thus, they are<br>
considered retroperitoneal organs. These<br>
bean-shaped organs are protected by the<br>
back muscles and the ribs as well as the fat<br>
(adipose tissue) that surrounds them like<br>
a protective padding. Located above each<br>
kidney is an adrenal gland which secretes<br>
adrenaline hormone. For each kidney, the<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
337<br>
<b>Regulation (Homeostasis)</b><br>
renal artery supplies blood which a renal<br>
vein returns to the vena cava. The ureter<br>
removes urine produced by the kidney,<br>
transfering it to the urinary bladder for<br>
temporary storage, before being released<br>
through the urethra. The exit from the<br>
bladder is controlled by a sphincter (a ring<br>
of muscles).<br>
<b>The bean-shaped kidneys have an outer</b><br>
convex side and an inner concave side<br>
called the renal hilus. A transverse section<br>
(T.S) of the kidney reveals an outer part<br>
called cortex and an inner part called<br>
medulla. A thin connective tissue called<br>
fibrous capsule surrounds each kidney.<br>
<b>This capsule maintains the kidneys’ shape</b><br>
and protects the inner tissues. The cortex<br>
contains parts of the nephrons, glomeruli<br>
and capsule, while the medulla contains<br>
tubular part of the nephrons and blood<br>
vessels, forming the renal pyramids<br>
which project into the pelvis, leading to<br>
the ureter. Kidneys are well supplied by<br>
blood vessels, forming a network of blood<br>
capillaries (Figure 7.6).<br>

<b>General functions of the kidneys</b><br>
<b>Kidneys are multifunctional organs, some</b><br>
core functions of the kidneys include:<br>
a)	 Excretion<br>
<b>Kidneys ﬁlter out toxins, excess salts,</b><br>
and nitrogenous wastes created by cell<br>
metabolisms. Urea is synthesised in the<br>
liver and transported through the blood to<br>
the kidneys for removal in urine.<br>
b)	 Water balance<br>
<b>As kidneys are key in the chemical</b><br>
breakdown of urine, they react to changes<br>
in the body’s water level every minute. As<br>
water intake decreases, the kidneys adjust<br>
accordingly and leave water in the body<br>
instead of helping to excrete it.<br>
c)	 Blood pressure regulation<br>
<b>The kidneys need constant pressure to</b><br>
ﬁlter the blood. When it drops too low, the<br>
kidneys increase the pressure. One way is<br>
by producing a blood vessel constricting<br>
protein, angiotensin, which also signals<br>
the body to retain sodium and water. Both<br>
constriction and retention help restore<br>
normal blood pressure.<br>
d)	 Red blood cell regulation<br>
<b>When the kidneys do not get enough</b><br>
oxygen, they send out a distress call in<br>
the form of erythropoietin; a hormone that<br>
stimulates the bone marrow to produce<br>
more red blood cells. This process is<br>
called erythropoiesis.<br>

Table 7.1 Summary of parts of the kidney and their functions<br>
<b>Parts of the kidney</b><br>
<b>Description</b><br>
<b>Renal hilus</b><br>
An indentation near the centre of the concavity of the kidney where the<br>
renal vein and ureter leave the kidney and the renal artery enters the<br>
kidney.<br>
<b>Renal capsule</b><br>
A tough, fibrous membrane, surrounding the kidney. It consists of<br>
dense, irregular connective tissues which protect and help to maintain<br>
the kidney’s shape. It is also surrounded by fatty tissue which helps to<br>
protect the kidney from damage.<br>
<b>Renal cortex</b><br>
The outer reddish part of the kidney that has a smooth texture; it is<br>
where the Bowman’s capsule, glomeruli, proximal and distal convoluted<br>
tubules and blood vessels are found.<br>
<b>Renal medulla</b><br>
The inner striated red-brown part of the kidney.<br>
<b>Renal pyramids</b><br>
Stripped, and triangular in structure within the medulla, which are made<br>
of straight tubules and corresponding blood vessels.<br>
<b>Renal pelvis</b><br>
The funnel-shaped cavity that receives urine drained from the nephrons<br>
through the collecting ducts and papillary ducts.<br>
<b>Renal artery</b><br>
The blood vessel that delivers oxygen- rich blood to the kidney; it<br>
enters the kidney through the hilus and divides into smaller arteries,<br>
which separate into afferent arterioles that serve each of the nephrons.<br>
<b>Renal vein</b><br>
The blood vessel that receives deoxygenated blood from the kidney<br>
and returns it to the systemic circulation.<br>
<b>Afferent arteriole</b><br>
The blood vessel that delivers oxygen-rich blood to the glomerulus<br>
under high pressure.<br>
<b>Efferent arteriole</b><br>
The blood vessel that receives oxygenated blood from the glomerulus.<br>
<b>Kidney nephrons</b><br>
The functional units where the kidney’s main functions are performed:<br>
There are about a million nephrons in each kidney.<br>
<b>Collecting duct</b><br>
This part of the kidney nephron collects urine and drains into papillary<br>
ducts, minor calyx, and major calyx, and ﬁnally into the ureter and<br>
urinary bladder.<br>
<b>Ureter</b><br>
The tubular structure which conveys urine from the pelvis of the<br>
kidney to the urinary bladder.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   339</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
340<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
e)	 Regulation of pH of the blood<br>
<b>Kidneys excrete hydrogen ions into</b><br>
urine. At the same time, they conserve<br>
bicarbonate ions which are an important<br>
buffer of hydrogen ions.<br>
f)	 Regulation of the ionic composition<br>
of  blood<br>
<b>Kidneys regulate the quantities of ions</b><br>
in the blood. Important examples of<br>
ions whose quantities are regulated by<br>
the kidneys include sodium, potassium,<br>
calcium, chloride and phosphate ions.<br>
g)	 Synthesis of vitamin D<br>
<b>Kidneys are involved in the synthesis</b><br>
of calciferol, which is an active form of<br>
vitamin D.<br>
<b>The nephron</b><br>
<b>The nephron is the kidney’s functional unit</b><br>
that is involved in production of urine in<br>
the process of removing waste and excess<br>
substances from the blood. Generally<br>
nephron is responsible for the filtration,<br>
excretion and re-absorption of most of the<br>
water and other materials. Each kidney<br>
has more than a million nephrons in the<br>
renal cortex, which gives it a granular<br>
appearance on sagittal section (Figure<br>
7.7). Nephrons are used to separate water,<br>
ions and small molecules from the blood<br>
molecule, and filter out wastes and toxic<br>
materials, then it selectively returns the<br>
needed molecules to the blood. The most<br>
primitive nephrons (pronephros) are found<br>
in the kidneys of primitive fish, amphibian<br>
larvae, and embryos of more advanced<br>
vertebrates. The nephrons found in the<br>
kidneys of amphibians and most fish,<br>
and in the late embryonic development<br>
of more advanced vertebrates, are only<br>
slightly more advanced in structure<br>
(mesonephros).<br>
<b>The</b><br>
most<br>
advanced<br>
nephrons occur in the adult kidneys, or<br>
metanephros of land vertebrates, such as<br>
reptiles, birds, and mammals.<br>
<b>There are two types of nephrons, namely</b><br>
cortical nephrons, (which are found deep<br>
in the renal cortex) and the juxtamedullary<br>
nephrons, which make up about 15<br>
percent of total nephrons and lie close to<br>
the medulla. The nephrons consist of a<br>
renal corpuscle, a tubule, and a capillary<br>
network. These originate from the small<br>
cortical arteries. Each renal corpuscle is<br>
composed of a glomerulus (a network of<br>
capillaries) and a Bowman’s capsule (the<br>
cup- shaped chamber that surrounds it).<br>
<b>The Bowman’s capsule connects to a long</b><br>
convoluted renal tubule which is divided<br>
into three functional parts. These consist<br>
of the proximal convoluted tubule, the<br>
loop of Henle (nephritic loop) with its<br>
descending and ascending limbs, and the<br>
distal convoluted tubule, which empties<br>
into the collecting ducts.<br>

<b>The collecting ducts fuse together and</b><br>
enter the papillae of the renal medulla.<br>
<b>Urine passes through the renal medulla</b><br>
as a ﬂuid with high sodium content and<br>
leaves through the renal papillae, into the<br>
renal calyces, the renal pelvis, and the<br>
bladder through the ureter.<br>
<b>The outer layer of the Bowman’s capsule</b><br>
is composed of squamous epithelial cells.<br>
<b>The inner layer is composed of specialised</b><br>
cells that allow easy passage of molecules,<br>
and the glomerulus consists of endothelial<br>
cells with fenestrae (pores). The renal<br>
corpuscle acts as an ultra-ﬁltration unit,<br>
ﬁltering the blood and separating the<br>
larger particles (which stay in the blood<br>
vessels) from the small ones (which pass<br>
into the renal tubule). Therefore, it is a<br>
site for ultra-ﬁltration; that is pressure<br>
filtration. This pressure is known as<br>
hydrostatic pressure, and it stems from<br>
blood pressure. Thus, the blood brought<br>
into the glomerular capillaries by the<br>
afferent arteriole is pumped by the heart<br>
at high pressure; which rises as the blood<br>
leaves the wide arteriole and enters the<br>
narrow capillaries.<br>
<b>The structures of the glomerulus and</b><br>
<b>Bowman’s capsule is adapted for ﬁltration</b><br>
in that, endothelial layers of glomerulus<br>
are very thin and have many pores to<br>
allow the passage of a glomerular ﬁltrate.<br>
<b>A network of ﬁbres such as collagen</b><br>

ﬁbres, with spaces between them, allows<br>
the passage of small solute molecules in<br>
the ﬁltrate. They restrict the passage of<br>
blood cells that are larger than the pores.<br>
<b>Large molecules such as proteins are also</b><br>
restricted from passing through and are<br>
repelled by the negatively charged ﬁbres.<br>
<b>Moreover, the epithelial layer of the</b><br>
<b>Bowman’s capsule contains podocytes,</b><br>
the highly modiﬁed ﬁltration cells. These<br>
have extensions which interact to form<br>
ﬁltration slits or pores to allow the passage<br>
of the glomerular ﬁltrate.<br>
<b>The proximal convoluted tubule are mainly</b><br>
concerned with selective reabsorption so<br>
that valuable substances such as glucose<br>
are taken back to the blood but not lost<br>
in the urine. The cells of the proximal<br>
convoluted tubule are adapted for re-<br>
absorption as follows:<br>
a)	 They have numerous microvilli and<br>
basal channels which increase the<br>
surface area for the absorption process.<br>
b)	 They have numerous mitochondria<br>
to supply energy which is constantly<br>
needed for active uptake of substances<br>
from the ﬁltrate.<br>
c)	 Their basement membranes are very<br>
close to the endothelial lining of<br>
blood capillaries to ensure fast uptake<br>
and efﬁcient transportation of the<br>
reabsorbed substances back into the<br>
body.<br>
d)	 Channel proteins are present for<br>
transportation<br>
of<br>
substances<br>
like<br>
amino acids and ions from the cells<br>
of the proximal convoluted tubule, to<br>
the spaces between these cells and the<br>
basal channels.<br>
e)	 At the base of the microvilli, small<br>
proteins from the renal ﬂuid are<br>
removed by the process of pinocytosis.<br>
<b>The proteins are then enclosed in</b><br>
pinocytic vesicles and are ﬁnally<br>
broken down by hydrolytic enzymes<br>
from the lysosomes (Figure 7.8).<br>

<b>Regulation (Homeostasis)</b><br>
<b>The loop of Henle acts as a counter current</b><br>
exchange mechanism creating a low water<br>
potential (high solute content) in the<br>
medulla of the kidney so that water can be<br>
reabsorbed by osmosis. The descending<br>
limb has a thin membrane which is freely<br>
permeable to water and impermeable<br>
to salts and urea. The thick ascending<br>
limb has numerous mitochondria which<br>
provide energy for active uptake of sodium<br>
chloride and other ions from the renal<br>
ﬂuids back into the interstitial regions of<br>
the medulla.<br>
<b>The distal convoluted tubule and collecting</b><br>
ducts are concerned with osmoregulation;<br>
varying the amount of water reabsorbed<br>
into the blood. The cells of the distal<br>
convoluted tubule have a similar structure<br>
to those of the proximal convoluted tubule,<br>
with microvilli lining the inner surfaces to<br>
increase the surface area for absorption,<br>
and numerous mitochondria to supply<br>
energy for active transport.<br>
<b>Formation of urea in mammals</b><br>
<b>Urea is the nitrogenous waste product of</b><br>
humans and other land living mammals.<br>
<b>The body is unable to store excess</b><br>
amino acids taken in the diet. Those not<br>
immediately needed for protein synthesis<br>
or making sugar must be removed by<br>
the process called deamination, which is<br>
followed by urea formation in the liver<br>
cells. The process of urea formation<br>
occurs in the urea cycle which is also<br>
called ornithine cycle (Figure 7.9) and<br>
involves the following stages:<br>
a)	 Formation of carbamoyl phosphate<br>
<b>Before the cycle, ammonia (NH3) from</b><br>
metabolism<br>
of<br>
nitrogen<br>
containing<br>
compounds combine with carbon dioxide<br>
(CO2) gas from respiration in a solution<br>
form (ammonium ions and bicarbonate<br>
ions respectively) resulting into the<br>
formation of carbamoyl phosphate, by the<br>
help of the enzyme carbamoyl phosphate<br>
synthetase-I. The reaction occurs in<br>
the mitochondria of the liver cells, and<br>
requires 2 ATP molecules.<br>
b)	 Synthesis of citrulline<br>
<b>The carbamoyl phosphate formed in</b><br>
the ﬁrst step enters the ornithine cycle<br>
and combine with ornithine resulting in<br>
the synthesis of citrulline, aided by an<br>
enzyme citrulline synthase or ornithine<br>
transcarbamoylase. In the reaction the<br>
phosphate group is released. Citrulline<br>
can easily pass through the mitochondrial<br>
membrane, thus it diffuses from the<br>
mitochondrion into cytosol (cytoplasm)<br>
of liver cells.<br>
c)	 Synthesis of argininosuccinate<br>
<b>In the cytosol, citrulline combines with</b><br>
the amino group of aspartate under<br>
condensation<br>
reaction<br>
catalyzed<br>
by<br>
enzyme<br>
argininosuccinate<br>
synthetase<br>
to form argininosuccinate. It requires<br>
<b>ATP which is hydrolysed to adenosine</b><br>
monophosphate (AMP) resulting in the<br>
utilization of two high energy bonds.<br>
Magnesium ions (Mg2+) act as cofactors.<br>
<b>This reaction incorporates the second</b><br>
nitrogen from aspartate.<br>
d)	 Cleavage of argininosuccinate<br>
<b>The cleavage of arginisuccinate involves</b><br>
the<br>
enzyme<br>
argininosuccinase,<br>
an<br>
intermediate enzyme in the urea synthesis<br>
pathway whose function is imperative<br>
to the continuation of the cycle. It acts<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   343</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
344<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
reversibly to cleave argininosuccinate into<br>
a free arginine and fumarate. The arginine<br>
continues with the cycle in the other stage,<br>
whereas fumarate enters the Tricarboxylic<br>
<b>Acid (TCA) cycle, which is also known</b><br>
as Kreb’s cycle. The linkage between<br>
<b>TCA cycle and urea cycle is known as the</b><br>
Kreb’s bi- cycle.<br>
e)	 Cleavage of arginine<br>
<b>Arginine is hydrolysed into ornithine and</b><br>
urea under the inﬂuence of the enzyme<br>
arginase; hence, arginine is known as a<br>
semi-essential amino acid.  Though it is<br>
synthesised in the body, it is not available<br>
for<br>
protein<br>
synthesis.<br>
<b>Ornithine</b><br>
is<br>
regenerated in this step and the urea cycle<br>
completes by the formation of urea. The<br>
ornithine produced is transported back to<br>
the mitochondria to start the cycle again<br>
while urea is transported to the kidney<br>
through blood vessels to be excreted.<br>
<b>Thus the urea cycle brings two amino</b><br>
groups (NH2) and hydrogen carbonate ions<br>
<b>(HCO3</b><br>
-) together to form urea.<br>

<b>Urinary disorders in human</b><br>
<b>The urinary tract consists of the kidneys,</b><br>
ureters, bladder and urethra. Urinary<br>
disorders include any infections or<br>
conditions that affect any of these parts<br>
of the urinary system or their functions.<br>
<b>The following are some of the common</b><br>
urinary disorders in human beings:<br>
a)	 Uremia<br>
<b>This condition results from toxic effects</b><br>
of abnormally high concentrations of urea<br>
in the blood resulting into kidneys’ failure<br>
to expel it via urine. The end products<br>
of protein metabolism accumulate in<br>
the blood but are normally ﬁltered out<br>
when the blood passes through the<br>
kidneys. However, urea accumulation<br>
in the blood is comparatively high in<br>
uremia victims. In such patients, urea<br>
can be removed by the process called<br>
haemodialysis. However, there are still<br>
some complications that affect people on<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   344</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
345<br>
<b>Regulation (Homeostasis)</b><br>
dialysis such as cardiovascular problems<br>
and severe itching from the imbalance of<br>
minerals in the body.<br>
b)	 Renal failure (RF) or kidney failure<br>
<b>Renal failure is a decrease or cessation</b><br>
of glomerular ﬁltration in humans (it<br>
is a partial or complete loss of kidney<br>
function). Kidney failure can be either<br>
acute or chronic. In acute renal failure<br>
(ARF), both kidneys abruptly stop<br>
working. The main feature of ARF is<br>
either oligouria (scanty urine production)<br>
in which the daily urine output is less<br>
than 250 ml or anuria in which daily urine<br>
output is less than 50 ml. The causes of<br>
renal failure may be low blood volume<br>
(atherosclerosis),<br>
decreased<br>
cardiac<br>
output, damaged renal tubules, kidney<br>
stones, nephritis, and some excessive use<br>
of antibiotics. The effect of renal failure<br>
includes oedema, that is accumulation<br>
of excess ﬂuids in various parts of the<br>
body which results into swelling of legs.<br>
<b>Also, the potassium level rises, leading to</b><br>
failure to produce enough erythropoietin<br>
for adequate red blood cells production,<br>
hence anaemia.<br>
c)	 Kidney stones (renal calculi)<br>
<b>Concentration of mineral and organic</b><br>
matter that forms in the kidneys results<br>
into small particles called stones. These<br>
may become too large, hence impairing<br>
the normal renal function. Urine contains<br>
many salts in solution. If the concentration<br>
of these mineral salts becomes excessive,<br>
the excess salts precipitate as crystals<br>
that may enlarge to become visible solid<br>
particles called stones. Such stones give<br>
rise to severe colic pain starting in the<br>
back and radiating down to the front of<br>
the thigh or the testicle or vulva on that<br>
side. The best way to prevent most kidney<br>
stones is to drink enough fluids every day.<br>
d)	 Urinary tract infections (UTI)<br>
<b>In humans, UTI is caused by the invasion</b><br>
of microorganisms, usually bacteria,<br>
into the urethra and bladder. The most<br>
common UTI cases that affect the bladder<br>
and urethra are:<br>
<b>Infection of the bladder (cystitis). This</b><br>
type of UTI is usually caused by bacteria,<br>
normally Escherichia coli (E. coli); a<br>
type of bacteria commonly found in the<br>
gastrointestinal tract (GIT). All women<br>
are at risk of cystitis because of their<br>
anatomy, specifically the short distance<br>
from the urethra to the anus and the<br>
urethral opening to the bladder.<br>
<b>Infection of the urethra (urethritis). This</b><br>
type of UTI can occur when GIT bacteria<br>
spread from the anus to the urethra. Also,<br>
because the female urethra is close to the<br>
vagina, sexually transmitted infections<br>
such as herpes, gonorrhea, chlamydia and<br>
mycoplasma can cause urethritis.<br>
<b>The infection of the urinary tract can</b><br>
result into either minor or major illnesses.<br>
<b>For example, an attack of cystitis-</b><br>
inﬂammation of the bladder may cause<br>
only minor illness. The attack of the renal<br>
system is characterised by frequent and<br>
painful urination. Other complications<br>
involve: recurrent infections; especially<br>
in women who experience twice or more<br>
<b>UTI cases in a six-month period within a</b><br>
year and permanent kidney damage from<br>
twice acute or chronic kidney infection<br>
(pyelonephritis) due to an untreated UTI.<br>

10/10/2019   14:08<br>

<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>

<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Likewise, pregnant women get a risk of</b><br>
delivering low birth weight or premature<br>
infants, whereas men experience urethral<br>
narrowing (stricture).<br>
<b>The preventive measures include: drinking</b><br>
plenty of liquids especially water so as to<br>
dilute urine and ensure frequent urination<br>
hence, allowing bacteria to be flushed from<br>
the urinary tract. Also, women are advised<br>
to wipe from front to back after urinating<br>
and after defecating so as to prevent the<br>
spread of bacteria from anal region to<br>
the vagina and urethra. Furthermore, a<br>
patient should seek medical advice from a<br>
recognised health centre or hospital.<br>
e)	 Urinary tract obstruction<br>
<b>This is due to blockage or constriction at</b><br>
any point in the urinary tract. This impedes<br>
the normal ﬂow of urine and causes urine<br>
to be retained in the bladder or kidneys.<br>
<b>Obstruction causes urine to become</b><br>
blocked up into the kidneys, the condition<br>
is known as hydronephrosis. Obstructions<br>
in the urinary tract causes distension of the<br>
walls of the bladder, ureter, urethra, and<br>
kidneys. This condition may stem from<br>
<b>Sexually Transmitted Diseases (STDs)</b><br>
such as syphilis and gonorrhoea. Its<br>
preventive measures include prevention<br>
against STDs and seeking for medical<br>
assistance whenever sensing signs of<br>
STDs.<br>
f)	 Diabetes insipidus (DI)<br>
<b>The term diabetes insipidus is made up</b><br>
of two words; diabetes, which means<br>
‘overﬂow’, and insipidus which means<br>
‘tasteless’. From this basic implication,<br>
diabetes<br>
insipidus<br>
means<br>
excessive<br>
production of very dilute urine. It is caused<br>
by deﬁciency of vasopressin hormone<br>
which is released by the posterior lobe<br>
of the pituitary gland. The vasopressin or<br>
antidiuretic hormone (ADH) facilitates<br>
reabsorption of water by the distal<br>
convoluted tubule and the collecting duct<br>
of the nephrons. Therefore, lack of this<br>
hormone implies that much water remains<br>
in renal ﬂuid; therefore, it is lost through<br>
urine. The symptoms of diabetes insipidus<br>
include excessive dilute urine, intense<br>
thirst, and tiredness. Regular physical<br>
exercises and healthy eating may prevent<br>
an individual from getting diabetes.<br>
<b>Diabetic patients are advised to go to</b><br>
the health centre or hospital for medical<br>
advice and proper treatment.<br>

<b>Regulation (Homeostasis)</b><br>
<b>7.4  Osmoregulation</b><br>
<b>In most vertebrates, kidneys are the</b><br>
most important organs involved in<br>
osmoregulation. The kidneys perform<br>
several functions critical to homeostasis.<br>
<b>Such functions include maintaining the</b><br>
balance between water and various types<br>
of salts. This is important because ions<br>
such as Na+, Ca2+, and K+ greatly affect<br>
the functioning of the body systems such<br>
as the skeletal, nervous and muscular<br>
systems. The kidneys produce urine; a<br>
liquid that contains a number of different<br>
metabolic wastes. The concentration<br>
of urine produced by an animal varies<br>
depending on the environment as well<br>
as on the factors, such as water and<br>
salt intake. The process of maintaining<br>
constant body’s osmotic condition is<br>
called osmoregulation. It is concerned<br>
with the regulation of water and solute<br>
concentration of the body ﬂuids.<br>
<b>Osmoregulation in marine</b><br>
elasmobranches<br>
<b>Marine elasmobranches are cartilaginous</b><br>
ﬁsh such as sharks, rays, and skates. They<br>
live in sea water whose salt concentrations<br>
are higher than those of their body ﬂuids.<br>
<b>Due to this difference in concentrations,</b><br>
the ﬁshes tend to lose water from their<br>
bodies into the sea. To overcome this<br>
problem, the marine elasmobranches have<br>
developed mechanisms of making their<br>
body ﬂuids less hypotonic to sea water.<br>
<b>Because of this, the animals face another</b><br>
problem of a natural and continuous<br>
diffusion of water into their bodies from<br>
their surrounding sea water. To overcome<br>
these problems and to make their body<br>
ﬂuids isotonic to sea water, such ﬁshes<br>
have developed the following adaptations:<br>
a)	 They have rectal glands which secrete<br>
salts to increase their osmotic pressure.<br>
<b>This mechanism aims at balancing the</b><br>
internal osmotic pressure to that of the<br>
surrounding sea water.<br>
b)	 They<br>
retain<br>
nitrogenous<br>
waste<br>
chemicals,<br>
such<br>
as<br>
urea,<br>
and<br>
trimethylamine oxide (TMAO) in<br>
their body cells. These chemicals are<br>
kept in high concentrations, and they<br>
change the diffusion gradient enabling<br>
a ﬁsh to absorb water instead of<br>
ingesting it. Despite the fact that these<br>
are waste products and may be harmful<br>
to the animals at high concentrations,<br>
the marine elasmobranches have<br>
been able to produce and retain urea<br>
because their gills are impermeable to<br>
it. Their renal tubules in the kidneys<br>
are capable of reabsorbing urea from<br>
the renal ﬂuid back to body cells. In<br>
addition, their cells are immune to the<br>
effects of high concentrations of urea.<br>
<b>Osmoregulation in mammals</b><br>
<b>An important evolutionary adaptation</b><br>
that allowed animals to survive on land<br>
was the development of a kidney that<br>
would produce concentrated (hypertonic)<br>
urine. The need for water conservation<br>
is particularly well illustrated in desert<br>
mammals such as the kangaroo rat. A<br>
major adaptation that allows the kangaroo<br>
rat to conserve water is the ability to form<br>
very hypertonic urine twenty times more<br>
concentrated than its blood plasma. The<br>
kidneys of the Kangaroo rat are able to<br>
accomplish this because the loop of Henle<br>
of their nephrons is much longer and more<br>
efﬁcient than that of most other mammals.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   347</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
348<br>
<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>Terrestrial mammals need to drink water</b><br>
at least occasionally to compensate for the<br>
water lost from the skin and respiratory<br>
passages and through urination.<br>
<b>Counter current multiplier</b><br>
<b>In the loop of Henle, a counter current</b><br>
exchange mechanism is combined with<br>
the active secretion of solutes. A system<br>
that uses this combined type of exchange<br>
is called counter current multiplier<br>
system. The loop of Henle functions as a<br>
counter current multiplier due to its close<br>
proximity of ascending and descending<br>
limbs,  permeability of the descending<br>
limb to water, impermeability of the<br>
descending limb to solute, permeability<br>
of the ascending limb to solute, passive<br>
transport of solute in thin ascending<br>
limb, and active transport mechanism for<br>
the thick ascending limb (Figure 7.10).<br>
<b>These features enable the loop of Henle to</b><br>
create a very high concentration gradient<br>
between the tissue ﬂuid and blood in the<br>
medulla of the kidney and the urine in<br>
the collecting ducts. The loop of Henle<br>
is connected at one end to the proximal<br>
convoluted tubule and at the other end<br>
to the distal convoluted tubule. It ﬁrst<br>
descends deep into the medulla and then<br>
bends and ascends into the cortex again.<br>
<b>Throughout its length, it is surrounded by</b><br>
ﬁne looped blood vessels called the Vasa<br>
recta. These vessels carry blood from the<br>
glomerulus to the renal vein.<br>
<b>Salts like sodium and chloride ions,</b><br>
diffuse passively out of the thin ascending<br>
limb and pumped actively out of the<br>
thick ascending limb into the surrounding<br>
tissue ﬂuids. This pumping of salts out<br>
of the limb creates an osmotic gradient<br>
which draws water out of the descending<br>
limb into the medulla. This is because the<br>
ascending limb is impermeable to water;<br>
therefore, water moves out of the limb<br>
only to the descending limb. When water<br>
in the descending limb is pumped out, it<br>
causes the ﬂuid in the descending limb to<br>
have a slightly higher salt concentration<br>
compared to the ascending limb. The<br>
process continues down the length of the<br>
loop so that this concentration effect is<br>
multiplied. The counter current multiplier<br>
means that the ﬂuid in and around the loop<br>
of Henle becomes saltier as it goes down<br>
the loop, and it is saltiest at the bottom<br>
end of the loop. In contrast, it becomes<br>
less salty as it goes up the ascending limb<br>
<b>Therefore, the ﬁnal salt concentration</b><br>
depends on the length of the loop, the<br>
longer the loop; the higher the ﬁnal salt<br>
concentration in the tissues.<br>

<b>Hormonal control of osmoregulation</b><br>
<b>As water moves in the loop of Henle, it</b><br>
leaves the loop of Henle with the water<br>
potential greater than that of blood plasma.<br>
<b>This creates a concentration gradient</b><br>
between ﬂuid in the distal convoluted<br>
tubule and the surrounding tissue ﬂuid.<br>
<b>The concentration gradient is enhanced</b><br>
when salts are actively pumped out of the<br>
distal convoluted tubule. Reabsorption of<br>
water in the distal convoluted tubule and<br>
the collecting duct will depend on their<br>
permeability, which is controlled by the<br>
hormone called Antidiuretic hormone<br>
(ADH). This hormone is released by the<br>
posterior lobe of the pituitary gland in<br>
response to an increased concentration of<br>
salts in the blood. When ADH is present,<br>
more water is reabsorbed (blood volume<br>
and pressure rises) and the urine produced<br>
is more concentrated.<br>
<b>The regulation of salt and blood pressure</b><br>
are closely connected. If the sodium ion<br>
concentration in the blood is low, the<br>
blood water potential increases and water<br>
moves by osmosis into the tissue, slightly<br>
lowering the blood pressure. When blood<br>
pressure is not sufﬁcient (below the set<br>
point), secretory cells near the glomerulus,<br>
juxtaglomerular apparatus secrete, renin.<br>
<b>The later is an enzyme that changes</b><br>
angiotensinogen (angiotensinogen is a<br>
large plasma protein produced by the liver)<br>
into Angiotensin I. Later Angiotensin<br>
<b>I is converted to Angiotensin II. When</b><br>
<b>Angiotensin II reaches the adrenal cortex,</b><br>
it stimulates the secretion of aldosterone.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   349</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:08<br>

<b>Biology for Advanced Level Secondary Schools</b><br>
<b>Student’s Book Form Five</b><br>
<b>This hormone promotes the excretion</b><br>
of potassium ions and the reabsorption<br>
of sodium ions at the distal convoluted<br>
tubules. The reabsorption of sodium ions<br>
is followed by the reabsorption of water.<br>
<b>Therefore, blood volume and blood</b><br>
pressure increase.<br>
<b>Adaptations of mammals to arid and</b><br>
semi-arid conditions<br>
<b>High temperature and low rainfall</b><br>
characterise some areas of this world. It<br>
becomes hard to believe that animals can<br>
survive in arid and semi-arid conditions.<br>
<b>However, these animals survive because</b><br>
they have adaptations that allow them<br>
to live in the hot, dry conditions.<br>
<b>These</b><br>
adaptations<br>
help<br>
to<br>
balance<br>
thermoregulation with water gain and<br>
loss. For instance, many mammals that<br>
live in the desert obtain much or all of<br>
their water from the food they consume.<br>
<b>The reduced water intake is partially</b><br>
balanced through concentrated urine and<br>
dry faeces. Evaporative cooling helps to<br>
regulate temperature.<br>
<b>To limit the water loss through evaporative</b><br>
cooling, some mammals are nocturnal;<br>
have light coloration and other body<br>
features to help them dissipate heat and<br>
use micro-environments to reduce heat<br>
gain. This is only a short list of the many<br>
amazing adaptations. Characteristically,<br>
arid regions receive 100-250 mm of rain<br>
a year and semi-arid regions receive 250-<br>
500 mm of rain per year. The following<br>
are some of adaptations of mammals to<br>
the life in arid or semi-arid conditions:<br>
a)	 Water and food consumption<br>
<b>Human beings obtain about 60% of the</b><br>
water they need from the ingested liquids,<br>
30% from ingested food, and 10% from<br>
metabolism. Rodents and camels adapted<br>
to arid conditions obtain approximately<br>
90% of water from metabolism and 10%<br>
from ingested food.<br>
b)	 Excretory adaptations<br>
<b>The ability to excrete concentrated urine</b><br>
and dry feaces is an important adaptation<br>
to arid conditions. Mammals that are<br>
adapted to the desert have very long<br>
loop of Henle compared to animals that<br>
live in less arid regions and in aquatic<br>
environments. A longer loop of Henle<br>
allows urine to become very concentrated<br>
due to osmotic gradients in the kidneys.<br>
<b>Desert rodents produce concentrated urine</b><br>
about ﬁve times as that of humans. Longer<br>
loop of Henle increases the efﬁciency of<br>
water reabsorption and hence a means for<br>
conserving water.<br>
c)	 Behavioural adaptations<br>
<b>Behavioural adaptations are used to</b><br>
reduce the amount of heat gained by<br>
animals. Therefore, they reduce the<br>
need for evaporative cooling. One basic<br>
behavioural adaptation is the timing of<br>
activity rhythms. Nocturnal animals<br>
are able to regulate their heat load by<br>
resting during the day, since night-time<br>
temperatures can be 15-20 °C lower than<br>
the daytime. Examples of nocturnal<br>
animals include the quoll, bilby, and the<br>
spinifex hopping mouse.<br>
<b>BIOLOGY FORM 5  KIWANDANI.indd   350</b><br>
10/10/2019   14:08<br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>GOVERNMENT PROPERTY</b><br>
<b>FOR ONLINE USE ONLY</b><br>
<b>DO NOT DUPLICATE</b><br>
351<br>
<b>Regulation (Homeostasis)</b><br>
<b>Burrows</b><br>
are<br>
another<br>
type<br>
of<br>
microenvironment that is used by smaller<br>
mammals. In Arizona burrow temperature<br>
of a round-tailed ground squirrel was<br>
recorded. The air temperature was 40 °C and<br>
the soil surface was 70 °C but the burrow<br>
temperature did not exceed 29 °C. Many<br>
burrows are at a depth where evaporative<br>
cooling is not needed; it does not get hot<br>
enough in the burrows to require this<br>
technique.<br>
d)	 Torpor and metabolic rate<br>
<b>Many mammals such as rodents and</b><br>
squirrels enter a period of torpor in<br>
response to severe heat. This is a period<br>
when metabolism decreases, the heart<br>
beat and respiratory system slows down<br>
based on a circadian rhythm. Torpor<br>
can be considered as a water conserving<br>
mechanism because the animal’s body<br>
temperature is lowered and it does not<br>
have to rely heavily on evaporation. If<br>
the period of torpor becomes longer, it is<br>
called aestivation or summer dormancy.<br>
<b>Aestivation allows an animal to survive</b><br>
when there are high temperatures and<br>
a scarcity of water and or food. An<br>
aestivating animal can live longer with<br>
its energy reserves due to lowered<br>
metabolism. Moreover, there is a reduced<br>
water loss through lowered breath rates.<br>
<b>Metabolic rates are lower during torpor</b><br>
and aestivation. Mammals adapted to<br>
desert climates have lower metabolic<br>
rates in general than similar mammals that<br>
live in extreme climates. This reduces the<br>
internal heat load as well as the water used<br>
for evaporation.<br>

between haploid and diploid organisms or refers to the<br>
occurrence in the plant life cycle of both a multicellular<br>
diploid organism and a multicellular haploid organism,<br>

<b>The archegonium is a multicellular, often flask-shaped</b><br>
structure that contains a single egg.<br>

<b>These are electron carrier agents and essential component</b><br>

reduction and oxidation (losing or gaining electron) but<br>
does not react with oxygen.<br>
<b>Dendrites</b><br>

other neural cells to the cell body<br>
<b>Dissection</b><br>

structure and body systems<br>

<b>Endotherm</b><br>
<b>Organisms that use internally generated heat to maintain</b><br>
body temperature. Their body temperature tends to stay<br>
steady regardless of the environmental temperatures. The<br>
endotherms primarily include birds and mammals.<br>
<b>Epiglottis</b><br>
<b>Elastic cartilage covered with a mucosa membrane,</b><br>
found in the throat that prevent the food from entering<br>
the windpipe (trachea) and the lungs<br>
<b>Facultative parasites</b><br>
<b>Organisms that can live either as parasites when the host</b><br>
is available or as a free living organism when the host is<br>
not available<br>
<b>Foetus</b><br>
<b>Developing offspring inside the maternal uterus (womb)</b><br>
in a prenatal stage between embryonic stage and birth<br>
<b>Fossorial</b><br>
<b>Animals that live underground, digging tunnels and</b><br>
burrows. Some of them are completely subterranean,<br>
while others come up to the surface for some hours of the<br>
day.<br>
<b>Glycogenolysis</b><br>
<b>A process whereby glycogen which is the primary</b><br>
carbohydrate stored in the liver and muscle cells of<br>
animals, is broken down to glucose in order to provide<br>
immediate energy and maintain blood glucose levels<br>
during fasting. This process occurs primarily in the<br>
liver and is stimulated by two hormones, glucagon and<br>
epinephrine (adrenaline).<br>
<b>Grazing</b><br>
<b>A type of herbivory in which an animal eats grass or</b><br>
forbs. Such animals are called grazers, and include cows<br>
and sheep<br>
<b>Guard cells</b><br>
<b>Specialized epidermal cells of the leaves and stems. They</b><br>
contain chloroplasts used in photosynthesis. They occur<br>
in pairs that form a pore between them called stomata, that<br>
regulate the gaseous exchange by opening and closing of<br>
the stomatal pore.<br>

<b>Hibernation</b><br>
<b>Adaptation that helps many animals conserve energy by</b><br>
remaining inactive, greatly slowing  their metabolism<br>
and reducing  their body temperature for days, weeks,<br>
or months. Animals hibernate in order to survive long<br>
periods.<br>
<b>Homoeothermic animals</b><br>
<b>Animals that maintain a stable body temperature by</b><br>
regulating metabolic processes. Examples are birds and<br>
mammals.<br>
<b>Hyperthermia</b><br>
<b>Elevated body temperature due to failed thermoregulation</b><br>
that occurs when a body produces or absorbs more heat<br>
than it dissipates.<br>
<b>Hypertonic</b><br>
<b>A condition where the external environment has higher</b><br>
concentration of solutes than the internal environment<br>
<b>Hypotonic</b><br>
<b>A condition where the external environment has lower</b><br>
concentrations of solutes than that of the internal<br>
environment<br>
<b>In situ</b><br>
<b>Item being in its natural, normal, or original place or</b><br>
position<br>
<b>Intercostal muscles</b><br>

chest wall. They run between one rib and another, and<br>
they are involved in the mechanical aspect of breathing.<br>
<b>Lean muscle mass</b><br>

weight due to your fat mass<br>
<b>Lean mass = total weight – fat mass</b><br>

<b>Plasmalemma</b><br>

from the outside environment (the extracellular space)<br>
that protects the cell from its environment, usually<br>
consisting of a lipid bilayer with embedded protein.<br>
<b>Pleural membrane</b><br>

pleura and inner layer visceral pleura with a space<br>
between them called pleural cavity filled with pleural<br>
fluid secreted by the membrane.<br>

<b>Poikilothermic animals</b><br>

such as certain insects for defence against predators<br>
Ribulose biphosphate (RuBP) An organic substance (a 5 carbon compound) involved<br>
in Calvin cycle which is part of the light independent<br>
reactions of photosynthesis<br>
<b>Schwan cells</b><br>
<b>Cells in the peripheral nervous system that form the</b><br>
myelin sheath around a neuron’s axon.  Schwan cells is<br>
also called neurilemmocytes.<br>
<b>Svedberg value (s or sv)</b><br>
<b>A measure of a particle’s size based on its sedimentation</b><br>
rate, i.e. how fast a particle of given size and shape ‘settles’<br>
to the bottom of a solution. The units of a ribosome are<br>
often described by their Svedberg (s) values, which are<br>
based upon their rate of sedimentation in a centrifuge.<br>
<b>Tegument</b><br>
<b>The outer covering of platyhelminths, made up by an</b><br>
epidermal layer and sometimes provided with cilia,<br>
spines and glandular tissue<br>
<b>Uniramia</b><br>
<b>A group of arthropods characterised by having</b><br>
appendages (antennae and legs) with only a single ramus<br>
(branch). They also have a single pair of antennae and an<br>
exoskeleton that is strengthened  by tanning process and<br>
made water proof by a wax layer. The group has three<br>
main classes: the Chilopoda (centipedes), Diplopoda<br>
(millipedes) and Hexapoda or Insecta (insects and their<br>
relatives).<br>
<b>Ureotelic organisms</b><br>
<b>Animals that excrete urea as the primary nitrogenous</b><br>
waste material. Examples include cartilagenous fish,<br>
adult amphibians, and mammals including humans.<br>
<b>Uricotelic organisms</b><br>
<b>Organisms that excrete uric acid, example birds and</b><br>
reptiles<br>
<b>Vaccine</b><br>
<b>A biological preparartion that provides active acquired</b><br>
immunity against a particular disease<br>
